title: Recover, Discover, Plan: Learning Skills and Concepts from Robot Failures
summary: Intelligent robots should not only recover from failures, but also acquire the abstract knowledge needed to avoid them in the future. While reinforcement learning (RL) can learn reactive recovery behaviors, training a separate policy for every distinct failure mode is highly inefficient. We introduce Recovery-Driven Synthesis of Relational Concepts (ReSYNC), the first approach that progressively discovers and refines state abstractions (relational predicates) from failure-recovery experience to support abstract planning. Unlike purely reactive methods, ReSYNC jointly learns skills and concepts through an incremental dual-learning process. In the skill-learning phase, the robot uses RL to learn to recover from failures seen in training tasks. In the concept-learning phase, the robot discovers new relational predicates and refines its abstract planning model to explain and generalize the learned recovery behaviors. This interaction enables ReSYNC to convert local recoveries seen during training into global failure avoidance at test time. Across four simulated domains, we show that ReSYNC's ability to continually expand and refine its abstraction library allows it to solve long-horizon, previously unseen problems, outperforming strong baselines by over 50%. Additionally, we demonstrate sim-to-real transfer of ReSYNC, where it performs real-world non-prehensile manipulation skills and generalizes to unseen scenarios through abstract planning. Overall, ReSYNC represents a significant step toward robots that autonomously acquire abstractions for scalable, failure-aware planning in the physical world.

AI.tldr: ReSYNC jointly learns recovery skills and relational abstractions to enable abstract planning for failure avoidance, yielding superior long-horizon performance and sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Training a separate policy per failure is inefficient; robots need abstract, generalizable knowledge to avoid failures in unseen tasks.
AI.method: Two-phase dual-learning: (1) skill-learning via RL to recover from observed failures; (2) concept-learning to discover/refine relational predicates and update the abstract planning model; iterative interaction.
AI.result: Across four simulated domains, ReSYNC outperforms strong baselines by >50%; demonstrates sim-to-real transfer with real-world non-prehensile manipulation and generalization to unseen scenarios.
AI.conclusion: ReSYNC enables autonomous acquisition of abstractions for scalable, failure-aware planning in physical robots, bridging failure recovery and abstract planning.

title: Guava: An Effective and Universal Harness for Embodied Manipulation
summary: Language models trained on large-scale vision-language data have demonstrated strong potential for embodied agents. Harnessing models through embodied tools use offers a promising alternative to end-to-end vision-language-action systems by combining high-level reasoning with external modules for perception, planning, and control. However, it remains unclear what makes an effective harness for embodied manipulation, and to what extent such a harness can unlock embodied capabilities in a wide range of reasoning models. In this work, we present Guava, a harness framework for embodied tool use developed through systematic exploration of the design space of agent workflows, action spaces, and observation spaces. Our study identifies three key ingredients for effective embodied agents: iterative perception-reasoning-action loops, semantic action abstractions, and multimodal observations. To understand whether these design principles are universal even to small models, we develop an end-to-end training pipeline that distills embodied manipulation capabilities into a 4B open-source model using fewer than 2K trajectories collected entirely in simulation. Experimental results in both simulation and real-world environments show performance comparable to frontier proprietary models while exhibiting strong generalization to unseen objects, novel instructions, and long-horizon tasks. Results suggest that a well-designed harness can serve as a scalable, model-agnostic interface for embodied manipulation, enabling strong emergent embodied capabilities in compact open-source models with minimal training data.

AI.tldr: Guava introduces a harness framework for embodied tool use, identifying iterative perception-reasoning-action loops, semantic action abstractions, and multimodal observations as key ingredients. It distills embodied manipulation into a 4B open-source model using fewer than 2K simulated trajectories, achieving performance on par with frontier models and strong generalization, suggesting harnesses are scalable, model-agnostic interfaces for emergent embodied capabilities.
AI.motivation: To determine what makes an effective harness for embodied manipulation and to assess whether such a harness enables universal benefits across reasoning models, including small ones.
AI.method: Systematically explore the design space of agent workflows, action spaces, and observation spaces to extract three core ingredients. Build an end-to-end training pipeline that distills embodied manipulation into a 4B open-source model using under 2K simulation trajectories. Evaluate in both simulation and real-world environments, focusing on generalization to unseen objects, novel instructions, and long-horizon tasks.
AI.result: The approach yields performance comparable to frontier proprietary models and demonstrates strong generalization to unseen objects and tasks, validating the effectiveness of the harness design and its data-efficient training pipeline.
AI.conclusion: A well-designed harness can serve as a scalable, model-agnostic interface for embodied manipulation, enabling strong emergent capabilities in compact open-source models with minimal training data.

title: PAIWorld: A 3D-Consistent World Foundation Model for Robotic Manipulation
summary: World foundation models (WFMs) are powerful simulators, yet they predominantly operate in a single-view setting and lack the multi-view 3D consistency required for robotic manipulation. While robotic systems rely on multiple cameras (egocentric, eye-to-hand, and wrist-mounted) for policy learning, current multi-view world models simply concatenate view tokens without explicit geometric reasoning. This causes cross-view object drift, depth inconsistency, and texture misalignment. We trace these failures to two deficiencies: the absence of an explicit inter-view communication mechanism and the lack of a 3D geometric prior. We argue that resolving both simultaneously is necessary and sufficient. To address this, we present PAIWorld, a framework that augments diffusion-transformer world models via three core components: (1) Geometry-Aware Cross-View Attention blocks that establish an explicit pathway across views, (2) Geometric Rotary Position Embedding that encodes camera ray directions and extrinsic poses into the attention mechanism, and (3) Latent 3D-REPA, which distills 3D-aware features from frozen 3D foundation models to ensure 3D consistency. Built upon a DiT-based world foundation model, PAIWorld achieves state-of-the-art multi-view 3D consistency on robotic manipulation benchmarks, ranking 1st on the WorldArena leaderboard and 2nd on the AgiBot-Challenge2026 leaderboard, while enabling downstream applications such as model-based planning, world action models, and multi-view policy post-training.

AI.tldr: PAIWorld improves multi-view diffusion-transformer world models for robotic manipulation by introducing explicit cross-view geometry-aware attention, ray-extrinsic encoded positional embeddings, and latent 3D feature distillation from frozen 3D foundation models, building on a DiT-based WFM to achieve state-of-the-art 3D consistency.
AI.motivation: Global world foundation models often operate in single-view or mere token-concatenation for multi-view inputs, leading to cross-view object drift, depth inconsistency, and texture misalignment. There is a need for explicit cross-view communication and a 3D geometric prior to ensure consistent, geometry-aware predictions across multiple camera views in robotic manipulation.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Geometry-Aware Cross-View Attention blocks enabling explicit information flow across views; (2) Geometric Rotary Position Embedding encoding camera rays and extrinsic poses into attention; (3) Latent 3D-REPA distills 3D-aware features from frozen 3D foundation models to enforce 3D consistency. Built atop a DiT-based WFM (diffusion-transformer).
AI.result: PAIWorld achieves state-of-the-art multi-view 3D consistency on robotic manipulation benchmarks, ranking 1st on the WorldArena leaderboard and 2nd on the AgiBot-Challenge2026 leaderboard, and enables downstream tasks such as model-based planning, world action models, and multi-view policy post-training.
AI.conclusion: Resolving both explicit inter-view communication and a 3D geometric prior is necessary and sufficient to address cross-view failures in multi-view world models; PAIWorld demonstrates that integrating geometry-aware attention, geometric positional encoding, and 3D feature distillation yields robust multi-view consistency and practical downstream benefits.

title: VEGA: Learning Navigation VLAs from In-the-Wild Egocentric Video with Geometric Trajectory Supervision
summary: We introduce VEGA, an approach for training navigation VisionLanguage-Action (VLA) models from unlabeled egocentric navigation videos. Internet-scale egocentric videos provide a scalable source of navigation-relevant visual observations, capturing cluttered scenes, close-range obstacles, and natural human motion through real-world spaces. However, these videos are not directly usable for policy learning because they do not provide obstacle-aware trajectories conditioned on explicit navigation goals in the robot's coordinate frame. VEGA addresses this gap by reconstructing local scene geometry from monocular video, sampling navigation goals (represented as text, image, or spatial waypoints) and generating obstacle-aware trajectories using the constructed geometry. The resulting trajectory distribution is then used to train a flow-matching VLA navigation policy. By using geometry exclusively during training, VEGA distills obstacle-aware planning directly into a vision-based policy. Furthermore, we introduce VEGA-Bench, a benchmark containing 250k scenes and approximately 5 million navigation goals paired with scene geometry, designed to evaluate goal progress, collision avoidance, and obstacle clearance of VLAs. Our evaluation shows that VEGA achieves competitive goal progress while reducing collisions by 33.0% and improving obstacle clearance by 17.9% over the strongest baseline on VEGABench, while improving success by at least 150.0%, reducing collisions by at least 66.7%, and improving obstacle clearance by at least 60.0% in real-world trials. Ultimately, we demonstrate that video-derived geometric supervision provides a scalable and effective signal for training obstacle-aware navigation VLAs. The code and benchmark will be released at the time of publication.

AI.tldr: VEGA trains obstacle-aware navigation VLAs from unlabeled egocentric videos by reconstructing local scene geometry, sampling navigation goals, and generating obstacle-aware trajectories to supervise a flow-matching policy; introduces VEGA-Bench with 250k scenes and ~5M goals; demonstrates safety and efficiency gains over baselines in both simulation and real-world trials.
AI.motivation: There is a mismatch between abundant unlabeled egocentric video data and the need for obstacle-aware navigation policies conditioned on explicit goals in the robot frame. Monocular geometry can provide a scalable supervisory signal to bridge this gap and distill planning into a vision-based policy without hand-annotated trajectories.
AI.method: Reconstruct local scene geometry from monocular video; sample navigation goals represented as text, image, or spatial waypoints; synthesize obstacle-aware trajectories using the reconstructed geometry; train a flow-matching Vision-Language-Action (VLA) navigation policy with this geometry-derived supervision; introduce VEGA-Bench dataset (≈250k scenes, ≈5M navigation goals) to evaluate goal progress, collision avoidance, and obstacle clearance.
AI.result: VEGA achieves competitive goal progress while significantly reducing collisions (−33.0%) and improving obstacle clearance (+17.9%) over the strongest VEGABench baseline; in real-world trials, VEGA improves success by ≥150%, reduces collisions by ≥66.7%, and improves obstacle clearance by ≥60%.
AI.conclusion: Video-derived geometric supervision provides a scalable and effective signal to train obstacle-aware VLAs, enabling distillation of planning capabilities into vision-based policies. Code and VEGA-Bench will be released with publication.

title: Denoising Distances in Metric Measure Spaces
summary: Recent work studied the problem of finding clusters and denoising pairwise distances from noisy distances of points sampled on a manifold. We study the same problems in more general metric measure spaces under \lowerphiregularity{}. We give an algorithm that extracts large localized clusters around every sampled point and uses them to denoise distances to any fixed accuracy, with near-linear running time in the dense fixed-accuracy regime. We also show how to achieve much higher accuracy with a non-efficient algorithm. This suggests that unlike the Riemannian case, denoising to higher accuracy in more general metric spaces has a statistical-computational gap.

AI.tldr: Algorithmic clustering and distance denoising in low-regularity metric measure spaces: per-point local clusters enable fixed-accuracy denoising in near-linear time, while higher-accuracy denoising requires non-efficient methods, suggesting a statistical-computational gap compared to Riemannian settings.
AI.motivation: Generalize clustering and distance denoising from manifolds to metric measure spaces with weaker regularity; characterize computational limits across accuracy regimes.
AI.method: For each sampled point, extract a large localized cluster; use these clusters to denoise pairwise distances to a fixed accuracy. Achieves near-linear running time in the dense fixed-accuracy regime. Also present a non-efficient algorithm that attains much higher accuracy.
AI.result: Existence of large, localized clusters around samples; fixed-accuracy denoising feasible in near-linear time; higher-accuracy denoising achievable only non-efficiently; indicates a statistical-computational gap in general metric spaces.
AI.conclusion: In low-regularity metric measure spaces, high-accuracy denoising may be statistically possible but computationally intractable, unlike the more favorable Riemannian setting where efficient high-accuracy denoising can be achieved.

title: Budget-Aware Adaptive Adversarial Patches for Black-Box Object Detection
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP 2026)
summary: Adversarial patches pose a practical threat to modern object detectors. Prior work shows vulnerability, but three gaps limit actionable insight: (i) few \emph{score-based black-box} attacks \emph{jointly} optimize patch \emph{location, texture, and size} under tight query budgets; (ii) success is rarely tied to the patch's \emph{visual footprint}; and (iii) evaluations often conflate EOT robustness with plain-view suppression. We present \method{}, a query-efficient, budget-adaptive black-box attack that couples a lightweight \emph{Contextual Thompson-Sampling} placer with NES-style pixel updates, growing the patch only when progress stalls. Reporting is anchored by a \emph{strict plain-image} suppression test; EOT is audited but never used as a substitute for success, and optional appearance/printability weights expose strength--visibility trade-offs. Across YOLOv5, Faster R-CNN, and YOLOS, \method{} achieves strong suppression on CNN-based detectors and substantial suppression on the transformer-based detector, using compact patches and exposing clear query--footprint trade-offs relative to fixed-size and heuristic baselines. A print--capture pilot further shows transfer across unseen physical objects and viewpoints.

AI.tldr: Introduces method, a query-efficient, budget-adaptive black-box adversarial patch attack that jointly optimizes patch location, texture, and size using a Contextual Thompson-Sampling placer and NES-style pixel updates, with strict plain-image suppression tests and optional appearance/printability weights; demonstrates strong suppression across YOLOv5, Faster R-CNN, and YOLOS using compact patches, plus a print-capture pilot for unseen objects.
AI.motivation: To address three gaps in prior work: (i) lack of score-based black-box attacks that jointly optimize patch location, texture, and size under tight query budgets; (ii) failure to relate attack success to the patch's visual footprint (size/visibility); (iii) evaluations that conflate EOT robustness with plain-view suppression.
AI.method: Proposes a Contextual Thompson-Sampling placer to decide patch location and size, coupled with NES-style (natural evolution strategies) pixel updates to optimize texture; grows the patch only when progress stalls (budget-adaptive). Evaluation includes a strict plain-image suppression test, auditing of EOT but not relying on it as sole success criterion, and optional appearance/printability weights to reveal success/visibility trade-offs.
AI.result: Achieves strong suppression on CNN-based detectors (YOLOv5, Faster R-CNN) and substantial suppression on a transformer-based detector (YOLOS) using compact patches; exposes clear query–footprint trade-offs relative to fixed-size and heuristic baselines; a print–capture pilot demonstrates transfer across unseen physical objects and viewpoints.
AI.conclusion: Offers a practical, interpretable black-box attack framework that foregrounds patch footprint and deployment constraints; improves evaluation rigor by separating plain-image suppression from EOT robustness; enables explicit exploration of appearance vs. printability trade-offs and paves the way for more robust defenses.

title: Gaussian Mixture Attention: Linear-Time Sequence Mixing via Probabilistic Latent Routing
summary: The dense token-to-token interaction pattern of standard dot-product attention remains a central bottleneck in scaling Transformer architectures to long contexts. We introduce \textbf{Gaussian Mixture Attention (GMA)}, a probabilistic attention-style sequence mixer that replaces explicit pairwise query--key comparison with routing through $K$ learned Gaussian mixture components. Queries and keys are mapped to posterior \textit{responsibility} vectors over a shared latent routing space; their overlap defines an implicit responsibility-space affinity, while values are written into and read from a $K$-slot latent memory. By exploiting the associativity of matrix multiplication, GMA avoids materializing the induced $N\times N$ affinity matrix and instead uses two responsibility matrices whose dominant activation storage scales as $\mathcal{O}(NK)$ rather than $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ for fixed $K$. We formulate bidirectional and causal variants of GMA, provide an end-to-end differentiable parameterization of the Gaussian mixture components, and analyze its responsibility-modulated gradient structure, constrained non-negative low-rank affinity interpretation, and local routing stability. Empirically, GMA exhibits the intended fixed-$K$ linear memory scaling and is competitive with attention-style baselines on long-context classification, while causal GMA improves over tested linear/random-feature attention variants on WikiText-103 but remains behind optimized causal SDPA and Mamba in the current implementation. Analysis of learned responsibilities further shows broad component usage and moderate alignment with surface-form token categories, supporting GMA as a probabilistic, interpretable, fixed-$K$ linear-time attention-style alternative rather than a universal replacement for optimized softmax attention or state-space models.

AI.tldr: GMA is a fixed-K Gaussian mixture attention that routes queries and keys through a shared latent space to form affinities, achieving O(NK) memory with competitive performance on long-context tasks, but not universally beating optimized baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the O(N^2) memory bottleneck of standard dot-product attention for long sequences; provide a probabilistic, interpretable, fixed-K alternative that scales linearly with sequence length.
AI.method: Map queries and keys to posterior responsibility vectors over K Gaussian components in a shared latent routing space; compute affinities via overlap of responsibilities; use a K-slot latent memory for values; leverage associativity of matrix multiplication to avoid materializing N×N affinity; parameterize Gaussian components end-to-end; provide bidirectional and causal variants; analyze gradient structure, non-negative low-rank affinity interpretation, and routing stability.
AI.result: Demonstrates fixed-K linear memory scaling O(NK); competitive with attention-style baselines on long-context classification; causal GMA improves over some linear/random-feature attentions on WikiText-103 but lags behind optimized causal SDPA and Mamba in current implementation; learned responsibilities show broad component usage and partial alignment with token categories.
AI.conclusion: GMA presents a probabilistic, interpretable, fixed-K linear-time attention-style alternative rather than a universal replacement for softmax attention or state-space models; shows promise for long-context modeling but requires further improvements to exceed optimized causal attention baselines.

title: NAVI-Orbital: First In-Orbit Demonstration of a Zero-Shot Vision-Language Model for Autonomous Earth Observation
summary: As Earth Observation data generation outpaces downlink bandwidth and human-in-the-loop processing, a widening gap has emerged between onboard collection and actionable ground intelligence. This paper presents NAVI-Orbital, a software system deployed on a Low Earth Orbit (LEO) spacecraft. On April 16, 2026, NAVI-Orbital achieved what is, to the authors' knowledge, the first in-orbit demonstration of a vision-language model performing autonomous multi-modal inference entirely onboard. NAVI-Orbital uses a local vision-language model (Gemma 3) to classify each captured scene, produce a text description of its content and the relationships between its features, and respond to operator follow-up via natural-language dialogue. The system is re-tasked through plain-English prompts in place of conventional command sequences, and is orchestrated by a graph-based state machine (LangGraph) coordinating dedicated agents for detection and dialogue. Results across ground benchmarking (88.16% accuracy on the 7,960-image curated AID benchmark), Flatsat validation, and live in-orbit captures of newly acquired, previously unseen Earth imagery (including uncorrected YAM-9 imagery, processed onboard with hardware-accelerated GPU inference and no fine-tuning for the flight instrument) demonstrate the feasibility of running foundation models on satellite-class edge computers to invert the conventional acquire-then-downlink-everything bandwidth profile through semantic compression of Earth observations in-orbit.

AI.tldr: NAVI-Orbital demonstrates onboard, autonomous vision-language inference on a LEO spacecraft, enabling semantic compression of Earth observations and operator dialogue without ground-based fine-tuning. It achieves 88.16% accuracy on AID and validates in-orbit, showing feasibility of satellite-class edge GPUs running foundation models to invert Acquire-Downlink-Everything to an on-orbit, semantic data product.
AI.motivation: To address the widening gap between rapid Earth observation data generation and limited downlink bandwidth/human-in-the-loop processing by pushing intelligent capabilities onto the spacecraft, enabling autonomous scene understanding and dialogue with operators.
AI.method: Deploys a local vision-language model (Gemma 3) on a satellite-class edge computer, orchestrated by a graph-based state machine (LangGraph) with dedicated detection and dialogue agents. Uses plain-English prompts instead of traditional command sequences, processes uncorrected YAM-9 imagery onboard with hardware-accelerated GPU inference and no fine-tuning, and performs end-to-end tasks: scene classification, image description, relation extraction, and live operator dialogue.
AI.result: Benchmark and in-orbit validation show 88.16% accuracy on the 7,960-image AID benchmark; Flatsat validation and live in-orbit captures of unseen imagery confirm feasibility of running foundation models onboard, enabling semantic compression of observations and reducing downlink needs.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the feasibility of satellite-class edge computing for onboard, autonomous, multimodal inference using foundation models. This enables semantic understanding and operator interaction directly on orbit, potentially transforming data workflows by shifting from raw data downlink to onboard semantic content.

title: N(CO)$^2$: Neural Combinatorial Optimization with Chance Constraints to Solve Stochastic Orienteering
summary: Neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) offers a promising alternative to traditional heuristic-based methods for solving complex graph optimization problems by proposing to learn heuristics through data. This class of problems frequently arises in automation, as it can be used to model a variety of applications. While NCO has been extensively studied for deterministic combinatorial optimization problems, there are only a few works that aim to solve stochastic combinatorial optimization problems. In this work, we present N(CO)$^2$: Neural Combinatorial Optimization with Chance cOnstraints to solve the Stochastic Orienteering Problem (SOP) without the use of hand-crafted heuristics. By integrating a reinforcement learning (RL) framework, the model optimizes path selection under uncertainty, effectively balancing exploration and exploitation. Empirical results demonstrate that our method generalizes well across diverse SOP instances, achieving competitive performance compared to the state-of-the-art mixed-integer linear program (MILP) for the task. The proposed approach reduces human effort in heuristic design while enabling adaptive and efficient decision-making in uncertain environments.

AI.tldr: Neural Combinatorial Optimization with Chance Constraints (N(CO)^2) uses reinforcement learning to solve the Stochastic Orienteering Problem without hand-crafted heuristics, achieving MILP-competitive performance and better adaptability under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Stochastic combinatorial optimization is underexplored and relies on hand-crafted heuristics. There is a need for end-to-end learned methods that can adapt to uncertainty and reduce human design effort.
AI.method: Integrates a reinforcement learning framework into neural combinatorial optimization with chance constraints to model uncertainty; learns path-selection heuristics without manual heuristic design, balancing exploration and exploitation.
AI.result: Empirically, the method generalizes well across SOP instances and matches or surpasses state-of-the-art MILP performance, reducing reliance on handcrafted heuristics.
AI.conclusion: N(CO)^2 demonstrates that end-to-end learning can effectively handle stochastic combinatorial optimization under uncertainty, offering adaptive decision-making and broader applicability to uncertain graph-based problems.

title: Repair Entropy in Dynamic Geometric Nearest-Neighbour Structures
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables; code and dataset provided as ancillary files
summary: We study dynamic geometric data structures for exact nearest-neighbour maintenance under small motions. For each point we store a certificate consisting of its nearest neighbour and the two smallest neighbour distances, with clearance $c_i=d^i_2-d^i_1$. A triangle-inequality argument gives a sharp validity radius: after a step of maximum displacement $\varepsilon$, every certificate with $c_i>4\varepsilon$ remains valid, so all possible failures are confined to a repair frontier $F_t$. We introduce repair-frontier entropy $H(F_t)$, the normalized Shannon entropy of failed certificates over index cells, as a workload descriptor for choosing between event-driven repair, batched repair, and full rebuild. The resulting maintenance rule repairs only the frontier in $O(|F_t|\log N)$ time under bounded cell occupancy, while a full rebuild costs $Θ(N)$; moreover, entropy lower-bounds the number of frontier cells touched by event-driven repair and shifts the empirical repair-rebuild crossover. We evaluate ten motion families in $d\in{2,3}$, with $N$ up to $16,000$, using an exact tiled GPU oracle and a GPU grid rebuild as ground truth and competitor. Across $2400$ labelled transitions, the validity rule misses no invalid certificate, low-pressure frontiers are usually cheaper to repair incrementally, and diffuse frontiers of the same size are more expensive for event-driven repair but not for batched repair. The released dataset records frontier geometry, certificate audits, per-strategy times, and best-strategy labels.

AI.tldr: Dynamic geometric data structures for exact nearest-neighbour maintenance under small motions using certificate-based validity and a repair frontier. Introduces frontier entropy H(F_t) to guide repair strategies, achieving O(|F_t| log N) incremental repairs versus Θ(N) full rebuild; validated empirically on 2D/3D motion data (N up to 16k) with GPU ground truth, with dataset release.
AI.motivation: Maintain exact nearest-neighbour information efficiently under small motions; avoid unnecessary full rebuilds; quantify workload with a principled metric to adapt repair strategy.
AI.method: For each point store a certificate: its nearest neighbour and the two smallest neighbour distances. The clearance c_i = d_2^i - d_1^i defines a validity radius after motion; after a step of maximum displacement ε, certificates with c_i > 4ε remain valid. Failures are confined to a repair frontier F_t. Define frontier entropy H(F_t) as the normalized Shannon entropy of failed certificates over index cells. Compare event-driven repair, batched repair, and full rebuild. The maintenance rule repairs only the frontier in O(|F_t| log N) time under bounded cell occupancy; full rebuild is Θ(N). Entropy lower-bounds the number of frontier cells touched by event-driven repair and influences the repair-rebuild crossover. Evaluation across ten motion families in d∈{2,3}, N≤16,000, using an exact tiled GPU oracle and GPU grid rebuild as ground truth and competitor. Dataset released with frontier geometry, audits, per-strategy times, and best-strategy labels.
AI.result: A frontier-aware maintenance rule can repair only the frontier in O(|F_t| log N) time, while full rebuild costs Θ(N). Entropy bounds the effort of event-driven repair and informs the repair-rebuild crossover. Across 2400 labeled transitions, validity holds (no missed invalid certificates); low-pressure frontiers favor incremental repair; diffuse frontiers of same size are costlier for event-driven repair but not for batched repair. A dataset documenting frontier geometry, certificate audits, times, and best strategies was released.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-guided frontier repair provides an effective framework for exact NN maintenance under small motions. The frontier-entropy metric yields principled strategy selection and predictable performance gains over full rebuild, with empirical validation and a dataset to enable benchmarking.

title: CAOA -- Completion-Assisted Object-CAD Alignment
summary: Accurately aligning CAD models to their corresponding objects in indoor RGB-D scans is a central challenge in 3D semantic reconstruction. The task requires estimating a 9-Degree-of-Freedom (DoF) pose-position, rotation, and scale along three axes-but is hindered by noisy and incomplete scans, as well as segmentation errors that cause geometric distortions. We present Completion-Assisted Object-CAD Alignment (CAOA), a method that integrates a semantically and contextually aware point cloud completion module with a symmetry-aware relative pose estimation algorithm, enabling precise alignment of CAD models to scanned objects. Existing completion methods are typically trained and evaluated on synthetic datasets, which often fail to generalize to real-world scans. To bridge this gap, we introduce a synthetic data generation strategy tailored to indoor scenes, significantly reducing the synthetic-to-real domain gap-validated through quantitative comparisons with widely used completion datasets. In addition, we release S2C-Completion, an expert-annotated dataset of over 8,500 object-CAD pairs from Scan2CAD, created for real-world indoor single-object completion and intended as a new benchmark for this task. For object-CAD alignment, we incorporate symmetry information via a symmetry-aware loss, improving robustness to symmetric ambiguities. On the Scan2CAD benchmark, CAOA achieves a 17% accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A completion-assisted, symmetry-aware 9-DoF CAD alignment method with synthetic-data bridging for indoor scans, achieving 17% accuracy gain on Scan2CAD.
AI.motivation: Aligning CAD models to objects in indoor RGB-D scans requires estimating 9-DoF pose (position, rotation, and scale). This is hindered by noisy/incomplete scans, segmentation-induced distortions, and symmetry ambiguities. Moreover, domain gap between synthetic training data and real scans limits generalization.
AI.method: CAOA combines a semantically/contextually aware point cloud completion module with a symmetry-aware relative pose estimation algorithm. It uses a symmetry-aware loss and a synthetic data generation strategy tailored to indoor scenes to bridge the domain gap. Additionally, it releases S2C-Completion (≈8.5k object-CAD pairs from Scan2CAD) for real-world single-object completion, serving as a new benchmark.
AI.result: On the Scan2CAD benchmark, CAOA achieves a 17% accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: CAOA improves robustness to incomplete/ambiguous geometry via completion and symmetry-aware estimation, enhances generalization to real-world scans through synthetic-data bridging, and provides a real-world benchmark dataset (S2C-Completion) to advance 3D object-CAD alignment research.

title: Breaking the Solver Bottleneck: Training Task Generators at the Learnable Frontier
summary: The limiting resource for training agents via reinforcement learning (RL) is increasingly frontier task supply: valid, solvable tasks just difficult enough to train the current model. As reasoning and agentic models improve, fixed task distributions saturate, while naive synthetic generation yields tasks that are trivial, impossible, or ill-posed. Training a task generator with RL to optimize validity and learnability can address this bottleneck, but direct optimization requires repeated solver rollouts per candidate. For software-engineering (SWE) tasks, a single rollout can take tens of minutes; solver-in-the-loop generator training is intractable. We introduce PROPEL, a solver-amortized framework for training task generators at the targeted solve rate. PROPEL trains a lightweight activation probe on a one-time labeled corpus of generated tasks and solver outcomes. The probe predicts target-solver pass rate from a frozen generator reference model and serves as a proxy for solve rate during generator optimization, reducing generator evaluation to a single forward pass. Across math, code, and software-engineering at multiple model scales, PROPEL shifts generation toward the targeted solve rate: for coding, tasks generated at the learnable frontier increase from $10.1\% \rightarrow 20.0\%$ for a Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct solver and from $5.3\% \rightarrow 12.6\%$ for a Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct solver. For SWE, PROPEL increases the share of generations at the targeted solve rate from $9.8\% \rightarrow 19.6\%$ for Qwen3.5-27B on repositories not seen during training of probe and generator.

AI.tldr: PROPEL amortizes solver cost by training a probe-based surrogate to predict solver success, enabling generator optimization toward a target solve rate; demonstrated improvements across math, code, and SWE across multiple model scales.
AI.motivation: RL-based task generation suffers when task distributions saturate and solver rollouts are expensive; there is a need for efficient, targeted task generation that stays aligned with current model capabilities.
AI.method: Train a lightweight activation probe on a labeled corpus of generated tasks and solver outcomes. Use a frozen generator reference to predict target solver pass rate; the probe serves as a proxy during generator optimization, reducing evaluation to a single forward pass.
AI.result: Across math, code, SWE and various model scales, PROPEL shifts generated tasks toward the targeted solve rate, e.g., coding: 10.1%→20.0% (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) and 5.3%→12.6% (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct); SWE: 9.8%→19.6% (Qwen3.5-27B on unseen repos).
AI.conclusion: PROPEL offers a practical, solver-amortized framework for frontier task generation, improving alignment with target solvability while reducing solver-cost, with demonstrated effectiveness across domains and model scales.

title: CaVe-VLM-CoT: An Interpretable Vision-Language Model Framework
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) remain prone to hallucinations, producing fluent but visually unfaithful outputs. Existing chain-of-thought and retrieval-augmented methods only partially address this, as they neither enforce step-level citation grounding nor route verification failures back to retrieval for correction. We present CaVe-VLM-CoT, a modular reflection-based agentic-RAG framework that enforces evidence-grounded reasoning through a five-stage closed-loop pipeline: Extractor, Retriever, Solver, Citation Injector, and Verifier, in which detected ungrounded claims trigger structured feedback to the Extractor for targeted re-retrieval. Since no existing framework jointly measures retrieval quality, step-wise citation faithfulness, and cross-modal grounding, we propose a suite of 23 component-wise metrics across all stages, anchored by CaVeScore, a composite metric weighting accuracy, citation precision and recall, attribution, and evidence grounding. Without any architectural or prompt modifications, CaVe-VLM-CoT achieves 87.1\% accuracy and 56.6\% CaVeScore on ScienceQA , and 55.2\% accuracy and 35.7\% CaVeScore on MMMU (30 subjects).

AI.tldr: CaVe-VLM-CoT: a modular five-stage agentic-RAG framework (Extractor, Retriever, Solver, Citation Injector, Verifier) with a closed-loop for evidence-grounded reasoning in Vision-Language Models; introduces 23 component-level metrics anchored by CaVeScore; achieves strong accuracy and grounding scores on ScienceQA and MMMU without architectural changes.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models frequently hallucinate, producing fluent but ungrounded outputs. Existing chain-of-thought and retrieval-augmented approaches lack step-level citation grounding and do not route verification failures back to retrieval for correction.
AI.method: Five-stage pipeline: Extractor -> Retriever -> Solver -> Citation Injector -> Verifier. Detected ungrounded claims trigger structured feedback to the Extractor for targeted re-retrieval. Introduces 23 component-wise metrics across stages, with CaVeScore as a composite metric.
AI.result: 87.1% accuracy and 56.6 CaVeScore on ScienceQA; 55.2% accuracy and 35.7 CaVeScore on MMMU (30 subjects).
AI.conclusion: CaVe-VLM-CoT enforces evidence-grounded reasoning and retrieval-guided correction without changing architecture or prompts. It also provides a comprehensive, multi-stage evaluation suite and a composite CaVeScore to quantify grounding and citation fidelity.

title: Task Allocation and Motion Planning in Dynamic, Cluttered Environments via CBBA and Graphs of Convex Sets
comment: 15 pages single column, 10 figures, AIAA-Scitech 2027 Submission
summary: Multi-agent task planning in cluttered, dynamic environments requires assigning tasks to agents while simultaneously determining safe, time-efficient trajectories through the environment. When tasks are dynamic, such as rendezvous objectives, allocation decisions depend not only on which agent is best suited for a task, but also on when and where that task can be reached. This paper presents a solution to this problem, which combines Graphs of Convex Sets (GCS) for trajectory optimization with the Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm (CBBA) for distributed task allocation. In our approach, GCS finds optimal trajectories through dynamic environments using a time-extended (3D+time) configuration space. At the same time, CBBA coordinates task assignments across agents, enabling informed decision-making in a moving environment. We then connect allocation and planning to allow the agents to avoid collisions in the 3D+time configuration space and provide accurate time estimates for task completion. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in simulated cluttered environments with static and dynamic tasks.

AI.tldr: A unified framework integrates Graphs of Convex Sets (GCS) for 3D+time trajectory optimization with the Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm (CBBA) for distributed task allocation to address dynamic, time-sensitive multi-agent planning in cluttered environments, demonstrated in simulation.
AI.motivation: Need to solve simultaneous task allocation and trajectory planning for multiple agents in cluttered, dynamic environments where tasks can be dynamic (e.g., rendezvous) and require time-aware decisions while avoiding collisions.
AI.method: Use GCS to compute optimal trajectories in a time-extended configuration space (3D+time) and CBBA to coordinate task assignments across agents in a distributed manner. The approach links allocation and planning to enforce collision avoidance in the 3D+time space and to produce accurate task completion time estimates.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness in simulated cluttered environments with both static and dynamic tasks, validating the integration of GCS-based planning with CBBA-based allocation.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully couples distributed task allocation with time-aware trajectory optimization, enabling safe, time-efficient multi-agent operation in dynamic environments and handling rendezvous-type tasks.

title: Tangent Spheres and Integer Distances
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. To appear at the Canadian Conference on Computational Geometry (CCCG 2026)
summary: The Erdős-Anning theorem states that any point set for which all distances are integers, in a Euclidean space of any dimension, must be either finite or collinear. We prove the same result in hyperbolic space of any dimension. A quantitative form of our result also extends for the first time to Euclidean spaces of dimension greater than two: if a set of points with integer distances in $\mathbb{E}^D$ or $\mathbb{H}^D$ has a subset of $D+1$ points in general position whose diameter is $d$, then the whole set has size $O(D(d+1)^D)$. To prove these results we formulate a lemma that, if the graph of external tangencies of a system of spheres in Euclidean or hyperbolic space contains a $K_{a,b}$ subgraph for $a,b\ge 3$, then the sets of spheres on each side of this biclique have centers that lie on a hyperplane. This lemma also implies that, in multilateration (determining a position from differences of distances to known landmarks), $D+1$ non-coplanar landmarks always suffice to limit the position to two possibilities.

AI.tldr: Generalizes the Erdős–Anning theorem to hyperbolic spaces of any dimension and gives a new quantitative bound in Euclidean and hyperbolic spaces, using a sphere-tangency graph lemma; shows D+1 non-coplanar landmarks suffice for unique multilateration up to two positions.
AI.motivation: Extend a classical integer-distance phenomenon beyond Euclidean space and to higher dimensions; obtain a quantitative size bound; connect to localization problems like multilateration.
AI.method: Proves a geometric-graph lemma: if the external tangency graph of a system of spheres contains a K_{a,b} subgraph with a,b≥3, then the centers of the spheres on each side lie on a hyperplane. Applies this to integer-distance point sets in E^D and H^D to deduce structure and bounds; uses this to extend Erdős–Anning and to derive the multilateration consequence.
AI.result: Erdős–Anning-type rigidity holds in hyperbolic space of any dimension. A quantitative bound O(D(d+1)^D) on the size of integer-distance point sets in E^D or H^D with a D+1-point general-position subset of diameter d. The lemma implies that D+1 non-coplanar landmarks suffice to restrict a position to two possibilities in multilateration.
AI.conclusion: The unified approach yields both existential and quantitative results in Euclidean and hyperbolic geometries and clarifies the role of sphere-tangency graphs in localization problems.

title: RegimeVGGT: Layer-Wise Spatially Preserving Redundancy Removal for Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 7 tables. Jinhao You, Shuo Lyu, Zhuohang Lyu, Tanxuan Li, and Zibo Zhao contributed equally. Shuo Lyu is the corresponding author
summary: Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) recovers dense 3D scene structure from multi-view images in one forward pass, but quadratic cross-frame attention limits its scalability. Existing training-free accelerators reduce computation uniformly along one axis, missing layer heterogeneity. Our spectral, probing, and causal analyses reveal three regimes: shallow layers lack cross-view structure, middle layers drive cross-view alignment, and deep layers are redundant for dense geometry yet their cross-frame attention remains essential for pose. RegimeVGGT applies layer-wise U-shaped compression along two axes: Saliency-Guided Banded Merging protects geometry- and edge-salient tokens, while Selectively Protected K/V Downsampling preserves cross-frame spatial coverage and the pose-critical path through a phase-shifted spatial grid, a reference-frame anchor, and uncompressed camera/register tokens. Training-free, RegimeVGGT achieves a 6.7x speedup over VGGT* at matched reconstruction quality.

AI.tldr: RegimeVGGT introduces layer-wise U-shaped compression for Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer to accelerate dense 3D reconstruction by protecting geometry- and pose-critical tokens, achieving 6.7x speedup with matched quality.
AI.motivation: VGGT's quadratic cross-frame attention limits scalability; need training-free accelerators that respect layer heterogeneity and preserve cross-view pose and geometry.
AI.method: Spectral, probing, and causal analyses reveal three regimes (shallow, middle, deep) with distinct cross-view roles. Propose Saliency-Guided Banded Merging and Selectively Protected K/V Downsampling to compress along two axes, using a phase-shifted spatial grid, a reference-frame anchor, and uncompressed camera/register tokens to maintain pose-critical paths.
AI.result: Training-free RegimeVGGT achieves about 6.7x speedup over VGGT* while preserving reconstruction quality.
AI.conclusion: A regime-aware, training-free compression strategy can significantly accelerate multi-view dense geometry transformers by exploiting layer-wise heterogeneity without sacrificing essential geometry and pose information.

title: CODEBLOCK: Learning to Supervise Code at the Right Granularity
summary: Supervised fine-tuning of code LLMs typically applies uniform cross-entropy loss to all response tokens, implicitly assuming that every token provides equally useful learning signal. Recent token-level selection methods challenge this assumption in natural-language SFT by supervising only high-value tokens. However, directly transferring token-level masking to code can break syntactically and semantically coherent program units, because code depends on structural completeness and definition-use relations. We therefore propose CodeBlock, a structure-aware sparse supervision framework that selects structure-complete code evidence rather than isolated tokens. CodeBlock first selects high-quality instruction-response pairs, then partitions code responses into syntactically coherent coding items, estimates their utility by aggregating generalized cross-entropy over core logic tokens, and reranks them with data-flow reach and bridge signals to prioritize blocks that propagate or connect important program dependencies. During training, the full response remains available as context, while loss is applied only to selected code items and informative natural-language tokens. Experiments on six code-generation benchmarks show that CodeBlock achieves stronger average pass@1 than full-token SFT and competitive selection baselines, while using only 1.9% of supervised response tokens.

AI.tldr: Structure-aware sparse supervision for code LLM fine-tuning. CodeBlock selects structure-complete code blocks rather than tokens, using syntax, data-flow, and control-flow signals to guide loss; achieves higher pass@1 with minimal token usage (1.9%).
AI.motivation: Token-level SFT treats all tokens equally, which can waste learning signal and break code structure due to incomplete blocks. Code in software has strong syntax and data-dependency constraints; thus supervision should align with complete coding units.
AI.method: Two-stage: first select high-quality instruction-response pairs; partition responses into syntactically coherent coding items; estimate each item's utility by aggregating generalized cross-entropy over core logic tokens; rerank items using data-flow reach/bridge signals to prioritize those propagating important program dependencies; training uses full context but applies loss only to selected code items and informative NL tokens.
AI.result: On six code-generation benchmarks, CodeBlock achieves stronger average pass@1 than full-token SFT and competitive baselines, while using only 1.9% of supervised response tokens.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware, sparse supervision improves learning efficiency and effectiveness for code LLM fine-tuning by focusing loss on coherent code blocks tied to program dependencies, reducing annotation signal requirements while maintaining or boosting performance.

title: Searching for Synergy in Shared Workspace Human-AI Collaboration
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Human-AI Co-Creativity. 13 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables
summary: Automated AI agents are increasingly capable, yet many scientific and professional tasks require human judgment and contextual expertise. We study shared-workspace human-AI teams, where AI agents and human collaborators must coordinate responsibilities before submitting a final answer. Using the Collaborative Gym environment with DiscoveryBench tasks, we examine when adding simulated human collaborators improves performance and when process loss turns additional collaborators into coordination overhead. Across 1,482 sessions, adding relevant collaborators can lower performance when teams lack structure to coordinate their contributions. We then evaluate scaffolding that combines shared group memory with simulated human-in-the-loop (HITL) gates, where selected actions require approval from a designated simulated participant. This scaffolding yields higher mean performance, most clearly in three-person teams, with clearer responsibility signals and stronger routing of expertise to team actions. Overall, how human-AI teams coordinate and integrate expertise matters as much as the capability available to them.

AI.tldr: Adding human collaborators in AI teams can hurt performance without clear coordination structure; scaffolding with shared memory and HITL gates improves performance, especially in three-person teams, underscoring that coordination design is as critical as capability.
AI.motivation: To understand how shared-workspace human-AI teams coordinate responsibilities and how scaffolding can mitigate coordination losses while leveraging expertise.
AI.method: empirical study in the Collaborative Gym environment with DiscoveryBench tasks; 1,482 sessions; tests effects of adding simulated human collaborators; introduces scaffolding combining shared group memory with HITL gates (approval by a simulated participant).
AI.result: Without coordination, extra collaborators may reduce performance due to process loss; scaffolding yields higher mean performance, especially with three-person teams, by providing clearer responsibility signals and better routing of expertise.
AI.conclusion: Coordination design matters as much as capability; effective human-AI collaboration requires structuring roles and decisions, not just augmenting agents.

title: As You Wish: Mission Planning with Formal Verification using LLMs in Precision Agriculture
summary: Though robotic systems are now being commercialized and deployed in various industries, many of these systems are highly specialized and often require an advanced skill set to operate and ensure they perform as instructed. To mitigate this problem, we recently introduced a mission planner leveraging LLMs to synthesize mission plans in precision agriculture based on mission descriptions provided in natural language. While the system demonstrates impressive performance, it also suffers from the inherent ambiguities of natural language. In this paper, we extend our system to address this issue by introducing multiple feedback loops in the planning architecture that leverage linear temporal logic (LTL) to ensure the mission planning system meets the specifications formulated by the user while still using natural language. To mitigate potential bias, this is achieved by using two different commercial LLMs in charge of the specification and verification subtasks. Through extensive experiments, we highlight the strengths and limitations of integrating mission verification into a fully autonomous pipeline, particularly regarding an LLM's ability to generate valuable LTL formulas, and show how our proposed implementation addresses and solves these challenges.

AI.tldr: Introduces LTL-based verification into an NL-driven mission planner for precision agriculture, using dual LLMs and feedback loops to reduce ambiguity and bias, with experiments showing viability and limitations of LTL generation by LLMs in an autonomous pipeline.
AI.motivation: Natural language descriptions cause ambiguity in autonomous mission planning; integrating formal verification (LTL) and bias mitigation (dual LLMs) can align plans with user specifications while maintaining NL inputs.
AI.method: Extend the existing NL-to-mission planner with multiple feedback loops that incorporate linear temporal logic (LTL) specifications and verification; employ two commercial LLMs to handle specification and verification tasks, reducing bias; evaluate the integrated pipeline in precision agriculture scenarios.
AI.result: Experiments highlight the strengths and limitations of embedding mission verification in a fully autonomous NL-driven pipeline, particularly the LLMs' ability to generate useful LTL formulas; the proposed architecture addresses these challenges and demonstrates improvements in satisfying user specifications while identifying remaining bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-LLM, LTL-enabled feedback architecture provides a viable path for robust autonomous mission planning from natural language, with formal verification improving compliance to user specs; while effective, LLM-generated LTL quality remains a key limitation to be addressed.

title: A Neural Network Framework for Geodesic-Like Curve Computation on Parametric Surfaces
summary: The concept of geodesic-like curves was introduced by Chen in 2010 as a method for estimating shortest paths (geodesics) on parametric surfaces, with its convergence established theoretically. However, an efficient numerical computational framework has not yet been developed. In this paper, we propose an elegant and efficient approach for computing geodesic-like curves by leveraging deep learning and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs). Under the proposed framework, not only can single parametric surfaces be handled efficiently, but a broad class of complex parametric surfaces including multi-surface systems with $C^0$ or higher continuity and surfaces of revolution can also be robustly addressed.

AI.tldr: A deep learning + physics-informed neural network framework to compute geodesic-like curves on parametric surfaces, enabling efficient computation for single surfaces and broad multi-surface systems (C0+ continuity, surfaces of revolution).
AI.motivation: Geodesic-like curves were proposed (Chen 2010) to estimate shortest paths on parametric surfaces with proven convergence, but a practical, efficient numerical framework was lacking. A data-driven, PDE-constrained approach can offer robust, scalable computation on complex surface geometries.
AI.method: Develop a PINN that encodes the geodesic equations on parametric surfaces, with a parameterized curve and surface coordinates. Train in an unsupervised/PDE-constrained manner to minimize the geodesic residuals and enforce boundary conditions, continuity across multi-surface interfaces, and special cases (e.g., surfaces of revolution). Extend to multiple surfaces with C0+ continuity.
AI.result: The framework yields efficient computation of geodesic-like curves on a range of surfaces, including single parametric surfaces and multi-surface systems with C0 or higher continuity and surfaces of revolution, demonstrating robustness and applicability to complex surface geometries.
AI.conclusion: PINN-based geodesic-like computation provides an elegant, efficient, and extensible framework for estimating shortest paths on parametric surfaces, with broad applicability to diverse surface types and potential for further enhancements.

title: Reasoning as Intersection: Consensus-Frame Alignment for Visual Focus in Video-MLLMs
summary: Reinforcement learning has improved the reasoning ability of large language models, but applying outcome-only rewards to video multimodal large language models (Video-MLLMs) provides limited guidance on which visual evidence should support the answer. Inspired by multisensory integration, where consistent cues can enhance the salience and reliability of perceptual estimates, we introduce Consensus Frame GRPO (CF-GRPO), a temporal-annotation-free process-level reward framework for evidence-aware video reasoning. CF-GRPO constructs a consensus frame prior from intrinsic video cues, including temporal coverage, scene-transition cues, and query-conditioned visual relevance. It then computes a model-side frame-use score from visual and response representations and optimizes their agreement through the Consensus Frame Reward (CFR). With salience-aware sparse aggregation and distribution sharpening, CFR provides a high-contrast reward signal without requiring human temporal annotations. Experiments show that VideoCFR achieves competitive performance across complex video reasoning benchmarks and improves several metrics over representative Video-MLLM and RL baselines, while the consensus prior provides an interpretable view of the evidence frames emphasized during training. The implementation is available at https://github.com/1Pansy/VideoCFR.

AI.tldr: Introduces CF-GRPO, a reward framework for evidence-aware video reasoning in Video-MLLMs that uses a consensus frame prior and CFR to align frame use with model outputs, enabling reward without temporal annotations and achieving competitive results.
AI.motivation: Outcome-only rewards in Video-MLLMs lack guidance on which visual evidence to trust; need interpretable, annotation-free, frame-level guidance inspired by multisensory integration.
AI.method: Build a consensus frame prior from intrinsic video cues (temporal coverage, scene transitions, query-conditioned relevance); compute a model-side frame-use score from visual and response representations; optimize their agreement via a Consensus Frame Reward; apply salience-aware sparse aggregation and distribution sharpening.
AI.result: VideoCFR achieves competitive performance on complex video reasoning benchmarks, improves several metrics over representative Video-MLLM and RL baselines, and provides interpretable evidence-frame emphasis via the consensus prior; code available.
AI.conclusion: CF-GRPO provides a high-contrast, annotation-free reward signal for evidence-aware video reasoning, enabling improved alignment of evidence frames with model outputs; it offers interpretability of the emphasis frames during training.

title: Artemis: Anatomy-Resolved inTervention for Eliminating Multimodal NeuroImage confounderS
summary: Multimodal neuroimaging, integrating functional connectivity from fMRI and structural connectivity from DTI, enables non-invasive analysis of brain networks using graph neural networks. However, demographic factors such as age and sex systematically confound the relationship between brain connectivity and clinical outcomes, causing GNNs to exploit spurious shortcuts rather than learning causally invariant representations. While recent causal GNN methods introduce causality at the graph-modeling level, their causal mechanisms remain domain-agnostic without accounting for the real-world confounders inherent in clinical neuroimaging data. Moreover, brain networks are constructed from atlas-based parcellations where each region exhibits distinct sensitivity to demographic factors, necessitating region-aware adjustment. We propose Artemis, a region-level causal framework that bridges this gap with causal intervention at each brain region independently by learning region-specific confounder representations with lightweight parameters. Our adjustment comprehensively utilized the multimodal functional and structural features for graph reasoning as a plug-in module compatible with arbitrary GNN backbones. Experiments on three benchmarks, ADNI for disease diagnosis, OASIS for dementia staging, and HCP for sex classification, demonstrate consistent improvements over representative GNN-based baselines. Multiple supporting experiments further demonstrate statistical significance and neuroscientific interpretability.

AI.tldr: Artemis: a region-aware causal plug-in for multimodal brain graphs that independently intervenes on each atlas-defined region to mitigate demographic confounding, boosting GNN performance on ADNI, OASIS, and HCP with interpretable region-level adjustments.
AI.motivation: Demographic factors (age, sex) confound brain–outcome associations in multimodal neuroimaging; existing causal GNNs are domain-agnostic and neglect region-specific sensitivity in atlas-based parcellations; a region-level, data-efficient adjustment is needed.
AI.method: Region-level causal framework Artemis: performs causal intervention at each brain region independently by learning region-specific confounder representations with lightweight parameters; leverages both functional and structural features; acts as a plug-in module compatible with arbitrary GNN backbones for graph reasoning.
AI.result: On three benchmarks (ADNI, OASIS, HCP) Artemis yields consistent improvements over representative GNN baselines, with supporting experiments demonstrating statistical significance and neuroscientific interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Region-aware causal adjustment enhances robustness and interpretability of multimodal GNNs in clinical neuroimaging by explicitly modeling and mitigating region-specific confounders, while remaining modular and backbone-agnostic.

title: CEO-Bench: Can Agents Play the Long Game?
summary: Language model agents are becoming proficient executors at isolated, short-horizon tasks such as software engineering and customer service. Yet real-world challenges require a combination of sophisticated skills that remain largely untested in agents: (1) navigating long horizons amid uncertainty; (2) acquiring information in noisy environments; (3) adapting to a changing world; (4) orchestrating multiple moving parts toward a coherent goal. We introduce CEO-Bench, which evaluates these capabilities together by simulating a representative real-world task: operating a startup for 500 days. An agent manages pricing, marketing, budgeting, and many other aspects of a fictional company through a programmable Python interface, operating in the same environment and facing the same challenges as a human CEO. Success demands analyzing noisy, interconnected business databases, translating signals into sound strategy, and coordinating many decisions with programming. The strongest agents write sophisticated code that simulates customer cohorts to forecast future cash and mines negotiation history to uncover hidden customer preferences. Even so, most state-of-the-art models struggle in this environment. Only Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 finish above the $1M starting balance, and neither consistently turns a profit. CEO-Bench takes a first step toward measuring the intelligence required to drive sustained, adaptive progress over time.

AI.tldr: CEO-Bench is a long-horizon startup-management benchmark for language-model agents. It tests planning over 500 days, noisy information processing, adaptation, and multi-part coordination via a programmable Python interface. Current top models (Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5) exceed the starting balance but do not reliably profit, indicating gaps in sustained, adaptive decision-making.
AI.motivation: Real-world AI use-cases require agents to operate over long horizons with uncertainty, noisy data, evolution of conditions, and coordination of many interacting components. Existing short-horizon benchmarks fail to capture these challenges; CEO-Bench provides a realistic, cohesive testbed to measure such capabilities and guide progress.
AI.method: A simulated startup environment runs for 500 days. An agent manages pricing, marketing, budgeting, and other functions through a programmable Python interface in a shared environment. The agent analyzes noisy business data, translates signals into strategy, and coordinates many decisions via code. The benchmark includes customers cohorts simulation and analysis of negotiation history to infer preferences, pushing agents to forecast cash flow and optimize profitability under uncertainty.
AI.result: The strongest agents (Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5) finish above a $1M starting balance but neither consistently turns a profit, indicating current models struggle with sustained, adaptive, long-horizon management tasks.
AI.conclusion: CEO-Bench is a first step toward measuring the intelligence required for sustained adaptive progress in real-world, long-horizon tasks. It reveals both the potential and the current limitations of modern LLM agents in complex, time-evolving organizational settings.

title: DREAM-Chunk: Reactive Action Chunking with Latent World Model
summary: Action chunking has become a common interface for vision-language-action (VLA) models, enabling low-frequency policy inference to drive high-frequency robot execution. However, once an action chunk is committed, its open-loop execution can be brittle under stochastic dynamics, hardware execution errors, and partial observability. We propose DREAM-Chunk, a test-time scaling method that augments chunking-based policies with a lightweight latent world model, without requiring additional policy fine-tuning. At test time, DREAM-Chunk samples multiple candidate action chunks, rolls out their predicted latent futures, and selects actions from the chunk whose predicted state best matches the observed rollout. In this way, DREAM-Chunk uses additional test-time computation to cover multiple plausible stochastic futures and improve reactivity during long-horizon chunk execution. On the Kinetix benchmark, DREAM-Chunk improves robustness under increasing action noise and benefits from larger candidate sample sizes, especially when demonstrations contain corrective behaviors. We further validate DREAM-Chunk on four manipulation tasks across two robot platforms and two VLA policies under various sources of stochasticity. Across simulation and hardware experiments, DREAM-Chunk improves the robustness of action-chunking policies in stochastic dynamics.

AI.tldr: DREAM-Chunk augments chunk-based VLA policies with a lightweight latent world model and test-time sampling to roll out futures, selecting chunks by latent-state consistency, improving robustness without policy fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Action-chunking enables low-frequency policy inference but is brittle under stochastic dynamics, hardware noise, and partial observability when executions are open-loop; a method is needed to increase robustness without costly policy retraining.
AI.method: At test time, generate multiple candidate action chunks, roll out their predicted latent futures via a lightweight world model, and select the chunk whose predicted state best matches the observed rollout, effectively covering multiple plausible stochastic futures without updating the policy.
AI.result: On Kinetix benchmark, robustness to action noise improves and benefits grow with more candidate samples; demonstrated across four manipulation tasks, two robot platforms, and two VLA policies under various stochastic conditions, in both simulation and hardware.
AI.conclusion: Test-time latent-world-model augmentation for action-chunking policies yields tangible robustness and reactivity gains without fine-tuning and generalizes across tasks, platforms, and sources of stochasticity.

title: Domain Generalizable Adaptation of 3D Vision-Language Models via Regularized Fine-Tuning
comment: Accepted at Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Domain adaptation remains a central challenge in 3D vision, especially for multimodal foundation models that align 3D point clouds with visual and textual data. While these models demonstrate strong general capabilities, adapting them to downstream domains with limited data often leads to overfitting and catastrophic forgetting. To address this, we introduce ReFine3D, a regularized fine-tuning framework designed for domain-generalizable tuning of 3D large multimodal models (LMMs). ReFine3D combines selective layer tuning with two targeted regularization strategies: multi-view consistency across augmented point clouds and text diversity through synonym-based prompts generated by large language models. Additionally, we incorporate point-rendered vision supervision and a test-time augmentation mechanism with confidence-based aggregation to further enhance robustness. Extensive experiments across different 3D domain generalization benchmarks show that ReFine3D improves base-to-novel class generalization by 1.36%, cross-dataset transfer by 2.43%, robustness to corruption by 1.80%, and few-shot accuracy by up to 3.11%, outperforming prior state-of-the-art methods with minimal added computational overhead.

AI.tldr: ReFine3D proposes a regularized fine-tuning framework for 3D LMMs to achieve domain generalization by selective layer tuning and regularizers including multi-view consistency, synonym-based prompts, point-rendered vision supervision, and test-time augmentation, yielding state-of-the-art gains with little overhead.
AI.motivation: Domain-adaptation for 3D multimodal LMMs with limited downstream data suffers from overfitting and forgetting; a generally applicable fine-tuning framework is needed.
AI.method: Selective layer tuning combined with two regularizers (multi-view consistency across augmented point clouds; text diversity via synonym prompts from LLMs), plus point-rendered vision supervision and a test-time augmentation with confidence-based aggregation.
AI.result: Empirical gains across 3D domain generalization benchmarks: base-to-novel +1.36%; cross-dataset +2.43%; robustness to corruption +1.80%; few-shot accuracy up to +3.11%; outperforms prior SOTA with minimal overhead.
AI.conclusion: ReFine3D effectively enables domain-generalizable tuning of 3D LMMs with modest compute, improving generalization across multiple settings.

title: A Link between Shock-wave Theory and Symmetry-reduced Stochastic Gradient Descent for Artificial Neural Networks
comment: Accepted to the 35th International Conference on Artificial Neural Networks (ICANN) 2026
summary: We develop a mathematically explicit link between shock-wave theory and the symmetry-quotiented learning dynamics of stochastic gradient descent, drawing on differential geometry, Lie group theory, and fluid mechanics. Specifically, after quotienting parameter symmetries and applying local-entropy coarse-graining, the effective dynamics satisfy a viscous Hamilton--Jacobi equation on the quotient manifold. Moreover, under the assumption that the raw parameter dynamics can be summarized by a gradient field on the quotiented space, the gradient of the coarse-grained loss function obeys a Burgers-type equation, and shock formation can be established rigorously. We apply our theory to multilayer perceptrons, convolutional neural networks, Transformers, and mean-field networks, and show that they obey the Hamilton--Jacobi or Burgers-type equations. We conjecture that this framework also yields practical diagnostics for deep learning. In architectures such as Transformers, raw parameter norms are often distorted by symmetry redundancy and may therefore be misleading, whereas symmetry-corrected quotient observables provide a principled basis for monitoring, forecasting, and controlling training-phase transitions.

AI.tldr: A physics-inspired PDE framework for SGD dynamics on symmetry-quotient manifolds, yielding viscous Hamilton–Jacobi and Burgers-type equations; applied to standard DL architectures; proposes symmetry-aware diagnostics.
AI.motivation: To connect parameter symmetries in neural nets to rigorous PDE dynamics, offering a principled view of training-phase transitions and diagnostics.
AI.method: Quotient out parameter symmetries; apply local-entropy coarse-graining; derive effective viscous Hamilton–Jacobi equation on quotient manifold; (assuming raw dynamics gradient-field) derive Burgers-type equation for the gradient of coarse-grained loss; apply to MLPs, CNNs, Transformers, mean-field nets; analyze shock formation; propose symmetry-corrected observables.
AI.result: The architectures studied obey the derived PDEs (HJ or Burgers-type); conjecture practical diagnostics and that symmetry-corrected observables monitor/train transitions; shows potential for forecasting and control of training.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a rigorous link between shock-wave theory and learning dynamics under symmetries, with practical implications for diagnostics and training management; symmetry reduction improves interpretability and monitoring of phase transitions.

title: DeFAb: A Verifiable Benchmark for Defeasible Abduction in Foundation Models
comment: 33 pages, 14 figures, 23 tables. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/PatrickAllenCooper/DeFAb ; code and evaluation harness: https://github.com/PatrickAllenCooper/blanc
summary: A rule-based logic solver resolves every instance in our benchmark in under 50 microseconds with 100% accuracy; the best frontier language model reaches 65% at best and drops to 23.5% under rendering-robust evaluation (worst case over four surface renderings). We introduce DeFAb (Defeasible Abduction Benchmark), a dataset and generation pipeline that converts four decades of publicly funded knowledge bases into formally grounded instances for defeasible abduction: constructing hypotheses that explain anomalies by overriding defaults while preserving unrelated expectations. Because every hypothesis must pass polynomial-time checks for valid derivation, conservativity, and minimality, DeFAb makes logical rigor the instrument for measuring creativity and theoretical reasoning, scoring the disciplined construction of theory revisions rather than fluent but theory-destroying prose. The pipeline pairs taxonomic hierarchies (OpenCyc, YAGO, Wikidata) with behavioral property graphs (ConceptNet, UMLS) to produce 372,648+ instances across 33.75M materialized rules from 18 sources, in three levels with polynomial-time verifiable gold standards. Four frontier models do not reliably internalize defeasible reasoning: rendering-robust Level 2 accuracy is 7.8-23.5%; chain-of-thought variance (~36 pp) exceeds any inter-model gap; and a matched contamination control isolates a +19.4 pp Level 3 gap. We further release DeFAb-Hard (a 235-instance Level 3 difficulty variant; best model 53.3% vs 100% symbolic) and CONJURE (a kernel-verified transformative-creativity variant of 560 Lean 4/Mathlib instances whose gold answers are definitions the proof kernel did not previously contain, judge-free verifier; a pilot finds zero novel concepts). The same verifier doubles as an exact reward for preference optimization (DPO, RLVR/GRPO). Released under MIT at https://huggingface.co/datasets/PatrickAllenCooper/DeFAb.

AI.tldr: A new defeasible-abduction benchmark (DeFAb) pairs taxonomic knowledge with behavior graphs to create many polynomial-time-verifiable instances; a rule-based solver outperforms frontier LMs on defeasible reasoning, and several variants (DeFAb-Hard, CONJURE) explore difficulty and creativity with a verifier-based reward system.
AI.motivation: To measure creativity and theoretical reasoning under defeasible abduction with rigorous, polynomial-time verifiability, addressing frontier language models' weaknesses in defeasible reasoning.
AI.method: Generate 372,648+ defeasible-abduction instances from 18 sources (OpenCyc/YAGO/Wikidata with ConceptNet/UMLS) amounting to 33.75M materialized rules across three levels; enforce polynomial-time checks for valid derivation, conservativity, and minimality; compare a rule-based solver against frontier LMs; release DeFAb, DeFAb-Hard, CONJURE, and a verifier-based reward framework (DPO/RLVR/GRPO) under MIT.
AI.result: Rule-based solver achieves 100% accuracy on all benchmark instances in under 50 microseconds; frontier models cap at 65% (best) and 23.5% under rendering-robust evaluation; DeFAb-Hard yields 53.3% vs 100% symbolic; CONJURE shows zero novel concepts; the verifier doubles as a reward mechanism for preference optimization (DPO/RLVR/GRPO).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that enforcing logical rigor via polynomial-time verification reframes evaluation of AI creativity and theoretical reasoning, with symbolic approaches outperforming frontier LMs on defeasible reasoning; provides a scalable benchmark and tooling (datasets, variants, and a verifier-based reward system) for future research.

title: Benchmarking Action Spaces in Reinforcement Learning for Vision-based Robotic Manipulation
summary: In real-world reinforcement learning (RL), the choice of action space can play a key role in shaping motion smoothness, safety, and overall task performance. In this study, we evaluate pose increment, pose velocity, joint position increment, and joint velocity across two vision-based manipulation tasks: object picking and pushing.
