We train policies in simulation and deploy them to the real world using sim-to-real transfer. We find that action-space representation indeed significantly affects sim-to-real performance. In particular, we find that the joint velocity action space is best for the vision-based picking and pushing tasks in terms of smoothness and final task performance. We also provide practical guidance for RL practitioners in choosing action spaces for both simulation and real-world experiments.

AI.tldr: Joint velocity action space outperforms the others for vision-based picking and pushing in sim-to-real, delivering smoother trajectories and higher final task performance.
AI.motivation: To understand how the representation of actions in RL affects motion smoothness, safety, and sim-to-real transfer in real-world robotics tasks.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of four action spaces (pose increment, pose velocity, joint position increment, joint velocity) across two vision-based manipulation tasks (object picking and pushing). Policies trained in simulation and deployed on the real robot via sim-to-real transfer; evaluated on trajectory smoothness and task performance.
AI.result: Joint velocity action space achieved the best performance in both tasks, with superior smoothness and final task outcomes; other spaces lag in either smoothness or task success.
AI.conclusion: For vision-based manipulation with sim-to-real transfer, adopting a joint velocity action space is advised to optimize smoothness and performance; the work offers practical guidance for selecting action representations in both simulated and real settings.

title: Data-Forcing Distillation: Restoring Diversity and Fidelity in Few-Step Video Generation
summary: Recent progress has shown promise in distilling multi-step video diffusion models into efficient few-step students. Among them, Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) and its successor DMD2 achieved strong generation quality and fast convergence. However, due to the nature of the reverse Kullback--Leibler (KL) objective, these methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: a substantial drop in sample diversity, and visibly over-saturated outputs that deviate from real-video appearance. In this work, we propose Data-Forcing Distillation (DFD), a simple post-training framework that restores diversity and fidelity in DMD with only a single-line of code change. At its core is the teacher score discrepancy to guide the student toward the real-data distribution, pulling it to missing modes (mitigating mode collapse) and away from problematic modes absent in real data (avoiding over-saturation). We provide an in-depth theoretical analysis of our framework and validate our approach on text-to-video, image-to-video, and autoregressive video generation. With only 100--300 steps of finetuning, DFD effectively restores diversity and fidelity on both Wan2.1-1.3B and Cosmos-Predict2.5-2B model, resolving the over-saturation artifacts with significantly better video dynamics and appearance, and even outperforms the teacher model.

AI.tldr: DFD offers a simple post-training fix to DMD/DMD2 that uses teacher-score discrepancy to restore diversity and fidelity, addressing mode collapse and over-saturation with 100–300 finetuning steps.
AI.motivation: Address persistent failure modes of reverse KL-based distillation in multi-step video diffusion—specifically loss of sample diversity and over-saturated outputs—without sacrificing efficiency.
AI.method: Data-Forcing Distillation (DFD): a post-training framework that uses teacher score discrepancies to steer the student toward the real-data distribution; pulls toward missing modes (mitigating mode collapse) and away from non-real modes (avoiding over-saturation); implemented as a minimal (single-line) code change.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis and empirical validation across text-to-video, image-to-video, and autoregressive video generation. With 100–300 steps of finetuning on Wan2.1-1.3B and Cosmos-Predict2.5-2B, DFD restores diversity and fidelity, resolves over-saturation, improves video dynamics and appearance, and can even outperform the teacher model in some settings.
AI.conclusion: DFD provides an effective, lightweight post-training remedy for reverse KL-based distillation in video diffusion, achieving better diversity and fidelity with modest finetuning and broad modality applicability.

title: Attribution-Guided and Coverage-Maximized Pruning for Structural MoE Compression
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale compute efficiently, yet remain expensive to deploy due to their substantial memory footprint and inference overhead. Prior compression methods mainly operate at the expert level, either removing entire experts or ranking experts by coarse-grained importance scores. However, such expert-wise decisions are often too coarse to capture fine-grained redundancy, leading to misallocated pruning budgets and limited compression. To address this problem, we observe that information within MoE experts is highly concentrated in a small subset of channels, leaving substantial redundancy even in experts deemed important. Based on this observation, we propose a structural pruning framework tailored for MoE models. Our method reformulates prune-ratio allocation as a channel-score coverage maximization problem and solves it efficiently using an attribution-based approximation. Experiments on DeepSeek and Qwen MoE models show that our method preserves model accuracy under 50% or 25% structured pruning when combined with 4-bit quantization. On Qwen3-30B-A3B, our approach reduces memory footprint by 5.27$\times$ and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A structural pruning framework for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models targets channel-level redundancy within experts, reframing prune-budget allocation as a channel-score coverage problem and solving it with attribution-based approximation; it enables substantial memory reductions (up to ~5.3x) with modest accuracy loss when paired with 4-bit quantization, outperforming baselines on large MoE models.
AI.motivation: MoE models incur high memory and inference costs. Expert-level pruning is too coarse to capture fine-grained redundancy; exploiting within-expert channel redundancy can enable more effective compression without severe accuracy loss.
AI.method: Introduce channel-level structural pruning for MoE. Reformulate prune-ratio allocation as a channel-score coverage maximization problem. Solve with attribution-based approximation. Combine with 4-bit quantization. Validate on DeepSeek and Qwen MoE models, including Qwen3-30B-A3B.
AI.result: Displays preserved accuracy under 50% (or 25% in some settings) structured pruning with 4-bit quantization. On Qwen3-30B-A3B, achieves 5.27× memory reduction and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Channel-level, attribution-guided structural pruning effectively reduces MoE memory footprint with minimal accuracy loss and can surpass existing compression methods when combined with low-bit quantization.

title: Optimizing Lithium Production Decisions under Geological, Demand, and Pricing Uncertainties: A POMDP Framework for Multi-Objective Decision Making
summary: Decision making in lithium production is challenging, whether from an investor's perspective or a strategic production standpoint. Determining which mines to open and when to open them involves not only geological and price uncertainties, but also complexities around the choice of extraction method, from direct lithium extraction to hard rock mining. Prior work explored models of this problem and different methods to optimize mining decisions; these models did not account for uncertainty in pricing, uncertainty in demand, or different mining technologies to extract lithium. Incorporating different pricing models and extraction technology into these models enables more robust strategies for determining not only when and where to open a mine, but also which method of production to pursue. We frame the problem as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) and solve using belief state planning methods to get optimal decision making. In our study, we show that POMDP solvers outperform human inspired heuristics by dynamically adapting to shifting lithium price regimes (static, linear, exponential, and stochastic) through belief state planning and explicit uncertainty management. By optimally sequencing exploration, production, and technology choice, the framework achieves higher demand fulfillment and more balanced economic environmental outcomes over the projects lifetime in all different pricing and deposit scenarios.

AI.tldr: Framing lithium mining decisions under price and technology uncertainty as a POMDP; uses belief-state planning to optimally sequence exploration, production, and technology choice; outperforms heuristics across diverse price regimes.
AI.motivation: To address uncertainty in lithium pricing, demand, and extraction technology in mining planning, which prior models did not jointly model.
AI.method: Model the decision problem as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) and solve using belief-state planning. Compare POMDP solvers to human-inspired heuristics across static, linear, exponential, and stochastic price regimes.
AI.result: POMDP solvers outperform heuristics by dynamically adapting to price regime shifts, achieving better demand fulfillment and more balanced economic–environmental outcomes throughout the project lifetime in various deposit and pricing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A belief-state, uncertainty-aware POMDP framework provides robust, adaptive decision support for mine portfolio design and technology choice, with potential generalization to other commodities and uncertain environments, albeit with computational considerations.

title: Admittance-Based Surface Alignment for Human-in-the-Loop Robotic Visual Inspection
summary: Precision visual inspection underpins quality assurance across aerospace, semiconductor, and medical manufacturing, where undetected surface anomalies on high-value parts translate directly into scrap, rework, and field failures. Robotic visual inspection requires precise alignment between the end-effector and local surface geometry in the presence of perception noise and surface irregularities. In industrial settings, a human operator is often kept in the loop via teleoperation or shared autonomy, introducing real-time adjustments that render purely offline motion planning inadequate. This motivates control architectures capable of reactive, compliant behavior under combined human and perceptual uncertainty. This paper presents a novel real-time, closed-loop robotic orientation control pipeline for precision visual inspection, with an admittance-based framework that unifies operator input and perception-driven surface alignment. We design the end-effector as a virtual sphere moving through a viscous medium, such that the resulting physically interpretable mass--damper system generates synchronized, compliant motion from orientation error and operator commands. We validate the framework on a 6-DOF manipulator demonstrating stable normal-tracking and a final mean orientation error of 0.4°.

AI.tldr: Real-time admittance-based orientation control for precision visual inspection; virtual-sphere end-effector in viscous medium; stable tracking with 0.4° mean orientation error on a 6-DOF robot.
AI.motivation: Industrial precision inspection requires accurate, compliant alignment under perception noise and human-in-the-loop control; offline planning is insufficient for dynamic environments.
AI.method: End-effector modeled as a virtual sphere in a viscous medium; mass–damper system generating compliant motion from orientation error and operator commands; admittance control unifies operator input with perception-driven surface alignment; implemented in real-time on a 6-DOF manipulator.
AI.result: Stable normal-tracking; final mean orientation error of 0.4°.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a real-time, closed-loop orientation control pipeline enabling reactive, compliant visual inspection in the presence of operator and perceptual uncertainty; applicable to QA across industries; future work could extend to full pose control, robustness analysis, and more complex surfaces.

title: Vines-DB: An RGB image dataset for multi-species ornamental vine segmentation
comment: 7 pages, 1 figure. Source data repository: OSF (DOI: 10.17605/OSF.IO/YJHCK)
summary: The Vines-DB dataset contains 1,218 original high-resolution RGB images of seven ornamental vine species collected under field conditions at the Utah Agricultural Experiment Station's Greenville Research Farm in Logan, Utah, USA. The dataset was generated from 168 individual vine plants that were transplanted in 2022 and photographed repeatedly across multiple months during the 2023 and 2024 growing seasons (July-October). Images were captured with an iPhone 16 Pro equipped with a 48 MP camera between 10:00 AM and 12:00 PM under daylight. Vines were grown on 1.2m x 2.4m trellises and photographed from a distance of 1m against black or white Styrofoam backdrops to improve contrast and reduce background noise. The dataset includes Akebia quinata, Campsis radicans, Hydrangea anomala petiolaris, Lonicera x heckrottii, Campsis x tagliabuana 'Madame Galen', Parthenocissus quinquefolia, and Wisteria floribunda. All original images were manually annotated in Roboflow by trained annotators to produce polygon-based instance segmentation masks for eight classes, including seven species and background. After preprocessing and data augmentation, the working dataset was expanded to 2,307 images for model development and evaluation. The augmented dataset was divided into 2,019 training images, 192 validation images, and 96 test images using stratified sampling to maintain balanced representation. Vines-DB supports the development and evaluation of deep learning models for multi-class instance segmentation in precision horticulture and urban ecology. The dataset enables applications such as automated canopy cover estimation, species identification, and scalable field phenotyping. In addition, repeated monthly imaging of the plants captures temporal variation in canopy development and plant appearance, increasing the dataset's utility for segmentation benchmarking under realistic field conditions.

AI.tldr: Vines-DB is a field-collected, augmented dataset of 2,307 images for multi-class instance segmentation of seven vine species, with polygon masks and a stratified train/val/test split, enabling canopy estimation, species ID, and phenotyping under realistic conditions.
AI.motivation: Fill the need for robust, field-grade multi-class instance segmentation benchmarks in precision horticulture and urban ecology, incorporating temporal variation across growing seasons.
AI.method: Field data collection using an iPhone 16 Pro (48 MP) at ~1 m distance under daylight; seven vine species photographed on 1.2m x 2.4m trellises with black/white backdrops; manual polygon-based instance segmentation masks for eight classes (seven species + background); preprocessing and data augmentation; dataset expanded from 1,218 originals to 2,307 images; stratified train/val/test split (2,019/192/96).
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility and utility of a field-grade, temporally-resolved segmentation dataset; provides a sizeable, balanced split suitable for benchmarking multi-class instance segmentation and downstream tasks like canopy cover estimation, species identification, and phenotyping.
AI.conclusion: Vines-DB offers a realistic benchmark for multi-class instance segmentation in horticulture/urban ecology, highlighting the value of temporal data and controlled imaging conditions for robust model evaluation.

title: Fisher Width: A Geometric Measure of Complexity on Statistical Manifolds
summary:
Gaussian width is a central geometric complexity measure in high-dimensional probability, compressed sensing, convex optimization, and learning theory. It quantifies the average extent of a set along random directions, thereby capturing the effective dimension of constraint sets, hypothesis classes, and descent cones. However, this notion is intrinsically Euclidean. Statistical models instead carry a natural Riemannian geometry induced by the Fisher information metric, where directions are scaled according to statistical distinguishability rather than ambient Euclidean length.
  We introduce Fisher width, a Fisher-geometric analogue of Gaussian width for statistical manifolds. At a parameter point $θ$, Fisher width replaces the Euclidean identity by the local metric tensor $G(θ)^{1/2}$, measuring the Gaussian width of the Fisher-rescaled set. This makes the resulting quantity sensitive to local statistical curvature and invariant under smooth reparameterizations.
  We develop the basic theory of Fisher width, showing that it retains key structural features of Gaussian width, including concentration, metric perturbation stability, and spectral comparison bounds with the Euclidean baseline, while also capturing anisotropic geometric effects invisible to Euclidean measures. As an application, we prove a generalization bound for Fisher-Lipschitz hypothesis classes and propose computable estimators, which we evaluate empirically on MNIST across three model classes.
  Fisher width is to statistical manifolds what Gaussian width is to Euclidean convex bodies. This work lays the foundation for studying complexity and learning on curved statistical manifolds.

AI.tldr: Introduces Fisher width, a Fisher-information–based analogue of Gaussian width on statistical manifolds; develops theory (concentration, stability, spectral bounds) and provides generalization bounds for Fisher-Lipschitz classes; proposes computable estimators and tests on MNIST across three models.
AI.motivation: Extend the concept of Gaussian width from Euclidean convex geometry to curved statistical manifolds endowed with the Fisher information metric, to better capture local statistical distinguishability and anisotropy.
AI.method: Define Fisher width by applying the local metric tensor G(θ)^{1/2} to the Gaussian width of the Fisher-rescaled set; prove concentration, metric-perturbation stability, and spectral comparison bounds; derive Fisher-Lipschitz generalization bounds; propose computable estimators; empirically evaluate on MNIST across three model classes.
AI.result: Fisher width preserves key properties of Gaussian width (concentration, stability, comparative bounds) while revealing anisotropic geometric effects; enables generalization bounds for Fisher-Lipschitz classes; provides practical estimators; empirical MNIST evaluation supports applicability across models.
AI.conclusion: Fisher width extends Gaussian width to curved statistical manifolds, offering a principled framework for complexity and learning theory in non-Euclidean settings and laying groundwork for analysis on curved spaces.

title: ForecastBench-Sim: A Simulated-World Forecasting Benchmark
comment: 15 pages, 5 main figures, 6 appendix figures. Spotlight presentation at Forecasting as a New Frontier of Intelligence / Workshop on AI Forecasting, ICML 2026
summary: Forecasting benchmarks for general-purpose AI systems usually inherit the constraints of the real world: outcomes resolve slowly, tail events are rare, and counterfactual questions are difficult to score. We introduce ForecastBench-Sim, a simulated-world forecasting benchmark built on game rollouts from Freeciv, a turn-based strategy game modelled on the Civilization series. Forecasters receive a fixed world report (a structured snapshot of the current game state) and answer questions about hidden future states; the benchmark then continues the simulation and scores forecasts. Because the world is simulated, the same setup can generate continuous or binary forecasting questions at arbitrary time horizons, paired intervention worlds for conditional or causal questions, and resolved examples of rare or disruptive outcomes. We describe the benchmark pipeline, question families, scoring protocol, and release artifacts, and report validation slices from model evaluations and an anonymized human pilot. ForecastBench-Sim is intended to complement real-world forecasting benchmarks by providing controlled, immediately resolvable tasks for studying probabilistic reasoning under dynamic world states.

AI.tldr: ForecastBench-Sim introduces a simulated-world forecasting benchmark using Freeciv rollouts to enable solvable, timely probabilistic forecasting tasks across continuous and binary horizons, including interventions and rare events, with a full pipeline of questions, scoring, and release artifacts.
AI.motivation: Real-world forecasting benchmarks struggle with slow outcomes, rare tail events, and difficult-to-score counterfactuals. A simulated benchmark can provide controlled, immediately resolvable tasks to study probabilistic reasoning under dynamic world states.
AI.method: Leverage Freeciv game rollouts to generate dynamic world states. Provide forecasters with fixed world reports and ask about hidden future states. The benchmark continues the simulation to score forecasts. Supports continuous/binary horizons, intervention/causal questions, and rare-disruptive outcomes. Includes a pipeline, question families, scoring protocol, and release artifacts. Validation slices from model evaluations and anonymized human pilots.
AI.result: Describes the benchmark pipeline, the families of forecasting questions, the scoring protocol, and release artifacts, plus validation slices from model evaluations and an anonymized human pilot.
AI.conclusion: ForecastBench-Sim complements real-world forecasting benchmarks by offering controlled, immediately resolvable tasks for studying probabilistic reasoning under dynamic world states.

title: SC3-Eval: Evaluating Robot Foundation Models via Self-Consistent Video Generation
summary: Evaluating generalist robot manipulation policies in the real world is expensive, slow, and difficult to scale. Action-conditioned video world models offer a scalable alternative by simulating policy rollouts. Autoregressive rollouts accumulate compounding errors, observations across multiple camera views must remain mutually consistent, and the evaluator must generalize to policies whose behaviors lie outside the training distribution. We address these challenges with SC3-Eval, a self-consistent video generation recipe that adapts a pre-trained video foundation model into an accurate policy evaluator by enforcing three complementary forms of consistency. First, forward-inverse dynamics consistency jointly trains the model to predict frames from actions and to recover actions from frames, anchoring generated rollouts to a physically plausible action manifold and counteracting the drift a forward-only model cannot penalize. Second, cross-view consistency trains the model to inpaint each camera view from the other, keeping the multi-camera observation coherent over long rollouts without any explicit memory mechanism. Third, test-time consistency reuses the inverse dynamics mode at inference as a per-action-chunk uncertainty signal that terminates rollouts whose generated frames drift away from the requested actions. We also demonstrate SC3-Eval rollouts reproduce the failure modes that policies exhibit in real-world rollouts, supporting fine-grained diagnostic comparison rather than aggregate ranking alone. Across seven real-world vision-language-action policies, SC3-Eval attains a closed-loop Pearson correlation of $0.929$ and MMRV of $0.119$, outperforming three strong prior video-model-based baselines, and generalizes to new tasks.

AI.tldr: SC3-Eval is a self-consistent video-based policy evaluator that repurposes a pre-trained video model to evaluate real-world robot manipulation policies by enforcing three consistencies: forward-backward dynamics, cross-view inpainting, and test-time uncertainty checks, enabling scalable, faithful rollouts and diagnostic failure-mode analysis.
AI.motivation: Evaluating generalist robot manipulation policies in the real world is expensive and hard to scale; action-conditioned video world models offer scalability but suffer from drift, multi-view inconsistency, and out-of-distribution policy behaviors.
AI.method: Train a video foundation model with (1) forward-inverse dynamics consistency to anchor rollouts to plausible actions, (2) cross-view consistency to keep multi-camera observations coherent via inpainting across views, and (3) test-time consistency that reuses inverse dynamics at inference as an uncertainty signal to terminate drifted rollouts. Use these self-consistent rollouts to diagnose policy failure modes, not just rank performance.
AI.result: SC3-Eval achieves high fidelity rollouts that reproduce real-world failure modes and yields strong evaluation metrics: closed-loop Pearson correlation 0.929 and MMRV 0.119 across seven real-world vision-language-action policies, outperforming three strong baselines and generalizing to new tasks.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, accurate policy evaluator for real-world robotics: self-consistent video rollouts grounded in forward-backward dynamics, cross-view coherence, and test-time uncertainty enable faithful evaluation and diagnostic insights beyond aggregate rankings.

title: Neural Phase Correlation
summary:
Correspondence is fundamentally relational: it seeks the unknown transformation between two observations of a common scene, not the content of either. Yet the dominant learning-based methods do not represent the transformation as a first-class object in the architecture. They encode each image independently and let a learned similarity function or a deep decoder discover the mapping implicitly. Phase correlation is the canonical exception, measuring the inter-image relationship directly in the Fourier domain, but the rigidity of its fixed basis confines it to global translation.
  We introduce a learned generalization of phase correlation that lifts this restriction by learning the basis on which the transformation decomposes. The same algebraic primitive extends to dense non-rigid deformations and to unitary dynamics. On the ACDC cardiac-MRI benchmark the framework matches or exceeds prior published baselines on both registration directions. On CAMUS echocardiography it matches state-of-the-art without auxiliary scoring or adaptive-smoothness mechanisms. Applied to time-evolved wavefunction pairs of the 1-D quantum harmonic oscillator, the same framework recovers the Hermite-function eigenstates and the quantized energy levels of the unknown Hamiltonian from observation pairs alone.

AI.tldr: Learned basis-based phase correlation generalizes transformation estimation to dense non-rigid deformations and unitary dynamics; achieves competitive results on medical-imaging benchmarks (ACDC, CAMUS) and on 1-D quantum harmonic oscillator dynamics.
AI.motivation: Phase correlation uses a fixed Fourier basis, which is limited to global translation. A trainable basis allows decomposition of more complex transformations and enables cross-domain applicability.
AI.method: Introduce a trainable basis for the transformation decomposition within the phase-correlation framework, extending the algebraic primitive to non-rigid deformations and unitary dynamics; demonstrate on medical-imaging benchmarks (ACDC, CAMUS) and time-evolved 1-D quantum oscillator wavefunction pairs.
AI.result: ACDC: matches or exceeds baselines in both registration directions; CAMUS: matches state-of-the-art without auxiliary scoring or adaptive-smoothing mechanisms; Quantum oscillator: recovers Hermite-function eigenstates and the quantized energy levels of the Hamiltonian from observation pairs alone.
AI.conclusion: A learned generalization of phase correlation provides a first-class transformation representation capable of handling non-rigid and dynamical transformations, achieving strong cross-domain performance across medical imaging and quantum physics tasks.

title: DRIFT: Refining Instruction Data via On-Policy Data Attribution
summary: Optimizing the training data distribution for Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) dictates the capability of Large Language Models (LLMs). While existing data curation methods excel at accelerating training under constrained budgets, they are less suited to elevating the capability upper bound. The challenge here is no longer to identify a smaller subset that preserves performance, but to refine the data distribution toward instances most capable of improving the final model. To address this problem, we explore instance-level data attribution using Influence Functions (IF). We identify that standard IF formulations struggle in this setting due to two structural limitations: a proximity gap caused by off-policy validation targets, and a severe bias towards gradient norm. We propose DRIFT (Data Refinement via On-Policy Influence Functions for Supervised Fine-Tuning). Instead of relying on external reference data, DRIFT utilizes the model's on-policy rollouts as validation targets, which empirically minimizes the parameter proximity gap and better aligns with the local neighborhood assumption of IF. It further applies signed weighting based on trajectory correctness and debiases influence scores against the gradient hacking issue, allowing a small set of validation queries to act as reliable anchors for attributing the full dataset. Experiments on 7B-parameter instruction and reasoning models show that DRIFT consistently raises the performance ceiling on both, outperforming existing data curation baselines.

AI.tldr: DRIFT refines SFT data by on-policy influence functions, addressing proximity bias and gradient hacking to push the model's performance ceiling higher.
AI.motivation: To raise the upper-bound capability of LLMs by optimizing the instance-level data distribution for supervised fine-tuning, beyond traditional subset selection.
AI.method: DRIFT uses on-policy validation targets via model rollouts, applies signed weighting based on trajectory correctness, and debiases influence scores against gradient norms; a small set of validation queries anchors attribution to the full dataset.
AI.result: Empirically, DRIFT consistently improves the performance ceiling on 7B-parameter instruction and reasoning models and outperforms existing data curation baselines.
AI.conclusion: On-policy influence functions with proper debiasing and signed weighting can provide reliable, high-impact data attribution for SFT, enabling greater final model capability.

title: What Must Generalist Agents Remember?
summary: This paper develops a formal account of what generalist agents must store in memory in order to act near-optimally across multiple environments and goals. It shows that when two domains share an observational bottleneck but require incompatible optimal actions, any uniformly near-optimal policy must induce distinct memory distributions at that bottleneck. The result yields a separation theorem: sufficiently successful agents cannot rely only on current state observations, but must preserve domain-relevant information in memory. The paper further shows that if an agent's memory contains enough information to estimate values for related goals, then that memory can be used to approximately reconstruct the agent's local transition dynamics. Together, these results characterize memory as the substrate that supports domain disambiguation, transition-model reconstruction, and planning for generalist agents.

AI.tldr: Distinct memory distributions at shared bottlenecks are necessary for near-optimal policies across domains with incompatible actions; memory underpins domain disambiguation and planning by enabling transition-model reconstruction.
AI.motivation: To identify what generalist agents must store in memory to remain near-optimally effective across multiple environments and goals, and to formalize the role of memory in disambiguating domains and enabling planning.
AI.method: Formal theoretical analysis introducing an observational bottleneck and memory distributions; proving a separation theorem: near-optimal policies require distinct bottleneck memories across domains with conflicting optimal actions; showing that memory containing goal-value information enables approximate reconstruction of local transition dynamics.
AI.result: Proves that if two domains share an observational bottleneck but require incompatible optimal actions, any uniformly near-optimal policy must induce distinct memory distributions at the bottleneck. Also shows that memory with sufficient information to estimate values for related goals can be used to approximately reconstruct local transition dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Memory is the substrate that supports domain disambiguation, transition-model reconstruction, and planning for generalist agents; relying on memory beyond current observations is necessary to achieve near-optimality across diverse environments.

title: SRL: Combining SLIP Model and Reinforcement Learning for Agile Robotic Jumping
summary: Robotic jumping is pivotal in applications such as search and rescue and logistics, where crossing obstacles and enhancing mobility efficiency are critical. The Spring-Loaded Inverted Pendulum (SLIP) model leverages simplified spring-mass dynamics that naturally encode biologically plausible hopping motions, yet its performance degrades on irregular terrain due to idealized assumptions regarding contact and joint dynamics. Meanwhile, Reinforcement Learning (RL) can adapt to diverse and complex environments but often requires extensive data from unguided exploration. The complementary strengths of SLIP's physically grounded baseline and RL's adaptive capabilities motivate a hybrid framework that overcomes these individual limitations. We therefore propose Spring-loaded Reinforcement Learning (SRL), which integrates SLIP-based feedforward control signals with RL-driven real-time feedback, enabling continuous optimization of robotic jumping. Experimental results demonstrate that SRL can achieve more stable jumps with much less training time than the baseline method, maintaining an average position tracking error below 0.1 m and velocity tracking errors within +/-3% of the target values. Through bipedal and quadrupedal simulations of ground and stair jumping, as well as sim-to-sim and sim-to-real validations, SRL exhibits robust adaptability to various task requirements and environmental complexities, underscoring its potential for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: Hybrid SRL framework merges SLIP-based feedforward control with reinforcement learning for real-time jumping control, achieving data-efficient, robust performance across diverse terrains and tasks.
AI.motivation: SLIP offers a physics-grounded baseline but struggles with irregular terrain; RL provides adaptation but data-hungry; combining them aims to improve robustness and sample efficiency.
AI.method: Integrates SLIP-based feedforward signals with RL-driven feedback; evaluations include bipedal and quadrupedal simulations across ground and stair jumping, plus sim-to-sim and sim-to-real validations.
AI.result: Achieves more stable jumps with substantially reduced training time; position error <0.1 m; velocity error within ±3% of targets; demonstrates robust adaptability across tasks and environments.
AI.conclusion: SRL shows promise for real-world deployment by uniting physics-based reliability with learning-based flexibility, offering data-efficient, versatile jumping control.

title: Architectural Bias in Face Presentation Attack Detection: A Comparative Study of Vision Transformers and Convolutional Neural Networks
summary: Face Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) systems constitute a critical security layer in biometric authentication; however, existing approaches exhibit systematic performance disparities across demographic groups, disproportionately affecting individuals with darker skin tones. This paper presents a comparative empirical investigation of whether Vision Transformer architectures reduce demographic bias in face PAD systems relative to convolutional baselines. Experiments are conducted on the CASIA-SURF Cross-Ethnicity Face Anti-Spoofing (CeFA) dataset. Three architectures are evaluated: a Multimodal ViT-Tiny trained from scratch, a ResNet18 CNN baseline, and a pretrained DeiT-S fine-tuned on CeFA across African, East Asian, and zero-shot Central Asian demographic groups. DeiT-S achieves the highest overall accuracy of 97.27% and the lowest EER of 0.86%, outperforming ResNet18 at 90.15% accuracy. In terms of fairness, DeiT-S reduces the inter-ethnic ACER gap between African and East Asian subjects to 0.13%, compared to 0.75% reported in an LBP-based work [6], representing an 83% reduction. Most notably, while ResNet18 records a BPCER of 10.44% on zero-shot Central Asian subjects, DeiT-S maintains 2.89% on the same unseen group, demonstrating a 3.6x generalization advantage. These results suggest that pretrained Vision Transformers achieve superior PAD accuracy, produce smaller demographic performance gaps, and generalize more equitably across unseen demographic groups, indicating that cross-demographic fairness in PAD may partly be influenced by architectural design.

AI.tldr: Vision Transformer (DeiT-S) outperforms CNN baselines in face PAD on CeFA, achieving higher accuracy and lower EER, with substantially smaller inter-demographic gaps and better zero-shot generalization, suggesting architecture shapes cross-demographic fairness.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether ViT architectures reduce demographic bias in face PAD compared to convolutional baselines, addressing fairness concerns in biometric security.
AI.method: Empirical comparison on CeFA with three architectures: Multimodal ViT-Tiny (from scratch), ResNet18 CNN baseline, and pretrained DeiT-S (fine-tuned on CeFA). Demographic groups: African, East Asian, and zero-shot Central Asian. Metrics include accuracy, EER, BPCER, ACER, and cross-demographic gaps.
AI.result: DeiT-S achieves highest overall accuracy (97.27%) and lowest EER (0.86%), outperforming ResNet18 (accuracy 90.15%). For fairness, inter-ethnic ACER gap (African vs East Asian) is 0.13% for DeiT-S vs 0.75% in a prior LBP-based study. On zero-shot Central Asian subjects, ResNet18 reports BPCER 10.44% while DeiT-S reports 2.89% (3.6x better generalization).
AI.conclusion: Pretrained Vision Transformers yield superior PAD accuracy, smaller demographic performance gaps, and stronger cross-demographic generalization, indicating architecture can influence fairness in PAD across demographic groups.

title: TRIDENT: Breaking the Hybrid-Safety-Physics Coupling for Provably Safe Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Safe coordination in networked cyber-physical systems forces learning algorithms to simultaneously handle hybrid discrete-continuous actions, hard training-time safety constraints, and physics-governed dynamics. We show that these three features form a directed cycle of biases that defeats any naive composition of off-the-shelf modules, and formalize this as a three-way coupling lemma. We then introduce TRIDENT, the first MARL framework whose three components are co-designed to cancel each leak: a Richardson-Romberg gradient correction reducing Gumbel-Softmax bias from O(tau) to O(tau^2), a Lyapunov-constrained sequential trust-region update enforcing per-iterate feasibility, and a physics-informed residual critic that decomposes value rather than reward. We prove an O~(1/sqrt(K)) convergence rate to a constrained Nash equilibrium and an O(sqrt(K)) cumulative-violation bound. On multi-UAV mobile-edge computing, autonomous intersection management, and a hybrid SMAC variant, TRIDENT cuts training-time violations by 95.5% over MADDPG and 76.3% over MACPO, while improving reward by 13.5% over the strongest unconstrained baseline.

AI.tldr: TRIDENT is a co-designed MARL framework that jointly addresses hybrid actions, safety constraints, and physics-based dynamics, achieving formal convergence guarantees and substantial training-time safety improvements over baselines.
AI.motivation: Safe coordination in networked cyber-physical systems requires simultaneously handling discrete-continuous actions, hard safety constraints during training, and physics-governed dynamics. Naive modular combinations introduce bias feedback loops that degrade performance.
AI.method: A triadic design: (1) Richardson-Romberg gradient correction to reduce Gumbel-Softmax bias from O(tau) to O(tau^2); (2) Lyapunov-constrained sequential trust-region updates to enforce per-iterate feasibility; (3) a physics-informed residual critic that decomposes value rather than reward, enabling physics-aware value estimation.
AI.result: Proves O~(1/sqrt(K)) convergence to a constrained Nash equilibrium and O(sqrt(K)) cumulative-violation bound. Empirically, TRIDENT reduces training-time violations by 95.5% over MADDPG and 76.3% over MACPO and improves reward by 13.5% over the strongest unconstrained baseline across multi-UAV MEC, autonomous intersection management, and a hybrid SMAC variant.
AI.conclusion: Co-design of the three components cancels leakage biases, enabling safe, data-efficient MARL with formal guarantees and strong empirical performance gains over existing constrained and unconstrained baselines.

title: R2D-RL: A RoboCup 2D Soccer Environment for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Robot soccer is a challenging testbed for multi-agent reinforcement learning because it combines partial observability, cooperative and adversarial interaction, sparse rewards, and long-horizon tactical behavior. RoboCup 2D Soccer Simulation (RCSS2D) provides a mature robot-soccer platform, but its competition-oriented server-client architecture is difficult to use directly with modern Python-based MARL workflows. We introduce R2D-RL, a reinforcement learning environment that connects RCSS2D and HELIOS-based player clients to a Python MARL interface through shared-memory communication and cycle-level synchronization. R2D-RL supports full-field and scenario-based training with configurable opponents, Base discrete and Hybrid parameterized action spaces, action masks, expected possession value (EPV)-based reward shaping, and parallel execution. We provide front-goal scenarios and an 11-vs-11 full-field benchmark, together with baseline results.

AI.tldr: R2D-RL introduces a reinforcement learning environment that bridges RCSS2D RoboCup soccer with HELIOS-based players to a Python MARL interface, enabling cycle-accurate, shared-memory communication, configurable action spaces, and reward shaping for scalable multi-agent learning.
AI.motivation: The RoboCup 2D soccer simulators and modern Python MARL workflows are disconnected, hindering reproducible, scalable MARL research in partial observability, cooperative/adversarial dynamics, sparse rewards, and long-horizon tactical behavior. A bridge is needed to leverage mature RoboCup platforms within contemporary MARL pipelines.
AI.method: R2D-RL builds a bridge between RCSS2D and HELIOS-based player clients and a Python MARL interface via shared-memory communication and cycle-level synchronization. Features include full-field and scenario-based training, configurable opponents, base discrete and hybrid parameterized action spaces, action masks, EPV-based reward shaping, and parallel execution. It provides front-goal scenarios and an 11-vs-11 full-field benchmark with baseline results.
AI.result: The paper presents a functional RL environment that enables Python MARL workflows on RoboCup RCSS2D, along with a suite of scenarios and an 11-vs-11 benchmark, accompanied by baseline results for evaluation.
AI.conclusion: R2D-RL offers a practical, scalable bridge to integrate RoboCup RCSS2D into Python-based MARL workflows, enabling reproducible experiments and broader access to RoboCup soccer as a testing ground for multi-agent reinforcement learning.

title: Self-Supervised Mask-Aware Transformers for Fault-Tolerant FBG Force Sensing in Minimally Invasive Surgical Robotics
summary: In minimally invasive surgical robotics, catheter-scale Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors are promising due to their ability to estimate multi-dimensional forces by multiplexing several optical channels. However, deploying these compact multi-channel sensors introduces two critical engineering challenges: inherent nonlinear cross-axis coupling during complex deformations, and intermittent channel dropouts caused by fiber fractures in constrained workspaces. These compounding issues severely degrade force estimation. Existing fault-tolerant approaches rely on combinatorial model banks, which scale exponentially with the channel count and demand prohibitively expensive per-pattern calibration. In this paper, we propose a unified, self-supervised mask-aware Transformer that explicitly models channel availability to enable graceful degradation under diverse and dynamic sensor failures. The encoder is pretrained via masked-channel reconstruction on unlabeled data streams and fine-tuned for force regression using a balanced clean-and-corrupted-view objective alongside a dynamic corruption curriculum. Furthermore, a parallel uncertainty head, trained via heteroscedastic Gaussian negative log-likelihood, predicts per-axis confidence in a single forward pass, circumventing the overhead of multi-pass ensembles. Evaluated on a catheter-scale 8-channel FBG dataset, our single unified model achieves a nominal Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.0066~N and degrades gracefully to 0.0126~N under severe 4-channel failures. This significantly outperforms a comprehensive model bank of 255 per-pattern neural networks (0.0154~N at 4-channel loss) while eliminating pattern-specific calibration.

AI.tldr: Unified self-supervised mask-aware transformer for 8-channel catheter-scale FBG sensors enabling graceful degradation under channel dropout, outperforming per-pattern model banks.
AI.motivation: Fault-tolerant force estimation in catheter-scale FBG sensors suffers from nonlinear cross-axis coupling and channel dropout; existing model banks scale poorly and require extensive calibration.
AI.method: Pretrain a Transformer via masked-channel reconstruction on unlabeled streams; fine-tune for force regression with a balanced clean-and-corrupted-view objective and a dynamic corruption curriculum; include a parallel uncertainty head trained with heteroscedastic Gaussian NLL for per-axis confidence, enabling a single forward pass.
AI.result: Nominal RMSE 0.0066 N; degrades to 0.0126 N under severe 4-channel failures; outperforms a 255-model bank calibrated per pattern (0.0154 N at 4-channel loss).
AI.conclusion: A unified model provides graceful degradation under sensor failures without per-pattern calibration, reducing calibration overhead and improving robustness for catheter-scale FBG force sensing.

title: A Prototypical Signature Approach for Writer-Independent Offline Signature Verification
comment: Accepted for oral presentation at the International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) 2026
summary: Offline handwritten signature verification aims to distinguish genuine from forged signatures using static images. Since real forgeries are rarely available, negative samples are usually randomly drawn from genuine signatures of other users to create training data. However, this random selection often lacks diversity, increases redundancy, and escalates computational cost, leading to inefficient training. We propose a data-driven strategy to generate diverse, informative negative samples using prototypical signatures, which are compact, non-identifiable summaries of genuine signature features. Based on the experiments results, we conclude that (i) prototypical signatures yield more informative negative samples, improving the detection of skilled forgeries; (ii) the proposed approach is backbone-agnostic, showing robustness across architectures; and (iii) when combined with a primal-form linear SVM, it serves as an alternative to RBF-based models while significantly improving scalability and computational efficiency. Implementation of the method is available at https://github.com/kdmoura/proto_hsv.

AI.tldr: Prototypical signatures for negative sampling in offline HSV improve diversity and efficiency; robust across backbones; compatible with primal-linear SVM as scalable alternative to RBF.
AI.motivation: Negatives drawn from other genuine samples lack diversity, cause redundancy and increase computation; need data-driven, informative negatives to improve forgery detection.
AI.method: Construct prototypical signatures as compact, non-identifiable summaries of genuine features; use them to generate diverse negative samples; evaluate backbone-agnostic performance across architectures; compare with RBF SVM; provide implementation.
AI.result: Informative negative samples via prototypical signatures improve detection of skilled forgeries; approach is backbone-agnostic; when combined with a primal-form linear SVM, it offers a scalable alternative to RBF-based models with improved efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Data-driven negative sampling using prototypical signatures is effective and scalable for offline HSV, offering a viable alternative to RBF models and robust across architectures.

title: SAGE: Retain-Aware Post-Hoc Sanitization of Final Unlearning Vector
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) unlearning aims to remove undesirable knowledge or behaviors while preserving retained capabilities. Current unlearning methods all involve a trade-off between unlearning and retention. We have found that the retention activation bias can also be used to quantify the damage an unlearning method inflicts on retention, without considering the specific implementation of the unlearning process. This allows us to restore retention performance for any unlearning method using a post-hoc approach. Therefore, we propose a complementary post-hoc setting to sanitize the final update vector without rerunning the original unlearning pipeline. In this setting, we design SAGE, Spectral Activation-GEometry Sanitization, a source-agnostic correction for final unlearning updates. SAGE collects real module inputs from a small retain proxy, extracts their dominant activation geometry, and solves a source-anchored optimization objective in closed form, which suppresses update components aligned with high-energy retained directions while preserving the source method's forgetting carrier. Across multiple unlearning methods, model scales, and benchmarks, SAGE consistently relieves the retain-forget trade-off, identifying post-hoc sanitization of final vectors as a practical and underexplored axis for machine unlearning.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc sanitization (SAGE) of final unlearning updates can restore retention without re-running the unlearning process, reducing the retain-forget trade-off across methods, scales, and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Unlearning in LLMs often sacrifices retained capabilities. A general, post-hoc method to quantify and mitigate retention damage is needed, without depending on the specific unlearning method.
AI.method: SAGE (Spectral Activation-GEometry Sanitization) collects real module inputs from a small retain proxy, extracts dominant activation geometry, and solves a source-anchored optimization in closed form to suppress update components aligned with high-energy retained directions while preserving the forgetting carrier.
AI.result: SAGE consistently relieves the retain-forget trade-off across multiple unlearning methods, model scales, and benchmarks, indicating post-hoc sanitization of final update vectors is an effective and practical axis for unlearning.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc sanitization of final vectors via activation-geometry-aware optimization is a viable, source-agnostic approach to improve retention while preserving forgetting, reducing dependency on the original unlearning pipeline.

title: ProfiLLM: Utility-Aligned Agentic User Profiling for Industrial Ride-Hailing Dispatch
summary: Bringing Large Language Models (LLMs) into industrial ride-hailing dispatch as semantic feature extractors over platform-scale behavioral logs is a compelling but under-explored data systems problem. Production matching pipelines remain dominated by structured numerical features, yet decisive behavioral signals (e.g., a driver's habitual aversion to certain regions) are inherently contextual and naturally expressible as LLM-generated user profiles. However, scaling such profiling to a live, millisecond-latency dispatcher faces three intertwined constraints rarely addressed together: on a platform with millions of daily orders, logs exceed any LLM's context window by orders of magnitude; most users are long-tail, with too few interactions for per-user profiling; and surface-fluent profiles do not necessarily improve downstream prediction utility. We present ProfiLLM, an agentic LLM data pipeline that operationalizes utility-aligned user profiling for production matching systems through two modules. (1) Tool-Augmented Global Knowledge Mining equips an LLM agent with 27 analytical tools to mine platform-scale data, producing reusable global knowledge, adaptive user clustering rules, and region-level supply-demand priors. (2) Utility-Aligned Profile Exploration generates multiple candidate profiles per cluster, evaluates them via a lightweight downstream utility proxy, iteratively refines the best candidates and constructs preference pairs for DPO fine-tuning. Deployed on DiDi's production dispatcher, ProfiLLM achieves up to +6.14% relative AUC improvement in outcome prediction, up to +4.35% GMV gain in dispatching simulation, and consistent improvements in a 14-day online A/B test including +0.47% GMV, +0.33% Completion Rate, and -0.82% Cancel-Before-Accept rate.

AI.tldr: ProfiLLM introduces an agent-based LLM data pipeline with Tool-Augmented Global Knowledge Mining and Utility-Aligned Profile Exploration to generate and refine downstream-valuable user profiles for production ride-hailing dispatch, achieving measurable improvements in AUC and GMV in simulation and live A/B tests.
AI.motivation: Address core challenges in deploying LLM-based user profiling at platform scale under strict latency, sparse interactions for long-tail users, and potential misalignment between expressive profiles and actual downstream utility.
AI.method: Two modules: (1) Tool-Augmented Global Knowledge Mining, equipping an LLM agent with 27 analytic tools to harvest platform-scale knowledge, derive global priors, and adaptive clustering rules; (2) Utility-Aligned Profile Exploration, generating multiple candidate profiles per cluster, evaluating them with a lightweight downstream utility proxy, refining top candidates, and creating preference pairs for DPO fine-tuning; deployed in DiDi's production dispatcher.
AI.result: Up to 6.14% relative improvement in outcome-prediction AUC; up to 4.35% GMV gain in dispatching simulation; online 14-day A/B: +0.47% GMV, +0.33% Completion Rate, -0.82% Cancel-Before-Accept rate.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the practicality of utility-aligned, LLM-based user profiling in a real-time dispatch system, showing meaningful performance gains and outlining a scalable architecture that combines global knowledge mining with downstream-utility-driven profiling and alignment.

title: DNN Koopman-Based Deviation Compensation for UGV Path Tracking Control on Coupled Slope and Potholed Road
summary: Unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) operating in off-road scenarios are confronted with complex terrain disturbances that can substantially degrade path tracking performance. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a deep neural network (DNN) Koopman-based deviation compensation strategy for UGV path tracking control. Firstly, based on the vehicle dynamic function on coupled slope, an adaptive forgetting recursive least squares method with decoupled error terms is designed to estimate tire cornering stiffness. On this basis, a Laguerre model predictive control (LMPC) path tracking control strategy is designed by incorporating Laguerre functions, which can reduce computational resource usage while maintaining reliable tracking performance across different coupled slope scenarios. Then, by integrating Koopman operator theory with DNN, a DNN Koopman (DK) path deviation compensation method is proposed, which significantly improves the path tracking accuracy of UGV under potholed road disturbances. Furthermore, an event-triggered parallel cooperative (EPC) compensation mechanism that couples LMPC with DK is established based on compensation activation criteria and credibility verification. This mechanism improves path tracking accuracy on potholed road while ensuring the feasibility of overall steering command and stability of vehicle after DK compensation. Finally, a hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) experimental platform is constructed for validation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed UGV path tracking strategy improves tracking performance by more than 11.5% across multiple operating conditions.

AI.tldr: A DNN-Koopman aided deviation compensation framework for UGV path tracking with adaptive tire stiffness estimation, Laguerre MPC, and EPC, validated on HiL with 11.5% gains.
AI.motivation: Off-road UGV path tracking is degraded by complex terrain disturbances; there is a need for accurate, robust deviation compensation that maintains stability and computational efficiency.
AI.method: 1) Adaptive forgetting recursive least squares with decoupled error terms to estimate tire cornering stiffness; 2) Laguerre model predictive control (LMPC) using Laguerre functions for efficient path tracking under coupled slope; 3) Deep neural network Koopman (DK) path deviation compensation to enhance tracking under pothole disturbances; 4) Event-triggered parallel cooperative (EPC) mechanism coupling LMPC and DK with activation criteria and credibility check; 5) Hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) experimental validation.
AI.result: The approach reports >11.5% improvement in path tracking accuracy across multiple operating conditions, with maintained steering feasibility and vehicle stability during DK compensation.
AI.conclusion: Integrating adaptive estimation, LMPC, and DNN-Koopman deviation compensation within an EPC framework yields robust UGV path tracking under disturbed off-road conditions, validated experimentally on HiL.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Modal Retrieval for Knowledge-Grounded News Image Captioning
summary: Traditional image captioning methods often struggle to generate comprehensive, context-rich descriptions, especially for details not directly observable from visual cues. To overcome this, we propose a novel retrieval-augmented image captioning framework that generates captions with deeper insights, such as object attributes, event context, and underlying significance, by leveraging external knowledge. Our approach features a hierarchical multi-modal article retrieval mechanism that moves beyond monolithic text entities. This retrieval considers article structure-aware features, including weighted textual components (e.g., headlines, body sections) and visual placement patterns, alongside multi-faceted similarity computations (content--visual, visual--visual, and discourse positioning). A subsequent contextual relevance refinement stage further enhances the retrieved information. The retrieved articles then serve as the knowledge base for caption generation: first, a VLM generates a concise image description; second, we segment relevant information from the retrieved articles based on this description; and finally, an LLM utilizes both the description and extracted knowledge to generate a comprehensive, contextually detailed caption. We participated in the ACM Multimedia EVENTA 2025 Challenge and achieved 5th place with an overall score of 0.2824 on the private test set of the OpenEvent-V1 dataset. Source code is publicly released at https://github.com/mf0212/EVENTA-Challange.

AI.tldr: Retrieval-augmented image captioning with hierarchical multi-modal article retrieval to produce deep, context-rich captions; achieved 5th at EVENTA 2025 with 0.2824 on OpenEvent-V1; code released.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations of traditional captioning that rely solely on visual cues by leveraging external knowledge and structured article retrieval for object attributes, event context, and significance.
AI.method: Hierarchical multi-modal article retrieval with structure-aware features (weighted headlines/body sections) and visual placement patterns; multi-faceted similarity (content-visual, visual-visual, discourse positioning); contextual relevance refinement; pipeline: VLM generates concise description; extract relevant information from retrieved articles; LLM fuses description and extracted knowledge to produce detailed caption.
AI.result: 5th place on private test set OpenEvent-V1 in ACM Multimedia EVENTA 2025 with score 0.2824.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge-grounded, retrieval-augmented captioning improves descriptive richness; demonstrates effective use of external knowledge in captioning tasks; code publicly available.

title: Ghost Attractor Networks: Basin-Structured Dynamical Decoders for Closed-Loop Sequential Generation
summary: Sequential output generation with large-scale Transformer and diffusion decoders pays a memory cost that grows with sequence length, plus iterative per-step computation. Replacing them with small feed-forward decoders restores efficiency but produces unstructured latent representations that limit closed-loop control: phase-conditioned action generation and cross-step latent carry-over both require a latent geometry with stable basins. This article proposes Ghost Attractor Networks, a theoretically derived dynamical decoder whose latent evolves under a learned potential with drift and produces a basin-attractor structure by construction. Three desiderata (multi-modality, decoder-level single-pass switching, and constant memory) motivate the potential-drift form, and mode transitions arise as saddle-node bifurcations with ghost-attractor escape. A hierarchical phase-space decomposition separates first-order basin convergence from second-order proprioceptive refinement. Empirically, a Ghost trained end-to-end with a behavioral-cloning and contrastive objective exhibits the predicted gradient-flow contraction in its potential, with the gradient norm decaying by 67 percent across five integration steps on 1430 held-out samples. Ghost is evaluated as a robotic action decoder. A 2.3-million-parameter Ghost matches the offline accuracy of a 1.07-billion-parameter Diffusion Transformer at 462 times fewer parameters and 32 times lower latency, and beats five alternative 2M-parameter decoders (MLP, Neural ODE, CVAE, Transformer, 1-step Diffusion) on offline mean squared error by 5.9 to 29 percent. On the LIBERO-10 closed-loop benchmark, phase conditioning on Ghost's basin-structured latent yields a 13.5 percentage-point success-rate gain over a feed-forward MLP baseline, and persistent-latent ensembling reaches a 95.7 percent final success rate.

AI.tldr: Ghost Attractor Networks (GANs) introduce a dynamical latent that evolves under a learnable potential with drift, creating basin-structured attractors for stable, phase-conditioned, low-latency decoding. They aim to replace large sequential decoders with compact, constant-memory decoders while enabling multi-modality and cross-step carry-over.
AI.motivation: Sequential decoders like large Transformers and diffusion models incur memory and compute that scale with sequence length, hindering efficient closed-loop control. A compact, structured latent with stable basins is needed to support phase-conditioned action generation and cross-step carry-over.
AI.method: Propose Ghost Attractor Networks where latent dynamics follow a learned potential with drift, creating basin-attractor structure. Three design desiderata drive the potential-drift form: multi-modality, decoder-level single-pass switching, and constant memory. Mode transitions appear as saddle-node bifurcations with ghost-attractor escape. Introduce a hierarchical phase-space decomposition to separate first-order basin convergence from second-order proprioceptive refinement. Train end-to-end with behavioral cloning and a contrastive objective to enforce gradient-flow contraction.
AI.result: Empirical: gradient-flow contraction observed; gradient norm decays by 67% over five integration steps on 1430 held-out samples. A 2.3M-parameter Ghost matches the offline accuracy of a 1.07B Diffusion Transformer at 462x fewer parameters and 32x lower latency. Outperforms five other ~2M-parameter decoders on offline MSE by 5.9–29%. On LIBERO-10 closed-loop benchmark, phase-conditioned Ghost yields 13.5 percentage-point improvement over a feed-forward MLP; persistent-latent ensembling reaches 95.7% final success rate.
AI.conclusion: Ghost demonstrates that a small, dynamically evolved latent with a trained potential can achieve competitive offline accuracy and strong closed-loop performance with far smaller parameter counts and lower latency, owing to stable basin geometry and phase-space decomposition. The results support the viability of stable, memory-efficient decoders for real-time control and multi-modality tasks.

title: WorldLines: Benchmarking and Modeling Long-Horizon Stateful Embodied Agents
summary: To assist humans over extended periods in real homes, embodied agents must remember user routines, world states, and past interactions. Existing long-term memory benchmarks mainly evaluate language-centric retrieval and question answering, while embodied benchmarks often focus on short-horizon task execution without testing long-term memory use in dynamic environments. We introduce WorldLines, a project-driven benchmark for long-horizon embodied household assistance. It constructs temporally extended household traces with dialogues, actions, execution feedback, object and device state changes, and converts them into evidence-linked samples for Memory QA and Embodied Task Planning. We further propose ObsMem, an observer-grounded memory framework that maintains visibility-aware memories and action-native state trails for state-aware decisions. Experiments reveal persistent challenges in partial observability, overwritten world states, and translating long-term memory into embodied plans, while ObsMem offers a stronger reference architecture for this setting.

AI.tldr: WorldLines provides a long-horizon, project-driven benchmark for embodied household memory; ObsMem is an observer-grounded memory framework; results show persistent challenges and position ObsMem as a stronger reference architecture.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks emphasize language-centric retrieval or short-horizon tasks; real-world embodied agents require robust long-term memory in dynamic environments. WorldLines fills this gap by providing temporally extended household traces and evidence-linked samples for Memory QA and Embodied Task Planning, enabling evaluation of long-term memory use in embodied settings.
AI.method: Construct temporally extended household traces including dialogues, actions, execution feedback, and object/device state changes; convert them into evidence-linked samples for Memory QA and Embodied Task Planning; propose ObsMem, an observer-grounded memory framework that maintains visibility-aware memories and action-native state trails for state-aware decisions.
AI.result: Experiments reveal persistent challenges in partial observability, overwritten world states, and translating long-term memory into embodied plans; ObsMem offers a stronger reference architecture for this setting.
AI.conclusion: WorldLines and ObsMem advance long-horizon embodied memory research by providing benchmarks and a reference architecture that highlight core challenges and guide future improvements in memory representation, memory-to-planning integration, and state-trace consistency.

title: ROBOSHACKLES: A Safety Dataset for Human-Injury Prevention in Embodied Foundation Models
summary: Embodied Foundation Models (EFMs) integrate multimodal understanding, future-state reasoning, and executable robot actions. Yet their safety alignment for human-injury prevention remains underexplored, primarily because real-world data of robots harming humans or creating hazardous household situations cannot be safely or ethically collected. To address this challenge, we propose a safety-critical data construction pipeline for human-injury prevention in EFMs.Starting from real DROID observations, our construction pipeline proceeds through scene understanding, hazard-aware image editing, temporal prompt generation, and single-pass rollout synthesis. The temporal prompts specify the expected scene evolution, while Wan2.7 synthesizes realistic robotic rollouts from the edited hazardous states in a single pass. Using this pipeline, we construct ROBOSHACKLES, a 10,000-clip robotic video dataset derived from real DROID observations, spanning two direct-harm and four indirect-harm categories. To ensure dataset quality, we assess task completion and visual quality with automatic metrics, and evaluate six representative EFMs under a refusal-based safety criterion. Results show that all evaluated models produce unsafe actions in the tested safety-critical scenarios, yielding a 100% unsafe action generation rate. ROBOSHACKLES serves as a scalable benchmark and training resource for refusal learning and hazard anticipation before robot action execution.The dataset is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/YZW00/RoboShackles.

AI.tldr: Safety-critical dataset construction for EFMs enabling refusal learning and hazard anticipation.
AI.motivation: To address lack of real-world hazardous interaction data for embodied foundation models by generating safe, synthetic, yet realistic scenarios of potential harm.
AI.method: From real DROID observations, apply scene understanding, hazard-aware image editing, and temporal prompt generation, followed by single-pass rollout synthesis (Wan2.7) to produce hazard-focused robotic clips.
AI.result: ROBOShackles: a 10,000-clip dataset spanning 2 direct-harm and 4 indirect-harm categories; automatic quality metrics for dataset and task feasibility; evaluation of six EFMs under a refusal-based safety criterion, with a 100% unsafe action generation rate in tested scenarios.
AI.conclusion: ROBOShACKLES provides a scalable benchmark and training resource for refusal learning and hazard anticipation prior to robot action execution.

title: Forged Calamity: Benchmark for Cross-Domain Synthetic Disaster Detection in the Age of Diffusion
summary: The rapid advancement of text-to-image diffusion models has enabled the creation of highly photorealistic synthetic images that closely resemble real photographs, making it increasingly difficult to distinguish authentic content from AI-generated fabrications. This poses challenges for cybersecurity, digital forensics, and disaster response, where fake imagery of floods, fires, or earthquakes can spread misinformation or disrupt emergency operations. To address this, we introduce Forged Calamity, a benchmark dataset for synthetic disaster detection containing 30,000 images, including 6,000 real and 24,000 synthetic samples generated by four diffusion models. Comprehensive experiments across fine-tuned and zero-shot settings reveal consistent weaknesses in current forensic approaches. Fine-tuned detectors perform well in-distribution but lose up to 50\% accuracy on unseen generators or disaster types, showing overfitting to model-specific artifacts. Zero-shot generalized detectors also struggle to maintain stable accuracy, with only limited resilience in a few representation-robust models. These findings highlight persistent generalization gaps and the urgent need for domain- and model-agnostic detection methods to ensure visual authenticity in the diffusion era.

AI.tldr: Forged Calamity introduces a 30k-image benchmark for synthetic disaster detection, revealing significant generalization gaps in both fine-tuned and zero-shot detectors, underscoring the need for domain- and model-agnostic detection methods.
AI.motivation: The rise of photorealistic text-to-image diffusion threatens authenticity verification in cybersecurity, forensics, and disaster response; current detectors overfit to known generators and fail to generalize to new disaster types or models.
AI.method: Construct Forged Calamity dataset (30k images: 6k real, 24k synthetic from four diffusion models). Evaluate detectors in in-distribution fine-tuned, out-of-distribution unseen generators, and disaster types; also test representation-robust models; report performance degradation and generalization gaps.
AI.result: Fine-tuned detectors perform well in-distribution but degrade on unseen generators or disaster types (up to ~50% accuracy loss). Zero-shot detectors show limited generalization; few representation-robust models offer stability. Overall, persistent generalization gaps exist.
AI.conclusion: There is an urgent need for domain- and model-agnostic detection approaches that generalize across generators and disaster scenarios to ensure visual authenticity in the diffusion era.

title: A Survey on Data-Driven Models for Soil Moisture Regression and Classification
summary: Soil Moisture (SM) modelling constitutes a complex spatiotemporal learning problem characterised by nonlinear environmental interactions, heterogeneous data sources, and limited ground observations. Physics-based approaches, such as water balance models, rely on explicit hydrological equations and high-quality inputs, but their computational cost and scalability limitations restrict large-scale deployment. Data-driven artificial intelligence (AI) methods have emerged as flexible alternatives, enabling the extraction of empirical relationships between soil moisture and environmental variables with reduced modelling assumptions. This work presents a structured survey of AI-based models for soil moisture estimation and classification. Existing approaches are organized into five categories: (a) statistical time-series models, (b) geostatistical methods (c) classical machine learning (ML) models, (d) Deep Learning (DL) models and (e) Probabilistic/Bayesian methods. These models leverage historical soil moisture records, meteorological variables, vegetation indices, topography, soil characteristics, and geolocation data to perform regression or classification tasks.

AI.tldr: Survey of AI-based soil moisture estimation/classification methods, organized into five categories, detailing data inputs and regression/classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Soil moisture modelling is a challenging spatiotemporal problem; physics-based approaches are accurate but costly and not easily scalable, motivating data-driven AI alternatives.
AI.method: Structured survey organizing existing approaches into five categories: (a) statistical time-series, (b) geostatistical, (c) classical ML, (d) deep learning, (e) probabilistic/Bayesian methods; discusses inputs used (historical SM, meteorological variables, vegetation indices, topography, soil properties, geolocation) and tasks (regression or classification).
AI.result: Provides a comprehensive taxonomy and synthesis of AI-based soil moisture methods, mapping data sources to model types, and highlighting trade-offs in performance and scalability.
AI.conclusion: AI-based approaches offer flexible, scalable alternatives for SM estimation/classification, contingent on data quality and coverage; future work may focus on multi-source data fusion, uncertainty quantification, and integration with physics-informed frameworks.

title: Externalizing Research Synthesis and Validation in AI Scientists through a Research Harness
summary: AI systems can increasingly automate scientific workflows, but the reasoning that links prior evidence, generated ideas, experiments and final claims often remains implicit inside model inference. Here we introduce Xcientist, a research harness that externalizes research synthesis and experimental validation into inspectable, contract-governed processes. Xcientist organizes literature evidence, idea states, implementation plans, ablation records and repair traces as persistent research artifacts, so that generated mechanisms can be grounded, executed, tested and revised without losing their evidential basis. We identify claim drift as a failure mode of automated research, where runnable artifacts no longer support the mechanism originally claimed. Across training-free memory systems, graph-structured traffic forecasting and multi-scale physics-informed neural networks, Xcientist preserves traceable trajectories from problem formulation to mechanism design, validation and bounded revision. These results suggest that AI scientists should be evaluated not only by their final artifacts, but by whether their synthesis and validation processes remain attributable, inspectable and scientifically accountable.

AI.tldr: Xcientist externalizes research synthesis and validation into inspectable, contract-governed artifacts to preserve traceability and prevent claim drift in AI-driven science.
AI.motivation: Addresses opacity and drift in automated scientific reasoning by ensuring explicit, attributable links between prior evidence, generated ideas, experiments, and claims; promotes accountability in AI-assisted research.
AI.method: Introduces Xscientist, a research harness that organizes literature evidence, idea states, implementation plans, ablation records, and repair traces as persistent artifacts governed by contracts, enabling grounding, execution, testing, and bounded revision of generated mechanisms; demonstrated across multiple domains.
AI.result: Preserves traceable trajectories from problem formulation to mechanism design, validation, and bounded revision across diverse systems (training-free memory systems, graph-structured traffic forecasting, and multi-scale physics-informed neural networks).
AI.conclusion: AI scientists should be evaluated on whether their synthesis and validation processes remain attributable, inspectable, and scientifically accountable, not solely on final artifacts.

title: EffiNav: Fusing Depth and Vision-Language for Efficient Object Goal Navigation
summary: To locate a target object while exploring the unknown environment is a fundamental capability for autonomous agents, with applications ranging from search-and-rescue to field robots. A simplified version of such task is Object Goal Navigation (ObjNav). In ObjNav, successful arrival at the target object provides a basic measure of performance; however, the efficiency of the navigation trajectory is equally important, as it indicates how intelligently the agent explores and how much time remains for subsequent tasks. In unknown environments, the key to efficient navigation lies in deciding where to explore next. While many prior works aim to address this core challenge and achieved promising performance in certain settings, recent training-based models and non-training frameworks still suffer from generalization and efficiency issues respectively, which in the worst cases can lead to excessive exploration of already-visited areas or redundant back-and-forth motion.
