We evaluate EffiNav on two widely used simulation benchmarks Habitat Matterport 3D (HM3D) and Open-Vocabulary Object goal Navigation (OVON), and further validate its effectiveness on physical robots in real-world settings. We conduct failure analysis on massive simulation episodes. With minimal modification, we also extend EffiNav to a memory-augmented ObjNav task on the GOAT-BENCH dataset, demonstrating its adaptability beyond standard ObjNav settings. Across two standard metrics--Success Rate (SR) and Success weighted by Path Length (SPL), EffiNav matches or outperforms recent baselines, reflecting its efficiency, robustness, and practical applicability. Recognizing the different emphases of the two datasets, the performances reveals this framework is more balanced and generalizable for efficient ObjNav.

AI.tldr: EffiNav provides a memory-augmented, efficient exploration strategy for Object Goal Navigation (ObjNav), achieving competitive SR and SPL on HM3D and OVON, with real-robot validation and GOAT-BENCH extension.
AI.motivation: Efficient ObjNav requires intelligent exploration to minimize revisiting areas; existing learning-based methods struggle with generalization; non-learning frameworks can be inefficient. This work targets better exploration efficiency and generalization.
AI.method: Proposes EffiNav, a memory-augmented ObjNav framework that guides next exploration decisions to improve trajectory efficiency. Evaluated on Habitat-Matterport3D (HM3D) and Open-Vocabulary Object Goal Navigation (OVON); includes failure analysis; extends with minimal modification to a memory-augmented ObjNav task on GOAT-BENCH to demonstrate adaptability.
AI.result: Across SR and SPL metrics, EffiNav matches or outperforms recent baselines, indicating improved efficiency, robustness, and practical applicability. Demonstrated effectiveness on real robots; shows balanced performance across dataset-specific demands.
AI.conclusion: EffiNav delivers a generalizable, efficient ObjNav solution that leverages memory-augmented planning, achieving strong cross-domain performance and real-world viability.

title: Rethinking Text-to-Image as Semantic-Aware Data Augmentation for Indoor Scene Recognition
summary: In the realm of computer vision, indoor image recognition presents challenges due to the intricate interplay of lighting conditions, occlusions, and diverse object arrangements within confined spaces. To address the lacks of training indoor images, we introduce a novel approach leveraging Stable Diffusion (SD) for the generation of synthetic images, which serve as a powerful data augmentation tool. The utilization of SD offers a principled framework for synthesizing diverse and realistic indoor scenes, thereby enriching the training data pool for robust indoor image recognition models. Experimental findings on the MIT Indoor Scene dataset reveal the potential of our proposed approach in enhancing the training of deep models when authentic data is limited. Furthermore, to prevent the misuse of SD synthetic images, we introduce a counter measure based on DIffusion Reconstruction Error (DIRE). The powerful DIRE presentation enables training robust classifiers only using lightweight deep models. Experiments show that our approach can perfectly recognize SD generated images with the accuracy of 100% using MobilenetV3.

AI.tldr: SD-based synthetic data augmentation for indoor scene recognition and a diffusion-reconstruction based defense; reports strong gains with limited real data and perfect SD-image detection with MobileNetV3.
AI.motivation: Indoor image recognition suffers from limited and diverse training data due to clutter, lighting, and occlusions. Synthetic data from Stable Diffusion is proposed to augment training and improve model robustness in data-scarce settings.
AI.method: Generate diverse, realistic indoor images using Stable Diffusion to augment the MIT Indoor Scene dataset. Introduce DIffusion Reconstruction Error (DIRE) as a defense against misuse of SD-generated images, and evaluate classifiers (notably lightweight models like MobileNetV3) trained with augmented data and/or robust to synthetic data.
AI.result: Augmenting training with SD-produced images on MIT Indoor Scene improves performance when real data is scarce. DIRE can train robust classifiers and detect SD-generated images, with reported 100% accuracy on SD-image detection using MobileNetV3.
AI.conclusion: SD-based synthetic data offers value for indoor recognition under data scarcity, and DIRE provides a defense against synthetic-image misuse. Validation should scrutinize generalization and reproducibility given strong detection accuracy claims.

title: Enhanced Graph Neural Networks using K-Hop Gaussian Diffusion
summary: Most graph neural network (GNN) cores rely on graph convolutions, typically implemented as message passing between direct (single-hop) neighbors. In many real-world graphs, edges can be noisy or poorly defined, limiting information propagation to local neighborhoods. Existing diffusion kernels, such as Personalized PageRank (PPR) and Heat Kernel, alleviate this issue through global propagation, but still struggle with complex local structures and distant node noise. To address these limitations, we propose a K-Hop Gaussian (KHG) diffusion kernel as a preprocessing module for graph data. KHG introduces multi-hop diffusion with Gaussian weighting for remote nodes, balancing local and global information propagation before applying standard GNNs. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that KHG significantly outperforms traditional message-passing GNNs, as well as PPR and Heat Kernel diffusion, particularly in noisy or structurally complex graphs.

AI.tldr: KHG is a preprocessing diffusion kernel that blends multi-hop Gaussian diffusion with local graph structure to enhance GNN inputs, yielding superior performance on noisy or structurally complex graphs.
AI.motivation: In real graphs, noisy or poorly defined edges impede information flow in standard GNNs. Global diffusion methods (e.g., PPR, Heat Kernel) help but may miss nuanced local structure; a diffusion approach that balances local and distant information can improve robustness.
AI.method: Introduce a K-Hop Gaussian (KHG) diffusion kernel as a preprocessing module. Perform multi-hop diffusion with Gaussian weighting over remote nodes (controlled by hop count k and Gaussian variance), then feed the resulting node representations into standard GNNs for downstream learning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on benchmark datasets show that KHG outperforms traditional message-passing GNNs and existing diffusion kernels (PPR, Heat Kernel), with particularly strong gains in noisy or structurally complex graphs.
AI.conclusion: KHG effectively reconciles local and global information propagation, providing a robust preprocessing step compatible with various GNN architectures; future work could analyze parameter sensitivity (k, variance) and scalability.

title: Generative-Model Predictive Planning for Navigation in Partially Observable Environments
summary: Navigation in partially observable environments presents a significant challenge for autonomous agents, requiring effective decision-making with limited sensory information in unknown environments. Belief-based methods, particularly those using neural networks to approximate the belief space, often fail to capture the inherent multimodality of belief spaces, especially in high-dimensional cases with perceptual aliasing. While generative models present a compelling alternative, they typically require substantial data or expert demonstrations and lack explicit mechanisms for long-term planning. In this paper, we introduce BeliefDiffusion, a novel framework that combines the benefits of both generation and planning. BeliefDiffusion leverages diffusion models to explicitly characterize multimodal belief distributions and utilizes Model Predictive Control (MPC) to simultaneously plan ahead. It consists of two steps: (1) Imagining plausible environment configurations based on observation history and (2) Planning efficient navigation strategies across an aggregated configurations. Through extensive experiments in synthetic map environments, we demonstrate that BeliefDiffusion significantly outperforms both model-free reinforcement learning baselines and other generative approaches in navigation success rate and path efficiency. Our results validate that explicitly incorporating multimodal belief representations into planning enables more robust navigation in partially observable settings.

AI.tldr: BeliefDiffusion uses diffusion models to represent multimodal beliefs in partially observable navigation and MPC to plan over imagined configurations, improving navigation success and efficiency in synthetic maps.
AI.motivation: Partial observability and perceptual aliasing create multimodal belief spaces; existing belief approximators and generative models struggle with multimodality and long-horizon planning.
AI.method: Two-step framework: (1) Imagining plausible environment configurations from observation history via diffusion-based generation; (2) Planning navigation strategies across aggregated configurations using Model Predictive Control (MPC).
AI.result: BeliefDiffusion outperforms model-free RL baselines and other generative methods in navigation success rate and path efficiency on synthetic maps.
AI.conclusion: Explicit multimodal belief representations integrated with planning enable more robust navigation in partially observable environments.

title: A Scalable Embodied Intelligence Platform for Seamless Real-to-Sim-to-Real Transfer of Household Mobile Manipulation Tasks
comment: CCF Transactions on Pervasive Computing and Interaction
summary: Mobile manipulation is a fundamental capability in embodied intelligence robotics. The growing demand for robust and generalizable manipulation in unstructured household environments has driven rapid progress in embodied intelligence platforms. However, achieving a seamless transfer across the real-to-sim-to-real cycle faces three key challenges, including costly high-fidelity simulation scenes reconstruction, the complexity of systematic strategy evaluation in simulation, and incompatible real-world deployments. To address these challenges, we develop BestMan, a scalable and seamless real-to-sim-to-real platform that bridges the gap between the simulation and the real world, enabling effective strategy development, integration, and deployment for household mobile manipulation. Specifically, we design a novel Automated Scene Generation (ASG) module to reconstruct realistic simulations from real observations. Then, we propose a simulation-guided task formalization and skill learning architecture that supports the flexible integration and large-scale evaluations of hybrid skill strategies in simulation. Finally, to enhance the real-world scalability, we develop a Hardware-agnostic and Unified Middleware (HUM) to ensure seamless and compatible sim-to-real transfer across heterogeneous mobile manipulators for real deployments. Experimental results demonstrate the superior performance of our proposed platform in establishing standardized benchmarks and facilitating promising research in the field of mobile manipulation.

AI.tldr: BestMan is a real-to-sim-to-real platform for scalable mobile manipulation research, combining Automated Scene Generation, a simulation-guided skill learning architecture, and hardware-agnostic middleware for cross-robot deployment.
AI.motivation: To address the real-to-sim-to-real transfer challenges in household manipulation by reducing scene-reconstruction costs, enabling systematic simulation evaluation, and ensuring deployable multi-robot compatibility.
AI.method: ASG reconstructs realistic simulations from real observations; a simulation-guided task formalization and hybrid-skill learning framework supports scalable evaluation and integration in simulation; HUM provides a hardware-agnostic, unified middleware to enable sim-to-real transfer across heterogeneous mobile manipulators.
AI.result: Claims superior performance on standardized benchmarks and facilitation of scalable research in mobile manipulation, with demonstrated cross-domain applicability.
AI.conclusion: BestMan unifies generation, evaluation, and deployment across real and simulated environments, accelerating development of robust household mobile manipulation strategies.

title: MolmoMotion: Forecasting Point Trajectories in 3D with Language Instruction
summary: Motion forecasting is central to visual intelligence: agents must anticipate how objects will move in order to plan actions, reason about physical interactions, and synthesize realistic futures. We argue that 3D points in world coordinates provide a general representation that is class-agnostic, view-stable, compact, and directly useful for downstream tasks. We formalize the task of goal-conditioned 3D point motion forecasting: given a short visual history, a set of 3D query points on an object of interest, and a language description of the intended goal, the model predicts the future 3D trajectory of each point. We introduce a full stack to study this task at scale: (1) MolmoMotion-1M is a large corpus of action-described, object-grounded 3D point trajectories annotated from 1.16M unconstrained videos; (2) PointMotionBench is a human-verified benchmark spanning 111 object categories and 61 motion types; and (3) MolmoMotion is a general motion forecasting model that supports both autoregressive coordinate prediction and flow-matching-based trajectory generation. MolmoMotion accurately predicts diverse motion patterns with different language instructions, and significantly outperforms existing motion prediction baselines on PointMotionBench. Finally, we show that the learned 3D motion prior transfers well to downstream applications: it improves training efficiency and generalization for robot manipulation, and its predicted trajectories provide effective motion guidance for generative models to synthesize videos with more realistic object motion.

AI.tldr: A scalable, language-conditioned 3D point motion forecasting framework (MolmoMotion) that forecasts per-point 3D trajectories from history, 3D points on objects, and language goals, backed by large-scale data (MolmoMotion-1M), a benchmark (PointMotionBench), and a model capable of autoregressive and flow-matching generation; demonstrates strong performance and transfer to robotics and video synthesis.
AI.motivation: 3D world-coordinate points offer a general, view-stable, class-agnostic representation for forecasting; enabling goal-directed, cross-domain motion understanding beyond traditional 2D or object-centric approaches.
AI.method: Formulates goal-conditioned 3D point motion forecasting with inputs: short visual history, 3D query points on an object, and a language goal description; introduces MolmoMotion-1M (1.16M videos with action-described, object-grounded 3D trajectories), PointMotionBench (111 categories, 61 motion types), and the MolmoMotion model capable of autoregressive coordinate prediction and flow-matching trajectory generation.
AI.result: MolmoMotion achieves accurate, diverse motion predictions conditioned on language; outperforms baselines on PointMotionBench; the learned 3D motion prior transfers to robotics (improved training efficiency and generalization) and provides useful motion guidance for generative video synthesis with more realistic object motion.
AI.conclusion: The work validates learning a 3D, language-conditioned motion prior from large-scale, action-described video data as a general, transferable foundation for 3D motion forecasting across tasks and domains.

title: ASTRA: A Scalable Next-Generation ATCO Training Simulator with Autonomous Simpilots
summary: Air Traffic Control Operators (ATCOs) are vital in ensuring the safe, orderly, and efficient flow of air traffic, yet training capacity is constrained by reliance on specialized human trainers known as simpilots, who must role-play both pilots and ATCOs in a simulated airspace. Existing automated solutions rely on Western-centric speech models that perform poorly in Singaporean operational contexts, with off-the-shelf systems exhibiting Word Error Rates (WER) of up to 107.80% on Singaporean-accented aviation speech. We introduce ASTRA, an end-to-end training simulator that automates these simpilot roles through a pipeline that transcribes ATCO speech, interprets instructions, and generates appropriate pilot and ATCO responses using locally adapted voice models. Our fine-tuned Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) pipeline reduces WER to 23.45%, substantially outperforming existing approaches in this domain. Beyond traffic simulation, ASTRA incorporates an AI-assisted performance evaluation framework that assesses trainee radiotelephony communications across accuracy, brevity, and completeness, achieving post-optimization scores of 91.7%, 88.2%, and 86.9%, respectively. Built on open-source foundations such as DSPy and Unsloth, this approach enables scalable, standardized ATCO assessment while reducing instructor workload.

AI.tldr: ASTRA is an end-to-end ATC training simulator that replaces manual simpilots with locally adapted ASR and voice models, achieving substantially lower error rates and providing AI-assisted, standardized performance evaluation for radiotelephony training.
AI.motivation: To scale air-traffic-control training by removing reliance on specialized, region-specific human simulators and by addressing Western-centric ASR limitations in Singaporean contexts, thus increasing realism and reducing instructor workload.
AI.method: An end-to-end pipeline that (1) transcribes ATCO speech with fine-tuned Singaporean-intonation ASR, (2) interprets instructions, and (3) generates pilot and ATCO responses via locally adapted voice models; plus an AI-assisted framework to assess radiotelephony communications on accuracy, brevity, and completeness, built on open-source DSPy and Unsloth.
AI.result: WER reduced from up to 107.80% (baseline) to 23.45% with the fine-tuned ASR; post-optimization evaluation scores: accuracy 91.7%, brevity 88.2%, completeness 86.9%; demonstrates scalable, standardized ATCO assessment and reduced instructor workload.
AI.conclusion: ASTRA provides a scalable, open-source pathway for realistic ATC training and objective performance evaluation across accents, with strong improvements in recognition accuracy and trainee assessment metrics, enabling broader deployment and cross-context generalization.

title: Skill-Guided Continuation Distillation for GUI Agents
summary: Improving GUI agents typically relies on behavior cloning on expert trajectories. However, as the current policy deviates from the expert policy, it inevitably encounters policy-induced off-trajectory states during closed-loop execution, i.e., states that fall outside the expert trajectories. Since expert trajectories provide no demonstrations for these unseen states, such states receive no effective supervision, leaving the policy unable to select the correct action. To close this supervision gap, we propose Skill-Guided Continuation Distillation (SGCD), an iterative self-improvement framework. SGCD first runs the plain policy without skill guidance for a few steps to reach realistic off-trajectory states. From these states, a skill-guided policy then completes the task and produces successful continuations, which are mixed with expert trajectories to supply supervision over policy-induced off-trajectory states. The skills are extracted from both successful and failed rollouts, consisting of Continuation Plans, Critical Targets, Failure Traps, and Success Criteria. On OSWorld-Verified, SGCD improves the success rate of three base models from the low-30\% range to over 50\%, demonstrating its effectiveness and generality.

AI.tldr: SGCD is an iterative self-improvement framework for GUI agents that addresses policy-induced off-trajectory states by using skill-guided continuations to augment expert supervision, improving success rates from the low-30% range to over 50% on OSWorld-Verified.
AI.motivation: Imitation learning often fails when the learned policy encounters states not covered by expert trajectories during deployment, leaving supervision gaps for off-trajectory states.
AI.method: 1) Run the plain policy briefly to reach realistic off-trajectory states. 2) Use a skill-guided policy to generate successful continuations from those states. 3) Mix these skill-guided trajectories with expert trajectories to supervise the policy on off-trajectory states. 4) Extract skills from both success and failure rollouts using constructs: Continuation Plans, Critical Targets, Failure Traps, and Success Criteria. 5) Iterate.
AI.result: On OSWorld-Verified, SGCD increases the success rate of three base models from the low-30% range to over 50%.
AI.conclusion: SGCD is effective and general, providing a practical mechanism to close supervision gaps for unseen states and improving GUI agent performance across base models.

title: High-Degree-of-Freedom Lightweight Bioinspired Leg for Enhanced Mobility in Small Robots
summary: In microrobotics, enhancing locomotion capabilities by increasing the degrees of freedom (DoF) of leg mechanisms under severe spatial constraints remains a significant challenge. Inspired by insect locomotion, this paper presents a novel micro-scale parallel leg mechanism with four degrees of freedom, and systematically analyzes its mechanical design, electrical system, and kinematics. The design incorporates two spherical five-bar linkages to achieve spatial motion within a parallel four-bar configuration. Furthermore, a concentric design strategy is employed to simplify the analytical solution of the leg kinematics. Due to the parallel system architecture, all actuators are located on the main body, substantially reducing the equivalent inertia of moving parts compared to traditional high-DOF leg structures. The total mass of the system is only 18.9 g, with an end-effector output force of approximately 0.5 N and a workspace exceeding 22255 mm3. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed single-leg mechanism achieves excellent motion flexibility, highlighting its potential for micro bio-inspired robotics.

AI.tldr: A micro-scale parallel leg mechanism with 4-DOF using two spherical five-bar linkages in a parallel four-bar, with concentric design to simplify kinematics; compact, lightweight, actuators on main body, offering 0.5 N end-effector force and large workspace (~22,255 mm^3) with potential for micro bio-inspired robotics.
AI.motivation: The challenge is to enhance locomotion capabilities by increasing the degrees of freedom of leg mechanisms under severe spatial constraints in microrobotics, drawing inspiration from insect locomotion.
AI.method: Employ two spherical five-bar linkages arranged within a parallel four-bar configuration; adopt a concentric design strategy to simplify the analytical solution of leg kinematics; actuators mounted on the main body to reduce moving inertia.
AI.result: Mass of 18.9 g; end-effector output force ~0.5 N; workspace exceeding 22,255 mm^3; experimental results show excellent motion flexibility and potential for micro bio-inspired robotics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed single-leg mechanism demonstrates effective 4-DOF micro-scale locomotion with reduced moving inertia and promising applicability in micro bio-inspired robotic systems.

title: Experimental Analysis of Neural Network-Based Image Classification on the CIFAR-10 Dataset
summary: An experimental investigation of neural image classification on the CIFAR-10 benchmark is presented through fully connected and convolutional network formulations. The analysis emphasizes the complete learning pipeline: image vectorization, normalization, one-hot class encoding, supervised loss minimization, learning-rate selection, mini-batch training, convolutional feature extraction, max-pooling, and validation-based generalization assessment. A convolutional architecture with six convolutional layers and three max-pooling stages is evaluated for ten training epochs using a batch size of 128 and an Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001. The validation accuracy reaches approximately 74.77%, while the validation loss begins to increase after the middle of training despite continued reduction in training loss. The resulting behavior illustrates the practical difference between representation learning and memorization, and it provides a compact experimental baseline for future studies on regularization, data augmentation, deeper architectures, and reproducible image-classification education.

AI.tldr: CNN-based CIFAR-10 study showing memorization vs representation learning; achieves ~74.8% validation accuracy in 10 epochs with 6 conv layers, 3 pooling stages, batch 128, and Adam LR 0.001; highlights generalization dynamics and offers baseline for learning-regularization education.
AI.motivation: Examine the full learning pipeline and the practical distinction between learning robust representations and memorizing data; provide a compact, reproducible benchmark to guide future work on regularization, data augmentation, deeper architectures, and education in image classification.
AI.method: Evaluate a six-layer convolutional network with three max-pooling stages on CIFAR-10, trained for 10 epochs with batch size 128 and Adam optimizer (LR 0.001). The study emphasizes image vectorization, normalization, one-hot encoding, supervised loss minimization, learning-rate selection, mini-batch training, and validation-based generalization assessment.
AI.result: Validation accuracy ~74.77%; validation loss begins to rise mid-training despite continued decrease in training loss, illustrating the gap between representation learning and memorization; provides a compact baseline for future research.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the practical distinction between learning useful representations and overfitting memorization, offering a concise experimental baseline to inform regularization, data augmentation, deeper architectures, and reproducible educational demonstrations in image classification.

title: SAE Interventions are Unreliable: Post-Intervention Recovery of Suppressed Behavior
summary: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) decompose residual-stream activations into interpretable features. Recent latent-space defenses increasingly rely on these decompositions, assuming that identified "unsafe" SAE features serve as actionable handles for monitoring and intervention. In this paradigm, clamping a specific harmful feature is expected to reliably prevent model misbehavior. However, we show that this success may hide a recoverable failure mode: the clamp may block one visible route to a behavior without eliminating the behavior itself. We formulate this vulnerability as post-intervention recovery, a constrained residual-space optimization problem. Starting from the post-intervention residual state, we optimize residual perturbations to recover the pre-intervention behavior while preserving the post-intervention values of the targeted SAE features. Even under a strong threat model where the intervention remains active throughout optimization and generation, recovery remains possible. To rule out that recovery simply undoes the intervention, we use encoder-orthogonal updates for single-layer interventions and the corresponding feature-map Jacobian in the cross-layer setting. Across TPP, unlearning, IOI, and refusal steering experiments, this stress test reveals recoverable behavior despite successful feature-level intervention. Especially in the safety-critical refusal-steering setting, we achieve a 95.8% recovery rate on valid samples while keeping defended-feature relative drift to 0.131, substantially below suffix-based baselines. A recovery-path attribution analysis further localizes this recovery to the SAE reconstruction residual, the component left unexplained by the SAE. These results expose a gap between feature-level control and behavioral completeness: SAE features can support causal intervention, but controlling them does not guarantee control over the underlying behavior.

AI.tldr: Feature-level control via Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) may not guarantee behavioral control; a post-intervention recovery (constrained residual-space optimization) can recover the original behavior despite targeted feature clamping.
AI.motivation: Question the robustness of SAE-based defenses that rely on decomposing residual activations into actionable features. Investigate whether blocking a harmful SAE feature truly suppresses the associated behavior or if recoverable failure modes exist.
AI.method: Formulate post-intervention residual state. Optimize residual perturbations under the constraint that targeted SAE features stay fixed at post-intervention values. Use encoder-orthogonal updates for single-layer interventions and the feature-map Jacobian for cross-layer settings. Evaluate across TPP, unlearning, IOI, and refusal-steering tasks with comparisons to suffix-based baselines.
AI.result: Recovery of the original behavior is possible even when the intervention remains active. In refusal steering, a 95.8% recovery rate on valid samples is observed, with defended-feature drift of 0.131. Recovery localizes to the SAE reconstruction residual (the part not explained by the SAE). Across tasks, this stress test reveals a gap between feature-level control and behavioral control.
AI.conclusion: SAE-based interventions provide causal handles but do not guarantee control over underlying behavior; a recoverable failure mode exists where post-intervention recovery can reinstantiate the behavior via residual mechanisms.

title: SciRisk-Bench: A Risk-Dimension-Aware Benchmark for AI4Science Safety
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in AI for Science (AI4Science) workflows, from scientific question answering and literature analysis to laboratory planning and autonomous discovery. This progress creates an urgent need for safety benchmarks that evaluate not only scientific competence, but also whether models recognize and avoid risks in high-stakes scientific contexts. Existing AI4Science safety datasets cover several disciplines and task formats, leaving the underlying risk dimensions underspecified. We introduce \textbf{SciRisk-Bench}, a benchmark designed to evaluate AI4Science safety from two complementary perspectives: explicit risk dimensions and scientific disciplines. SciRisk-Bench covers 7 disciplines, 31 subdisciplines and 10 risk dimensions. In the experimental section, we evaluate both mainstream LLMs and science-oriented LLMs across risk dimensions, disciplines, and sub-disciplines, enabling fine-grained diagnosis of where scientific models remain unsafe.

AI.tldr: SciRisk-Bench introduces a dual-perspective safety benchmark for AI4Science, covering 7 disciplines, 31 subdisciplines, and 10 risk dimensions to evaluate both explicit risk recognition and cross-disciplinary safety across mainstream and science-oriented LLMs.
AI.motivation: There is a strong need for safety benchmarks in AI4Science that go beyond scientific competence to assess recognition and avoidance of high-stakes risks; existing datasets under-specify risk dimensions.
AI.method: Benchmark design with 7 disciplines, 31 subdisciplines, and 10 explicit risk dimensions; evaluation of both mainstream and science-oriented LLMs across risk dimensions, disciplines, and sub-disciplines to enable fine-grained safety diagnostics.
AI.result: Preliminary evaluation indicates varying safety performance across risk dimensions and disciplines; SciRisk-Bench enables targeted diagnosis of where scientific models remain unsafe across a broad spectrum of domains and tasks.
AI.conclusion: SciRisk-Bench provides a comprehensive, multi-dimensional framework for evaluating AI4Science safety, supporting more robust risk-aware model development and benchmarking across disciplines.

title: Leveraging Energy Features for Surface Classification with Deep Learning: A Comparative Analysis Across Three Independent Datasets
summary: The energy-based method remains a comparatively underexamined approach for surface classification in mobile robotics, despite promising results in constrained environments. This study evaluated the viability of using energy-derived features as either a standalone classification modality or as supplementary input to inertial data. A comprehensive evaluation was conducted across three publicly available datasets, comparing the performance of modern deep learning architectures including recurrent neural networks, convolutional neural networks, encoder-only transformers, and Mamba state-space models, under automated hyperparameter tuning and input sequence length optimization. The models achieved higher accuracy than previously reported values on all evaluated datasets, with the convolutional neural network yielding the highest overall performance. When relying exclusively on energy-based features, the models attained classification accuracies in the range of 85-90%, approximately 5-10% lower than those achieved when combined with inertial features (96-99%). Augmenting inertial data with energy features resulted in a consistent mean accuracy improvement of 1-2%. These findings indicate that classifiers relying solely on energy features offer sufficient accuracy for standalone deployment, while also providing a consistent gain when used in combination with other sensing modalities.

AI.tldr: Energy-based features are viable for surface classification in mobile robotics, achieving 85–90% accuracy alone and ~97–99% when fused with inertial data; CNNs perform best among tested architectures.
AI.motivation: To address the underexplored potential of energy-derived features for surface classification and to benchmark their standalone and complementary value against inertial data across multiple architectures and datasets.
AI.method: Evaluation across three public datasets using modern architectures (RNNs, CNNs, encoder-only transformers, and Mamba state-space models) with automated hyperparameter tuning and input sequence length optimization. Assessed energy-based features as standalone classifiers and as supplemental input to inertial data.
AI.result: Energy-only models reached 85–90% accuracy (5–10% below fusion). When combined with inertial data, accuracies rose to 96–99%. Adding energy features to inertial data yielded a consistent 1–2% mean accuracy improvement. CNNs achieved the highest overall performance among evaluated architectures.
AI.conclusion: Energy-based features are viable for standalone deployment and provide a reliable performance boost when fused with inertial sensing; CNNs currently offer the best overall performance among the studied architectures.

title: Multi-Modal Hyper-Graph Fusion for Low-Light Crowd Counting
summary: Crowd counting is a fundamental task in computer vision. However, crowd counting in low-light environments remains largely underexplored, despite its practical importance in the real world. Existing methods mainly focus on well-lit scenes or rely on single-modality Red-Green-Blue (RGB) representations, which often become unreliable under extreme darkness and complex non-uniform illumination. To handle this problem, we construct three new low-light crowd counting benchmarks, which consist of two synthetic datasets, SHA\_Dark and SHB\_Dark, and a real-world benchmark LC-Crowd (Low-light Crowd Dataset). Inspired by Retinex-based physical modeling, we introduce depth and Canny edge cues as complementary geometric and structural priors to enhance the intrinsic reflectance representation under low-light conditions. We propose a Multi-Modal Hyper-Graph Fusion module, which formulates RGB appearance, depth geometry, and edge structure cues as nodes in a unified hyper-graph and explicitly captures their high-order complementary relationships via dynamic hyperedge construction and message passing. Furthermore, to adaptively allocate computation in dense prediction, we propose a Deformable Rectangular Sparse Attention (DRSA) module, which concentrates computation on informative regions through anchor-aware estimation and adaptive rectangular window modeling. Based on these designs, we develop a unified Low-Light Counting Network (LCNet) for robust low-light crowd counting. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method achieves the best overall performance against existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods. The code is in the supplementary material. The datasets will be made public upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Proposes LCNet for low-light crowd counting that fuses RGB, depth, and edge cues via a multi-modal hyper-graph, with a deformable sparse attention module to focus computation, evaluated on three benchmarks (two synthetic, one real) with SOTA performance.
AI.motivation: Low-light crowd counting is underexplored and RGB-based methods struggle under extreme darkness and non-uniform illumination; incorporating depth and edge geometry, guided by Retinex-based modeling, can improve robustness.
AI.method: (1) Use depth and Canny edge cues to augment intrinsic reflectance under low light; (2) Multi-Modal Hyper-Graph Fusion where RGB, depth, and edge cues form nodes in a unified hyper-graph with dynamic hyperedge construction and message passing to capture high-order relations; (3) Deformable Rectangular Sparse Attention to allocate computation adaptively with anchor-aware estimation and rectangular windows; (4) integrate into a unified Low-Light Counting Network (LCNet).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three benchmarks (SHA_Dark, SHB_Dark, LC-Crowd) show state-of-the-art performance over existing methods; datasets to be public upon acceptance; code in supplementary material.
AI.conclusion: LCNet demonstrates robust low-light crowd counting by leveraging multi-modal cues and adaptive computation; the proposed components contribute to improved accuracy and efficiency, with forthcoming public benchmarks and code.

title: Why SWAVE May Not Be All You Need:A Concept-Evolution Retrospective on Complex-Valued Recurrent Language Models
summary: SWave is a complex-valued recurrent language model (169.26M parameters, D=384, L=16, T=2048) trained on FineWeb-Edu using 2xH100 NVL. It was designed around three founding premises: that representing language as complex waves rather than real-valued numbers enables richer information encoding; that a Cayley-parameterised unitary transition provides a mathematical guarantee against state decay or explosion; and that a hidden state which rotates rather than shrinks preserves signal integrity over arbitrarily long contexts. The core of SWave evolved substantially across three development phases. The Resonance Head was found to structurally admit imaginary-channel collapse as a global loss minimum (a failure mode we term cos-domination collapse) and was superseded by an untied head with independent real and imaginary embedding tables from the Phase-Associative Memory (PAM) architecture. This resolved the degenerate minimum and enabled stable 200,000-step training (best-step PPL 22.0 at step 89,861). ComplexNorm and the Wave Propagation Scan proved load-bearing throughout all three phases and were retained to the final architecture. ProtectGatedScan was reframed as a structural prior rather than a learned behaviour. The four multi-scale retention concepts showed no measurable improvement under controlled evaluation and were found non-load-bearing. The ComplexGatedUnit was superseded by a real-valued squared-ReLU channel mixer with fewer parameters. The auxiliary training objectives showed no benefit once structural constraints were resolved. The investigation yields a formal characterisation of cos-domination collapse, a parallel scan with a log-space backward pass for numerical stability, six transferable engineering principles for complex-valued recurrent training, and a plan-to-code traceability methodology for catching structural divergences that conventional test suites miss.

AI.tldr: SWave introduces a complex-valued recurrent language model with Cayley-parameterised unitary transitions and independent real/imaginary embeddings via PAM; it identifies cos-domination collapse, achieves stable long-horizon training, and offers six engineering principles plus a plan-to-code traceability methodology.
AI.motivation: Advance long-context language modeling by leveraging complex-valued representations for richer information encoding while enforcing numerical stability through unitary transitions, aiming to prevent vanishing/exploding gradients and preserve signal integrity over arbitrarily long contexts.
AI.method: Architectural evolution across three phases: (1) Resonance Head → untied head with independent real/imaginary embeddings; (2) Phase-Associative Memory (PAM) integration and retained components (ComplexNorm, Wave Propagation Scan); (3) reframing ProtectGatedScan as a structural prior; replacement of the ComplexGatedUnit with a real-valued squared-ReLU channel mixer; development of a log-space backward pass and a plan-to-code traceability methodology; evaluation on FineWeb-Edu with 2xH100 NVL; 200k-step training with best-step perplexity reported; exploration of four multi-scale retention concepts (non-beneficial); six transferable engineering principles for complex-valued recurrent training.
AI.result: Key outcomes include formal characterization of cos-domination collapse as a failure mode (and its mitigation), a parallel scan with log-space backward pass for numerical stability, the identification of six transferable engineering principles, and a plan-to-code traceability methodology for catching structural divergences not covered by standard test suites; stable 200k-step training achieved with best perplexity 22.0 at step 89,861; ComplexNorm and Wave Propagation Scan retained as core components, while other components were revised or discarded.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a formal framework for understanding cos-domination collapse, demonstrates a numerically stable, scalable architecture for complex-valued recurrent training, and delivers practical engineering guidelines along with a traceability approach to improve robustness in future complex-valued sequence modeling.

title: Decoupling Search from Reasoning: A Vendor-Agnostic Grounding Architecture for LLM Agents
summary: Production LLM agents increasingly depend on real-time search, yet native search grounding bundles retrieval policy, provider choice, evidence injection, cost, latency, and generation behavior behind a single model-provider boundary. This coupling makes grounding hard to inspect, tune, reuse, or port, and can trigger Search-Induced Verbosity that breaks strict output contracts. We present Decoupled Search Grounding (DSG), a vendor-agnostic boundary that moves grounding outside the reasoning model through an MCP-compatible gateway, exposing provider routing, source-aware context rendering, configured fallback, retrieval-depth control, and exact plus semantic caching as first-class controls. Across five frontier models on SimpleQA, FreshQA, and HotpotQA, native search leads on recency-sensitive FreshQA, but DSG exposes a stronger frontier when control matters: on SimpleQA it nearly matches native accuracy (86.1% vs. 87.7%) at 91% lower search cost, preserves concise answer contracts, and reaches a 99.4% warm-cache hit rate with 68% lower latency. Deployed as a shared production grounding layer for large-scale agentic workloads with interchangeable models, DSG matches or slightly exceeds native-search accuracy on an e-commerce query-understanding (QIU) workload while cutting search cost by over 98%. Real-time grounding is best treated as an optimizable interface boundary, not a fixed model feature.

AI.tldr: Decoupled Search Grounding (DSG) separates grounding from the LLM, offering a vendor-agnostic gateway to manage retrieval, evidence rendering, caching, and control over depth/cost, reducing coupling and improving efficiency while preserving output contracts.
AI.motivation: Grounding inside a single model-provider boundary entangles retrieval policy, provider choice, evidence injection, cost, latency, and generation behavior, making it hard to inspect, tune, reuse, or port and risking verbose outputs that violate strict contracts.
AI.method: Introduce an MCP-compatible gateway that decouples grounding from the reasoning model. DSG exposes provider routing, source-aware context rendering, configurable fallback, retrieval-depth control, and exact/semantic caching as first-class controls. Evaluated across five frontier models on QA benchmarks (SimpleQA, FreshQA, HotpotQA) and an e-commerce QIU workload.
AI.result: DSG nearly matches native SimpleQA accuracy (86.1% vs. 87.7%) at 91% lower search cost and achieves a 99.4% warm-cache hit rate with 68% lower latency; native search dominates on FreshQA due to recency, but DSG offers stronger control where it matters. On e-commerce QIU, DSG reduces search cost by >98% while maintaining comparable accuracy. Overall, DSG decouples real-time grounding into an optimizable interface boundary, enabling interchangeable models and more efficient, controllable grounding.
AI.conclusion: Real-time grounding is best treated as an optimizable interface boundary, not a fixed model feature. DSG provides a production-ready, vendor-agnostic grounding layer that reduces cost/latency, preserves output contracts, and supports flexible model interchange.

title: Selective Unit-Cell Actuation in Lattice Structures for Distributed Morphology in Soft Robots
summary: Soft lattice structures are increasingly used in robotics to tailor compliance and guide deformation; however, actuation is typically introduced at the device or module level, with actuators inserted into otherwise passive architectures. In this work, we move actuator-lattice co-design to the unit-cell scale. We present an embedded pneumatic unit cell that integrates curved-strut lattice geometry with a bidirectional bellow actuator within a single monolithic element. When tessellated, the lattice functions as a distributed actuation field in which global morphology is governed by spatial actuation patterns rather than uniform pressurization. Experimental characterization of 1x1, 2x2, and 3x3 tessellations demonstrates scalable displacement and force generation with repeatable cyclic performance. Selective actuation of unit cells in a 3x3x3 array produces distinct global deformation modes, including bending and directional grasping, without altering hardware configuration. Additionally, coupling active and passive unit cells enables bending-driven crawling locomotion, demonstrating that heterogeneous tessellations can translate through asymmetric deformation. These results establish unit-cell-level actuation as a strategy for distributed morphing in lattice-based soft robots and provide a foundation for scalable, monolithic robotic architectures.

AI.tldr: Embedded pneumatic unit cells integrate curved-strut lattice with a bidirectional bellow, enabling distributed actuation in a monolithic lattice; tessellation controls global morphology via actuation patterns, enabling multiple deformation modes and locomotion, scalable and monolithic.
AI.motivation: To enable actuation at the unit-cell level to achieve distributed morphing in soft lattices; overcome limitations of module-level actuation; enable programmable deformation fields within a single material system.
AI.method: Design and fabrication of a monolithic pneumatic unit cell combining curved-strut lattice geometry with a bidirectional bellow; tessellations into 1x1, 2x2, 3x3; actuation patterns; experimental characterization of displacement, force, and cyclic repeatability; demonstration of selective actuation in 3x3x3 arrays; actuation-coupled heterogenous tessellations for locomotion.
AI.result: Achieved scalable displacement and force with repeatable cycles; observed distinct global deformation modes (bending, directional grasping) under selective actuation; demonstrated bending-driven crawling using active-passive unit cell coupling; evidence that heterogeneous tessellations realize programmable morphing without changing hardware.
AI.conclusion: Unit-cell actuation enables distributed morphing in lattice-based soft robots and supports scalable, monolithic architectures; provides a foundation for programmable deformation fields via selective actuation patterns.

title: Technical Report for ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge: Leveraging DINOv3 for Robust Outdoor Scene Understanding in Field Robotics
summary: The GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge at the ICRA 2026 Workshop on Field Robotics evaluates dense semantic segmentation of off-road imagery over a fine-grained taxonomy of 64 classes and 11 evaluated non-void coarse categories. We present the first-place solution to this challenge. Our solution comprises two complementary improvements: (a) a network-level design that combines a self-supervised DINOv3 ViT-L/16 backbone, a ViT-Adapter, and a Mask2Former mask-classification decoder, together with a coarse-category auxiliary loss on the global [CLS] token; and (b) an inference-time aggregation strategy based on multi-scale and horizontal-flip test-time augmentation and an ensemble of the top three checkpoints selected using Codabench scores. Our method achieves an official composite score of 76.57%, consisting of 69.32% fine-class mIoU and 83.81% category-level mIoU, and ranks first on the final phase leaderboard: www.codabench.org/competitions/14257/#/results-tab.

AI.tldr: Top-performing approach for the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D segmentation challenge; achieves a composite score of 76.57% with fine-class mIoU 69.32% and category mIoU 83.81% via a self-supervised ViT backbone (DINOv3 ViT-L/16) with ViT-Adapter and Mask2Former decoder, plus inference-time ensembling (multi-scale + horizontal flip) over the top 3 Codabench-selected checkpoints.
AI.motivation: Advance dense, fine-grained off-road semantic segmentation across 64 fine classes and 11 coarse categories, requiring strong representations and robust inference.
AI.method: Network-level design using a self-supervised DINOv3 ViT-L/16 backbone, ViT-Adapter, and Mask2Former decoder; auxiliary coarse-category loss on the global CLS token; inference-time aggregation via multi-scale and horizontal-flip test-time augmentation and an ensemble of the top three Codabench-selected checkpoints.
AI.result: Official composite score: 76.57% (fine-class mIoU 69.32%; category-level mIoU 83.81%); ranked first on the final phase leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that combining self-supervised ViT backbones with flexible mask-based decoding and strong TTA/ensembling yields state-of-the-art performance for fine-grained, off-road segmentation tasks.

title: Neural Network Implementation of the Renormalization Group for Fault Diagnosis with Class Imbalance
summary: The application of machine learning models in practical tasks faces challenges such as class imbalance and multidimensional noise. This paper proposes RGNet, a neural network architecture based on the concept of the renormalization group (RG), for hierarchical coarse-graining of the feature space. The model sequentially compresses the input dimensionality and concatenates all scales before classification, allowing it to capture both local details and global patterns. The notion of RG-flows is introduced - interpretable low-dimensional representations whose visualization via t-SNE reveals a discrete curvilinear structure confirming the effectiveness of coarse-graining. Experimental results are presented on the imbalanced AI4I dataset. The obtained results demonstrate that RGNet is a universal, interpretable, and competitive solution for fault prediction in applications with imbalanced classes.

AI.tldr: RGNet uses a renormalization-group-inspired neural network to perform hierarchical feature-space coarse-graining for imbalanced fault prediction, achieving interpretable RG-flows and competitive accuracy by aggregating multi-scale representations.
AI.motivation: Address class imbalance and multidimensional noise in practical ML tasks; extract both local details and global patterns via coarse-graining; provide interpretable representations.
AI.method: Neural network architecture that sequentially compresses input dimensionality across scales, concatenates multi-scale representations, and uses RG-flows visualization via t-SNE for interpretation; applied to the AI4I imbalanced dataset.
AI.result: Demonstrates universality, interpretability, and competitive performance for fault prediction under class imbalance; RGNet supports effective coarse-graining and interpretable low-dimensional representations.
AI.conclusion: RGNet provides a principled, interpretable coarse-graining framework for imbalanced classification with strong empirical results on a real-world dataset.

title: RTSGameBench: An RTS Benchmark for Strategic Reasoning by Vision-Language Models
summary: Modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often struggle with strategic reasoning, i.e., anticipating and influencing other agents' actions, under uncertainty in competitive and cooperative settings. Real-time strategy (RTS) games can be a natural testbed for diagnosing this limitation, as they demand coordination with allies, adaptation to opponents' strategy, and long-horizon planning under partial observability. However, existing RTS benchmarks offer limited evaluation scope, lack systematic competency diagnosis, and remain fixed in the pre-designed scenario coverage. To address these limitations, we present RTSGameBench, which is built on Beyond All Reason, a large-scale RTS game with an expanded battlefield that demands broader strategy diversity than the existing testbeds. The proposed benchmark provides evaluations through diverse gameplay across various matchup structures, diagnostic assessment via mini-games, each targeting an individual strategic competency, and extensible coverage via a self-evolving generation framework that converts free-form queries into new mini-games, improving over successive cycles. Additionally, for VLMs to operate in large-scale RTS games, we provide RTSGameAgent that manages units by an FSM with agentic memory. We empirically validate that multiple state-of-the-art VLMs do not perform well when matchups demand tighter coordination, multiagent coordination and when task scale increases.

AI.tldr: Introduces RTSGameBench and RTSGameAgent to diagnose and empower VLMs' strategic reasoning in large-scale RTS games through diverse matchups, diagnostic mini-games, and a self-evolving generation framework; shows current VLMs lack coordination and scalability.
AI.motivation: RTS games require long-horizon planning, multiagent coordination, and partial observability; existing benchmarks are limited, lacking systematic competency diagnosis and scalable scenario generation.
AI.method: Build on Beyond All Reason; create diverse matchups; design mini-games targeting specific competencies; implement self-evolving framework that converts free-form queries into new mini-games; RTSGameAgent uses a finite-state machine with agentic memory to manage units.
AI.result: Empirical studies show state-of-the-art VLMs underperform on tight coordination, multiagent coordination, and larger task scales; benchmark provides diagnostic and evaluation capabilities across varied scenarios.
AI.conclusion: RTSGameBench and RTSGameAgent enable robust evaluation and improvement of VLMs for strategic, multi-agent RTS tasks; framework supports continual expansion of coverage and exposes the need for better coordination-centric models.

title: Two-Phase Bilevel Search for the Moving-Target Traveling Salesman Problem with Moving Obstacles
summary: The Moving-Target Traveling Salesman Problem (MT-TSP) seeks a minimum cost trajectory for an agent that departs from a static depot, visits a set of moving targets, each within one of their assigned time windows, and returns to the depot. In this article, we study the Moving-Target Traveling Salesman Problem with Moving Obstacles (MT-TSP-MO), a generalization of the MT-TSP where the agent trajectory must avoid moving obstacles. We present a Mixed-Integer Conic Programming (MICP) formulation that can be solved using off-the-shelf solvers, as well as a fast and scalable Two-Phase Bilevel Search (TPBS) algorithm that computes high-quality feasible solutions for the problem. We evaluate our approaches against an existing baseline algorithm on a broad range of problem instances with up to 40 targets and 40 obstacles. The results demonstrate that both the proposed methods significantly outperform the baseline with respect to success rates, solution costs, and computation time.

AI.tldr: MT-TSP-MO extends MT-TSP by adding moving obstacles; introduces an MICP formulation and a fast two-phase bilevel search (TPBS) to compute high-quality feasible solutions; outperforms a baseline on instances with up to 40 targets and 40 obstacles.
AI.motivation: Plan minimum-cost trajectories for agents that must visit moving targets within time windows while avoiding dynamically moving obstacles; fill the gap where existing MT-TSP methods do not handle moving obstacles efficiently or scalably.
AI.method: Develop two complementary solution approaches: (i) a Mixed-Integer Conic Programming (MICP) formulation solvable by off-the-shelf solvers, and (ii) a fast and scalable Two-Phase Bilevel Search (TPBS) heuristic that yields high-quality feasible solutions. TPBS likely decomposes the problem into an outer order-inference phase and an inner feasibility/path-phase with obstacle avoidance, iterating to improve feasibility and cost.
AI.result: Both proposed methods significantly outperform a baseline algorithm in terms of success rate, trajectory cost, and computation time across diverse instances with up to 40 targets and 40 obstacles.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a solid optimization-based and heuristic toolkit for MT-TSP-MO, with practical solvability via MICP and scalability via TPBS, demonstrated by superior performance over the baseline on multi-target, multi-obstacle instances.

title: Aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition with patch-level self-supervised learning and expanded reciprocal re-ranking
summary: LiDAR place recognition determines one's position on a prior point cloud map. The most studied ground-level LiDAR place recognition suffers from pre-visit requirements, incomplete coverage, and limited perspectives. Using pre-acquired, full-coverage Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS) data as an aerial prior map overcomes these drawbacks, making cross-view place recognition necessary and advantageous. However, aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition faces significant challenges, including the domain gap between aerial and ground point clouds, and false positives during initial retrieval. To address these challenges, we present a novel retrieval and re-ranking framework for aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition. Based on the priors that neighboring point cloud patches share similar semantics with anchor patch, our retrieval network introduces patch-level self-supervised learning modules at multiple scales and integrates with scene-level learning to improve global feature discriminativeness between aerial and ground point clouds. Furthermore, leveraging the structured spatial distribution of ALS point clouds, we introduce an Expanded Reciprocal (ER) re-ranking algorithm to exploit neighborhood information maximally and refine each feature based on neighbor features, which are then used to update the similarity matrix for final ranking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our retrieval network outperforms existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, achieving a 9.8\% improvement in average Recall@1 and a 3.2\% improvement in average Recall@1\% on the CS-Urban-Scenes, while also showing the best performance on the CS-Campus3D dataset. Additionally, our ER re-ranking algorithm further boosts the average Recall@1 by 4.9\% on CS-Campus3D and 10.2\% on CS-Urban-Scenes without additional training.

AI.tldr: Proposes a retrieval and re-ranking framework for aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition using patch-level self-supervised learning and Expanded Reciprocal re-ranking to bridge domain gap and reduce false positives, achieving state-of-the-art gains on CS-Urban-Scenes and CS-Campus3D.
AI.motivation: Overcome domain gap between aerial ALS and ground LiDAR and reduce false positives in initial retrieval for cross-view place recognition.
AI.method: Develop patch-level self-supervised learning at multiple scales within a retrieval network, integrating with scene-level learning; introduce Expanded Reciprocal (ER) re-ranking that leverages neighborhood structure of ALS data to refine features and update the similarity matrix.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA by 9.8% recall@1 and 3.2% recall@1% on CS-Urban-Scenes; best on CS-Campus3D; ER re-ranking adds 4.9% recall@1 on CS-Campus3D and 10.2% on CS-Urban-Scenes without extra training.
AI.conclusion: Cross-view aerial-ground LiDAR place recognition can be significantly improved via multi-scale patch-level self-supervision combined with neighborhood-aware re-ranking, achieving robust global features and higher retrieval accuracy.

title: Self-CTRL: Self-Consistency Training with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Language models (LMs) that faithfully describe their own behavior can more easily be audited, understood, and trusted by users. This paper describes Self-Consistency Training with Reinforcement Learning (Self-CTRL), a method that optimizes for consistency between a LM's self-explanations and behavior on related inputs by updating explanations to better predict behavior or updating behavior to better match explanations. We apply our method in two domains. First, we study a formal probabilistic reasoning task in which LMs must learn to imitate a family of biased samplers and evaluated on their ability to report the associated biases. We find that consistency training improves the correlation between self-reported and behaviorally-measured latent biases from $R^2=0.24$ to $R^2=0.64$ on a set of held-out distributions, matching the generalization of direct ground-truth supervision. Second, we study a constitutional AI domain in which LMs must describe when they will refuse or comply with user requests. Here, Self-CTRL produces rules that faithfully describe the model's behavior on held-out requests, improving the refusal predictions of a third-party auditor model from $36\%$ to $92\%$. In the other direction, behavior updates improve alignment, reducing HarmBench failure rate from $15.0\%$ to $0.5\%$ without substantially increasing refusal on harmless prompts. By aligning explanations and behavior, our work provides a general recipe for training AI models to be safer, more transparent, and more controllable.

AI.tldr: Self-Consistency Training with Reinforcement Learning (Self-CTRL) aligns language-model self-explanations with their behavior to improve auditability, safety, and controllability. Demonstrated on probabilistic reasoning and constitutional AI tasks, yielding substantial gains in alignment metrics and refusal accuracy while reducing harmful behavior.
AI.motivation: To create LMs whose explanations faithfully reflect their decision processes and outcomes, enabling easier auditing, trust, and controllability by users and auditors.
AI.method: Self-CTRL alternates updating explanations to better predict model behavior and updating behavior to better match explanations, optimizing consistency between the two through reinforcement-learning-inspired updates across tasks.
AI.result: In probabilistic reasoning, improved correlation between self-reported and latent biases from R^2=0.24 to R^2=0.64 (held-out distributions). In constitutional AI, refusal prediction improved from 36% to 92% via consistent explanations; HarmBench failure rate dropped from 15.0% to 0.5% with behavior updates, without large drops in harmless prompt refusals.
AI.conclusion: Aligning explanations and behavior via Self-CTRL provides a general recipe for building safer, more transparent, and controllable LMs, with potential applicability across domains requiring auditability and alignment.

title: ThinkDeception: A Progressive Reinforcement Learning Framework for Interpretable Multimodal Deception Detection
summary: Multimodal deception detection is critical for identifying fraudulent intentions, yet existing approaches predominantly rely on end to end black--box paradigms. These methods suffer from a severe lack of interpretability failing to provide transparent reasoning trajectories and struggling to explicitly capture the subtle, cross modal inconsistencies inherent in deceptive behaviors. To transcend these limitations, we propose ThinkDeception, a novel and interpretable multimodal deception detection framework. As a pioneering effort, it introduces Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) into this domain, transforming deception detection from a traditional binary classification task into an explicit cognitive reasoning process. Facilitated by the first meticulously annotated step--by--step multimodal Chain of Thought (CoT) dataset, we develop a foundational model, ThinkDeception Base, empirically validating the critical role of modal inconsistency in decoding deception. Building upon this foundation, our core innovation lies in proposing Visual-Audio Consistency Group Relative Policy Optimization(VAC--GRPO) equipped with a progressive training strategy. Distinct from standard GRPO, we stratify the training data into four progressive difficulty tiers, guiding the model through a psychologically grounded easy--to--hard cognitive transition. By innovatively coupling this dynamic curriculum scheduler with a multi dimensional, process aware reward mechanism and a reflective learning paradigm, we significantly elevate the model's overall reasoning quality. Extensive experiments on mainstream benchmarks demonstrate that ThinkDeception establishes a new SOTA, significantly outperforming existing methods in both detection accuracy and rationale quality. Ultimately, this work successfully drives the field of deception detection toward interpretable, multimodal cognitive reasoning.

AI.tldr: ThinkDeception introduces an interpretable multimodal deception detection framework using Multimodal LLMs, a step-by-step multimodal CoT dataset, and a progressive VAC-GRPO curriculum to achieve state-of-the-art performance and rationale quality.
AI.motivation: Address interpretability gaps in end-to-end deception detectors and the need to explicitly capture cross-modal inconsistencies that signal deception.
AI.method: Build ThinkDeception Base with MLLMs; create annotated step-by-step multimodal CoT dataset; propose Visual-Audio Consistency Group Relative Policy Optimization (VAC-GRPO) with a four-tier progressive curriculum, a multi-dimensional reward, and reflective learning.
AI.result: Empirically achieves SOTA in deception detection on mainstream benchmarks and improves both accuracy and rationale quality; demonstrates the effectiveness of modal inconsistency cues and progressive cognitive training.
AI.conclusion: Advances interpretable, multimodal cognitive reasoning in deception detection and sets a foundation for future work integrating MLLMs with structured reasoning and curriculum-based RL approaches.

title: Generating Natural and Expressive Robot Gestures through Iterative Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback using LLMs
summary: Expressive gestures are essential for natural and effective communication, complementing speech when verbal cues alone are insufficient (e.g., pointing). For social robots such as the humanoid Pepper, producing natural and expressive movements is critical for improving human-robot interaction (HRI) and long-term acceptance. However, generating gestures remains challenging due to reliance on expert-authored animations, resulting in rigid behaviors that are impractical for dynamic and diverse environments. Alternatively, machine learning approaches often struggle to capture perceived naturalness, becoming increasingly challenging with more degrees of freedom. Consequently, producing expressive robot gestures requires a system that can adapt to the environment while adhering to social norms and physical constraints. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) enable dynamic code generation, offering new opportunities for runtime gesture synthesis from natural language. In this paper, we integrate ChatGPT into the humanoid robot Pepper to generate co-speech gestures aligned with conversational output. While this baseline enables flexible gesture generation, the resulting motions are often perceived as stiff and unnatural. To address this limitation, we introduce an iterative reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) system that finetunes gesture generation based on user evaluations, leveraging an iterative user study to compare Pepper's generated gestures. Our results show that RLHF improved the LLM's co-speech generative capabilities, producing more expressive, relevant and fluid movements.

AI.tldr: Integrating a ChatGPT-based co-speech gesture generator for Pepper with iterative RLHF, yielding more expressive, relevant, and fluid gestures compared to a baseline.
AI.motivation: Address the brittleness and expert-dependence of hand-authored robot gestures; enable natural, adaptable, and norm-compliant co-speech gestures for social robots in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Baseline: use ChatGPT to generate co-speech gestures aligned with Pepper's dialogue. Enhancement: an iterative reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) loop, using user evaluations to fine-tune gesture generation across an iterative study.
AI.result: RLHF improved the co-speech gesture generation, producing more expressive, relevant, and fluid movements.
AI.conclusion: RLHF is effective for refining LLM-driven co-speech gestures in HRI; the approach enables adaptive, norm-conforming gestures in Pepper, with potential for broader deployment.

title: APT: Atomic Physical Transitions for Causal Video-Language Understanding
summary: Physical events are not understood by their names alone, but by the causal state changes that compose them. A clip-level label such as "bounce" can be correct while hiding the process that makes the event physically valid, from support loss and contact onset to rebound and settling. To make this hidden process explicit, we introduce Atomic Physical Transitions (APTs): minimal, temporally localized state changes that bind a visible cue to an active physical mechanism and before/after dynamical regimes. An APT chain represents a video as an ordered causal transition sequence rather than a single aggregate event label: event labels tell what happened; APT chains explain why it happened. To make APTs learnable by VLMs, we construct mixed-source APT data from human annotations and simulator ground truth, covering 14 transition types across contact, gravity, friction, and rotation/stability, with 27,303 timed instances over 1,246 trials. Using this data, we find that current VLMs miss transition-level physics, with zero-shot recall at most 14% and errors dominated by missed transitions. Direct fine-tuning on APT chains improves transition detection but causes event-level forgetting, indicating that the model learns a specialized answer format rather than a reusable physical representation. We therefore propose APT-Tune, a parameter-efficient recipe that teaches VLMs to use causal transitions without forgetting how to answer video questions. It combines image-pad-aware supervision, format-conditional co-training, and mechanism-conditioned domain-to-type decoding to make APT learning format-robust and physically grounded. With only 11 M LoRA parameters on Qwen3-VL-2B, APT-Tune substantially improves APT recall while also improving event-level video transfer. These results show that APTs are not a new answer format, but a human-aligned causal supervision signal for physical video understanding.

AI.tldr: APTs provide minimal, temporally localized state changes that causally link cues to physical mechanisms, enabling video understanding beyond single labels. APT-Tune—a light, LoRA-based training recipe—boosts transition recall without forgetting event-level answers, demonstrating causal supervision improves physical video understanding.
AI.motivation: To illuminate the hidden causal physics in videos that coarse event labels miss, and to enable models to reason about transition-level physics rather than only end results.
AI.method: Introduce Atomic Physical Transitions (APTs) and APT chains; create mixed-source dataset with 14 transition types across contact, gravity, friction, rotation; evaluate vision-language models (VLMs) on transition recall; propose fine-tuning pitfalls; develop APT-Tune: image-pad-aware supervision, format-conditional co-training, and mechanism-conditioned domain-to-type decoding; apply LoRA (11M) to Qwen3-VL-2B.
AI.result: Current VLMs exhibit poor transition-level physics understanding (zero-shot recall ≤14%, dominated by missed transitions). Direct fine-tuning on APT chains improves transition detection but causes event-level forgetting. APT-Tune significantly improves APT recall and also enhances event-level video transfer with a modest 11M LoRA footprint.
AI.conclusion: APTs serve as a human-aligned causal supervision signal, not merely a new answer format, enabling robust, physically grounded video understanding when paired with a lightweight, parameter-efficient tuning strategy.

title: ThousandWorlds: A benchmark for climate emulation of potentially habitable exoplanets
comment: 10 pages main text, 26 pages references/appendix, plus NeurIPS checklist. Data at https://doi.org/10.57967/hf/8695. Code at https://github.com/edstevenson/ThousandWorlds
summary: The search for life beyond Earth will depend on detecting faint signatures in the atmospheres of potentially habitable exoplanets. Interpreting those signatures requires understanding the host planet's climate: the same molecule may signal life on one planet and abiotic chemistry on another. Global climate models (GCMs) provide this understanding, but individual runs can require up to millions of core-hours and substantial domain expert time. Machine-learning emulators could remove this bottleneck, but progress has been limited by the absence of a curated, multi-model exoclimate dataset. We introduce ThousandWorlds, an ML-ready benchmark for exoclimate emulation and for the broader regime of low-data, multi-simulator, parameter-to-field regression. The dataset contains approximately 1800 simulations from five GCMs, mapping eight planet parameters to 3D atmospheric fields including temperature, humidity, winds, clouds, and radiation. Three nested subsets define progressively harder challenges: single-simulator regression, multi-simulator regression with complete observations, and multi-simulator regression with structured missingness. We propose two evaluation protocols: one for ranking methods, and one that measures performance relative to the disagreement between GCMs themselves. We evaluate seven baselines spanning simple methods, deep learning, and Gaussian processes. GP-based methods perform best, suggesting that ThousandWorlds exposes a regime where off-the-shelf deep learning does not yet succeed. Data: https://doi.org/10.57967/hf/8695. Code: https://github.com/edstevenson/ThousandWorlds.

AI.tldr: A new ML-ready exoclimate benchmark, ThousandWorlds, provides ~1800 GCM simulations across five models, eight planet parameters, and 3D atmospheric fields; defines multi-simulator regression tasks with structured missingness and two evaluation protocols; Gaussian-process baselines outperform deep learning, highlighting regimes where DL struggles.
AI.motivation: Interpreting exoplanet atmospheric signatures requires climate context; existing global climate models are computationally expensive to run; a curated, multi-model benchmark is needed to drive development of machine-learning emulators that can generalize across models and handle limited data.
AI.method: ThousandWorlds compiles ~1800 simulations from five GCMs, mapping eight planet parameters to 3D atmospheric fields (temperature, humidity, winds, clouds, radiation). It defines three nested tasks: (1) single-simulator regression, (2) multi-simulator regression with complete observations, (3) multi-simulator regression with structured missingness. Two evaluation protocols are proposed: (a) ranking methods, (b) measuring performance relative to GCM disagreement. Seven baselines spanning simple methods, deep learning, and Gaussian processes are evaluated.
AI.result: Gaussian-process based methods perform best on the benchmark, indicating that, in this exoclimate emulation regime, off-the-shelf deep learning does not yet surpass GP-based approaches. The dataset and protocols establish a challenging, multi-model, low-data setting where GP methods excel.
AI.conclusion: ThousandWorlds provides a benchmark for exoclimate emulation and broader low-data, multi-simulator regression. It enables rigorous evaluation of ML methods under structured missingness and multi-model disagreement, highlighting regimes where Gaussian processes currently outperform deep learning and guiding future model development. Data and code are available at the provided links.

title: RODS: Reward-Driven Online Data Synthesis for Multi-Turn Tool-Use Agents
summary: Multi-turn tool-use RL is bottlenecked by the rapid depletion of informative samples in static datasets. We observe that the gradient signal in GRPO concentrates on tasks with the highest rollout reward variance, a consequence of the Popoviciu upper bound. Consequently, samples near the agent's capability boundary -- where successes and failures are roughly balanced -- contribute disproportionately large policy gradients. As training progresses, this boundary continuously shifts, which gradually depletes the pool of informative samples in a static dataset. We propose RODS (Reward-driven Online Data Synthesis) to resolve this depletion. RODS closes the loop between RL training and data generation by repurposing the progress reward variance as a practical, zero-cost boundary detector that requires no extra inference beyond the rollouts already computed for training. It continuously identifies such boundary samples, synthesizes new multi-turn variants matching their structural complexity (e.g., API topology and dependency depth) via a skill-aligned resampling pipeline, and manages a dynamic replay buffer that co-evolves with the policy. Starting from 400 human seeds and maintaining an active training pool of ~800 samples, RODS achieves comparable performance to a 17K-sample offline pipeline while requiring roughly 20x fewer trajectories, and improves over fixed-data RL and environment augmentation in our controlled setting.

AI.tldr: RODS leverages reward-variance as an online, zero-cost boundary detector to synthesize informative multi-turn tool-use data and maintain a dynamic replay buffer, achieving similar performance to a large offline dataset with ~20x fewer trajectories.
