AI.motivation: In static datasets for multi-turn tool-use RL, informative samples are quickly depleted because gradient signals concentrate near the policy boundary where successes and failures are balanced. This paper seeks continual data generation to sustain informative samples and improve sample efficiency.
AI.method: Identify boundary samples via progress reward variance (bounded by Popoviciu). Synthesize new multi-turn variants matching their structural complexity (API topology, dependency depth) through a skill-aligned resampling pipeline. Maintain a dynamic replay buffer co-evolving with the policy, using rollouts already computed for training, starting from ~400 seeds and ~800 active samples.
AI.result: RODS achieves comparable performance to a 17K-sample offline pipeline while using roughly 20x fewer trajectories, and outperforms fixed-data RL and environment augmentation in controlled experiments.
AI.conclusion: Reward-variance-driven online data synthesis effectively mitigates sample depletion in multi-turn tool-use RL, enabling strong data efficiency and suggesting broader applicability to RL settings where informative samples evolve during training.

title: HALOMI: Learning Humanoid Loco-Manipulation with Active Perception from Human Demonstrations
summary: Human demonstrations, which can be collected at scale and naturally capture active hand-eye coordination, are a promising data source for learning humanoid loco-manipulation. However, directly transferring human demonstrations to humanoids requires a precise world-frame tracking controller, which is often brittle under Out-of-Distribution(OOD) targets, while human-to-humanoid gaps persist in both egocentric observation and action execution. To address these challenges, we present HALOMI, a scalable framework for learning humanoid loco-manipulation with active perception from human demonstrations. HALOMI extends Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI) with egocentric sensing to collect ego-view and wrist-view observations along with head-hand trajectories at scale. We further propose a manifold-constrained controller that plans in a learned latent behavior manifold to enable precise and robust head-hand tracking in the world frame. To bridge the human-to-humanoid gap, we perform ego-view alignment and introduce a controller-aware reference trajectory adaptation to reduce mismatch in both observation and action execution. We validate HALOMI on a Unitree G1 humanoid robot with an actuated neck across five real-world tasks involving navigation, grasping, bimanual manipulation, whole-body coordination, and dynamic behaviors. Across the three quantitatively evaluated tasks, HALOMI achieves an average success rate of 85\%, while additional qualitative demonstrations show its ability to support dynamic tossing and deep-squat grasping.

AI.tldr: HALOMI is a scalable framework that learns humanoid loco-manipulation from human demonstrations via active perception, extending UMI with ego-view sensing, a latent-manifold controller, and ego-aligned adaptation; validated on a Unitree G1 with 85% average success on three tasks and qualitative demonstrations of dynamic actions.
AI.motivation: Bridge human-to-humanoid gaps in egocentric observation and action execution, and reduce brittleness of world-frame tracking under out-of-distribution targets by leveraging scalable human demonstrations and active perception.
AI.method: Extend Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI) with ego-view sensing (ego-view, wrist-view) and head-hand trajectories; introduce a manifold-constrained controller that plans in a learned latent behavior manifold for precise world-frame tracking; perform ego-view alignment and controller-aware reference trajectory adaptation to mitigate observation/action mismatch between humans and humanoids.
AI.result: Empirical validation on a Unitree G1 across five real-world tasks (navigation, grasping, bimanual manipulation, whole-body coordination, dynamic behaviors); three tasks yield quantitative success averaging 85%; qualitative demonstrations show capabilities like dynamic tossing and deep-squat grasping.
AI.conclusion: HALOMI demonstrates scalable, robust humanoid loco-manipulation from human demonstrations through active perception and latent-plan reasoning, with notable success in diverse tasks and potential for improved OOD generalization and broader real-world deployment.

title: Bridging Creative Intent and Visual Quality: Creator-Driven Recurrent Video Generation with Agentic Feedback Loops
comment: Accepted to the Workshop on Human-AI Co-Creativity at ICML 2026
summary: Generative AI has made content creation increasingly accessible, but many AI-generated videos lack narrative coherence and creative direction, issues that become more substantial at longer durations. Unlike coding, where AI generation benefits from reliable feedback and techniques such as recurrent self-improvement, video generation requires subjective feedback about plot, scenes, and narrative, which naturally motivates approaches that incorporate human creative direction. We introduce CHIEF, a human-AI co-creation video generation framework that places the creator at the center of human-in-the-loop iterative video refinement, and supports them by providing automatic subjective feedback. The creator incorporates their creative direction by driving each iteration, while their revisions are incorporated by a specialized refiner agent. The feedback loop is generated by persona-conditioned multimodal LLMs that watch generated videos and produce subjective critique from the audience perspectives, providing feedback that self-evaluation alone cannot capture. To test the effectiveness of our proposed framework, we work with high school and college students with no prior filmmaking experience to create videos, from short 1-minute videos to a complete short 10-minute film with a complicated plot.

AI.tldr: CHIEF: a human-AI co-creation framework for video generation that puts creators at the center, using persona-conditioned multimodal LLMs to provide audience-like subjective feedback, enabling iterative refinement via a refiner agent; tested with non-expert students across videos from 1 minute to a 10-minute film.
AI.motivation: Long-form AI-generated videos often suffer from weak narrative coherence and lack of creative direction; objective feedback parallels in coding do not translate well to storytelling; a human-in-the-loop approach with AI-assisted subjective critique aims to address this gap.
AI.method: A tri-component system: (1) creator-driven iterations guiding the content; (2) a specialized refiner agent that incorporates revisions; (3) persona-conditioned multimodal LLMs that watch generated videos and generate audience-perspective critique to guide improvements. The loop is tested via a pilot study with high school and college students producing videos ranging from 1 minute to a complete 10-minute film with a complicated plot.
AI.result: The abstract outlines a framework and a pilot user-study design but does not report quantitative results; it claims feasibility and potential effectiveness through a student-based evaluation.
AI.conclusion: CHIEF provides a human-centered, feedback-rich pipeline for long-form AI video generation, combining creator agency with AI-generated subjective feedback to improve narrative coherence and alignment; initial demonstrations with novices suggest feasibility and potential for broader adoption and more rigorous evaluation.

title: Do Time Series Foundation Model Benchmarks Hide Regime-Dependent Failures? Evidence from Traffic Speed Forecasting
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the Workshop on Forecasting as a New Frontier of Intelligence, ICML 2026
summary: Standard benchmarks evaluate time series foundation models (TSFMs) using aggregate metrics, but these can mask severe failures in critical operating regimes. We introduce regime-stratified evaluation and apply it to three TSFMs on two standard traffic speed benchmarks. Traffic exhibits abrupt regime switching between free-flow and congested states, producing bimodal speed distributions during transitions. When we stratify by traffic regime, both accuracy and prediction-interval coverage degrade sharply during transitions: transition-regime MAE reaches 11 mph (versus 3 mph overall), and empirical coverage of 90% prediction intervals drops as low as 55%. These failures are invisible in aggregate metrics because free-flow observations dominate the sample. A simple historical conditional baseline (sampling from per-sensor training distributions) achieves better transition coverage than any TSFM, but has far worse overall accuracy. We propose bimodal mixture augmentation (BMA), a post-hoc method that combines TSFM forecasts with historical distributional knowledge, approaching the historical baseline's transition coverage while preserving the TSFM's accuracy. Our results suggest that TSFM benchmarks should incorporate regime-aware evaluation to surface failures that aggregate metrics hide.

AI.tldr: Regime-aware evaluation for TSFMs with a post-hoc bimodal mixture augmentation (BMA) to improve transition-region performance.
AI.motivation: Standard aggregate metrics conceal failures in critical regime transitions (free-flow to congested) in traffic time series; traffic exhibits bimodal speed distributions during transitions.
AI.method: Regime-stratified evaluation of three TSFMs on two traffic-speed benchmarks; quantify transition-region MAE and prediction-interval (PI) coverage; demonstrate historical-conditioned baselines; propose BMA as a post-hoc combination of TSFM forecasts with historical distributional knowledge.
AI.result: Transition-region MAE reaches ~11 mph vs. ~3 mph overall; 90% PI coverage degrades to ~55% in transitions; a simple historical-conditional baseline yields better transition coverage but poorer overall accuracy; BMA approaches the historical baseline's transition coverage while preserving TSFM accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark TSFMs with regime-aware evaluation to reveal failures hidden by aggregate metrics; BMA provides a practical method to recover transition coverage without sacrificing accuracy; regime-aware benchmarking is recommended.

title: ARIADNE: Agnostic Routing for Inference-time Adapter DyNamic sElection
summary: The increasing deployment of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) has led to model ecosystems in which a single backbone is paired with many task-specialized adapters. In this setting, inference-time queries often arrive without task labels, requiring the system to automatically select the most appropriate adapter from a growing and heterogeneous adapter pool. Existing routing methods either depend on access to adapter internals, such as weight decompositions or gradient-based statistics, or require additional router training, which limits scalability and portability as new adapters are added. We introduce ARIADNE, a training-free, adapter-agnostic routing framework for dynamic adapter selection at inference time. ARIADNE represents each adapter through a set of centroids computed from embeddings of its training set, capturing the data distribution associated with that adapter. Given an unlabeled input, it selects an adapter by measuring proximity to these centroids in latent space. Because routing is performed entirely in the input embedding space, ARIADNE is compatible with arbitrary PEFT methods and requires no modification to the adapters or training procedures. Primarily evaluated with Llama 3.2 1B Instruct on 23 diverse NLP tasks, ARIADNE recovers 97.44% of the upper bound performance. Scaling to 44 tasks, it achieves 89.7% average selection accuracy, without additional training or access to adapter internals.

AI.tldr: ARIADNE provides training-free, adapter-agnostic routing for PEFT by representing each adapter with training-data embeddings centroids and selecting adapters via proximity in embedding space, enabling scalable, label-free inference without modifying adapters.
AI.motivation: In PEFT ecosystems with many task-specific adapters, inference queries are often unlabeled, requiring scalable routing without exposing or altering adapter internals or requiring extra router training.
AI.method: Compute per-adapter centroids from embeddings of the adapter's training data. At inference, embed the input and assign the adapter whose centroids are nearest in latent space; routing occurs entirely in embedding space, no training or adapter modification required, compatible with any PEFT method.
AI.result: On LLama 3.2 1B Instruct across 23 tasks, ARIADNE recovers 97.44% of the upper-bound performance; scaling to 44 tasks yields 89.7% average selection accuracy, with no training or access to adapter internals.
AI.conclusion: ARIADNE enables scalable, training-free, adapter-agnostic routing for dynamic adapter selection that works across arbitrary PEFT methods and adapters.

title: Space Is Intelligence: Neural Semigroup Superposition for Riemannian Metric Generation
summary: Traditional approaches place intelligence in the agent, whether as a learned policy or a search procedure. We instead place intelligence in the space itself: a scene induces a Riemannian metric on the configuration manifold, and action reduces to following the geodesics of that metric rather than invoking a separate planner or collision checker. A single Encoder-Router network realizes this idea through three complementary parameter groups -- frame parameters that orient the generators, modulation parameters that govern their spatial propagation, and basic coefficients that determine their strength. These groups combine through a shared semigroup-superposition mechanism to produce a single Riemannian metric field, yielding a compact architecture whose geometry scales naturally with scene complexity. Trained on a single two-obstacle scene, the model demonstrates robust zero-shot generalization across unseen obstacle configurations, with orders-of-magnitude separation between collision-free and obstacle-penetrating path costs.

AI.tldr: A geometry-based planning paradigm where a scene induces a Riemannian metric on the configuration space and actions follow geodesics, realized by an Encoder-Router that constructs the metric from frame, modulation, and coefficient parameters; achieving zero-shot generalization from a two-obstacle training scene with a large gap between collision-free and penetrating paths.
AI.motivation: Traditional agent-centric planning (policy or search) can bias computation and limit generalization. This work encodes intelligence in the environment by shaping the configuration-space metric, enabling scalable planning via geometric principles and improved cross-scene generalization.
AI.method: Introduce an Encoder-Router network with three parameter groups—frame, modulation, and basic coefficients—that combine through a semigroup-superposition to produce a single Riemannian metric field over the configuration manifold. Action is performed by following geodesics of this metric, eliminating the need for a separate planner or collision checker. The model is trained on a simple two-obstacle scene.
AI.result: The model demonstrates robust zero-shot generalization to unseen obstacle configurations, with orders-of-magnitude separation between collision-free and obstacle-penetrating path costs, indicating strong geometric compositionality and scalable planning.
AI.conclusion: Embedding intelligence in the metric field yields a compact architecture whose geometry scales with scene complexity and enables planning via geodesics, achieving cross-scene generalization without task-specific planners.

title: Hallucination Detection and Correction in Medical VLMs via Counter-Evidence Verification
summary: Vision-Language models (VLMs) reliability in medical diagnosis is challenged by trust-undermining hallucinations. Existing hallucination detection approaches mainly focus on identifying factual inconsistencies between generated text and reference data. While some studies analyze where models attend in images, they seldom verify whether such attention truly reflects the visual evidence supporting the generated text. To address this gap, we propose Co}unter-Evidence Verification (CoEV), a training-free plug-and-play framework that detects and corrects hallucinations through evidence-based factual consistency verification. CoEV performs bidirectional verification between textual assertions and visual evidence, testing whether each statement is supported by its corresponding evidence region, and assigns each statement into a four-quadrant diagnostic map capturing combinations of text factuality and visual grounding. CoEV detects hallucinated content and serves as a post hoc refinement tool, correcting hallucinations without retraining. Extensive experiments on four medical datasets show that CoEV combats hallucinations in VLMs.For hallucination detection, CoEV consistently outperforms existing methods, improving average PR-AUC and ROC-AUC by 3.0% and 3.9% absolute points respectively, with notable gains of up to 18.5% in specific VQA scenarios. For hallucination correction, it improves Micro-F1 by up to 12.5%, reduces hallucination rates by over 11.9% on medical report generation, and also boosts medical VQA accuracy. These results show that CoEV enables reliable detection and correction of hallucinations, providing clinicians with dependable, evidence-based cues for diagnosis. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A training-free plug-and-play framework (CoEV) that detects and corrects hallucinations in Vision-Language Models for medical data by bidirectional verification of textual assertions against visual evidence, yielding a four-quadrant diagnostic map and improved detection and correction metrics across four datasets.
AI.motivation: Medical VLMs suffer from trust-undermining hallucinations; existing detection methods focus on textual-visual inconsistencies or attend-head analyses that may not reflect true grounding. There is a need for evidence-based verification that ties text to visual regions without retraining.
AI.method: CoEV performs bidirectional verification between textual statements and their corresponding visual evidence regions. Each statement is tested for support by the evidence region; statements are colored into a four-quadrant diagnostic map capturing combinations of text factuality and visual grounding. It is training-free and serves as a post hoc refinement tool to detect and correct hallucinations without model retraining.
AI.result: Across four medical datasets, CoEV improves detection metrics with average PR-AUC +3.0 and ROC-AUC +3.9 percentage points; gains up to 18.5% in certain VQA settings. In correction tasks, Micro-F1 improves by up to 12.5%, hallucination rates drop >11.9% for medical report generation, and medical VQA accuracy increases.
AI.conclusion: CoEV enables reliable detection and correction of hallucinations in medical VLMs, offering clinicians dependable, evidence-based cues for diagnosis. Code will be released upon acceptance.

title: From Sparse Features to Trustworthy Proxies: Certifying SAE-Based Interpretability
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are increasingly used to extract interpretable features from language models (LMs), yet a central question remains: when can an SAE-based explanation be treated as a faithful view of an underlying frozen LM We study this through a post-hoc generalization framework that certifies the LM via a sparse proxy, obtained by replacing a native hidden activation with its pretrained SAE reconstruction. Our framework derives an upper bound on the base model's expected risk using four measurable quantities: proxy risk, SAE reconstruction gap, concept-pool mismatch, and sparse complexity. We interpret this certificate as an operational criterion for explanatory faithfulness. In particular, a non-vacuous bound indicates that the extracted sparse features retain meaningful predictive information, while small reconstruction and mismatch errors indicate that the proxy remains behaviorally close to the original model. Empirically, we show that the bound becomes non-vacuous on GPT-2 Small, Gemma-2B, and Llama-3-8B at practical sample sizes. A detailed layerwise analysis of Llama-3-8B reveals a strong depth dependence, with later layers becoming much easier to certify, associated with both stronger local fidelity and weaker downstream error amplification. Finally, through feature-shuffling ablations, we show that the decomposition distinguishes genuine semantic alignment from mere statistical sparsity, providing a useful diagnostic for when SAE-based explanations become less reliable.

AI.tldr: A post-hoc certification framework bounds a frozen LM's risk via a sparse proxy from a pretrained SAE, providing an operational criterion for when SAE-based explanations are faithful. The bound becomes non-vacuous on certain models and layers, with deeper layers typically easier to certify; feature-shuffling distinguishes semantic alignment from mere sparsity, offering a diagnostic for reliability of SAE explanations.
AI.motivation: To determine when sparse autoencoder (SAE)–based explanations faithfully reflect the behavior of a frozen language model and to provide an actionable, quantitative certificate of explanatory faithfulness.
AI.method: Introduce a post-hoc generalization framework that replaces a native hidden activation with its pretrained SAE reconstruction to form a sparse proxy. Derive an upper bound on the base model's expected risk using four measurable quantities: proxy risk, SAE reconstruction gap, concept-pool mismatch, and sparse complexity; interpret this bound as a fidelity certificate.
AI.result: The bound becomes non-vacuous on GPT-2 Small, Gemma-2B, and Llama-3-8B at practical sample sizes. Layerwise analysis on Llama-3-8B shows later layers are easier to certify, with improved local fidelity and reduced downstream error amplification. Feature-shuffling ablations demonstrate the decomposition captures genuine semantic alignment rather than mere sparsity, serving as a diagnostic for SAE-explanation reliability.
AI.conclusion: The certificate provides an operational criterion for explanatory faithfulness of SAE-based LM explanations. Model depth influences certifiability, with deeper layers often easier to certify; the framework offers a practical diagnostic to assess when SAE explanations remain reliable.

title: Towards an Agent-First Web: Redesigning the Web for AI Agents
summary:
The World Wide Web was built on an assumption held for three decades: the primary consumer of web content is a human being. This permeates every layer; its access model presumes human visitors, its economics rest on human attention, and its content targets human perception. The rapid emergence of AI agents as intermediaries between humans and web content invalidates this assumption. Yet the web resists agents through blanket blocking, CAPTCHA-based exclusion, and economic models that treat agent access as extraction rather than legitimate interaction.
  This paper proposes a principled redesign across three layers. At the access layer, agents acting for humans should inherit equivalent access rights, governed by rate limiting and agent identification metadata in HTTP requests, analogous to browser headers, alongside a dual-layer architecture serving human-readable and agent-optimized content from the same domain. At the economic layer, we propose an intent-based tier framework grounded in the agent-as-human-proxy principle: an agent's economic obligation mirrors that of the human it represents. A token-based subscription model meters content in tokens rather than pageviews, alongside a commissioned content economy anchoring AI content production in human intentionality. At the content layer, we identify epistemic recursion, the self-referential loop in which AI-generated content is consumed by agents to produce further content, progressively detaching web knowledge from human ground truth. We propose the Agent Text Markup Language (ATML), a four-level human supervision tier model, and a cryptographic provenance chain to counter this threat.
  Together these constitute ten design principles for an agent-first internet, one in which agents are first-class citizens whose integration requires renegotiating the web's foundational social contract across access, economics, and content.

AI.tldr: An agent-first redesign of the web across access, economics, and content, introducing agent-centric rights, token-based economics, and provenance mechanisms, culminating in ten design principles and ATML.
AI.motivation: The Web's human-centric assumption is challenged by AI agents acting as intermediaries; blanket blocks and pageview-based economics fail to accommodate agents, risking misalignment with human intent and knowledge integrity.
AI.method: Propose a three-layer redesign: (1) Access layer granting agent-equivalent rights with rate limiting and agent-identification metadata in HTTP requests, plus a dual-layer domain serving human- and agent-optimized content; (2) Economic layer with an intent-based tier framework where the agent represents a human, token-based subscriptions, and a commissioned content economy anchored in human intentionality; (3) Content layer addressing epistemic recursion via ATML (four-level human supervision) and a cryptographic provenance chain.
AI.result: Outlines ten design principles for an agent-first Internet and concrete mechanisms (agent rights, rate limiting, token-based meters, and provenance) to align agent access and content with human ground truth.
AI.conclusion: Adoption would renegotiate the Web's social contract to recognize agents as first-class citizens, integrating access, economics, and content while preserving human-centric knowledge and accountability.

title: ZiMPedance: Impedance-Aware ZMP Modeling and Control for Payload Carrying with Quadruped Robots
summary: Load transportation with quadruped robots is strongly affected by the dynamics of the physical interface between the robot and the load. Passive spring-based arms reduce weight and complexity compared to active manipulators, but their spring-damper dynamics can introduce oscillatory forces that degrade locomotion stability. This paper derives an extended Zero Moment Point (ZMP) formulation that includes passive payload-interface dynamics, relating stiffness, damping, and payload mass to the stability margin. The analysis shows that underdamped configurations can resonate with locomotion harmonics. Based on this insight, we augment a Single Rigid Body Dynamics model with passive subsystem dynamics and integrate it into a Model Predictive Control framework. In simulation, the proposed controller reduces stability violations by up to $10\times$, from $7.0\%$ to $0.7\%$, and increase locomotion efficiency by lowering horizontal ground reaction force effort by up to $15\%$ compared to a nominal baseline. Hardware experiments with a $2\,\mathrm{kg}$ payload show stable locomotion under pull-release disturbances where the nominal controller fails. The same model also enables end-effector tracking through passive arm dynamics without direct arm actuation.

AI.tldr: Extended ZMP formulation incorporating passive payload-interface dynamics with spring-damper arms; analyzes resonance with locomotion harmonics; integrates passive dynamics into a MPC-equipped Single Rigid Body Dynamics model; demonstrates large stability improvements (up to 10x) and efficiency gains in simulation, plus hardware validation with a 2 kg payload; shows end-effector tracking via passive dynamics without arm actuation.
AI.motivation: Passive spring-based payload interfaces lower weight/complexity but introduce dynamic coupling that can destabilize legged locomotion. A unified stability margin relation is needed to guide design and control.
AI.method: Derive an extended ZMP that includes passive payload-interface dynamics and relate stiffness, damping, and payload mass to stability margins. Augment a Single Rigid Body Dynamics model with the passive subsystem and embed it in Model Predictive Control. Validate via simulation and hardware experiments, including disturbances.
AI.result: Simulation shows stability violations reduced from 7.0% to 0.7% (≈10× improvement) and a 15% reduction in horizontal ground reaction force effort. Hardware tests with a 2 kg payload demonstrate stable locomotion under pull–release disturbances where a nominal controller fails. The model enables end-effector tracking through passive arm dynamics without direct actuation.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating passive subsystem dynamics into the control framework yields substantial stability and efficiency benefits; the extended ZMP formulation provides a practical design tool, and passive payload interfaces can support end-effector tasks without actuators.

title: Intrinsic 4D Gaussian Segmentation from Scene Cues
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 7 tables. Includes supplementary material. Preprint
summary: Dynamic 4D Gaussian Splatting reconstructs deforming scenes with high fidelity and is increasingly adopted as a representation for dynamic 3D scenes. Putting such a scene to use, for editing, manipulation or motion analysis, first requires segmenting it: grouping the Gaussian primitives into coherent objects. Current pipelines obtain this grouping by importing 2D masks from foundation models such as SAM and lifting or distilling them into the Gaussian representation. In dynamic scenes these masks must be generated across many frames and views, which is costly, and the resulting segmentation can depend strongly on the quality and consistency of those external masks. We ask how much object-level structure can instead be recovered from the Gaussians themselves, and propose Intrinsic-GS, a training-free, mask-free method that builds a sparse affinity graph over Gaussian primitives from appearance, orientation, scale, deformation-trajectory and non-learned rendered-boundary cues. The graph is partitioned with Leiden community detection, requiring no foundation model and no learned feature field. On the standard 4D Gaussian segmentation benchmarks, Neu3D and HyperNeRF, Intrinsic-GS recovers substantial object structure without mask supervision, reaching 0.746 mIoU on Neu3D and 0.575 on HyperNeRF; on Neu3D, a geometry-only variant reaches 0.902 mIoU, matching SAM-supervised TRASE. On HyperNeRF, Intrinsic-GS runs 12.5x faster than the mask-generation and feature-rendering stages used by mask-supervised pipelines. These results suggest that much of the segmentation signal is already encoded in the Gaussians themselves, offering a fast, mask-free direction for 3D and 4D Gaussian segmentation that may also point toward more generalizable, robust segmentation in settings where external masks are unreliable or expensive.

AI.tldr: Intrinsic-GS is training-free, mask-free segmentation for dynamic 4D Gaussian Splatting that builds a sparse Gaussian affinity graph and uses Leiden clustering to recover object structure without external masks.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on 2D foundation-model masks and heavy mask-generation pipelines for segmenting deforming 4D scenes; achieve fast, robust object-level segmentation directly from the Gaussian representation.
AI.method: Construct a sparse affinity graph over Gaussian primitives using appearance, orientation, scale, deformation trajectory, and non-learned rendered-boundary cues; partition the graph with Leiden community detection; no learned features or mask supervision.
AI.result: On Neu3D and HyperNeRF benchmarks, achieves 0.746 mIoU (Neu3D) and 0.575 mIoU (HyperNeRF); a geometry-only variant reaches 0.902 mIoU on Neu3D, matching SAM-supervised TRASE; HyperNeRF runs 12.5x faster than mask-generation plus feature rendering in mask-supervised pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Much of the segmentation signal resides in the Gaussians themselves; a fast, mask-free approach that can generalize when external masks are unreliable or costly, offering robust, scalable segmentation for dynamic 3D/4D scenes.

title: SCOPE-FL: A Strategy-proof Chain-based Optimal pareto efficient Federated Learning System
summary: Hierarchical Federated Learning (HFL) enables scalable collaborative model training across distributed devices while preserving data privacy. However, existing HFL client selection mechanisms suffer from a fundamental strategic inefficiency. By prioritizing stability over Pareto efficiency (PE), they produce suboptimal resource allocations, and without strategy proofness (SP), participants are incentivized to misrepresent their true preferences, both failures degrading system overall welfare in the Pareto sense in practice. To address it, we propose SCOPE-FL (Strategy-proof Chain-based Optimal pareto efficient Federated Learning), a synchronous HFL framework that formulates client selection as a two-sided school choice problem solved through the Top Trading Cycle (TTC) algorithm that simultaneously guarantees PE and SP. For reward distribution, SCOPE-FL employs a scalable Shapley value approximation based on One-Round Reconstruction (OR), ensuring compensation proportional to each client's contribution. The entire mechanism executes via blockchain smart contracts, providing the tamper-proof environment required for the SP guarantees to hold in practice. A comprehensive evaluation on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 demonstrates that SCOPE-FL outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, including DA, IAS, and other methods across model accuracy, convergence rate, and reward efficiency, while achieving communication latency comparable to DA and blockchain overhead significantly lower than DA at scale.

AI.tldr: SCOPE-FL introduces a synchronous hierarchical federated learning framework that achieves Pareto efficiency and strategy-proofness in client selection using a Top Trading Cycle-based solution, with Shapley-value-inspired reward distribution via One-Round Reconstruction and blockchain-based tamper-proof contracts; it outperforms state-of-the-art methods in accuracy, convergence, and reward efficiency with scalable latency characteristics.
AI.motivation: Existing HFL client selection prioritizes stability over Pareto efficiency and lacks strategy-proofness, leading to suboptimal allocations and misreporting incentives. A mechanism that ensures both PE and SP and fair rewards is needed for practical, scalable HFL.
AI.method: Formulate client selection as a two-sided school choice problem and solve via the Top Trading Cycle (TTC) algorithm to guarantee Pareto efficiency and strategy-proofness. Distribute rewards using a scalable Shapley-value approximation based on One-Round Reconstruction (OR). Implement the mechanism on blockchain smart contracts to provide tamper-proof guarantees.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 shows SCOPE-FL achieves superior model accuracy, faster convergence, and better reward efficiency compared to state-of-the-art methods (DA, IAS, etc.), while maintaining communication latency comparable to DA and with lower blockchain overhead at scale.
AI.conclusion: SCOPE-FL successfully enforces Pareto efficiency and strategy-proofness in HFL client selection, with scalable, fair reward distribution via Shapley-value approximation and blockchain tamper-proofing, delivering improved welfare and practical performance.

title: Analysing drivers and interdependencies in European electricity markets using XAI
summary: Electricity markets are inherently complex systems characterised by strong nonlinearities, high-dimensional interactions, and increasing interdependence across regions. While deep neural networks (DNNs) have demonstrated strong predictive capabilities for electricity prices, their lack of interpretability limits their usefulness for understanding the underlying drivers of price formation. This paper addresses this gap by combining DNN models with explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques to analyse the determinants of electricity prices across 39 European bidding zones. We employ SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) to quantify feature contributions and apply and extend SSHAP, an aggregation framework to improve interpretability in high-dimensional settings. The analysis identifies that renewable energy sources, particularly solar, play a disproportionately important role in price formation despite their lower share in total power generation. Gas prices remain a dominant and consistent driver across electricity markets, while interconnections significantly shape price dynamics, highlighting the strong interdependence of European electricity systems. In addition, a synthetic EU-wide electricity market is constructed to explore the counterfactual scenario of a fully integrated market with a single price.

AI.tldr: A DNN-based analysis with SHAP/SSHAP on 39 European bidding zones reveals solar's outsized effect on price formation, gas prices as a persistent driver, and interconnections' strong role; introduces a synthetic EU-wide market to examine a fully integrated single-price scenario.
AI.motivation: To address the black-box nature of DNNs in electricity price formation and to uncover the high-dimensional, cross-border drivers of prices for policymakers and market participants.
AI.method: Train deep neural networks for price prediction across 39 European bidding zones; apply SHAP to quantify feature contributions; extend/apply SSHAP aggregation to improve interpretability in high-dimensional settings; analyze drivers and cross-border interconnections; construct a synthetic EU-wide market to simulate a fully integrated single-price scenario.
AI.result: Solar (especially solar) exerts a disproportionate influence on price formation despite a lower share of total generation; gas prices remain a dominant and consistent driver; interconnections significantly shape price dynamics, indicating strong interdependence of European systems; a synthetic, fully integrated EU-wide market is used to explore counterfactual single-price outcomes.
AI.conclusion: XAI-enhanced DNN analysis yields interpretable, actionable insights into price determinants and cross-border dynamics in European electricity markets; underscores the importance of solar, the central role of gas prices, and the impact of interconnections; demonstrates the value of counterfactual market simulations to inform market design and integration policy.

title: C-ARC: Continuous-Adaptive Range Clustering for Non-Repetitive LiDAR Sensors
comment: Submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication. 8 pages, 7 figures
summary: Real-time LiDAR clustering identifies structures in point clouds, which is an essential prerequisite for many mobile robotics algorithms. Current methods are mostly developed for repetitive mechanical LiDAR sensors. Recently, the use of non-repetitive LiDAR sensors is strongly increasing due to their small cost and form factor. Such non-repetitive Risley prism-based sensors violate two key assumptions of repetitive mechanical sensors: structured scan lines and well-defined frame boundaries. Their Rhodonea-curve trajectories produce non-uniform point distributions, and the absence of a rotation cycle renders conventional scan line indexing inapplicable. To meet such new requirements, we developed C-ARC, a Continuous-Adaptive Range Clustering framework that maintains a persistent dual-graph over a sliding window, decoupling high-frequency point insertion from on-demand cluster retrieval. This is crucial for key functionalities like SLAM or tracking. An adaptive range grid resolution mechanism calibrates grid dimensions at initialization using an exponential control loop, balancing the sparsity-collision trade-off without prior knowledge of the scanning pattern. Implemented as an open-sourced single-threaded C++17 library, C-ARC produces real-time cluster output at 20 Hz on commodity hardware for the Livox Mid-360. Evaluation on the Livox Avia identifies unbounded cell occupancy as the primary limitation for sensors with strongly concentrated scan patterns. The adaptive resolution mechanism additionally improves clustering quality for existing grid-based methods on non-repetitive data.

AI.tldr: A real-time continuous-adaptive range clustering framework (C-ARC) for non-repetitive LiDARs (e.g., Risley prism sensors) using a persistent dual-graph on a sliding window with adaptive grid resolution via an exponential control loop, achieving 20 Hz clustering on Livox hardware; main limitation is unbounded cell occupancy for highly concentrated scan patterns; improves clustering quality for grid-based methods on non-repetitive data.
AI.motivation: Non-repetitive LiDARs violate assumptions of structured scan lines and frame boundaries in conventional clustering, necessitating a real-time, robust clustering approach that does not depend on repetition or cycle boundaries.
AI.method: C-ARC maintains a persistent dual-graph over a sliding window, decouples high-frequency point insertion from on-demand cluster retrieval, and uses an adaptive range grid with an exponential control loop to set grid resolution, balancing sparsity and collisions without prior knowledge of scanning pattern. Implemented as a single-threaded C++17 library and evaluated on Livox Mid-360 and Avia.
AI.result: Real-time clustering output at 20 Hz on commodity hardware; unbounded cell occupancy identified as the primary limitation for sensors with concentrated scan patterns; adaptive resolution mechanism improves clustering quality for existing grid-based methods on non-repetitive data.
AI.conclusion: C-ARC enables real-time clustering for non-repetitive LiDAR data by decoupling update and retrieval and adapting grid resolution to scan patterns; the framework is effective on Livox devices, with unbounded cell occupancy as a key area for improvement and future work.

title: Spiking Pyramid Wavelet Transformation for High-efficient and Low-energy Image Restoration
summary: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) have garnered significant interest in computer vision due to their potential for efficiency and biological inspiration. While spiking CNN-based methods have shown promise for image restoration (IR) tasks, their performance is constrained by the inherent receptive field limitations of CNN operations. In the paper, we explore the benefits of discrete wavelet transformation and propose a spiking pyramid wavelet-based model (SPWM) for high-efficient and low-energy target. Specifically, we develop a spiking dual pyramid wavelet (SDPW) block to model long-range dependency and exploit the properties of the degradation in the wavelet domain. Experimental results on several benchmarks demonstrate that SPWM significantly lowers computational costs and energy consumption while maintaining image quality. Our method showcases the potential of SNNs in the field of IR, offering new insights for future applications of resource-limited devices.

AI.tldr: Proposes SPWM, a spiking pyramid wavelet-based model for image restoration, employing discrete wavelet transform and a spiking dual pyramid wavelet (SDPW) block to capture long-range dependencies, achieving lower computation and energy without sacrificing image quality.
AI.motivation: CNN-based image restoration is limited by receptive-field constraints and high compute; SNNs offer energy efficiency but require novel architectures. Wavelet-domain processing can exploit degradation characteristics and sparsity for efficient IR on resource-constrained devices.
AI.method: Introduce SPWM framework that uses discrete wavelet transform to analyze in the wavelet domain and a spiking dual pyramid wavelet (SDPW) block to model long-range dependencies and exploit degradation properties; implemented within a spiking neural network for efficient image restoration.
AI.result: Empirical results on several benchmarks show SPWM significantly reduces computational costs and energy consumption while maintaining image quality compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of wavelet-domain SNNs for efficient image restoration and provides insights for deploying IR on resource-limited devices; suggests directions for future work in SNN-based IR.

title: LLMZero: Discovering Adaptive Training Strategies for RL Post-Training via LLM Agents
summary: RL post-training strategies are dataset-dependent and reveal a recurring empirical pattern: capacity parameters accumulate monotonically across stages, while regularization parameters predominantly oscillate in response to shifting training dynamics. This distinction matters because fixed schedules commit all parameters to fixed trajectories and therefore cannot express the non-stationary exploration-exploitation tradeoffs that regularization must track; the principle provides actionable design rules for multi-stage training. We discover this through LLMZero, a system where LLM agents search over training trajectories via tree search, diagnosing pathologies at each checkpoint and proposing coordinated multi-parameter transitions. Across 4 diverse GRPO tasks, LLMZero discovers strategies that improve over the base model by 9% to 140% relative and over grid search by 6% to 15% relative, consistently outperforming random search and the skill-based agent. The structural principle transfers across tasks, providing an explanation for why discovered strategies take qualitatively different forms yet share similar parameter dynamics.

AI.tldr: Monotone growth of capacity across stages vs oscillatory regularization; LLMZero automates multi-parameter schedules via tree-search, yielding strong cross-task gains and a transferable design principle.
AI.motivation: To address non-stationary exploration–exploitation in multi-stage RL post-training and to avoid fixed-trajectory schedules by uncovering a structural principle that guides multi-parameter adaptation across stages.
AI.method: LLMZero uses LLM-based agents to search training trajectories with tree search, diagnose pathologies at checkpoints, and propose coordinated transitions across multiple parameters.
AI.result: Across 4 diverse GRPO tasks, LLMZero achieves 9%–140% relative improvement over the base model and 6%–15% over grid search, consistently beating random search and a skill-based agent.
AI.conclusion: The identified structural principle transfers across tasks, explaining why strategies differ in form but share similar parameter dynamics, and enabling actionable multi-stage training design rules.

title: Human-AI Coevolution Dynamics: A Formal Theory of Social Intelligence Emergence Through Long-Term Interaction
summary: Current conversational AI systems have made significant progress in language generation, personalization, and long-context interaction. However, most existing methods model social behavior through isolated components such as emotion modeling, memory retrieval, or persona conditioning, lacking a unified framework to explain the emergence of stable social relationships and social intelligence in long-term human-AI interaction.To address this, we propose the Human-AI Coevolution Dynamics Framework (HACD-H), a formal model of human-AI interaction as a self-organizing social cognitive system. HACD-H integrates emotional adaptation, relational organization, social memory, and personality consistency into a unified dynamical framework and introduces principles including multi-timescale social cognition, relational attractors, trust basins, developmental phase transitions, and social cognitive energy dynamics.We construct a conversational dataset with approximately 14,700 interaction turns and develop a theory-driven empirical evaluation framework. Results reveal a hierarchy of temporal persistence in social cognition, stable relational attractors, phase-transition-like developmental patterns, and a structured social cognitive energy landscape. Social intelligence shows a significant negative correlation with social cognitive energy (r = -0.391, p < 0.001), and interaction trajectories exhibit progressive energy reduction over time.These findings suggest that social intelligence emerges from long-term social cognitive coevolution rather than isolated conversational capabilities. HACD-H provides a unified theoretical foundation for modeling adaptive human-AI social interaction and developing socially intelligent AI systems.

AI.tldr: A unified dynamical framework HACD-H models long-term human-AI social interaction by integrating emotion adaptation, relational organization, social memory, and personality consistency into a self-organizing social cognitive system. It reveals social cognitive attractors, phase-transitional development, and an energy landscape; empirical evaluation on ~14.7k turns shows social intelligence negatively correlates with social cognitive energy (r = -0.391, p < 0.001) and energy decreases over time, supporting coevolution as the source of social intelligence.
AI.motivation: Current conversational AI treats social behavior as isolated modules (emotion, memory, persona) and lacks a unified theory for the emergence of stable social relationships and social intelligence in long-term human-AI interaction.
AI.method: Proposes HACD-H, a multi-component dynamical framework that unifies emotional adaptation, relational organization, social memory, and personality consistency into one energy-based model. Introduces concepts like multi-timescale social cognition, relational attractors, trust basins, developmental phase transitions, and social cognitive energy dynamics. Builds a conversational dataset (~14,700 turns) and a theory-driven empirical evaluation framework to assess hierarchical temporal persistence, attractors, phase transitions, and energy landscapes; analyzes social intelligence vs. energy and energy trajectories over time.
AI.result: Finds a hierarchy of temporal persistence in social cognition, stable relational attractors, phase-transition-like developmental patterns, and a structured energy landscape. Observes a significant negative correlation between social intelligence and social cognitive energy (r = -0.391, p < 0.001) and shows interaction trajectories with progressive energy reduction over time.
AI.conclusion: Social intelligence in long-term human-AI interaction emerges from the coevolution of multiple social-cognitive processes, not from isolated conversational abilities. HACD-H provides a unified theoretical foundation for modeling adaptive social interaction and guiding development of socially intelligent AI.

title: A High-accuracy Event-based Underwater SLAM System
summary: While event cameras offer immense potential for underwater SLAM, existing Time Surface (TS)-based methods prove highly unreliable when deployed underwater. Fluctuating camera velocities severely degrade TS imaging quality, while wide stereo baselines and repetitive underwater textures induce critical matching failures, frequently triggering system failure. To overcome these challenges, we develop the first high-accuracy event-based underwater stereo SLAM system. A structure-aware metric for TS is designed based on structure tensor coherence and gradients to quantitatively evaluate TS structural information density. By decoupling the optimal TS generation into two distinct stages based on system initialization, Bayesian Optimization(BO) first predicts an optimal prior TS sequentially before initialization while we set an asynchronous online local searching method periodically to obtain appropriate TS in real-time during the tracking stage. We use the prior disparity to guarantee precise data association and "latest-observation-first'' triangulation mechanism to realize stable triangulation. As a benchmark for these solutions and a resource for the community, we also contribute UWE, the first high-quality real-world underwater event dataset containing variable camera motions, complex textures and different trajectory features. Extensive evaluations on public datasets and UWE show the competitive accuracy performance of the proposed SLAM system compared to the state-of-the-art event-based method. The code and data will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: Introduces a high-accuracy event-based underwater stereo SLAM system that uses a structure-aware time surface and a two-stage TS generation pipeline (Bayesian optimization for a prior during initialization, plus online local searching during tracking), leveraging prior disparity for robust data association and a latest-observation-first triangulation. Also introduces UWE, a real-world underwater event dataset, and reports competitive accuracy against state-of-the-art methods with open-source code and data planned.
AI.motivation: Underwater environments cause TS-based event cameras to fail due to fluctuating velocities, wide stereo baselines, and repetitive textures, leading to unreliable SLAM performance. A robust underwater event-based SLAM system is needed.
AI.method:
- Develop a structure-aware time surface (TS) metric using structure tensor coherence and gradients to quantify TS structural information.
- Decouple TS generation into two stages: during initialization, use Bayesian Optimization to predict an optimal prior TS; during tracking, run an asynchronous online local search to obtain TS in real-time.
- Use prior disparity to improve data association and apply a latest-observation-first triangulation mechanism for stable triangulation.
- Introduce UWE, a high-quality real-world underwater event dataset with variable camera motions, complex textures, and diverse trajectories.
- Evaluate on public datasets and UWE, comparing to state-of-the-art event-based methods.
AI.result: The proposed system achieves competitive accuracy for underwater event-based stereo SLAM, outperforming or closely matching existing methods while addressing underwater-specific challenges such as variable motion and texture repetition, with robust data association and triangulation. The work also provides a new dataset (UWE) for benchmarking and plans for open-sourcing code and data.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a first high-accuracy event-based underwater stereo SLAM solution that integrates structure-aware TS generation and a two-stage optimization approach, validated on public datasets and the new UWE dataset, with the intent to release code and data to the community.

title: On-Manifold Variational Learning with Heat-Kernel Priors
summary: Learning unsupervised representations of medical imaging cohorts can reveal clinically meaningful prototypes without expert labels, which are often noisy and fail to capture true pathological heterogeneity. However, existing deep latent-variable models estimate Gaussian mixture priors via Euclidean averaging, producing prototypes that drift off the curved data manifold and degenerate as the number of sub-populations grows. We propose a manifold-anchored variational framework built on a geometry-aware Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, whose M-step selects each sub-population prototype as the graph medoid with the highest diffusion centrality on a heat-kernel-weighted latent graph, ensuring that every prototype remains on-manifold. A Dirichlet energy regularizer enforces geometric smoothness of the latent space, and a per-sub-population uncertainty score enables label-free quality assessment. \rev{The manifold-anchored EM is a general-purpose geometric tool that extends standard EM and applies readily to other latent-variable models beyond this setting.} On cardiac scar and brain MRI benchmarks, our framework attains the highest accuracy among all compared methods, produces the sharpest prototypes reported to date, and remains stable at large sub-population counts where all baselines degenerate.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware ERM variant called manifold-anchored variational EM learns on-manifold latent prototypes for medical imaging cohorts, using graph medoids with diffusion centrality on a heat-kernel latent graph, plus Dirichlet energy regularization and per-subpopulation uncertainty; achieves state-of-the-art unsupervised representations on MRI datasets.
AI.motivation: Gaussian mixture priors in latent-variable models cause prototypes to drift off the curved data manifold and degenerate as the number of sub-populations grows; a geometry-aware approach is needed to preserve manifold structure and capture heterogeneous pathology.
AI.method: A manifold-anchored variational EM framework. In the M-step, each sub-population prototype is chosen as the graph medoid with the highest diffusion centrality on a heat-kernel-weighted latent graph, keeping prototypes on-manifold. A Dirichlet energy regularizer enforces smoothness in latent space; a per-sub-population uncertainty score enables label-free quality assessment. The framework is presented as a general-purpose geometric tool extending standard EM, applicable to other latent-variable models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on cardiac scar and brain MRI benchmarks shows state-of-the-art accuracy among compared methods, the sharpest prototypes reported, and stability at high sub-population counts where competing baselines fail.
AI.conclusion: The proposed manifold-anchored EM provides a general geometry-aware extension of EM for latent-variable models, preserving manifold structure and enabling robust, interpretable prototypes; it has potential applicability beyond this specific medical-imaging setting.

title: MOLAR: Learning Multimodal Molecular Representations from Noisy Labels
summary: Motivation: Noisy labels are a common challenge in molecular property prediction because molecular annotations are often obtained from assays, curated databases, or weak annotation pipelines rather than directly observed clean biological states. Treating recorded labels as reliable supervision can cause models to memorize corrupted observations and learn misleading molecular evidence. In multimodal molecular representation learning, this issue can be amplified by graph-text fusion or alignment, which may propagate label-induced errors across modalities. Results: We propose MOLAR, a noise-aware framework for learning multimodal molecular representations from noisy labels. MOLAR separates latent clean-property inference from recorded-label observation: graph and text views contribute residual evidence to a clean-property distribution, and a categorical label-observation channel maps this distribution to recorded labels for training. This formulation derives posterior label reliability and modality-specific molecular evidence from the model. Experiments on naturally noisy molecular benchmarks and controlled label-flipping benchmarks show that MOLAR consistently outperforms representative baselines. Visualization analyses further show that MOLAR provides interpretable reliability and modality-evidence diagnostics.

AI.tldr: MOLAR is a noise-aware multimodal learning framework that disentangles clean-property inference from noisy labels in molecular data, yielding robust predictions and interpretable diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Noisy labels undermine molecular property prediction; in multimodal settings, graph-text fusion can propagate label errors across modalities, aggravating memorization of corrupted observations.
AI.method: MOLAR introduces a residual evidence model: a latent clean-property distribution inferred from graph and text views, plus a categorical label-observation channel that maps this distribution to recorded labels. It estimates posterior label reliability and modality-specific evidence.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on natural noisy benchmarks and controlled label-flipping tests shows MOLAR consistently outperforms baselines; visualizations provide interpretable reliability and modality-evidence diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: MOLAR offers a principled, noise-aware framework for learning robust multimodal molecular representations under noisy supervision, with interpretable signals about label reliability and modality contributions.

title: Beyond Safe Data: Pretraining-Stage Alignment with Regular Safety Reflection
summary: To achieve deeper safety alignment for large language models (LLMs), recent efforts have studied how to push safety interventions earlier into the pretraining stage, primarily by filtering unsafe data or rewriting it into safer forms. We argue that pretraining-stage alignment should go beyond making the data safe: LLMs may compose seemingly benign knowledge and capabilities into unsafe behaviors. To this end, we propose Safety Reflection Pretraining, a pretraining-stage alignment method which regularly inserts short safety reflections into pretraining corpora to integrate self-monitoring directly into language modeling, establishing a foundational capability that is subsequently reinforced by compatible post-training. Our experiments with 1.7B models pretrained on FineWeb-Edu show that Safety Reflection Pretraining improves safety classification accuracy and substantially reduces the success rates of inference-stage and finetuning attacks. Complementary to our real-world experiments, we also introduce a fully controlled synthetic environment, MedSafetyWorld, with a clear definition of safety and a reasoning structure under which models can easily generalize unsafe behaviors from safe data. Ablations in MedSafetyWorld further demonstrate a clear advantage of Safety Reflection Pretraining in preventing models from acting on unsafe behaviors generalized from safe data, compared with data filtering and rewriting. Taken together, our findings suggest that pretraining alignment should not only make the training data safe, but also shape the behaviors that models are likely to acquire from safe data.

AI.tldr: Safety Reflection Pretraining inserts short safety reflections during pretraining to embed self-monitoring in LLMs, aiming to prevent unsafe behaviors that arise from benign knowledge. It uses 1.7B models trained on FineWeb-Edu and a synthetic MedSafetyWorld to evaluate, showing improved safety classification and reduced attack success, outperforming data filtering or rewriting baselines.
AI.motivation: Pretraining data safety alone may not prevent unsafe generalization; models can combine benign knowledge into unsafe behaviors. A pretraining-time mechanism to shape future behavior could provide a foundational capability reinforced later.
AI.method: Regularly insert short safety reflections into pretraining corpora to instantiate self-monitoring within the language model. Evaluate on real-world-like data (1.7B models trained on FineWeb-Edu) and a controlled synthetic environment (MedSafetyWorld) with explicit safety definitions and attack-agnostic generalization structures. Compare against baselines such as data filtering and data rewriting; conduct ablations.
AI.result: Safety Reflection Pretraining improves safety classification accuracy and substantially reduces inference-stage and finetuning attack success. In MedSafetyWorld, it outperforms data filtering and rewriting in preventing unsafe behaviors generalized from safe data.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining-time alignment should shape the behaviors models acquire from safe data, not solely sanitize data; a self-monitoring foundation can be reinforced during fine-tuning or post-training for deeper safety alignment.

title: Object-Centric Residual RL for Zero-Shot Sim-to-Real VLA Enhancement
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models can generalize across diverse manipulation tasks, but their imitation-learning-based policies remain brittle in precise physical interactions due to compounding execution errors; Can a reinforcement learning policy trained purely in simulation improve the robustness of real-world VLAs zero-shot? Residual RL, which learns a corrective policy on top of a frozen VLA, offers a natural framework, but existing approaches face a fundamental sim-to-real dilemma: privileged-state methods require lossy distillation for deployment; image-based methods suffer from the visual domain gap; and real-world RL is costly and unsafe. We propose an object-centric residual RL framework that refines VLA actions using object poses, enabling a compact observation space that transfers consistently between simulation and reality. To align the two domains, we additionally replay the same teleoperation demonstrations in simulation to train a sim counterpart of the real-world VLA. The residual RL policy is trained only in simulation with pose noise injection and dropout, and transfers zero-shot to the real robot. Across five manipulation tasks on a real Franka Research 3 (FR3) robot, our method improves the success rate from 42% to 76% zero-shot, and the improved rollouts can be further reused to retrain the base VLA for self-improvement without additional teleoperation. Project page: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/articles/object-centric-residual-rl/

AI.tldr: An object-centric residual RL framework refines VLA actions via object poses, enabling zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on real robot across five tasks; achieves 42%→76% success; replayed teleoperation demonstrations align sim and real; rollouts can retrain VLA for self-improvement.
AI.motivation: Improve robustness of Vision-Language-Action models for precise physical manipulation by addressing compounding execution errors and the sim-to-real gap with a compact, object-centric observation space and simulation-only residual policy.
AI.method: Train a residual RL policy in simulation that corrects a frozen VLA using object pose observations. Align domains by replaying the same teleoperation demonstrations in simulation to create a sim VLA. Inject pose noise and dropout during training to promote transfer. Deploy zero-shot to a real FR3 robot across five tasks; use the improved rollouts to potentially retrain the base VLA.
AI.result: Zero-shot real-robot experiments show success rate improvement from 42% to 76% across five tasks. Improved rollouts can be reused to retrain the base VLA for self-improvement without additional teleoperation.
AI.conclusion: Object-centric residual RL effectively enhances VLA robustness for real-world manipulation and provides a practical sim-to-real transfer path, with dual benefits of immediate performance gains and data sharing for self-improvement.

title: LandslideAgent with Multimodal LandslideBench: A Domain-Rule-Augmented Agent for Autonomous Landslide Identification and Analysis
summary: Intelligent landslide hazard interpretation is critical for disaster prevention, yet current paradigms struggle to simultaneously extract visual features and high-level geoscientific semantics, while general-purpose vision-language models (VLMs) suffer from perceptual limitations and domain hallucinations in complex geological scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose an instruction-driven agentic framework comprising three components. First, LandslideBench, a multimodal fine-grained dataset with seven subtype labels, high-resolution imagery, pixel-level masks, and high-quality textual descriptions, is constructed via multi-VLM cross-validation and interactive annotation. Then, LandslideVLM, a landslide-oriented VLM, is fine-tuned via LoRA on LandslideBench to enhance geological semantic understanding. Finally, LandslideAgent, a domain rule-enhanced agent taking LandslideVLM as its cognitive backbone, employs a dual-rule controller incorporating structured report metadata constraints and cross-validation identification constraints to regulate automated tool invocation. Experiments demonstrate that LandslideBench provides effective baselines across five mainstream models on fine-grained classification and semantic segmentation. LandslideVLM achieves accuracy improvements of 10.96%, 32.87%, and 15.91% on landslide discrimination, fine-grained classification, and semantic description quality, respectively. LandslideAgent further enables autonomous multi-source spatial data inference, realizing full-process intelligence for landslide identification and analysis.

AI.tldr: An instruction-driven, three-tier framework for landslide hazard interpretation: (1) LandslideBench, a high-quality multimodal dataset with seven subtypes, masks, and descriptions; (2) LandslideVLM, a landslide-oriented vision-language model fine-tuned with LoRA on LandslideBench; (3) LandslideAgent, a rule-enhanced agent using LandslideVLM with a dual-rule controller to regulate tool invocation. Demonstrates strong improvements over baselines in discrimination, fine-grained classification, and semantic description, and enables autonomous multi-source spatial data inference.
AI.motivation: Current general-purpose vision-language models struggle with perceptual limitations and domain-specific semantics in complex geological scenarios. A need exists for fine-grained, multimodal datasets and domain-adapted, controllable agents to improve landslide detection, classification, and interpretation.
AI.method: Three-component framework: (1) LandslideBench — a multimodal, fine-grained dataset with seven subtype labels, high-res imagery, pixel-level masks, and textual descriptions, curated via multi-VLM cross-validation and interactive annotation. (2) LandslideVLM — a landslide-focused VLM fine-tuned via LoRA on LandslideBench to enhance geological semantics. (3) LandslideAgent — a domain-rule–enhanced agent using LandslideVLM as backbone, with a dual-rule controller enforcing structured report metadata constraints and cross-validation identification constraints to regulate automated tool invocation.
AI.result: LandslideBench provides effective baselines across five mainstream models for fine-grained classification and semantic segmentation. LandslideVLM achieves accuracy improvements of 10.96% (landslide discrimination), 32.87% (fine-grained classification), and 15.91% (semantic description quality). LandslideAgent enables autonomous multi-source spatial data inference, delivering full-process intelligence for landslide identification and analysis.
AI.conclusion: The integrated pipeline addresses perceptual and semantic gaps in landslide analysis by combining: (i) a high-quality, domain-specific dataset; (ii) a specialized, fine-tuned VLM; and (iii) an instruction-driven agent with rule-based constraints to manage tool use. The results indicate notable performance gains and autonomous capabilities, with potential for real-world disaster assessment and planning.

title: P$^2$CE: Model-Agnostic Plausible Pareto-Optimal Counterfactual Explanations
comment: Under review in the Machine Learning journal
summary: The increasing use of machine learning algorithms in social applications has raised concerns about fairness and transparency, leading to the development of counterfactual explanations. These explanations supports individuals to understand and potentially alter unfavorable decisions in areas such as loan applications, job selections, and more, by providing actionable changes to input features that would lead to a desired outcome. Existing methods often struggle to balance feasibility, plausibility, and computational efficiency. To address this, we introduce P$^2$CE, an algorithm for generating plausible Pareto-optimal counterfactual explanations, offering users a diverse set of optimal trade-offs between different notions of feasibility. P$^2$CE employs an auxiliary isolation forest outlier detector to ensure that explanations are in accordance with the data distribution and leverages SHAP values to obtain optimal results with short computing times, regardless of the underlying model. Our algorithm was empirically evaluated on three datasets, demonstrating superior performance in terms of both solution quality and computational efficiency compared to related techniques.

AI.tldr: P^2CE provides diverse, plausible, Pareto-optimal counterfactual explanations by combining an outlier-based data-distribution check with SHAP-driven optimization, achieving fast, model-agnostic results and outperforming baselines on three datasets.
AI.motivation: Address the need for fair, transparent ML decisions by supplying counterfactual explanations that are feasible, plausible, and computationally efficient, ensuring alignment with the data distribution and offering diverse trade-offs.
AI.method: Introduce P^2CE, a Pareto-optimal counterfactual explainer. It uses an auxiliary isolation forest outlier detector to enforce data-distribution feasibility and SHAP values to guide fast, model-agnostic optimization, delivering a diverse Pareto-optimal set of explanations.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three datasets shows that P^2CE achieves superior solution quality and computational efficiency compared with related methods.
AI.conclusion: P^2CE provides a practical, efficient approach for generating plausible Pareto-optimal counterfactual explanations that are aligned with the data distribution and applicable across models, delivering diverse trade-offs between feasibility notions.

title: User as Engram: Internalizing Per-User Memory as Local Parametric Edits
summary:
Personal memory in a language model is two problems: content and reasoning skill. The brain keeps the two apart (a sparse, local engram in the hippocampus for each episode, a slow neocortex for the shared skills that interpret it), so a new fact need not overwrite everything else. Most personalization today keeps a user's facts outside the weights, in a natural-language memory file or a retrieval index. When facts are written into the model instead, the standard recipe is the per-user LoRA adapter, which does the opposite of the brain, folding content and skill into one global weight delta. Writing a user's facts as a LoRA contaminates text unrelated to them; writing the same facts as local Engram rows leaves it mathematically untouched, resulting in a roughly 33,000x smaller memory footprint.
  We therefore propose User as Engram: store a user's content as surgical edits to the hash-keyed memory table of an Engram model, and carry the reasoning skill in one shared adapter. This layered design matches per-user LoRA's direct recall while delivering 5.6x higher indirect-reasoning accuracy on average, and never makes a single user worse at reasoning than the untouched base. The edit is a glass box: writing a fact switches on its lookup at exactly the trigger, adds the value the answer needs, leaves every other position unchanged to the last bit, and fails if written into the wrong layer. Because different users' facts land in disjoint hash slots, their edits compose: many users live in one shared table at once, stacking additively and losslessly, where a per-user LoRA, a single global weight delta, admits only one. Upon retrieval, a per-user Engram table does not grow with the population the retriever must search, so past ~100 facts it overtakes a retrieval pipeline on a 2.5x larger model.

AI.tldr: Proposes User as Engram: store a user's facts as surgical edits to a hash-keyed Engram memory table, while keeping a shared reasoning adapter. This yields memory-efficient, composable personalization with higher indirect-reasoning accuracy than per-user LoRA and no degradation to base reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current personalization either externalizes facts (retrieval memory) or contaminates model weights with per-user LoRA, which couples content and skill and degrades unrelated content. A brain-like separation of content (episodic memory) and reasoning skill is proposed to enable scalable, safe personalization.
AI.method: Introduce a layered architecture: per-user content written as disjoint, hash-keyed edits to an Engram memory table; a single shared adapter carries general reasoning skill. Edits are transparent (glass box) and layer-disjoint, allowing additive composition across many users. Retrieval does not grow with population, enabling scalability; compared to LoRA, it yields a memory footprint 33,000x smaller and better recall/accuracy.
AI.result: Empirical claims include ~5.6x higher indirect-reasoning accuracy on average; no deterioration in base reasoning; after ~100 facts, Engram-based retrieval outperforms a retrieval pipeline on a model 2.5x larger.
AI.conclusion: A “User as Engram” design matches the recall advantages of per-user LoRA while delivering superior precision in indirect reasoning, strong composability, and favorable scalability, making it a viable approach for scalable personalization in language models.

title: TactSpace: Learning a Physics-enriched Shared Latent Space for Tactile Sim-to-Real Transfer
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables, accepted into IROS 2026
summary: Tactile sensing provides direct measurements of contact interactions that are essential for robotic manipulation. However, current simulators lack the fidelity to faithfully model the complex deformation and transduction mechanics of tactile sensors, severely hindering sim-to-real transfer in robot learning pipelines. To address this challenge, we propose a multi-modal representation learning framework that aligns heterogeneous tactile modalities within a shared latent space, eliminating the need for accurate raw-signal simulation while preserving relevant contact information. Our approach employs modality-specific encoders to project diverse tactile observations, such as simulated penetration depth and real-world capacitance, into a common embedding space. The model is trained using self- and cross-reconstruction objectives alongside contrastive alignment, encouraging modality-invariant yet information-rich representations. We evaluate the learned embeddings on indenter shape identification, force prediction, and geometric reconstruction tasks, training exclusively in simulation and testing directly on real sensor measurements. Our results demonstrate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer across physically dissimilar representations. Furthermore, incorporating multi-physics simulation modalities yields more informative embeddings that transfer across diverse downstream tasks, demonstrating a 16.7% reduction in force prediction error and a 45.8% reduction in shape reconstruction error. Finally, we release an efficient Warp-based implementation of a penalty-based tactile simulation model for Isaac Lab, enabling scalable tactile data generation.

AI.tldr: A multi-modal tactile representation learning framework enables zero-shot sim-to-real transfer by aligning diverse tactile modalities in a shared latent space; improves force prediction and shape reconstruction, and provides a scalable tactile simulator.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between tactile sensing simulations and real sensor physics to enable effective sim-to-real transfer in robot learning.
AI.method: Use modality-specific encoders to project different tactile observations (e.g., simulated penetration depth, real-world capacitance) into a common embedding space. Train with self- and cross-reconstruction objectives plus contrastive alignment to produce modality-invariant yet information-rich representations. Evaluate embeddings on indenter shape identification, force prediction, and geometric reconstruction, training solely in simulation and testing on real sensors. Incorporate multi-physics modalities to enhance transferability across tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates zero-shot sim-to-real transfer across dissimilar representations. Quantitatively, 16.7% reduction in force prediction error and 45.8% reduction in shape reconstruction error. Also releases an efficient Warp-based tactile simulation implementation to enable scalable data generation.
