AI.conclusion: The framework yields modality-invariant, informative embeddings enabling effective sim-to-real transfer for tactile sensing tasks and provides a scalable tool for generating tactile data.

title: BrainFusionNet: a deep learning and XAI model to understand local, global, and sequential features of MRI images for improved brain tumour detection
summary: The noise of Magnetic Resonance Imaging MRI poses challenges for Deep Learning DL when tumor boundaries are obscured tumor location and appearance are complex Therefore we develop BrainFusionNet that combines Convolutional Neural Networks CNNs Vision Transformers ViT and Gated Recurrent Units GRUs to extract spatial contextual and sequential features from MRI images for improved brain tumor classification Furthermore explainable AI such as SHAP LIME and GradCAM are integrated to visualise and highlight image regions that contribute to BrainFusionNets decisionmaking process The proposed BrainFusionNet model is evaluated on two publicly available MRI datasets Kfold validation suggests 98 accuracy on both datasets The model was compared with the six stateoftheart SOTA CNNs and transfer learning Among the SOTA CNNs DenseNet121 and VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy of 96 The novelty of BrainFusionNet is that the hybrid model effectively extracts local and global features from MRI images even in smallscale tumor regions and small tumor sizes The model has a balanced sequential CNN architecture to capture lowlevel and deeperlayer features a customized ViT that captures local features stabilizes gradient flow and reduces the risk of vanishing gradients during MRI image training The CNN and ViT outputs are fed into a GRU for final classification Furthermore we analyze pixel intensities to determine whether MRI image quality affects image classification Our findings are very novel in image interpretation as we found that the distribution of pixel intensities in MRI images affects DL performance

AI.tldr: Hybrid BrainFusionNet (CNN + ViT + GRU) with SHAP/LIME/GradCAM achieves 98% accuracy on two MRI datasets, outperforming six SOTA CNNs; pixel-intensity distribution affects DL MRI classification.
AI.motivation: MRI noise and obscured tumor boundaries complicate deep learning; need architectures that capture local/global features and provide explanations.
AI.method: Develop BrainFusionNet integrating CNNs for local features, a customized Vision Transformer for contextual/global features, and a GRU to fuse outputs; incorporate SHAP, LIME, and GradCAM for explainability; evaluate with k-fold on two public MRI datasets; compare against six SOTA CNNs and transfer learning; analyze relation between pixel intensities and accuracy.
AI.result: Achieves 98% accuracy on both datasets; DenseNet121 and VGG16 reach 96% as best SOTA; model claims effective extraction of local/global features, especially for small tumors; demonstrates interpretability and suggests pixel-intensity distribution impacts performance.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid architecture effectively handles small tumor regions, provides explainability via XAI tools, and reveals image quality (pixel intensities) influences DL performance in MRI tumor classification; potential for improved clinical decision support.

title: Measurement noise limits the advantage of nonlinear models over linear models in biomedical prediction
summary: On biomedical tabular data, flexible models such as deep networks, gradient-boosted trees, and kernel methods are repeatedly matched or beaten by linear and logistic regression given the same features. The usual reaction is to treat this as a model-side shortfall, to be fixed with more data, a better architecture, or tuning, on the assumption that the nonlinear structure is there and the model has failed to capture it. We argue that these fixes cannot help when the binding limit is the measurement rather than the model, as it frequently is in biomedicine. Additive noise blurs the population-optimal predictor, and because blurring removes a function's fine, rapidly varying detail before its broad shape, it erases nonlinear structure faster than linear structure. A degree-$k$ interaction is attenuated by the $k$-th power of feature reliability, while the linear part is attenuated only once. At the reliabilities typical of biomedical measurement, the nonlinear advantage can vanish even when the underlying biology is strongly nonlinear, and what the noise removes cannot be recovered by a larger cohort or a more flexible model, only by better measurement. The nonlinearity is hidden, not absent, and a tie between linear and flexible models is not by itself a verdict on the biology. These pieces are classical, drawn from measurement-error statistics, psychometrics, and Gaussian analysis, and we assemble them into an exact excess-risk identity. Measurement reliability is one of three conditions, alongside sample size and feature representation, that must align for a flexible model to help, and together they leave only a narrow window that most biomedical tasks fall outside. Across 140 UK Biobank tasks, the gap between flexible and linear models, where it exists, carries the predicted noise signature, and the three conditions can be separated by intervention but not by a benchmark alone.

AI.tldr: Measurement noise can obscure nonlinear signals in biomedical tabular data, making linear models competitive. Flexible models help only when three conditions align: sufficient sample size, informative feature representation, and reliable measurements. Across 140 UK Biobank tasks, observed nonlinear advantages align with the predicted noise signature; improving measurement quality is essential rather than merely collecting more data or tuning models.
AI.motivation: Explain why flexible models often do not outperform linear methods in biomedicine and quantify how measurement error diminishes nonlinear structure. Provide an exact excess-risk identity showing that nonlinear terms are attenuated by the k-th power of feature reliability, while linear terms are attenuated less, making measurement reliability the bottleneck.
AI.method: Derive an exact excess-risk identity incorporating measurement reliability; quantify attenuation of k-th order interactions by the reliability to the k-th power; separate three conditions (sample size, feature representation, measurement reliability) and validate predictions on 140 UK Biobank tasks, showing the noise signature in the performance gap between flexible and linear models.
AI.result: Nonlinear advantages vanish at typical biomedical measurement reliabilities; gaps between flexible and linear models follow the predicted noise signature; increasing data or model flexibility does not recover the nonlinear signal under measurement limitations; the only remedy is improved measurement to recover the underlying nonlinear structure.
AI.conclusion: Nonlinearity is hidden by measurement noise; a tie between model classes is not a biological verdict. To leverage nonlinear structure, improve measurement reliability along with adequate sample size and informative representation; most biomedical tasks fall outside the favorable window where flexible models help.

title: TxBench-PP: Analyzing AI Agent Performance on Small-Molecule Preclinical Pharmacology
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) agents promise to accelerate drug discovery by compressing interpretation and decision-making loops, but practical deployment requires trusted evaluation on realistic program decisions. We introduce TherapeuticsBench Preclinical Pharmacology (TxBench-PP), a verifiable benchmark for small-molecule preclinical pharmacology and the first focused slice of a broader TherapeuticsBench effort across drug-discovery stages and therapeutic modalities. TxBench-PP tests whether agents can recover accurate conclusions from real-world assay data rather than memorized facts from literature. The benchmark contains 100 evaluations indexed by program stage, assay type, and task structure, spanning mechanism-of-action (MoA) and pharmacodynamic (PD) reasoning, compound-target engagement, causal target validation, developability and safety, and translational efficacy. Agents receive realistic workflow snapshots, inspect files in a coding environment, and return structured answers graded deterministically. Across 16 model-harness configurations, comprising 11 models and 4,800 trajectories, no system reliably recovered preclinical pharmacology decisions. The strongest configuration, Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi, passed 59.3\% of endpoint attempts (178/300; 95\% CI, 51.1-67.6), followed by GPT-5.5 / Pi at 55.3\% (166/300; 47.0-63.6).

AI.tldr: A verifiable benchmark (TxBench-PP) for small-molecule preclinical pharmacology, testing AI agents on 100 evaluations across MoA/PD, engagement, validation, developability, safety, and translational efficacy. It uses realistic workflows and deterministic grading across 16 model-harness configurations (11 models, 4,800 trajectories). No model reliably recovered preclinical pharmacology decisions; best models achieved ~59% endpoint success.
AI.motivation: To enable trusted, data-driven evaluation of AI agents in drug discovery by requiring conclusions to be drawn from real-world assay data rather than memorized literature, providing a realistic, testable setting across early discovery tasks.
AI.method: TxBench-PP provides 100 evaluations indexed by program stage, assay type, and task structure. Agents receive workflow snapshots, access files in a coding environment, and produce structured answers. Evaluation uses 16 model-harness configurations (11 models, 4,800 trajectories) with deterministic grading of endpoint outcomes.
AI.result: Across configurations, no system reliably recovered preclinical pharmacology decisions. The top configuration Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi achieved 178/300 endpoints (59.3%; 95% CI 51.1–67.6%), GPT-5.5 / Pi achieved 166/300 (55.3%).
AI.conclusion: The benchmark reveals a substantial gap in current AI agents’ ability to infer preclinical pharmacology from real-world data in a realistic workflow, highlighting the need for improved reasoning and generalization and providing a baseline for future drug-discovery AI evaluation.

title: Congestion-Aware Robot Tour Planning in Crowded Environments
summary: Autonomous mobile service robots are often required to complete tours that require navigating through a set of locations in an environment. Example domains include guiding people through a shopping mall, delivering packages in a fulfilment centre, or giving guided tours in a museum. However, in crowded environments, the presence of people may negatively impact robot performance. For example, humans will activate robot collision avoidance manoeuvres that slow the robot down. Crowds move stochastically and vary throughout the day. In this paper we present a probabilistic tour planner for crowded environments which explicitly reasons over human congestion. We learn circular linear flow field (CLiFF) maps which predict human trajectories given an initial observation. We then use these predictions to build and solve a Markov decision process online which efficiently routes the robot through the environment. Our approach is scalable enough to re-plan as new people are observed. We evaluate our approach on a real-world crowd dataset in a shopping mall.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic tour planner for crowded environments that predicts human trajectories with CLiFF maps and plans online via an MDP, with online replanning as new observations come in; evaluated on a real-world mall crowd dataset.
AI.motivation: Crowded environments perturb robot motion and slow performance due to collision avoidance; human crowds are stochastic and vary temporally, necessitating planning that reasons about congestion to maintain efficient routing.
AI.method: Learn circular linear flow field (CLiFF) maps to predict future human trajectories from initial observations. Use these predictions to construct and solve a Markov decision process online to route the robot. Support online replanning as new people are observed; scalable to dynamic crowds.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on a real-world shopping mall crowd dataset demonstrates the method's effectiveness (robust routing and adaptability) though the abstract does not provide numeric metrics.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, real-time planning framework for crowded environments that accounts for human congestion via predictive flow fields and online MDP planning, with continuous replanning as observations update.

title: Moving Beyond Diversity: Visual Token Pruning as Subspace Reconstruction for Efficient VLMs
summary: Despite their remarkable performance, Vision Language Models (VLMs) incur substantial computational overhead due to the large number of visual tokens. While diversity maximization has become a dominant strategy for token reduction, existing methods rely on cosine-based normalized similarity that discards magnitude information, failing to faithfully approximate the original feature representation and leading to suboptimal performance, particularly on compositional multi-skill reasoning tasks. In this paper, we introduce SPARE, a subspace reconstruction method that reformulates token pruning as a column subset selection problem and explicitly minimizes reconstruction error. By iteratively selecting tokens with large projection residuals, SPARE performs reconstruction-driven pruning beyond angular diversity. Moreover, we reveal a counterintuitive anti-relevance phenomenon: tokens with lower image-text relevance score can better preserve contextual information. Based on this finding, we incorporate anti-relevance into SPARE as an additional selection criterion to promote context-aware token selection. Extensive experiments across multiple VLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that SPARE consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, with strong gains on compositional tasks. When applied to LLaVA, SPARE removes up to 94% of visual tokens while retaining 95% of the baseline performance, all in a fully training-free manner.

AI.tldr: SPARE reframes token pruning as a subspace reconstruction and column subset selection problem, outperforming cosine-based pruning by preserving feature magnitudes and incorporating context-aware anti-relevance; achieves strong, training-free token reduction with minimal performance loss (e.g., up to 94% tokens pruned while retaining ~95% of baseline).
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models incur heavy computational overhead due to many visual tokens. Existing diversity-based pruning relies on cosine-based similarity and discards magnitude information, leading to suboptimal representations, especially for compositional, multi-skill reasoning tasks. A reconstruction-driven, context-aware approach is needed.
AI.method: Formulates token pruning as a column subset selection problem. Iteratively selects tokens with large projection residuals to minimize reconstruction error. Introduces anti-relevance as an additional criterion, capturing tokens that, despite low image-text relevance, better preserve contextual information. Applies in a fully training-free manner, demonstrated across multiple VLMs and benchmarks (notably LLaVA).
AI.result: SPARE achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse VLMs and benchmarks, with pronounced gains on compositional tasks. In LLaVA, it can remove up to 94% of visual tokens while retaining 95% of baseline performance, without training.
AI.conclusion: A reconstruction-driven, context-aware token pruning framework that overcomes magnitude loss in cosine-based pruning. By combining projection-residual-based selection with anti-relevance, SPARE delivers robust, efficient VLMs with strong performance, especially on compositional reasoning tasks.

title: Signature filtering: a lightweight enhancement for statistical watermark detection in large language models
summary: Statistical watermarks help organizations attribute large language model (LLM) outputs, yet existing detectors often struggle when watermark signals are weak, texts are repetitive, or watermarks are edited. We propose signature filtering, a detection-time module that enhances watermark detection without modifying watermark embedding and text generation. It learns a small set of ``signature'' tokens whose presence makes watermark tests unreliable, and removes these tokens before detection. The signatures are obtained by solving a mixed-integer linear program on a small training set, with constraints that maximize the true positive rate. We additionally derive finite-sample and asymptotic bounds under several attacker models (color-blind, color-adaptive, and distributionally correlated). On four well-known watermark families (Kgw, Sweet, Unigram, Exp), four benchmark corpora (C4, MBPP, HumanEval, Code-Search-Net), and six LLMs (Opt-1.3b, Opt-6.7b, Llama2-13b, Llama3.1-8b, Qwen2.5-14b, Phi-3-medium-14b), 2- and 3-gram signatures raise detection rates in weak-signal and low-entropy settings from 8~31% without filtering to 78~99% with filtering, while keeping false positives controllable and often negligible. In stress tests where we scramble sentences and perturb 25~50% of tokens by dilution, deletions, and substitutions, 2-gram filters for Kgw-style watermarks preserve most of the clean-text detection gains, often matching or outperforming the advanced WinMax watermark detector. Signature filtering thus provides a simple, scalable, and model-agnostic add-on to strengthen watermark-based provenance checks for LLM text in information processing workflows.

AI.tldr: Signature filtering is a lightweight detection-time module that learns a small set of tokens (signatures) to strip before watermark detection, substantially boosting detection rates under weak signals while remaining model-agnostic and scalable.
AI.motivation: Existing watermark detectors struggle when the watermark signal is weak, text is repetitive, or watermarks are edited; a detection-time filter that preserves embedding/generation while improving robustness is needed.
AI.method: Identify a small set of signature tokens via a mixed-integer linear program trained on a small dataset, constrained to maximize true positives; remove these tokens before watermark testing; derive finite-sample and asymptotic bounds under attacker models (color-blind, color-adaptive, distributionally correlated).
AI.result: Across four watermark families (Kgw, Sweet, Unigram, Exp), four corpora (C4, MBPP, HumanEval, Code-Search-Net), and six LLMs (Opt-1.3b, Opt-6.7b, Llama2-13b, Llama3.1-8b, Qwen2.5-14b, Phi-3-medium-14b), 2- and 3-gram signatures raise detection rates in weak-signal/low-entropy settings from 8–31% without filtering to 78–99% with filtering; false positives remain controllable and often negligible. In stress tests with sentence scrambling and 25–50% token perturbations, 2-gram filters preserve most gains and often match or outperform WinMax. Signature filtering is a simple, scalable, model-agnostic add-on to strengthen watermark-based provenance checks for LLM text.
AI.conclusion: Signature filtering provides a practical, scalable, and model-agnostic enhancement to watermark-based provenance checks, improving robustness against weak signals and edits without modifying watermark embedding or generation.

title: X+Slides: Benchmarking Audience-Conditioned Slide Generation
summary: Automatically generating slide decks from source documents is an important application of large language models (LLMs). Existing benchmarks primarily assess slide completeness and technical depth, while overlooking the target audience as a critical real-world factor. For instance, specialists demand rigorous proofs, whereas decision-makers prioritize actionable conclusions. To bridge this gap, we introduce X+Slides, a benchmark specifically designed for audience-conditioned slide generation. Built on a diverse corpus spanning 113 topics and seven presentation scenes, X+Slides employs a dynamic evaluation framework constructed from 8,133 deduplicated, source-grounded probes. By assigning audience-specific utility weights to the same source-grounded probes, X+Slides reports four complementary metrics: Audience Coverage measures how much audience-essential information is conveyed, Domain-wise Coverage shows which information types are covered, Efficiency measures delivered utility per unit of attention cost, and Correctness verifies whether slide claims are supported by the source. Experiments on DeepPresenter, SlideTailor, and NotebookLM show that current systems can recover a substantial but still incomplete part of audience-essential information: at $τ_A=0.7$, DeepPresenter reaches a best Audience Coverage of 0.714, SlideTailor reaches 0.594, and the NotebookLM ablation reaches 0.853 while showing clear grounding differences. These results indicate that visual quality and broad topic coverage should not be treated as evidence support without source-grounded evaluation.

AI.tldr: X+Slides benchmarks audience-specific slide generation using 8,133 source-grounded probes across 113 topics/7 scenes, delivering four metrics (Audience Coverage, Domain-wise Coverage, Efficiency, Correctness) and shows current systems partially capture audience-essential info, underscoring the need for audience-aware, source-grounded evaluation.
AI.motivation: Benchmarks for slide generation often neglect audience needs and source grounding; real-world utility requires tailoring content to audience type (e.g., specialists vs decision-makers) and verifying claims against sources.
AI.method: Introduce X+Slides with a diverse corpus (113 topics, 7 scenes) and a dynamic evaluation framework of 8,133 deduplicated, source-grounded probes. Assign audience-specific utility weights to probes and report four metrics. Evaluate systems DeepPresenter, SlideTailor, NotebookLM at τ_A=0.7.
AI.result: Best Audience Coverage: DeepPresenter 0.714, SlideTailor 0.594, NotebookLM ablation 0.853; observe grounding differences and incomplete recovery of audience-essential information.
AI.conclusion: Audience-conditioned, source-grounded evaluation is essential; conventional focus on visual quality or broad topic coverage misrepresents a system's utility for real-world audiences.

title: Sensor Configuration Matters: A Systematic Evaluation of Multimodal SLAM on Quadruped Robots
summary: Autonomous navigation of quadrupedal robots in diverse environments fundamentally relies on resilient Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM). While visual-inertial SLAM has matured across wheeled, handheld, and aerial platforms, a critical evaluation gap remains regarding how hardware-level sensor configurations affect performance under the aggressive dynamics of legged locomotion. Quadrupeds introduce distinct embodiment-induced sensory challenges, including foot-impact shocks, high-frequency mechanical vibrations, and rapid angular rotations, which degrade standard perception pipelines. To address this gap, we present a systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art visual, visual-inertial, and LiDAR-visual-inertial SLAM methods using the GrandTour dataset recorded on an ANYmal D quadruped. We isolate and quantify the impacts of camera modalities, shutter techniques, and inertial sensor tiers, analyzing their trade-offs across localization accuracy, algorithmic robustness, and computational resource utilization. Our empirical findings demonstrate that hardware selection has substantial influence on system resilience: stereo configurations consistently outperform monocular and RGB-D modalities, global shutter cameras significantly mitigate motion-induced tracking failures compared to rolling shutter cameras, and, crucially, standard inertial integration can degrade the performance of primarily vision-based frameworks under harsh legged locomotion. These insights additionally offer concrete design guidelines for tailoring custom sensor payloads to achieve dependable perception on agile legged systems.

AI.tldr: Hardware configuration materially affects SLAM robustness for legged robots; stereo with global shutter generally outperforms mono/RGB-D and rolling shutter; inertial data can degrade vision-dominant SLAM under aggressive locomotion; results guide sensor payload design for reliable perception on agile quadrupeds.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in understanding how embodiment-specific sensing (camera modality, shutter type, inertial sensing) influences SLAM performance under the high-dynamics of legged locomotion.
AI.method: Systematic empirical evaluation of state-of-the-art visual, visual-inertial, and LiDAR-visual-inertial SLAM methods using the GrandTour dataset collected on an ANYmal D quadruped. The study isolates effects of camera modality (stereo/mono/RGB-D), shutter technique (global vs rolling), and inertial sensor tier, examining localization accuracy, robustness, and computational load.
AI.result: Stereo configurations consistently outperform monocular and RGB-D setups. Global shutter cameras significantly reduce motion-induced tracking failures compared with rolling shutter. Standard inertial integration can degrade performance of vision-only SLAM frameworks under harsh legged locomotion.
AI.conclusion: Sensor hardware selection substantially influences SLAM resilience in agile legged systems; the work provides concrete design guidelines for tailoring sensor payloads to achieve dependable perception in quadruped locomotion.

title: Multi-Class Brain Tumor Classification Using Advanced Deep Learning Models: A Comparative Study
summary: Despite recent advancements in deep learning, accurately classifying brain tumors from MRI images continues to pose challenges. In this research, we present a comprehensive evaluation of five different convolutional neural networks (CNN) architectures, including a customized baseline model and four pre-trained models - for use in classifying multi-class brain tumors using a clinically-sourced dataset of approximately 10,000 MRI images. We have utilized five different architectures; VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, and EfficientNetB0, which were all tested and trained within an identical experimental framework. Performance was measured by both overall accuracy and tumor-wise recall as a means to measure the clinically-relevant performance of each architecture. We found that EfficientNetB0 had the best overall classification accuracy at 95%, when compared to the other architectures tested; specifically VGG16 (94.37%), VGG19 (92.29%), DenseNet121 (90.91%) and the customized CNN (78.00%). An especially important finding of our research was the considerable improvement in detecting meningiomas; specifically, while simple CNNs could detect meningiomas with a recall rate of approximately 20%, EfficientNetB0 was able to detect meningiomas with a recall rate of 89%. Meningiomas are often difficult to detect because they can appear very subtly on MRI images. Additionally, an interesting finding was that the deeper VGG19 performed worse than the shallower VGG16. This indicates that in many cases the architectural efficiency of a CNN model may be more important than its depth when working with medical images. Overall, EfficientNetB0 appears to provide the optimal trade-off between classification accuracy, number of parameters used in the model and clinically meaningful performance.

AI.tldr: EfficientNetB0 achieved the best overall accuracy (95%) for multi-class brain tumor MRI classification on a clinically sourced ~10k-image dataset, with substantial improvement in meningioma recall (89%). Deeper networks like VGG19 underperformed relative to shallower VGG16. The customized CNN performed worst. Overall, EfficientNetB0 offers the best trade-off between accuracy, parameter efficiency, and clinically meaningful performance.
AI.motivation: To systematically compare multiple CNN architectures on a clinically sourced brain-tumor MRI dataset in order to identify the most accurate and clinically relevant model for multi-class classification while considering model efficiency.
AI.method: Five architectures were evaluated under a unified experimental framework: VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and a customized baseline CNN. The dataset comprised approximately 10,000 MRI images of brain tumors. Performance was assessed using overall accuracy and tumor-wise recall as proxies for clinical relevance.
AI.result: EfficientNetB0: 95% overall accuracy; VGG16: 94.37%; VGG19: 92.29%; DenseNet121: 90.91%; customized CNN: 78.00%. Meningioma recall: simple CNNs ~20%; EfficientNetB0 ~89%. Deeper VGG19 underperformed compared to VGG16.
AI.conclusion: EfficientNetB0 provides the optimal balance between high classification accuracy, parameter efficiency, and clinically meaningful performance, particularly in detecting harder-to-see meningiomas; architectural depth alone (as in VGG19) does not guarantee better medical-image performance.

title: Beyond Prediction: Tail-Aware Scheduling for LLM Inference
summary: LLM serving exhibits extreme length variability, making size-based scheduling difficult in practice. Recent LLM schedulers approximate SJF/SRPT using predicted decode lengths or ranks and primarily report mean-centric metrics such as TTFT and TBT. We show that these prediction-driven policies can be fragile under distribution shifts, bursty arrivals, and GPU memory pressure, while offering limited control over the tail latency (P90-P99) that dominates user experience, even with perfect decode-length knowledge. We introduce a distribution-aware, prediction-free scheduling framework that replaces explicit length prediction with soft priority boosting driven by lightweight statistical signals. Our design co-optimizes scheduling and cache-aware preemption to account for memory-coupled decode dynamics across workload mixes. Evaluated on production and open-source traces, our method reduces P99 TTLT by up to 35-50% relative to SRPT with perfect length knowledge and reduces TTFT by 34-47% across workloads, including reasoning-heavy and chat-heavy tasks. These results demonstrate a robust alternative for optimizing tail latency in online LLM serving.

AI.tldr: A prediction-free, distribution-aware LLM scheduler that uses soft priority boosts from lightweight statistics and memory-aware preemption to reduce tail latency, outperforming SRPT with perfect decode-length knowledge across diverse traces.
AI.motivation: Prediction-based length estimates are brittle under distribution shifts, bursty arrivals, and memory pressure, and primarily optimize mean latency, leaving tail latency (P90-P99) poorly controlled.
AI.method: Introduce soft priority boosting driven by lightweight statistical signals instead of explicit length prediction; co-design scheduling with cache-aware preemption to reflect memory-coupled decode dynamics across workloads.
AI.result: P99 TTLT reduced by 35-50% relative to SRPT with perfect length knowledge; TTFT reduced by 34-47% across workloads, including reasoning-heavy and chat-heavy tasks; validated on production and open-source traces.
AI.conclusion: Offers a robust, prediction-free approach to tail-latency optimization in online LLM serving, with strong empirical gains and broader applicability across workload mixes.

title: NeSyCat Torch: A Differentiable Tensor Implementation of Categorical Semantics for Neurosymbolic Learning
summary:
Neurosymbolic semantics is fragmented: classical, fuzzy, probabilistic and neural systems each define truth by their own inductive rules. NeSyCat, extending ULLER, subsumes them under a single inductive definition of truth, parametric in a strong monad and an aggregation structure on truth-values. NeSyCat has so far lacked an account of predicates and functions learned by neural networks.
  We provide NeSyCat Torch as the missing link and interpret computational symbols via neural networks, implementing the framework in probabilistic programming and tensor-based backends. We use the distribution monad for reference semantics and metric evaluation, and complement it by a monad for numerically stable, differentiable training: the lazy log-tensor monad over the log-semiring. For efficient training in batches, we furthermore employ a batch monad. The axioms are the source code: written once in monad-based do-notation, monadic bind performs marginalisation, lazily pruning unneeded branches.
  On MNIST addition, our HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch implementations outperform LTN and DeepProbLog in speed and accuracy, while achieving nearly the accuracy of DeepStochLog. However, unlike DeepStochLog, we stay in a uniform framework that applies to many first-order NeSy approaches. Namely, the construction is parametric in the monad; instantiating it with, e.g., the Giry monad extends the approach to continuous probability (working out a neural representation here is left for future work).

AI.tldr: NeSyCat unifies neurosymbolic semantics through a monad-parametric truth definition, implements neural predicates/functions via Torch-backed interpreters, and demonstrates competitive MNIST results with a uniform, extensible framework; plans to extend to continuous probability via the Giry monad.
AI.motivation: Neurosymbolic semantics are fragmented across classical, fuzzy, probabilistic, and neural paradigms. A single inductive definition of truth parameterized by a monad can unify these approaches and support learned predicates/functions.
AI.method: Extend ULLER to NeSyCat, instantiate with strong monad and aggregation on truth-values. Use the distribution monad for reference semantics and a lazy log-tensor monad over the log-semiring for differentiable training; employ a batch monad for efficient training. Implement in HaskTorch/JAX/PyTorch with probabilistic programming and tensor backends. Axioms encoded in monad-based do-notation; monadic bind performs marginalisation.
AI.result: On MNIST addition, HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch implementations outperform LTN and DeepProbLog in speed and accuracy and reach near-DeepStochLog accuracy. The approach remains within a uniform, monad-parametric framework, unlike DeepStochLog which is less uniform. Extending to continuous probability via the Giry monad is noted as future work.
AI.conclusion: NeSyCat provides a uniform, extensible foundation for first-order neurosymbolic AI by parameterizing truth with monads; it accommodates neural interpretation of symbols and supports probabilistic programming. Extension to continuous probability is feasible via the Giry monad and remains for future work.

title: ReSiReg: Towards Spatially Consistent Semantics in Language-Conditioned Robotic Tasks
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) enable robots to follow open-language instructions. However, dense VLM embeddings have shown to be noisy and lack spatial consistency. This is problematic for robotic applications, which require simultaneous reasoning over semantics and 3D space. We examine spatial structure across recent VLMs and propose ReSiReg, a feature reconstruction method that uses spatially consistent VLM intermediates to improve dense language-grounded retrieval. ReSiReg clusters intermediates into visual prototypes, derives their language descriptors, and reconstructs each patch as a soft mixture of prototype-level language embeddings. We evaluate quantitatively on OVSS and 3D mapping across backbones, and qualitatively in real-world manipulation scenes. Quantitative results show improved dense retrieval; manipulation scenes show more spatially consistent target activations. We further provide a compact 25M dense VLM for robotic applications, substantially smaller than and competitive with ViT-B baselines. Available at https://resireg.github.io

AI.tldr: Proposes ReSiReg, a feature reconstruction method that uses spatially consistent VLM intermediates to improve dense language-grounded retrieval in robotics; yields a compact 25M dense VLM and shows improved performance on OVSS/3D mapping and real manipulation scenes.
AI.motivation: Dense VLM embeddings are noisy and lack spatial consistency, hindering simultaneous semantic and 3D spatial reasoning in robotic applications.
AI.method: ReSiReg clusters VLM intermediates into visual prototypes, derives language descriptors for prototypes, and reconstructs each image patch as a soft mixture of prototype-level language embeddings to achieve spatially consistent dense retrieval across backbones.
AI.result: Quantitatively, improved dense retrieval on OVSS and 3D mapping; qualitatively, more spatially consistent target activations in real-world manipulation scenes; provides a compact 25M dense VLM, smaller than ViT-B baselines.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging spatially structured VLM intermediates via prototype-based language reconstruction yields more spatially coherent language-grounded perception in robotics and delivers an efficient, competitive dense VLM suitable for real-world tasks.

title: Spatially Stratified Distillation for Heterogeneous Radar Place Recognition
comment: IEEE ICRA Workshop on Open Challenges for Rigorous Robot Perception 2026
summary: Scalable, all-weather place recognition increasingly relies on heterogeneous radar place recognition to bridge diverse hardware platforms. A notable application is matching queries from cost-effective 4D automotive radars against high-fidelity reference maps built by dense spinning radars. This process is fundamentally limited by the extreme sparsity (and narrow field-of-view) of the 4D sensor, which captures only a fraction of the structural density present in the spinning radar database. Prior efforts address this issue by unifying different radar signals. That is, projecting both signals into a common representational space. Yet, they suffer performance degradation in multi-session environments. In this paper, we propose spatially-stratified distillation (SSD); a strategy that replaces standard uniform distillation with an asymmetric spatial alignment derived directly from physical radar returns. In regions where both radars exhibit overlapping returns, SSD enforces strong feature alignment. Crucially, in sparse regions where the 4D student lacks returns but the teacher contains valid structure within the shared field of view, SSD applies heavily discounted distillation weights. Extensive evaluations of the recent HeRCULES dataset demonstrate that SSD significantly outperforms prior place recognition methods, achieving state-of-the-art results on its challenging dynamic sequences.

AI.tldr: SSD (spatially-stratified distillation) for heterogeneous radar place recognition. It uses asymmetric spatial alignment based on actual radar returns to align features in overlapping regions and applies discounted distillation in sparse regions, achieving state-of-the-art on HeRCULES.
AI.motivation: Address the adverse effect of extreme sparsity and narrow FOV of 4D automotive radars when matching against dense spinning-radar maps, which degrades performance of uniform distillation and signal-unification approaches, especially across multiple sessions.
AI.method: Introduce SSD: replace uniform distillation with asymmetric spatial alignment derived from physical radar returns. In overlapping regions, enforce strong feature alignment; in sparse regions where the 4D sensor lacks returns but the teacher has structure, apply heavily discounted distillation weights.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on the HeRCULES dataset show that SSD outperforms prior place recognition methods and achieves state-of-the-art results on challenging dynamic sequences.
AI.conclusion: SSD provides a targeted distillation strategy that leverages spatial overlap and sparsity-aware weighting to improve heterogeneous radar place recognition across sessions and dynamic environments.

title: TMR-GGNN: Credit Card Fraud Detection based on Time-Aware Multi-Relational Guided Graph Neural Network
comment: 2025 2nd International Conference on Software, Systems and Information Technology (SSITCON), Pages 7
summary: In recent years, credit card fraud detection has faced significant challenges due to highly imbalanced data, evolving fraud patterns, and complex relational structures among transaction entities. To address these issues, this research proposes a novel framework called Timeaware Multi Relational Guided Graph Neural Network (TMR GGNN). Particularly, the proposed TMR GGNN extends the encoder decoder Graph Neural Network GNN architecture by modeling heterogeneous interactions across customers, merchants, devices, and IPs over temporal windows. Subsequently, the proposed TMR GGNN approach constructs a dynamic, multi relational graph and incorporates a time aware relational attention mechanism within the encoder to adaptively weigh the transaction relevance based on temporal proximity and semantic context. Consequently, the decoder employs a contrastive learning module to distinguish between real and synthesized transaction patterns, while improving the models generalization of rare fraud cases. Additionally, to effectively manage severe class imbalances and emphasize discriminative learning, a composite loss function combining Information Noise Contrastive Estimation (InfoNCE) based contrastive loss with Focal Loss is introduced. This integration assists in improving fraud identification while mitigating false negatives.

AI.tldr: Proposes Timeaware Multi Relational Guided Graph Neural Network (TMR GGNN) for credit card fraud detection using time-aware, multi-relational GNN with contrastive learning and a hybrid loss to handle imbalanced data.
AI.motivation: Address significant challenges in fraud detection due to highly imbalanced data, evolving fraud patterns, and complex relational structures among transactions (customers, merchants, devices, IPs). Leverage temporal context and heterogeneous relations to improve detection and generalization to rare fraud cases.
AI.method: Extend encoder-decoder GNN architecture to model heterogeneous interactions among customers, merchants, devices, and IPs within temporal windows; construct a dynamic, multi-relational graph; apply time-aware relational attention in the encoder to weight transactions by temporal proximity and semantic context; decoder uses a contrastive learning module to distinguish real vs. synthesized patterns; introduce a composite loss combining InfoNCE-based contrastive loss with Focal Loss to address severe class imbalance and improve discrimination of fraud.
AI.result: Not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The framework aims to improve fraud detection performance on imbalanced data, mitigate false negatives, and enhance generalization for rare fraud patterns through temporal relational modeling, contrastive learning, and a hybrid loss.

title: Rethinking Reward Supervision: Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation
summary: Post-training of reasoning language models is commonly driven by supervised distillation and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. Distillation often relies on chain-of-thought annotations that are expensive to obtain and may themselves be noisy, incomplete, or partially incorrect; even when the final solution is correct, an imperfect rationale can interfere with learning. Reinforcement learning with verified rewards, on the other hand, typically compresses evaluative feedback into a scalar signal, obscuring which aspects of a response should be improved. We propose \textbf{Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation}, a framework that incorporates rubrics as structured, fine-grained feedback for on-policy self-distillation. Our method conditions the teacher model on criterion-level rubrics and uses it to provide token-level guidance on the student's own sampled trajectories. This design avoids treating a single reference rationale as the sole supervision target. Instead, rubrics specify what a strong response should satisfy, enabling more fine-grained credit assignment over the reasoning process than scalar reward optimization. We instantiate this framework with a two-stage pipeline that first learns to generate task-specific rubrics and then trains a rubric-guided reasoner. We evaluate on a diverse suite of science reasoning benchmarks and results show that rubric-conditioned self-distillation effectively converts rubric-level criteria into token-level guidance over the reasoning process, surpassing GRPO by 1.0 points and OPSD by 0.9 points on average.

AI.tldr: A rubric-conditioned self-distillation framework uses rubric-guided token-level feedback to improve reasoning in language models, bypassing single-reference opinions and enabling fine-grained credit assignment; two-stage pipeline learns task rubrics then trains rubric-guided reasoners, outperforming baselines on science reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of supervised chain-of-thought distillation (expensive/noisy rationales) and reinforcement learning with scalar rewards (loss of actionable guidance). Provide fine-grained, interpretable feedback to improve reasoning tokens.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) learn task-specific rubrics (criterion-level) and (2) train a rubric-guided reasoner via on-policy self-distillation where a teacher model, conditioned on rubrics, provides token-level guidance to the student’s sampled reasoning trajectories. This avoids relying on a single reference rationale and enables detailed credit assignment over the reasoning process.
AI.result: Empirical results on diverse science reasoning benchmarks show rubric-conditioned self-distillation better transfers rubric-level criteria into token-level guidance, achieving average gains of 1.0 points over GRPO and 0.9 points over OPSD.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-conditioned self-distillation is an effective mechanism to convert high-level rubric criteria into actionable, token-level guidance for reasoning in LMs, improving performance over existing distilled or scalar-reward methods.

title: ART-VS: Adaptive Resolution Tiling for Vision Transformer Visual Servoing
summary: Visual servoing with self-supervised Vision Transformer (ViT) features enables training-free robotic positioning with strong generalization, but faces a fundamental trade-off between robustness and precision. Coarse patch-level descriptors provide stable correspondences yet limit positioning accuracy. Increasing image resolution improves precision but yields only marginal robustness gains - under perturbation, high-resolution processing improves convergence success rate from 76.6% to just 81.0% despite 12x more ViT patches. Therefore, we propose Adaptive Resolution Tiling Visual Servoing (ART-VS), a two-phase method that adapts feature granularity to servoing progress: a coarse phase at native ViT resolution for stable alignment, then a tiled high-resolution phase that restricts matching to local neighborhoods improving positioning accuracy. Without any task-specific training, ART-VS achieves 95.4% convergence under perturbation, outperforming standard and full-resolution ViT-based servoing by 18.8 and 14.4 percentage points. Over the former it reduces positioning error by 53%, while running at over 10x higher speed and 27% lower VRAM than the latter. We validate ART-VS across three ViT backbones and demonstrate real-world category-level grasping of unseen object instances, achieving 95/100 on transparent bottles and 98/100 on shoes. Code available under https://art-vs.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ART-VS introduces adaptive resolution tiling for ViT-based visual servoing, enabling training-free robust and precise robot positioning. It uses a two-phase coarse-to-fine approach that significantly improves convergence under perturbations, with efficiency gains, validated across multiple backbones and unseen objects.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised Vision Transformer (ViT) features enable  training-free robotic positioning but face a robustness-precision trade-off: coarse patch descriptors are robust but imprecise; high-resolution processing improves precision but yields limited robustness gains. An adaptive scheme should tune feature granularity during servoing progress.
AI.method: Adaptive Resolution Tiling Visual Servoing (ART-VS): a two-phase approach. Phase 1 uses native ViT resolution for stable coarse alignment; Phase 2 uses tiled, high-resolution processing restricted to local neighborhoods to refine pose with higher accuracy. No task-specific training is required. Evaluations span three ViT backbones and real-world category-level grasping of unseen objects.
AI.result: Convergence under perturbation of 95.4%, outperforming standard and full-resolution ViT-based servoing by 18.8 and 14.4 percentage points respectively. Positioning error reduced by 53% compared to standard, with >10x speed and 27% lower VRAM than full-resolution. Validated on unseen object instances: 95/100 for transparent bottles, 98/100 for shoes.
AI.conclusion: ART-VS delivers robust and precise training-free visual servoing, achieving higher convergence, faster operation, and lower memory usage, with strong generalization across backbones and unseen objects.

title: UniTemp: Unlocking Video Generation in Any Temporal Order via Bidirectional Distillation
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models have emerged as a promising approach for long video generation, achieving strong performance in streaming settings. However, existing methods are restricted to forward temporal generation, whereas practical video creation often requires flexible generation order, e.g., conditioning on future context to extend backward, or on both past and future context for inbetween generation. We bridge this gap by training an autoregressive model that supports generation in arbitrary temporal directions. A key technical challenge arises from the Causal 3D VAE widely used in video diffusion models, which encodes latents strictly conditioned on past context. While suited for forward generation, this causal structure causes inter-block discontinuities when generation proceeds backward. To address this, we introduce blockwise anchor latents, a set of auxiliary latents that restore the missing past context at block boundaries during backward generation. Built on this design, we propose UniTemp, a bidirectional distillation framework that trains a single autoregressive student model for any-direction video generation. At inference time, UniTemp conditions on arbitrary past and/or future frames, improving controllability for both bidirectional and inbetween generation. Experiments show that UniTemp maintains competitive performance on short and long video generation compared to forward-only methods, while enabling diverse workflows such as bidirectional video extension, inbetween generation, looping video generation, scene transition, and visual story generation. Project website: https://lzhangbj.github.io/projects/unitemp/

AI.tldr: UniTemp enables bidirectional and inbetween video generation for autoregressive diffusion models by using blockwise anchor latents and a single student model trained via bidirectional distillation, achieving competitive quality while allowing arbitrary temporal conditioning.
AI.motivation: Long video generation requires flexible generation orders (past, future, or mixed); existing forward-only autoregressive methods struggle with controllability and continuity when generation proceeds backward due to causal latent conditioning (Causal 3D VAE).
AI.method: Introduce blockwise anchor latents to restore missing past context at block boundaries during backward generation. Propose UniTemp, a bidirectional distillation framework that trains a single autoregressive student model capable of any-direction video generation. At inference, condition on arbitrary past and/or future frames.
AI.result: UniTemp delivers competitive performance for both short and long video generation compared to forward-only methods while enabling diverse workflows: bidirectional video extension, inbetween generation, looping, scene transitions, and visual story generation.
AI.conclusion: A single distillation-based autoregressive model can support any-direction video generation with blockwise anchors, significantly improving controllability and enabling flexible workflows without sacrificing performance.

title: A Cross-Model VLM-Judge Protocol for Single-Image 3D Mesh Quality (and Why Cheap Proxies Fall Short)
summary: Single-image-to-3D generators are improving quickly, but there is no agreed, human-free way to tell whether one generated mesh is better than another. Practitioners commonly rely on cheap automatic proxies (render-space CLIP similarity and mesh geometry-validity statistics), yet how well these track perceived quality is unestablished. We make two contributions. First, we propose and validate a reproducible VLM-judge evaluation protocol: a fixed 24-view headless render rig, two independent vision-language judge families, and a mandatory position-bias correction that queries both presentation orders and keeps only order-consistent verdicts. The two judge families agree substantially with each other (Cohen's kappa = 0.66), well above the chance-agreement floor. Second, using this protocol as the reference, we show the cheap proxies do not substitute for it. Geometry validity is only a weak signal on average (because, as we show, it is bimodal) and stays below our pre-registered target, while render-CLIP is at chance. A learned Bradley-Terry head collapses onto a single manifoldness statistic (giving render-CLIP a negative weight) and matches geometry-only exactly, so learning the feature weights buys nothing. The proxy is also bimodal: it is significantly above chance on contrasts with visible geometric defects but at chance on ambiguous contrasts, consistent with geometry validity tracking the judge only when the defect is visually salient. We therefore recommend the VLM-judge protocol as a reliable, reproducible evaluator under the conditions tested (two feed-forward generators on Google Scanned Objects, with a face-drop degradation regime) and advise against geometry/CLIP proxies as optimization targets.

AI.tldr: A reproducible VLM-based evaluation protocol for single-image-to-3D meshes reveals cheap proxies (geometry validity, render-CLIP) fail to predict perceived quality; two independent VLM judgments show substantial agreement, while geometry/CLIP proxies perform poorly and learning weights provide no benefit.
AI.motivation: To establish a human-free, reproducible, and scalable measure of perceived 3D mesh quality for SIG(−to−3D) generators, addressing the unreliability of common automatic proxies.
AI.method: Fixed 24-view headless render rig; two independent vision‑language judge families; mandatory position-bias correction by sampling both orders and retaining only order-consistent verdicts; evaluate consistency (Cohen's kappa) and proxy correlations; compare against a learned Bradley–Terry head and standard proxies (render-CLIP, geometry validity).
AI.result: Two judge families agree substantially (Cohen's kappa ≈ 0.66). Proxies generally fail: geometry validity is bimodal and weak on average; render-CLIP performs at chance. The learned BT head collapses to a single manifoldness metric, effectively matching geometry-only performance and giving render-CLIP a negative weight. Proxy performance is bimodal—effective on visually salient defects, but at chance on ambiguous cases. Recommendation: adopt the VLM-judge protocol as a reliable evaluator under tested conditions (Google Scanned Objects, two feed-forward generators, face-drop degradation) and avoid geometry/CLIP proxies as optimization targets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed VLM-judge protocol provides a robust, reproducible human-aligned evaluation for S-I to 3D mesh generation; relying on geometry validity or CLIP proxies for optimization is not advised, as they do not generalize to perceived quality under the tested conditions.

title: GCNGrasp-VP: Affordance-Guided View Planning for Efficient Task-Oriented Grasping
summary: Task-oriented grasping performance degrades significantly when object views suffer from occlusions. Existing task-oriented grasping methods typically assume task-relevant regions are visible in the initial frame, while view planning approaches enable active perception but often ignore task semantics and rely on time-consuming scene reconstruction. To address these limitations, we present GCNGrasp-VP, an efficient framework integrating affordance field prediction with active view planning. Central to this framework is GCNGrasp-v2, a task-oriented grasp model that simultaneously supports grasp evaluation and affordance field prediction, achieving constant-time inference complexity. Leveraging this capability, our Affordance-guided View Planner (Affordance-VP) utilizes the affordance field as an information gain metric to guide camera observation of task-relevant regions without requiring scene reconstruction. View planning results show that our method significantly outperforms scene-uncertainty-driven baselines with only one view adjustment. Real-world validation further confirms substantial improvements in grasp success rates for single-object scenarios while maintaining millisecond-level computational latency. Code and models are available at https://github.com/Instinct323/GCNGrasp-VP.

AI.tldr: GCNGrasp-VP combines an efficient task-oriented grasp model (GCNGrasp-v2) with an affordance-guided view planner to enable active perception for occluded, task-focused grasping; achieves constant-time inference and millisecond latency with real-world validation.
AI.motivation: Occlusions degrade task-oriented grasping; prior work either assumes visibility or uses costly scene reconstruction; need fast, task-aware active perception.
AI.method: GCNGrasp-v2 performs grasp evaluation and affordance field prediction with constant-time inference; Affordance-VP uses the affordance field as an information gain metric to guide a single-view adjustment without reconstructing the scene.
AI.result: View planning outperforms scene-uncertainty baselines with one view; real-world tests show improved grasp success under single-object scenarios; latency remains millisecond-scale; code released.
AI.conclusion: Approach enables efficient, semantics-aware active perception for robust task-oriented grasping under occlusion, suitable for real-time deployment.

title: PEFT-MedSAM: Efficient Fine-Tuning of Medical Foundation Models for Explainable Skin Lesion Segmentation
summary: Automated segmentation of skin lesions using deep learning models for dermoscopic images can be very helpful in finding melanomas earlier than they would normally be detected. However, most deep learning methods available do not perform well. The aim of this paper is to present a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method called PEFT-MedSAM for adapting the Medical Segment Anything Model (MedSAM) to automatically segment dermoscopic skin lesions. The PEFT-MedSAM method uses only the lightweight mask decoder for training the model while keeping the pre-trained image encoder and prompt encoder frozen. The experiments performed on the ISIC 2018 benchmark dataset shows that PEFT-MedSAM obtains a dice coefficient of .9411 and an intersection over union value of .8918 when compared to both a fully trained U-Net baseline (.8715 dice coefficient) and zero-shot MedSAM inference (.8997 dice coefficient). The external validation of the model using PH2 dataset shows .9467 dice coefficient with +/- .0310 standard deviation. Supportive evidence for these claims include a p-value less than .0001 for Wilcoxon signed rank tests comparing the two datasets and bootstrap-estimated 95% confidence intervals of [.9364,.9447] that represent the estimated range of possible values for the average dice coefficient obtained by repeating the test. To increase clinical trustworthiness, we used Grad-CAM explainability along with a pointing game based evaluation methodology to evaluate the CNN baseline model on the validation set. The results showed that we had an accuracy rate of 98.27% on the validation set of 519 images and confirmed that the model classified regions containing skin lesions.

AI.tldr: Introduces PEFT-MedSAM, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning approach for dermoscopic skin lesion segmentation that trains only the mask decoder of MedSAM (encoder and prompt encoder frozen), achieving state-of-the-art Dice/IU on ISIC 2018 and strong external validation on PH2, with explainability and a CNN baseline evaluation.
AI.motivation: Improve segmentation performance and clinical trustworthiness while reducing training cost by adapting an existing segmentation foundation model to dermatology tasks.
AI.method: Freeze image encoder and prompt encoder; train only the lightweight mask decoder; use Grad-CAM and pointing game for explainability; evaluation on ISIC 2018 and PH2; statistical tests: Wilcoxon signed-rank test and bootstrap CIs.
AI.result: Dice 0.9411, IoU 0.8918 on ISIC 2018 vs U-Net 0.8715 and zero-shot MedSAM 0.8997; external Dice 0.9467 ± 0.0310; p<0.0001; bootstrap 95% CI [0.9364, 0.9447]; CNN baseline accuracy 98.27% on 519 images; Grad-CAM validated.
AI.conclusion: PEFT-MedSAM demonstrates competitive segmentation performance with efficient fine-tuning and added explainability, supporting potential clinical deployment; limitations include reliance on a pre-trained foundation model and limited scope of external validation.

title: Veriphi: Attack-Guided Neural Network Verification with Dataset-Dependent Training Methods
summary: We present Veriphi, a GPU-accelerated neural network verification system that combines fast adversarial attacks with formal bound certification using alpha,beta-CROWN methods. Through systematic experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 using three training methodologies (standard, adversarial, certified), we demonstrate that training method effectiveness is fundamentally dataset-dependent. Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) achieves 78% certified accuracy on simple MNIST (784 dimensions) but provides negligible certification performance on the more complex CIFAR-10 dataset, where PGD adversarial training dominates with 94% certification at small perturbations. We achieve 5x verification speedup through attack-guided falsification and scale our approach to production-size models (105.8M parameters) for real-world aerospace logistics optimization. Our results challenge the assumption that certified training universally outperforms adversarial training, showing context matters critically for verification strategy selection.

AI.tldr: Veriphi fuses GPU-accelerated verification with attack-guided falsification and alpha,beta-CROWN bounds; demonstrates dataset-dependent training efficacy; 5x speedups; scalable to large models; challenges universal superiority of certified training.
AI.motivation: To reconcile conflicting performance claims in certified vs adversarial training; to improve verification efficiency; to enable real-world deployment with large models; to understand dataset impact on certification strategies.
AI.method: Integrate fast adversarial attacks with formal bound certification (alpha,beta-CROWN); compare IBP and alpha,beta-CROWN; evaluate on MNIST/CIFAR-10 with standard/adversarial/certified training; apply attack-guided falsification to speed up verification; scale to 105.8M-parameter models; demonstrate aerospace logistics optimization.
AI.result: 5x speedup; MNIST IBP certified accuracy 78%; CIFAR-10 IBP negligible; PGD adversarial training yields 94% certification for small perturbations; production-scale model verification; context matters for verification strategy.
AI.conclusion: Certified training does not universally outperform adversarial training; dataset and task context dictate which strategy is best; combining attacks and certification can yield practical gains; implications for selecting verification strategies in real-world applications; future work to explore other datasets, perturbation regimes, and scaling behaviors.

title: Monocular 3D Occupancy Perception for Robots on Sidewalks via Hybrid 2D-3D Learning
summary: Sidewalks in the real world are crowded, cluttered, and less structured than roads, making 3D occupancy prediction a key ingredient for the safe navigation of mobile robots such as delivery bots and electric wheelchairs. Existing occupancy learning pipelines are largely designed for on-road autonomous driving and often train on large-scale paired LiDAR-RGB datasets with dense 3D supervision and multiple camera inputs, which are costly to collect and do not adequately capture sidewalk-specific characteristics. We propose WalkOCC, a hybrid Ray-marching monocular 3D occupancy perception framework for robots operating on sidewalks. WalkOCC explicitly couples geometric grounding from LiDAR-RGB paired data with scalable learning from large-scale unpaired monocular images. It bootstraps pseudo occupancy supervision from paired sequences and jointly learns image-level representations on additional 2D-only data. It yields stable optimization and improved generalization without requiring costly 3D occupancy annotations. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent gains in prediction accuracy, fine-grained segmentation of subtle urban structures such as curbs and gutters, and robustness to environmental and cross-embodiment shifts compared with self-supervised image-based baselines. To facilitate evaluation and benchmarking, we also introduce Sidewalk3D, a large-scale sidewalk perception dataset with LiDAR-camera paired sequences collected across multiple locations and time periods, along with 3D semantic occupancy annotations for evaluation. Code and data will be made available.

AI.tldr: WalkOCC: a Ray-marching monocular 3D occupancy perception framework for sidewalks that leverages LiDAR-RGB grounding with unpaired monocular data; introduces Sidewalk3D dataset; improves 3D occupancy prediction and segmentation without dense 3D labels.
AI.motivation: Real-world sidewalks are crowded and less structured than roads, making 3D occupancy prediction difficult. Existing on-road pipelines rely on dense 3D supervision and multi-camera data, which are costly and don’t capture sidewalk-specific characteristics.
AI.method: WalkOCC is a hybrid Ray-marching monocular 3D occupancy framework that couples geometric grounding from LiDAR-RGB paired data with scalable learning from large-scale unpaired monocular images. It bootstraps pseudo occupancy supervision from paired sequences and jointly learns image-level representations on additional 2D-only data, enabling stable optimization and better generalization without dense 3D occupancy annotations.
AI.result: The approach yields consistent gains in 3D occupancy prediction accuracy and fine-grained segmentation of sidewalk structures (e.g., curbs and gutters), and shows robustness to environmental and cross-embodiment shifts compared to self-supervised image-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Sidewalk3D, a large-scale sidewalk perception dataset with LiDAR-camera paired sequences and 3D semantic occupancy annotations, is introduced to facilitate evaluation. Code and data will be released, supporting evaluation and benchmarking of sidewalk occupancy perception.

title: Rethinking the Pointer Loss in Table Structure Recognition: Geometry-Aware Pointer Loss for Spatial Locality
summary: Table Structure Recognition (TSR) using a pointer network achieves impressive results by predicting HTML sequences while aligning tags to detected text (or cell) regions. However, our analysis reveals that when pointer networks fail, 79.6% of errors occur between spatially adjacent cells (Manhattan distance <= 2). Despite this, standard cross-entropy loss weights all negative candidates equally. In this work, we propose Geometry-Aware Pointer (GAP) Loss, which reweights the cross-entropy objective based on spatial proximity to ground truth. By applying inverse distance weighting, GAP focuses gradient flow where the model struggles most: immediate neighbors receive stronger gradients than distant cells. Our approach requires only a straightforward modification to the loss computation, maintaining the same model architecture with zero additional inference cost. Extensive experiments on PubTabNet and SynthTabNet demonstrate that GAP consistently reduces adjacent-cell errors, achieving new state-of-the-art performance. Our findings suggest that incorporating geometric inductive biases at the loss level provides a simple yet effective approach to robust TSR. Our code is available at https://github.com/teamreboott/GAP

AI.tldr: GAP Loss reweights cross-entropy by inverse distance to ground-truth cells to emphasize learning on spatially proximate (adjacent) cells, reducing adjacent-cell errors with no inference cost and achieving state-of-the-art TSR on PubTabNet and SynthTabNet.
AI.motivation: Pointer-based TSR often confuses a predicted cell with spatially neighboring cells because all negatives are weighted equally by standard cross-entropy. The majority of errors occur among adjacent cells, so incorporating a geometric bias into the loss could direct learning to differentiate near neighbors more effectively.
AI.method: Introduce Geometry-Aware Pointer (GAP) Loss by weighting each candidate's cross-entropy loss with a factor inverse to its distance to the ground-truth cell (e.g., Manhattan distance). This reweighting concentrates gradient flow on immediate neighbors (distance small) and attenuates distant negatives, while keeping the model architecture and inference unchanged.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on PubTabNet and SynthTabNet shows reduced adjacent-cell errors and improved overall performance, achieving new state-of-the-art results for TSR with the same model and training regime. The approach requires only a loss-weights modification and negligible computational overhead at inference.
AI.conclusion: A simple geometric inductive bias at the loss level can robustly improve table structure recognition by focusing learning on challenging near-neighbor distinctions, suggesting broader applicability to other structured prediction tasks with strong spatial relations. The authors provide code at the referenced repository.

title: Task-Restricted Symmetries in Recurrent Weight Space
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Weight-Space Symmetries
summary:
Recurrent networks can contain substantial functional redundancy in
  weight space: changing a recurrent matrix may leave the input-output
  rollout nearly unchanged on a task distribution, while similar-scale
  changes can destroy the same behavior. We study this redundancy in
  one-layer tanh RNNs using ordered real Schur coordinates. The Schur
  form separates spectral blocks from directed nonnormal couplings,
  giving a diagnostic basis for structured ablations that keep the input
  and readout maps fixed. In a fixed-length copy task, selected
  nonnormal Schur couplings can be removed with little loss in some
  trained solutions, whereas other couplings are necessary for accurate
  autonomous replay. Across flip-flop, sine generation, and
  context-dependent integration, the loss-preserving ablation profile
  varies across tasks and trained solutions. These results identify
  candidate approximate functional invariances, not universal symmetries
  of recurrent weight space. Schur-coordinate ablations provide a
  practical diagnostic for which structured perturbations preserve a
  trained recurrent solution and which ones disrupt its computation.

AI.tldr: RNN weight-space redundancy revealed via Schur-based structured ablations: many nonnormal couplings can be removed without harming some trained solutions, but others are essential; ablation patterns are task- and solution-dependent.
AI.motivation: To understand how recurrent networks maintain input-output behavior under weight perturbations, and to identify invariances in the weight space that could explain robustness or guide pruning.
AI.method: Analyze one-layer tanh RNNs using ordered real Schur decomposition; create ablations that alter Schur couplings while keeping input and readout fixed; evaluate on fixed-length copy task and other tasks (flip-flop, sine generation, context-dependent integration).
AI.result: Certain nonnormal Schur couplings can be removed with minimal loss for some trained solutions, while other couplings are required for autonomous replay; the ablation profile varies across tasks and solutions; invariances are approximate and not universal; Schur-coordinate ablations provide a diagnostic tool.
AI.conclusion: Schur-coordinate perturbations are a practical diagnostic for preserving vs. disrupting recurrent computation; they reveal candidate invariances but caution against universal claims; supports targeted perturbations as a way to understand and potentially compress or regularize RNNs.

title: Viking Hill Dataset: A Lidar-Radar-Camera Dataset for Detection and Segmentation in Forest Scenes
summary: Autonomous robots operating under forest canopies need robust perception of trees and surrounding vegetation across varying seasonal conditions. Existing forestry datasets provide lidar or camera data with per-tree annotations, but none include co-registered 4D imaging radar -- a modality of growing interest for its resilience to visual degradation, surface contamination, and vegetation occlusion. We introduce a multi-sensor forest dataset collected by a mobile robot equipped with a high-resolution FMCW imaging radar, lidar, RGB camera, IMU, and RTK-GNSS. The site was recorded in two sessions under contrasting vegetation states, and 3D cuboid annotations -- including per-tree diameter estimates -- provide shared semantic labels across all three perception modalities. Furthermore, we provide baseline results for semantic segmentation of the radar and lidar point clouds using MinkowskiUNet. Radar achieves IoU scores competitive with lidar for dominant classes (ground 91%, canopy 86%) while lagging on geometrically fine structures such as tree trunks (56% vs. 74%). A cross-modality analysis further compares lidar and radar trunk segmentation against an RGB detection model, and a diameter-stratified evaluation reveals how trunk segmentation quality varies with tree size. Beyond segmentation, the co-registered multi-modal data and RTK-GNSS-aided reference positioning support research in mapping, localization, and sensor fusion under canopy. The dataset and annotation tools are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces a multi-sensor forest dataset with co-registered 4D imaging radar, lidar, RGB, IMU, and RTK-GNSS; provides 3D cuboid per-tree annotations and diameter estimates; baseline semantic segmentation shows radar competitive with lidar on dominant classes but weaker on trunks; cross-modality and diameter-stratified analyses; dataset supports mapping/localization/sensor fusion under canopy; data/tools publicly available.
AI.motivation: To enable robust perception for forestry robots under canopy and across seasonal variations, addressing the lack of datasets that include co-registered 4D imaging radar data for multi-modal perception and fusion.
AI.method: Mobile robot equipped with high-resolution FMCW imaging radar, lidar, RGB camera, IMU, and RTK-GNSS collected data in two sessions with contrasting vegetation states. Provided 3D cuboid tree annotations including per-tree diameter estimates, shared semantic labels across radar, lidar, and RGB. Baselines using MinkowskiUNet for radar and lidar segmentation. Cross-modality analysis compared trunk segmentation across modalities and RGB detection. Diameter-stratified evaluation examined trunk segmentation quality by tree size. Public dataset and annotation tools.
AI.result: Radar IoU for dominant classes: ground 91%, canopy 86% (comparable to lidar). Trunk segmentation weaker: radar 56% vs lidar 74%. Cross-modality analysis aligned radar/lidar trunk results with RGB detections. Diameter-stratified results show trunk segmentation quality varies with tree size. The data support improvements in mapping, localization, and sensor fusion under canopy.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and annotation tools are publicly available, enabling further research in robust, multi-modal forest perception, including mapping and fusion under challenging canopy conditions using co-registered radar, lidar, and vision data.

title: Clinically Aligned Geometry Constraints for Robust IVUS Vessel Boundary Segmentation
summary: Intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) lumen and external elastic membrane (EEM) segmentation is important for quantitative coronary plaque burden assessment. Errors in lumen or EEM delineation directly propagate to plaque area, plaque burden and geometric measurements. However, standard methods prioritising overlap scores often suffer from boundary drift and topology errors, leading to inaccurate clinical measurements. We present GeoCat, a geometry-consistent network that processes 5-frame IVUS clips using dual Cartesian-polar encoders with cross-domain attention and temporal fusion. A differentiable geometry consistency loss directly supervises clinically relevant descriptors including diameters, orientations, and cross-sectional areas. The model is trained on 12,242 annotated frames from 146 patients acquired with two commercial IVUS systems. We evaluate performance using both segmentation accuracy and plaque-relevant clinical metrics, including Dice/IoU, boundary measures(95HD (mm), ASSD), topology violation rate, and clinical geometry errors (dmax/dmin, angles, and areas). On our dataset, GeoCat achieves a Dice of 0.93, reduces 95HD to 0.14 mm, and lowers topology violations to 1.0%. Importantly, it significantly improves geometric fidelity, yielding diameter errors of 0.13-0.16 mm and angular errors of ~8 degrees, supporting reliable plaque burden quantification.

AI.tldr: GeoCat is a geometry-consistent network for IVUS lumen and EEM segmentation that uses dual Cartesian–polar encoders, cross-domain attention, and temporal fusion with a differentiable geometry-consistency loss to improve clinically relevant metrics for plaque burden quantification.
AI.motivation: Standard overlap-driven lumen/EEM segmentation methods often exhibit boundary drift and topology errors, compromising plaque area, plaque burden, and derived geometric measurements.
AI.method: GeoCat processes 5-frame IVUS clips with dual Cartesian–polar encoders, cross-domain attention, and temporal fusion. It uses a differentiable geometry-consistency loss to supervise clinically relevant descriptors (diameters, orientations, cross-sectional areas). Trained on 12,242 annotated frames from 146 patients using two commercial IVUS systems.
AI.result: On the dataset, GeoCat achieves a Dice score of 0.93, 95HD reduced to 0.14 mm, and topology violations lowered to 1.0%. Geometry fidelity improves with diameter errors of 0.13–0.16 mm and angular errors around 8 degrees, supporting reliable plaque-burden quantification.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-consistent segmentation yields high segmentation accuracy while substantially improving clinical geometry fidelity and reducing topology errors, enabling more reliable plaque burden quantification across IVUS platforms.

title: What Does the Weight Norm Control in Grokking? Logit-Scale Mediation under Cross-Entropy
comment: 16 papges, 10 tables and 4 figures. Code and data to reproduce all numbers, tables, and figures: https://github.com/ClevixLab/grokking-logit-scale
summary: Grokking, the delayed jump from memorization to generalization, is usually tied to the weight norm: a smaller norm generalizes sooner. We ask what the norm actually controls.
