Holding the weight norm fixed by clamping and varying only an output temperature, we slide the grokking delay across its entire norm-induced range under cross-entropy; matching the effective logit scale back to baseline recovers about 85% of the delay at two moduli. Across a grid of norms and temperatures the delay collapses onto the logit scale alone (R2 = 0.97), with the norm adding 1-2% beyond it. The effect is loss-dependent: under mean-squared error the logit scale is pinned and the norm acts through a different route. A memorization control, a float64 softmax-collapse audit, and a no-LayerNorm transformer point to the same channel. Forking arms from one identical state, the delay follows the held norm value and not the clamp operation, which closes a rescaling-artifact concern. The proximal variable is the logit scale and the softmax saturation it drives; the weight norm is only an upstream handle. All numbers, tables, and figures reproduce from released code and data.

AI.tldr: Grokking delay is driven by the logit scale (softmax saturation) rather than weight norm; fixing norm and varying temperature shows the delay tracks the logit scale with high fidelity (R^2 ≈ 0.97); norm adds only ~1–2% beyond the logit-scale explanation; under MSE the logit-scale story changes, suggesting a different mechanism.
AI.motivation: Disentangle what the weight norm controls in grokking, i.e., whether the delay to generalization is a consequence of norm or of the effective logit scale driving softmax saturation.
AI.method: Clamp the weight norm to fix it while varying an output temperature across a grid of norms and temperatures; analyze across cross-entropy loss, mapping to effective logit scale; corroborate with memorization controls, a softmax-collapse audit, and a no-LayerNorm transformer; perform forking-from-identical-state tests to test rescaling artifacts; rely on released code/data.
AI.result: Across norms and temperatures, the grokking delay collapses onto the logit scale (R^2 = 0.97); the weight norm contributes a mere 1–2% beyond the logit-scale explanation; under cross-entropy, the proximal variable is the logit scale and the softmax saturation it drives; the effect is loss-dependent: with mean-squared error the logit-scale description is pinned and the norm acts through a different route; the results reproduce across multiple controls and simulations.
AI.conclusion: The logit scale is the proximate driver of the grokking delay, with weight norm acting mainly as an upstream handle; softmax saturation governs the mechanism, and norm explains only a small residual portion (except under MSE where the mechanism differs); the findings reduce rescaling-artifact concerns and are supported by multiple validation controls and code/data reproducibility.

title: HT-Bench: Benchmarking and Learning Dexterous Full-Hand Tactile Representations with Egocentric Vision
summary: Establishing a universal benchmark for tactile representation learning in robotic manipulation remains challenging due to the diversity of tactile sensor designs, data formats, and robot embodiments. Rather than seeking to establish such, we explore a scalable and promising direction for future development: egocentric vision paired with full-hand tactile data. To this end, we introduce \textbf{HT-Bench}, a large-scale multi-task benchmark for dexterous full-hand tactile sensing, comprising 10M RGB frames and 7.8M tactile frames collected across 226 tasks. HT-Bench evaluates tactile representations from three key perspectives: whether they encode meaningful contact geometry, whether they can align tactile observations with visual information, and whether they generalize to unseen tasks. To assess these capabilities, HT-Bench includes four tasks: fine-grained tactile similarity retrieval, masked tactile inpainting, vision-to-tactile synthesis, and multimodal tactile frame prediction. We further propose \textbf{HandTouch}, a vector-quantized vision--tactile encoder that learns tactile representations through progressive spatial, cross-modal, and temporal training. Across HT-Bench, HandTouch consistently outperforms representative tactile encoder baselines, improving Recall@5 on fine-grained tactile similarity retrieval from 74.65\% to 85.23\%, reducing RMSE on masked tactile inpainting from 0.022 to 0.010, and increasing OOD cIoU on vision-to-tactile synthesis from 0.628 to 0.705. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of HandTouch and suggest that large-scale egocentric full-hand tactile data provides a scalable basis for evaluating and advancing tactile representation learning in dexterous manipulation.

AI.tldr: HT-Bench provides a scalable, large-scale benchmark for dexterous tactile representation learning by pairing egocentric vision with full-hand tactile data, featuring 10M RGB frames, 7.8M tactile frames across 226 tasks. HandTouch is a vector-quantized encoder trained progressively across spatial, cross-modal, and temporal dimensions, achieving strong gains across four evaluation tasks (tactile similarity, inpainting, vision-to-tactile synthesis, multimodal frame prediction).
AI.motivation: Universal tactile benchmarks are hindered by diverse sensor designs, data formats, and robot embodiments. The authors propose a scalable direction—egocentric vision with full-hand tactile data—to evaluate and advance tactile representations without constraining to a single sensor type.
AI.method: HT-Bench aggregates a large, multi-task dataset and defines four tasks: fine-grained tactile similarity retrieval, masked tactile inpainting, vision-to-tactile synthesis, and multimodal tactile frame prediction, enabling evaluation of contact geometry, visual-tactile alignment, and generalization to unseen tasks. HandTouch is a vector-quantized vision–tactile encoder trained through progressive stages focusing on spatial (within-frame), cross-modal (vision-tactile), and temporal (across frames) representations to learn a cohesive multimodal tactile representation.
AI.result: HandTouch consistently outperforms representative tactile encoder baselines: Recall@5 on fine-grained tactile similarity retrieval improves from 74.65% to 85.23%; RMSE on masked tactile inpainting decreases from 0.022 to 0.010; out-of-distribution (OOD) cIoU on vision-to-tactile synthesis rises from 0.628 to 0.705.
AI.conclusion: The results indicate that large-scale egocentric full-hand tactile data is a scalable basis for evaluating and advancing tactile representation learning in dexterous manipulation, and HandTouch is an effective encoder that complements such data.

title: Toward Training-Free Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection in 3D Medical Images: A Batch-Based Approach Using 2D Foundation Models
summary: Zero-shot anomaly detection (ZSAD) is attractive for medical imaging because clinical systems must handle heterogeneous acquisition protocols, changing patient populations, and pathologies for which annotated training data may be unavailable. Most existing zero-shot anomaly detection methods are designed for 2D images, and their direct extension to 3D medical volumes is limited by the scarcity of large-scale volumetric foundation models or by the difficulty of utilizing volumetric context. We propose CS3F, a training-free batch-based framework for ZSAD in 3D medical images using 2D foundation models. Each volume is decomposed along multiple anatomical axes and encoded slice-wise by a 2D vision transformer. These are then converted into localized volumetric tokens by pooling neighboring slice features. Anomaly scores are obtained from cross-subject mutual similarity: tokens that lack close analogues in other subjects are assigned higher anomaly scores. To reduce the attenuation of focal lesion signals caused by depth pooling, we introduce a coarse-to-fine tokenization strategy that enables fine-resolution volumetric scoring without exhaustive matching. CS3F is evaluated on brain MRI across metastases, glioma, and stroke, as well as validated on lung CT to test generalizability beyond atlas-aligned brain MRI. The results show that frozen 2D foundation models can support anomaly localization in 3D medical images, and that the benefit of fine tokenization depends strongly on lesion contrast and imaging modality.

AI.tldr: A training-free 3D anomaly detection framework (CS3F) uses 2D foundation models to localize anomalies in medical volumes by slice-wise encoding, cross-subject mutual similarity, and coarse-to-fine tokenization, achieving generalization across brain MRI and lung CT without 3D pretrained models.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of large-scale volumetric foundation models and the challenge of extending 2D ZSAD methods to 3D medical data, while handling heterogeneous acquisitions and unseen pathologies.
AI.method: Decompose 3D volumes along multiple anatomical axes; encode slices with a frozen 2D vision transformer; pool neighboring slice features to form localized 3D tokens; obtain anomaly scores via cross-subject mutual similarity; apply a coarse-to-fine tokenization to preserve small lesions and enable fine-resolution scoring without exhaustive matching.
AI.result: Demonstrates that frozen 2D foundation models can enable 3D anomaly localization and that fine tokenization benefits depend on lesion contrast and imaging modality; validated on brain MRI datasets (metastases, glioma, stroke) and lung CT, indicating generalization beyond atlas-aligned brain MRI.
AI.conclusion: Frozen 2D foundation models can support 3D anomaly localization in medical imaging through a training-free, cross-subject similarity framework with coarse-to-fine tokenization, showing modality-dependent gains and generalization across datasets.

title: Structured Representation Learning with Locally Linear Embeddings and Adaptive Feature Fusion
comment: Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (04/2026)
summary: Neuroscientific research has revealed that the brain encodes complex behaviors by leveraging structured, low-dimensional manifolds and dynamically fusing multiple sources of information through adaptive gating mechanisms. Inspired by these principles, we propose a novel reinforcement learning (RL) framework that encourages the disentanglement of dynamics-specific and reward-specific features, drawing direct parallels to how neural circuits separate and integrate information for efficient decision-making. Our approach leverages locally linear embeddings (LLEs) to capture the intrinsic, locally linear structure inherent in many environments, mirroring the local smoothness observed in neural population activity, while concurrently deriving reward-specific features through the standard RL objective. An attention mechanism, analogous to cortical gating, adaptively fuses these complementary representations on a per-state basis. Experimental results on benchmark tasks demonstrate that our method, grounded in neuroscientific principles, improves learning efficiency and overall performance compared to conventional RL approaches, highlighting the benefits of explicitly modeling local state structures and adaptive feature selection as observed in biological systems.

AI.tldr: A neuroscience-inspired RL framework disentangles dynamics- and reward-specific features using locally linear embeddings (LLEs) to capture local structure, with an attention-based gating mechanism to fuse representations per state, yielding improved sample efficiency and performance on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Motivated by how neural circuits separate and integrate information for efficient decision-making, the approach aims to disentangle dynamics from rewards and exploit local geometry to improve learning.
AI.method: The method employs locally linear embeddings to capture intrinsic locally linear state structure, derives reward-specific features via the standard RL objective, and uses an attention mechanism to adaptively fuse these representations on a per-state basis.
AI.result: Empirical results on benchmark tasks show improved learning efficiency and overall performance compared to conventional RL approaches.
AI.conclusion: Modeling local state structure and adaptive feature selection, inspired by biological systems, can enhance RL performance and learning efficiency.

title: Hardware- and Vision-in-the-Loop Validation of Deep Monocular Pose Estimation for Autonomous Maritime UAV Flight
summary: Autonomous UAV operations on ships require reliable vision-based relative pose estimation, yet at-sea validation is costly, weather-dependent, and risky. This paper presents a hardware-validated vision-in-the-loop framework that enables fully autonomous indoor flight while emulating photorealistic maritime environments. Rendered maritime views are processed onboard by a deep transformer-based monocular pose estimator. Delayed vision measurements are fused with high-rate IMU data using a delayed Kalman filter to provide consistent state estimates for geometric control. The system captures critical embedded effects, including perception latency, asynchronous updates, and computational constraints, that are absent in pure simulation. Autonomous takeoff, trajectory tracking, and landing experiments demonstrate stable closed-loop flight. The results establish a safe and hardware-realistic intermediate stage for developing maritime UAV autonomy prior to shipboard deployment.

AI.tldr: Hardware-validated vision-in-the-loop framework enabling indoor, photorealistic maritime environment rendering for autonomous UAV pose estimation, fused with IMU via a delayed Kalman filter; demonstrates autonomous takeoff, tracking, and landing.
AI.motivation: At-sea validation for maritime UAVs is costly, weather-dependent, and risky. A hardware-realistic, latency-aware platform is needed to develop reliable vision-based relative pose estimation before shipboard deployment.
AI.method: Indoor facility with photorealistic maritime rendering processed onboard by a deep transformer-based monocular pose estimator. Vision measurements are delayed and fused with high-rate IMU data using a delayed Kalman filter to maintain consistent state estimates. The framework captures perception latency, asynchronous updates, and computational constraints. Autonomous takeoff, trajectory tracking, and landing experiments validate closed-loop stability.
AI.result: Achieved stable closed-loop flight with autonomous takeoff, tracking, and landing, validating a safe, hardware-realistic intermediate stage for maritime UAV autonomy prior to shipboard deployment.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a practical bridge between simulation and real deployment by embedding realistic sensing/compute constraints and validation in a hardware-in-the-loop framework, enabling development of maritime UAV autonomy in a controlled environment.

title: SMART: A Flexible, Interpretable, and Scalable Spatio-temporal Brain Atlas from High-Resolution Imaging Data
summary: We introduce SMART, a framework for learning a flexible, interpretable, and scalable spatio-temporal brain atlas from longitudinal high-resolution 3D medical images. Existing approaches to spatio-temporal atlas construction rely on black-box generative models that lack flexibility, limit interpretability, and struggle to scale to high-dimensional data. SMART addresses these challenges by learning a continuous disease-time atlas that decouples global group-wise disease dynamics from their patient-specific anatomical manifestation. Guided by anatomically inspired priors, SMART models interpretable global trajectories of regional progression along a shared disease timeline through region-specific differential equations. Global trajectories are further personalized to individual anatomies via dense diffeomorphic displacements parameterized by a flexible and scalable multi-scale Neural Cellular Automata. Evaluated on five longitudinal MRI datasets in Alzheimer's disease (ADNI-1/GO/2, OASIS-3, AIBL; > 1,300 subjects), SMART produces anatomically meaningful predictions of disease progression and achieves state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy and improved temporal consistency over adversarial and diffusion baselines. Our approach establishes a new paradigm for flexible, interpretable, and scalable modeling of spatio-temporal change in high-dimensional medical image time-series.

AI.tldr: SMART is a framework to learn a flexible, interpretable, and scalable spatio-temporal brain atlas from longitudinal high-resolution 3D MRIs, decoupling disease dynamics from anatomy and using neural cellular automata for personalization, achieving state-of-the-art forecasting.
AI.motivation: Current spatio-temporal atlas methods rely on black-box generative models, lacking interpretability and scaling to high-dimensional longitudinal MRI data; there is a need for interpretable models that can capture disease trajectories and anatomical variation.
AI.method: Learn a continuous disease-time atlas with global disease trajectories along a shared timeline via region-specific differential equations; personalize to anatomy using dense diffeomorphic displacements parameterized by multi-scale Neural Cellular Automata; guided by anatomically inspired priors.
AI.result: Evaluated on five longitudinal MRI datasets (ADNI-1/GO/2, OASIS-3, AIBL; >1,300 subjects); SMART yields anatomically meaningful disease progression predictions, state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy, and improved temporal consistency over adversarial and diffusion baselines.
AI.conclusion: Presents a new paradigm for flexible, interpretable, and scalable modeling of spatio-temporal changes in high-dimensional medical image time-series.

title: The Illusion of Improvement: Reject Inference Strategies in Credit Scoring
summary: Reject inference methods are widely used to mitigate survival bias in credit scoring, yet their effectiveness remains poorly understood. We systematically evaluate several such methods and uncover a structural failure mode: in a natural retraining cycle, models whose accuracy improves while recall collapses create an illusion of improvement that leads practitioners to believe the system is getting better when, in fact, its rejection quality -- the ability to correctly screen out defaulters -- is deteriorating. We then propose a controlled exploration strategy that breaks the feedback loop without statistical assumptions: the lender deliberately approves a fraction of rejected applicants and observes their true outcomes. We show that accuracy and rejection quality give opposite recommendations on whether to explore: accuracy favors no exploration, while rejection quality improves with it, confirming that standard evaluation metrics are misleading under selection bias. Even minimal exploration rates (2--5\%) prove sufficient in our experiments to diagnose the severity of the feedback loop at near-zero cost. Our findings are consistent across two machine learning methods and three real-world datasets, and suggest that standard evaluation protocols are inadequate for assessing models trained under survival bias.

AI.tldr: Reject inference can create a misleading sense of improvement due to a feedback loop; without controlled exploration, accuracy may rise while rejection quality deteriorates; minimal exploration reveals the bias.
AI.motivation: Address survival bias in credit scoring via reject inference; assess effectiveness of existing methods and identify a structural failure mode of the retraining cycle.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of several reject inference methods; identify failure mode; propose controlled exploration strategy in which a fraction of rejected applicants are approved; run experiments with two ML methods and three real-world datasets.
AI.result: Discovery of a structural failure: accuracy improves while recall collapses; controlled exploration reveals opposite guidance from accuracy vs rejection quality; even 2-5% exploration suffices to diagnose bias; standard metrics mislead under selection bias; results consistent across methods and datasets.
AI.conclusion: Standard evaluation protocols are inadequate for models trained under survival bias; recommend controlled exploration to break feedback loop; minimal exploration to diagnose severity; reevaluate evaluation practices in credit scoring with survival bias.

title: Learning to Annotate Delayed and False AEB Events: A Practical System for Extreme Class Imbalance and Asymmetric Label Noise
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, accepted by IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB) optimization relies on accurately annotated real-world trigger events, particularly rare but critical delayed and false AEB triggers that expose system deficiencies. However, these minority samples comprise less than 5% of thousands of daily triggers, making manual annotation prohibitively expensive at scale. We present the first automated AEB annotation framework to address this problem. During development, we identified two fundamental challenges that severely impair delayed/false trigger annotation accuracy: (1) Extreme class imbalance where delayed/false triggers are overwhelmed by true triggers; (2) Asymmetric label noise where mislabeled majority samples (true triggers) suppress minority samples (delayed/false triggers) learning. To overcome these challenges, we propose two key innovations: (1) Specific data augmentation that synthesizes realistic samples by manipulating focal target attributes, transplanting ego-vehicle dynamics, and masking non-focal agents; (2) noise suppression using stable hardness estimation and probe-guided adaptive threshold to clean mislabeled true trigger samples. Crucially, we deploy our model as a practical annotation system with full-stack architecture, efficiently identifying critical delayed/false triggers from thousands of daily AEB events. Production results demonstrate 80% improvement in recall of delayed/false triggers and 50% reduction in manual workload. Beyond immediate gains, the system enables continuous self-improvement through accumulated high-quality annotations, establishing a necessary data foundation for on-vehicle AEB system optimization

AI.tldr: Automates AEB event annotation to recover rare delayed/false triggers, addressing severe class imbalance and label noise with specialized data augmentation and stable noise-suppression methods; achieves high recall and reduced manual workload.
AI.motivation: Manual annotation of rare delayed/false AEB triggers is impractical due to extreme class imbalance (<5% minority) and asymmetric label noise, which hampers robust AEB system optimization.
AI.method: Two innovations: (1) targeted data augmentation that creates realistic samples by manipulating focal trigger attributes, transplanting ego-vehicle dynamics, and masking non-focal agents; (2) noise suppression via stable hardness estimation and probe-guided adaptive threshold to clean mislabeled true triggers; plus full-stack deployment for practical annotation at scale.
AI.result: Production deployment yields ~80% improvement in recall for delayed/false triggers and ~50% reduction in manual annotation workload.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers a practical, self-improving annotation system that builds high-quality data necessary for on-vehicle AEB optimization and continuous system improvement.

title: SpectralDiT: Timestep-Conditioned Spectral Residual Correction for Flow-Matching DiTs
summary: We propose SpectralDiT, a lightweight modification to flow-matching Diffusion Transformers that adds timestep-conditioned spectral correction to the MLP residual branch. The module decomposes each residual update into low- and high-frequency components on the patch-token grid, then learns a zero-initialized additive gate so the model initially matches the baseline DiT. On CIFAR-10 pixel-space generation, SpectralDiT improves FID from 20.78 to 19.71 at patch size 1 and reduces the radial Fourier spectrum gap. Furthermore, we scale our method to latent diffusion on ImageNet-100. With 0.6% additional theoretical FLOPs and 1.36% additional parameters, SpectralDiT improves latent flow-matching, achieving an 8.7% relative FID reduction under classifier-free guidance (CFG 2.0). All reported results are averaged over five seeds. Ablations and gate visualizations on CIFAR-10 reveal stable block-specific spectral correction patterns.

AI.tldr: SpectralDiT adds a timestep-conditioned spectral correction to the MLP residual of diffusion transformers, decomposing residual updates into low/high-frequency components with a zero-initialized gate. It yields modest compute overhead but notable FID improvements on CIFAR-10 and latent diffusion on ImageNet-100, with ablations showing stable, block-specific spectral patterns.
AI.motivation: Improve generative performance of flow-matching diffusion transformers by leveraging spectral information in residual updates, enabling targeted corrections at different spatial frequencies while maintaining training stability and minimal overhead.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight spectral correction module that splits each residual update into low- and high-frequency components on the patch-token grid. Add a zero-initialized additive gate conditioned on timesteps so the model initially matches the baseline DiT. Apply to pixel-space CIFAR-10 and latent diffusion on ImageNet-100, with modest FLOP and parameter overhead.
AI.result: CIFAR-10 pixel-space generation: FID improves from 20.78 to 19.71 at patch size 1; radial Fourier spectrum gap reduced. ImageNet-100 latent diffusion: 0.6% additional theoretical FLOPs and 1.36% additional parameters yield an 8.7% relative FID reduction under CFG 2.0. All results averaged over five seeds.
AI.conclusion: SpectralDiT provides a lightweight, timestep-aware spectral refinement of residuals that yields consistent performance gains with small computational cost. Ablations indicate stable, block-specific spectral correction patterns across CIFAR-10.

title: SFT Overtraining Predicts Rank Inversion via Entropy Collapse Under RLVR
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures. Accepted at the Deep Learning for Code (DL4C) Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: The standard heuristic of selecting the SFT checkpoint with the highest pass@1 for GRPO can fail when SFT compresses the rollout distribution. For binary rewards, the expected within group advantage variance is $p(1{-}p)(g{-}1)/g$; when early GRPO drives $p$ below $p^*(g)$, most groups have identical rewards and provide no group relative signal. We study SFT depth ladders for Qwen2.5-Coder-3B and DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B. We test Qwen2.5-Coder-3B across five depths and three seeds, and DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B across four matched depths and three seeds. On Qwen, pre RL pass@1 rises with SFT depth, but peak GRPO pass@10 falls from $0.806$ to $0.481$ (3 seed mean, $n{=}20$); pre RL entropy is positively associated with the GRPO outcome ($ρ{=}{+}0.69$). On DeepSeek, pass@1 remains far above $p^*(8){=}0.083$, and GRPO outcomes compress rather than invert. A two stage diagnostic, combining pre RL entropy triage with an early GRPO entropy monitor, flags high risk checkpoints and can stop failing runs early. Simple KL to reference regularisation and label smoothing variants do not rescue the collapsed Qwen checkpoint in our setting, suggesting the failure is not a trivial GRPO hyperparameter artefact.

AI.tldr: SFT checkpoint selection by highest pass@1 can fail when SFT compresses the rollout distribution; a two-stage entropy-based diagnostic improves early stopping; standard KL regularization and label smoothing do not rescue a collapsed Qwen checkpoint.
AI.motivation: Investigate why SFT depth choice degrades GRPO performance and develop practical diagnostic tools to identify high-risk checkpoints.
AI.method: Empirical study across SFT depths for Qwen2.5-Coder-3B and DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B. Evaluate pre-RL pass@1, GRPO pass@10, and reward distribution characteristics (entropy) across seeds. Analyze p*(g) thresholds for group signals. Test simple KL regularization and label smoothing variants. Propose a two-stage diagnostic: pre-RL entropy triage + early GRPO entropy monitor.
AI.result: For Qwen2.5-Coder-3B: pre-RL pass@1 increases with SFT depth, but peak GRPO pass@10 declines (0.806 to 0.481). Pre-RL entropy correlates with GRPO outcomes (ρ = +0.69). For DeepSeek-Coder-6.7B: pass@1 remains well above p*(8)=0.083; GRPO outcomes compress rather than invert. A two-stage diagnostic flags high-risk checkpoints and can halt failing runs early. KL to reference regularization and label smoothing do not salvage the collapsed Qwen checkpoint, suggesting the failure is not a trivial GRPO hyperparameter artefact.
AI.conclusion: The relationship between SFT depth and GRPO is complex; increases in pre-RL performance do not guarantee improved GRPO outcomes and can even degrade them due to distribution compression. Entropy-based diagnostics offer a practical approach to early failure detection, while simple regularization tricks are insufficient to rescue collapsed checkpoints in this setting.

title: FAST-LIVGO: A Degeneracy-Robust LiDAR-Inertial-Visual-GNSS Fusion Odometry
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS 2026)
summary: Robust state estimation and mapping in long-term, large-scale, and highly dynamic environments remains a key challenge in robotics. Existing LiDAR-Inertial-Visual Odometry (LIVO) systems achieve strong local accuracy but suffer from accumulated drift over long distances and may fail in geometrically degraded or textureless scenes. Meanwhile, GNSS-aided fusion frameworks often rely on LiDAR or visual odometry for state prediction and outlier rejection, making them vulnerable when odometry degenerates. To address these limitations, we propose a tightly coupled LiDAR-Inertial-Visual-GNSS fusion framework based on an Error-State Iterated Kalman Filter. An online spatiotemporal alignment module using Dynamic Time Warping is introduced for highly dynamic conditions. To better exploit GNSS precision, we develop observation models based on Doppler shifts and fixed-anchor Time-Differenced Carrier Phase, providing millimeter-level relative constraints without augmenting historical anchor states. We further design a degeneracy-aware dual-mode outlier rejection strategy that switches between LIVO-prior-guided rejection and GNSS-aided recovery according to the LIVO degeneracy level. Experiments on the public M3DGR dataset and a custom 20~m/s fixed-wing UAV dataset demonstrate that our system reduces accumulated drift and map ghosting, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and robustness.

AI.tldr: Tightly coupled LiDAR-Inertial-Visual-GNSS fusion with an error-state iterated Kalman filter, online spatiotemporal alignment via Dynamic Time Warping, Doppler/TDCP GNSS models, and degeneracy-aware outlier rejection, achieving reduced drift and robust mapping in dynamic, large-scale environments.
AI.motivation: Long-term, large-scale robotics mapping suffers from drift in LiDAR-Inertial-Visual Odometry (LIVO) and vulnerability when GNSS relies on degenerate odometry; robust, globally consistent fusion in dynamic scenes is needed.
AI.method: Integrates LiDAR, IMU, vision, and GNSS using an Error-State Iterated Kalman Filter; online spatiotemporal alignment with Dynamic Time Warping; GNSS observations modeled by Doppler shifts and fixed-anchor Time-Differenced Carrier Phase (TDCP) to provide mm-level relative constraints without augmenting historical anchors; a degeneracy-aware dual-mode outlier rejection switches between LIVO-prior-guided rejection and GNSS-aided recovery depending on LIVO degeneracy.
AI.result: Experiments on the public M3DGR dataset and a custom 20 m/s fixed-wing UAV dataset demonstrate reduced accumulated drift and map ghosting, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tightly coupled LIVO-GNSS fusion framework with dynamic alignment, GNSS Doppler/TDCP constraints, and degeneracy-aware outlier rejection yields improved long-term accuracy and robustness in dynamic, large-scale environments.

title: SAMA: Semantic Anchor-aligned Augmentation for Unified Low-Resource Multimodal Information Extraction
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
summary: Multimodal Information Extraction (MIE)-covering tasks such as Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (MNER), Relation Extraction (MRE), and Event Extraction (MEE)-is essential for understanding multimedia content but remains constrained by severe data scarcity. Although data augmentation is a promising remedy, existing approaches are impeded by coarse cross-modal alignment and fragmented, task-specific designs that fail to exploit shared semantic knowledge. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Semantic Anchor-aligned Multimodal Augmentation (SAMA), a unified framework for generating high-fidelity, task-aware synthetic data. SAMA constructs structured semantic anchors from ground-truth labels to guide a Collaborative Multi-Experts Multimodal Large Language Model (CME-MLLM), which integrates a Universal Adapter for shared semantics with Task-Specific Adapters to produce diverse yet constraint-compliant textual samples. For image synthesis, SAMA employs an Anchor-Preserving Diffusion mechanism that uses anchor-weighted prompts and latent conditioning to maintain critical semantic anchors while diversifying visual contexts. To eliminate the need for manual verification, SAMA further introduces a Dual-Constraint Filtering module that selects synthetic samples based on both cross-modal consistency and anchor fidelity. Extensive experiments across benchmark datasets for MNER, MRE, and MEE demonstrate that SAMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art augmentation baselines under both fully supervised and low-resource settings, underscoring its versatility, robustness, and effectiveness.

AI.tldr: A unified data augmentation framework (SAMA) for multimodal information extraction that uses semantic anchors, a collaborative multimodal LLM with adapters, anchor-preserving diffusion for images, and dual-constraint filtering to generate high-fidelity, task-aware synthetic data, outperforming baselines on MNER, MRE, and MEE in both high- and low-resource settings.
AI.motivation: Multimodal information extraction tasks suffer from severe data scarcity. Existing augmentation methods struggle with coarse cross-modal alignment and lack cross-task semantic sharing, limiting generalization and effectiveness.
AI.method: Construct structured semantic anchors from ground-truth labels. Use a Collaborative Multi-Experts Multimodal LLM (CME-MLLM) with a Universal Adapter for shared semantics and Task-Specific Adapters for task alignment to produce diverse yet constraint-compliant textual samples. For images, apply an Anchor-Preserving Diffusion mechanism using anchor-weighted prompts and latent conditioning to preserve semantic anchors while diversifying visuals. Implement a Dual-Constraint Filtering module to select synthetic samples based on cross-modal consistency and anchor fidelity, avoiding manual verification.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark datasets for MNER, MRE, and MEE shows that SAMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art augmentation baselines under both fully supervised and low-resource settings, demonstrating robustness and effectiveness across tasks.
AI.conclusion: SAMA provides a versatile and effective data augmentation framework for multimodal information extraction by tightly integrating semantic anchors, shared semantic modeling, anchor-preserving image synthesis, and principled sample filtering; its gains are strongest in low-resource scenarios.

title: Quantum Annealing Enhanced Reinforcement Learning for Accurate Remaining Useful Lifetime Prediction
summary: Remaining useful life (RUL) estimation is central to predictive maintenance, where an unplanned failure can cost far more than the asset itself. Statistical degradation models miss the strong nonlinearity of real systems, and data-driven models often converge to suboptimal solutions in high-dimensional, non-convex search spaces. We propose a Quantum Annealing enhanced Q-Learning (QAQL) framework that couples the sampling behaviour of quantum annealing with the sequential decision making of Q-learning. Each Q-value update is encoded as a small quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) whose ground state is the greedy action; rather than acting as a deterministic optimizer, the annealer returns a distribution over near-optimal actions across many reads, and this stochastic action selection supplies the exploration that curbs premature convergence on nonlinear degradation trajectories. The QUBO is solved on the D-Wave Advantage system using minor embedding, with the annealer woven into the reinforcement-learning loop rather than bolted on after training. We validate QAQL on two public benchmarks: the NASA C-MAPSS turbofan engine datasets and a device-fleet predictive maintenance dataset. Averaged over many independent runs and across six error metrics, QAQL outperforms the classical and quantum baselines considered in this study, with statistically significant improvements. The results indicate that quantum annealing is a usable, not merely theoretical, optimizer inside a reinforcement-learning loop for industrial predictive-maintenance applications.

AI.tldr: Quantum Annealing enhanced Q-Learning (QAQL) integrates a quantum annealer into the Q-learning loop by encoding each Q-value update as a QUBO. The D-Wave system samples near-optimal actions, providing stochastic exploration. Tested on NASA C-MAPSS and device-fleet datasets, QAQL shows statistically significant improvements over classical and quantum baselines, suggesting practical viability of quantum-assisted RL for predictive maintenance.
AI.motivation: To address two key challenges in RUL estimation and predictive maintenance: (1) the nonlinearity of degradation processes that static statistical models struggle to capture, and (2) the difficulty of optimization in high-dimensional, non-convex landscapes for data-driven RL methods, which may converge to suboptimal policies.
AI.method: Formulate Q-value updates as small QUBOs whose ground state corresponds to the greedy action. Use a quantum annealer to sample a distribution over near-optimal actions across multiple reads, providing exploration. Integrate the annealer within the RL loop rather than post-training. Solve QUBOs on the D-Wave Advantage system with minor embedding. Validate on NASA C-MAPSS turbofan datasets and a device-fleet predictive-maintenance dataset, averaging results over many runs and six error metrics.
AI.result: QAQL outperforms both classical and other quantum baselines across six metrics and multiple runs, with statistically significant improvements on the two public benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Quantum annealing can be effectively embedded inside a reinforcement-learning loop for industrial predictive maintenance, yielding practical performance gains and offering exploration benefits without relying on full deterministic optimization.

title: Invertible Neural Network Adapter for One-Step Flow Matching in Robot Manipulation
summary: This paper presents an invertible neural network adapter for general robotic manipulation, designed to generate precise high-dimensional actions conditioned on multimodal observations, including visual, linguistic, and proprioceptive inputs, through a one-step denoising process. Built upon a flow-matching formulation, the proposed adapter effectively constrains the action generation trajectory within an invertible latent space, thereby enabling efficient and high-quality dexterous action synthesis with only a single inference step. Compared with conventional iterative flow-matching policies, the proposed framework substantially reduces inference complexity while maintaining strong action prediction accuracy and stability. Extensive experiments are conducted across a diverse set of simulation benchmarks and real-world robotic platforms to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method. Across simulation benchmarks, the proposed adapter consistently demonstrates superior or near state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of manipulation tasks. Furthermore, real-world experiments reveal a significant improvement in inference efficiency for vision-language-action (VLA) models, reducing the average inference latency from 110 ms to 61 ms while maintaining strong task performance.

AI.tldr: An invertible neural network adapter for one-step, high-dimensional action generation in robotic manipulation via flow-matching; reduces inference complexity and latency while preserving accuracy; validated in simulation and real-world with notable latency reductions for vision-language-action models.
AI.motivation: Enable efficient, high-quality dexterous action generation from multimodal observations by constraining the action trajectory within an invertible latent space to allow single-step inference.
AI.method: Invertible adapter based on a flow-matching framework; conducts one-step denoising to generate actions conditioned on multimodal inputs (visual, linguistic, proprioceptive); aims to keep the generation trajectory within a latent space, improving stability and efficiency relative to iterative flow-matching policies.
AI.result: In simulations, the adapter achieves superior or near-state-of-the-art performance across diverse manipulation tasks. In real-world experiments, inference latency for vision-language-action models reduces from 110 ms to 61 ms while maintaining strong task performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed one-step invertible adapter enables efficient, high-quality dexterous action synthesis across tasks and platforms, with substantial inference-time improvements in real-world VLA deployments.

title: SCR-Guided Difficulty-Aware Optimization for Infrared Small Target Detection
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026 Workshops (PBVS). Published version: https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2026W/PBVS/html/Sevim_SCR-Guided_Difficulty-Aware_Optimization_for_Infrared_Small_Target_Detection_CVPRW_2026_paper.html
summary: Infrared small target detection remains challenging due to severe background clutter, low contrast, and weak spatial responses where geometric overlap alone is insufficient to characterize detection quality. In this work, we propose REEM (Reweighted Explicit-visibility Enhanced Modulation), a lightweight SCR-guided difficulty-aware optimization framework that incorporates Signal-to-Clutter Ratio (SCR) as a physically meaningful visibility prior during training. Instead of modifying the network architecture or directly optimizing SCR, REEM computes a ground-truth local SCR from the input image and applies a differentiable modulation to the soft-IoU learning signal, emphasizing low-visibility targets while preserving stable optimization and identical inference behavior. REEM is integrated into a U-Net-based MSHNet without introducing additional parameters, architectural modifications, or inference-time overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements over the baseline, achieving higher IoU and detection probability (Pd) together with substantially reduced false alarms (FA), particularly under challenging low-visibility conditions. These results suggest that SCR-guided difficulty-aware optimization provides an effective and physically grounded complement to conventional overlap-based objectives for infrared small target detection. The code is available at https://github. com/yall-in-one/Reemm.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, SCR-guided reweighting strategy (REEM) improves infrared small target detection by modulating the soft-IoU loss with a ground-truth local SCR; integrated into a U-Net-based MSHNet with no extra parameters or inference overhead, yielding higher IoU/Pd and fewer false alarms under low visibility.
AI.motivation: Infrared small target detection suffers from severe background clutter, low contrast, and weak spatial cues. Overlap-based IoU optimization alone is insufficient; a physically meaningful visibility prior (SCR) can guide learning to emphasize low-visibility targets.
AI.method: Compute a ground-truth local SCR from the input; apply a differentiable modulation of the soft-IoU learning signal guided by SCR (reweighting difficult targets). Integrate into a U-Net-based MSHNet without introducing new parameters or inference-time changes; no architectural modifications. Training-time only, with SCR as a visibility prior.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over the baseline in IoU and detection probability, with substantially reduced false alarms, especially under low-visibility conditions. Demonstrates that SCR-guided optimization complements traditional overlap-based objectives.
AI.conclusion: SCR-guided difficulty-aware optimization provides a physically grounded, efficient enhancement for infrared small target detection, compatible with existing architectures and inference behavior; the approach improves robustness to low-visibility clutter without extra inference cost.

title: Beyond AHI: An Interpretable Causal-Discovery-Guided Framework for Sleep Recovery in Connected Health
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Sensing and Computing for Smart and Connected Health (SCH), co-located with IEEE/ACM CHASE 2026
summary: Objective sleep assessment relies on polysomnography (PSG), yet clinical impact is often better reflected in patient-reported outcomes (PROs) such as sleepiness and fatigue. Existing summary indices, including the Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI), provide limited insight into the multidomain physiology underlying functional recovery. We propose an interpretable, causal-discovery--guided framework for deriving a hierarchical Sleep Recovery Score (SRS) from multimodal PSG. Using two large population cohorts (MESA: n=1540; MrOS: n=825), we apply directed acyclic graph (DAG) learning to identify candidate physiological drivers spanning respiratory burden, hypoxic burden, sleep fragmentation, sleep architecture, and autonomic regulation. Although derived from clinical PSG, these domains map naturally to sensing streams increasingly available in connected health technologies, including wearable ECG, oximetry, and sleep-stage estimation devices. To preserve mechanistic plausibility, we introduce a two-stage screening process that combines physiology-based constraints with constrained LLM-assisted auditing to identify and remove structural confounders and construct-overlapping variables. Across cohorts, these five domains emerge as recurrent physiological domains associated with recovery, and the resulting SRS shows up to 2.5$\times$ stronger alignment with perceived recovery than AHI. By linking multimodal sleep physiology to patient-centered outcomes through an interpretable, bias-aware, and domain structured framework, this work provides a practical foundation for recovery modeling across both clinical sleep studies and emerging smart and connected health settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces a causal-discovery-guided framework to derive Sleep Recovery Score (SRS) from multimodal PSG, identifying five physiological domains and achieving up to 2.5x better alignment with patient-reported recovery than AHI across two large cohorts.
AI.motivation: Address the limited sensitivity of AHI and conventional PSG metrics to capture multidomain physiology underlying recovery; provide an interpretable, bias-aware framework linking sleep physiology to patient-centered outcomes.
AI.method: Two large cohorts (MESA, MrOS) analyzed with directed acyclic graph (DAG) learning to identify candidate drivers across respiratory burden, hypoxic burden, sleep fragmentation, sleep architecture, and autonomic regulation. A two-stage screening combines physiology-based constraints with constrained LLM-assisted auditing to remove confounders and construct overlapping variables, yielding a hierarchical Sleep Recovery Score (SRS).
AI.result: The SRS clusters emerge around five recurrent physiological domains and exhibits up to 2.5× stronger alignment with perceived recovery than AHI across cohorts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a practical and interpretable approach to recovery modeling in clinical sleep studies and consumer wearables, enabling bias-aware, domain-structured linking of sleep physiology to patient-centered outcomes.

title: Constant Time-Delay Leader Following with Neural Networks and Invariant Extended Kalman Filters for Arbitrary Trajectories
summary: This paper proposes a constant time-delay trajectory tracking method for vehicle convoys operating without inter-vehicle communication, a common coordinate system, or global positioning. The method integrates a probabilistic sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) neural network with an invariant extended Kalman filter (IEKF) to warm-start the prediction process, allowing accurate estimation of a leader vehicle's relative trajectory on the SE(2) manifold. A geometric model predictive controller is further incorporated to fully exploit the manifold-based trajectory predictions for improved control performance. The system can handle arbitrary nonlinear trajectories with varying speeds and motion profiles while reducing the need for expert-based domain knowledge for the design of trajectory following systems, even under long trajectory delays. The effectiveness of the method is validated through comparisons with a pure IEKF baseline, learning-based methods, and the ground-truth trajectory in kinematic simulations, as well as in experiments using real robotic vehicles.

AI.tldr: A constant time-delay convoy tracking approach combining Seq2Seq leader-trajectory prediction with an invariant EKF warm-start and SE(2) geometric MPC, enabling accurate, delay-tolerant tracking without inter-vehicle comms or GPS, validated in simulations and real robots.
AI.motivation: Convoy autonomy under communication/GPS constraints and varying dynamics requires robust, delay-tolerant tracking. Operating on SE(2) with a geometric controller can improve accuracy and reduce reliance on hand-crafted designs.
AI.method: A probabilistic Seq2Seq network predicts the leader's relative trajectory, used to warm-start an invariant EKF on SE(2). An SE(2)-based geometric model predictive controller exploits these predictions for tracking, enabling constant time-delay handling across arbitrary nonlinear trajectories and speeds, without inter-vehicle communication or global positioning.
AI.result: Compared to a pure IEKF baseline, learning-based methods, and ground-truth trajectories, the approach shows improved tracking in kinematic simulations and is validated on real robotic vehicles, demonstrating robustness to delayed, nonlinear leader trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Integrating Seq2Seq prediction with invariant filtering on SE(2) and a geometric MPC yields a delay-robust convoy tracking framework that requires less expert tuning, works without GPS/comms, and generalizes to complex, variable-speed trajectories; validated in simulation and hardware.

title: Learned Radius Estimation for UDF-Based Point Cloud Reconstruction
summary: Surface reconstruction from point clouds is important for consumer-grade 3D capture, including AR/VR and indoor scanning. Local-patch Unsigned Distance Field (UDF) methods are lightweight and generalizable, but their accuracy depends on the support radius, traditionally fixed or selected by a one-dimensional curvature heuristic that cannot capture heterogeneous local geometry. We propose a learned per-query radius selector that predicts a continuous support radius and plugs into a frozen LoSF-UDF backbone. The selector is trained using off-grid target radii obtained by parabolic interpolation of cached UDF error curves. Experiments show improved fine-scale reconstruction accuracy.

AI.tldr: Introduces a learned per-query radius selector for local-patch unsigned distance field (UDF) surface reconstruction, enabling continuous radius prediction and improved fine-scale accuracy with a frozen LoSF-UDF backbone.
AI.motivation: Current radius choices (fixed or simple curvature heuristics) fail to capture heterogeneous local geometry in point-cloud surface reconstruction, limiting fine-scale accuracy.
AI.method: A neural radius selector predicts a continuous support radius per query and feeds into a frozen LoSF-UDF backbone. The selector is trained using off-grid target radii derived from parabolic interpolation of cached UDF error curves.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate improved fine-scale reconstruction accuracy compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, per-query radius selection enhances local geometry modeling in surface reconstruction while leveraging a lightweight, frozen backbone.

title: Concept Modulation Models: A Unified Framework for Identifiability and Extrapolation
summary: Reliable generalization in conditional latent variable models requires understanding both identifiability and extrapolation: how observed variation across attributes determines latent structure, and how that structure determines distributions at unseen attributes. However, existing identifiability and extrapolation guarantees are largely model-specific, with separate analyses in nonlinear ICA, causal representation learning, perturbation modeling, and related conditional latent variable models. We introduce concept modulation models (CMMs), an attribute-indexed class of conditional generative models with structure $A\to Λ\to C\to X$, where attributes select modulators, modulators induce latent concept laws, and concepts generate observed features. CMMs lift transition-based identifiability to conditional settings by showing that feature agreement on observed attributes induces a latent concept transition constrained by the CMM class. We express these constraints through attribute potentials, log-density ratios between attribute-conditioned concept laws, separating the generic lifting step from model-specific rigidity arguments. The same potentials control extrapolation: agreement at unseen attributes holds exactly when the transported attribute-potential identities extend to those attributes. This yields algebraic extrapolation criteria, identifies the common potential-based proof objects behind several existing identifiability and extrapolation results, and, when combined with the model-specific rigidity arguments in those works, recovers their stated conclusions.

AI.tldr: Introduces concept modulation models (CMMs) as an attribute-indexed class of conditional generative models (A→Λ→C→X). They lift transition-based identifiability to conditional settings via attribute-conditioned latent concept transitions and use attribute potentials to characterize identifiability and extrapolation, yielding algebraic criteria and unifying existing results.
AI.motivation: Existing guarantees for identifiability and extrapolation in conditional latent-variable models are model-specific and fragmented across nonlinear ICA, causal representation learning, and perturbation models. There is a need for a unified framework to understand how observed attribute variation shapes latent structure and how that structure extrapolates to unseen attributes.
AI.method: Propose CMMs with structure A→Λ→C→X where attributes select modulators, modulators induce latent concept laws, and concepts generate observed features. Show that feature agreement on observed attributes constrains a latent concept transition via CMM class. Introduce attribute potentials (log-density ratios between attribute-conditioned concept laws) to separate the lifting step from rigidity arguments, and derive extrapolation criteria that hold when these potentials extend to unseen attributes.
AI.result: Derivation of algebraic extrapolation criteria within the CMM framework. Identification of common potential-based proof objects underlying various identifiability and extrapolation results. Demonstration that, when combined with existing model-specific rigidity arguments from prior work, the framework can reproduce their conclusions.
AI.conclusion: CMMs provide a modular, generic mechanism to analyze identifiability and extrapolation in conditional latent-variable models. The attribute potentials capture the essential constraints linking observed attribute variation to latent transitions and enable a unified, algebraic approach that unifies and recovers existing results when paired with their rigidity arguments.

title: Mobile Pedipulation for Object Sliding via Hierarchical Control on a Wheeled Bipedal Robot
summary: In this letter, we present a hierarchical control framework that enables wheeled bipedal robots to perform planar object sliding tasks with their wheeled legs. The proposed approach formulates a nonlinear model predictive controller (NMPC) based on a reduced-order three rigid bodies (TRB) dynamical model that explicitly accounts for the hip roll degree of freedom and multiple wheel-environment contact modes, which is essential for lateral stepping and pedipulation tasks. Within this framework, the NMPC simultaneously regulates robot locomotion and interaction forces, allowing the robot to stably execute both rolling and object manipulation behaviors. A trajectory-optimization-based robot-object motion planner is developed to generate reference motions that incorporate stick-slip transitions in ground-object contact. Two representative pedipulation motions, namely scooting and lateral sliding, are validated through real-world hardware experiments, in which the robot successfully retrieves a 1 kg object from under a desk and slides a 4 kg object over a distance of 0.228 m via scooting.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical NMPC framework for wheeled bipedal robots enabling planar object sliding via reduced-order TRB dynamics with hip roll and multi-contact modeling; validated on hardware for scooting and lateral sliding.
AI.motivation: To enable simultaneous locomotion and manipulation (pedipulation) with wheeled biped robots by explicitly modeling contact modes and hip dynamics, achieving stable rolling and object interaction.
AI.method: Develop a nonlinear model predictive controller based on a reduced-order three rigid bodies model that includes hip roll and multiple wheel-environment contact modes; integrate a trajectory-optimization-based planner to generate reference motions with stick-slip transitions; validate on hardware for scooting and lateral sliding tasks.
AI.result: The framework enables stable rolling and manipulation; hardware experiments demonstrate retrieval of a 1 kg object from under a desk and sliding a 4 kg object by 0.228 m via scooting.
AI.conclusion: The integrated NMPC and planning approach successfully achieves coordinated locomotion and pedipulation on wheeled bipeds; it handles contact transitions and interaction forces, enabling practical soft manipulation tasks with light to moderate mass objects.

title: HandwritingAgent: Language-Driven Handwriting Synthesis in Scalable Vector Space
summary: Teaching machines to emulate natural handwriting styles remains an open challenge, as it requires synthesizing stroke sequences that dynamically vary in shape, texture, pressure and script - not only across individuals, but also within a single person's handwriting. Attempts at this challenge have largely explored deep learning methods in both online and offline settings. However, these approaches are often constrained by style-specific architectural choices, heavy reliance on large datasets, high compute costs, and a lack of flexible control over writing styles through natural language. To this end, we introduce HandwritingAgent, a language-driven agent that can synthesize natural handwriting sequences directly in Scalable Vector Graphics (SVG) format with no need for style-specific training. The agent leverages a large reasoning model to geometrically analyse and autoregressively generate target handwritten glyphs as stroke sequences in a discrete grid canvas environment. Generation is conditioned on texts provided in either conversational or non-conversational mode, along with a reference handwriting-style image. Experiments on diverse handwriting tasks spanning imitation, recognition, multi-lingual handwriting synthesis, and generation of complex handwritten maths and science expressions indicate substantial improvement in performance, with HandwritingAgent matching or surpassing state-of-the-art generative handwriting models, while providing a more efficient, controllable, and generalizable synthesis method.

AI.tldr: HandwritingAgent is a language-driven SVG handwriting synthesis framework that avoids style-specific training, using a large reasoning model to generate stroke sequences on a discrete grid conditioned on text and a reference style, achieving competitive or superior results across imitation, recognition, multilingual, and mathematical handwriting tasks with efficient, controllable, and generalizable outputs.
AI.motivation: Emulating natural handwriting is hard due to dynamic stroke variation across and within individuals; current deep-learning approaches rely on style-specific architectures, large datasets, high compute, and have limited natural-language interpretability and control.
AI.method: Introduce HandwritingAgent, a language-driven agent that geometrically analyzes and autoregressively generates target handwritten glyphs as stroke sequences in a discrete grid canvas, outputting SVG without style-specific training. It conditions on textual input (conversational or non-conversational) and a reference handwriting-style image, leveraging a large reasoning model to plan and render strokes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across imitation, recognition, multilingual handwriting synthesis, and complex mathematical/scientific expressions shows substantial improvement, with HandwritingAgent matching or surpassing state-of-the-art generative handwriting models, while offering greater efficiency, controllability, and generalizability.
AI.conclusion: A language-driven, style-agnostic handwriting synthesis framework that can produce diverse, high-quality handwriting outputs without task-specific training, supporting multilingual and mathematical content and enabling flexible control via natural language.

title: PSyGenTAB: A Privacy-Preserving Framework for Synthetic Clinical Tabular Data Generation via Constrained Optimization
summary: The development of medical AI is constrained by limited access to high-quality clinical data due to institutional silos and strict privacy regulations such as HIPAA and GDPR. Synthetic data generation offers a potential solution, but existing methods lack principled mechanisms to explicitly manage the privacy-utility trade-off, often degrading clinically meaningful patterns or risking patient re-identification. We present PSyGenTAB, a privacy-preserving generative framework that formulates synthetic healthcare data generation as a constrained optimization problem solved using the Augmented Lagrangian Method. By embedding configurable privacy constraints directly into model training, PSyGenTAB enforces minimum privacy thresholds while maximizing clinical data utility. Across multiple clinically motivated benchmarks, PSyGenTAB preserves inter-feature clinical relationships and minority-class diagnostic patterns essential for reliable health AI. Downstream evaluation using Train-on-Synthetic, Test-on-Real and Train-on-Real, Test-on-Synthetic protocols shows that models trained on synthetic data achieve performance comparable to those trained on real patient records. Privacy auditing further demonstrates reduced exact record reproduction and strong resilience to membership inference attacks. These results establish PSyGenTAB as a principled framework for balancing privacy protection and clinical utility in synthetic healthcare data, supporting secure cross-institutional AI development.

AI.tldr: PSyGenTAB is a principled privacy-preserving framework for synthetic healthcare data that uses constrained optimization (Augmented Lagrangian) to balance privacy and utility, preserving clinical patterns and enabling effective downstream AI while reducing re-identification risk.
AI.motivation: To overcome data access barriers in healthcare due to privacy regulations and institutional silos, enabling cross-institutional AI via synthetic data without sacrificing essential clinical patterns or privacy.
AI.method: Formulates synthetic data generation as a constrained optimization problem solved with the Augmented Lagrangian Method. Embeds configurable privacy constraints directly into model training to guarantee minimum privacy thresholds while maximizing data utility. Includes privacy auditing and evaluates with Train-on-Synthetic/Test-on-Real and Train-on-Real/Test-on-Synthetic protocols.
AI.result: PSyGenTAB preserves inter-feature clinical relationships and minority-class diagnostic patterns. Models trained on synthetic data perform comparably to those trained on real data across benchmark tasks. Privacy auditing shows reduced exact record reproduction and resilience to membership inference attacks.
AI.conclusion: PSyGenTAB provides a principled framework to balance privacy protection and clinical utility in synthetic healthcare data, enabling secure cross-institutional AI development.

title: Seeing Through Occlusion: Deterministic Arm Kinematic Correction for Robot Teleoperation
summary: Markerless, single-RGB-D-camera motion capture provides a low-cost and non-invasive alternative to conventional marker-based systems for robot teleoperation; however, depth estimation often degrades in the presence of self-occlusion, particularly during upper-limb motion. This paper presents an Arm Kinematic Correction (AKC) method that improves depth estimation by enforcing geometric constraints based on constant arm lengths. The proposed approach reconstructs occluded joint depths by leveraging wrist positions and predefined arm lengths via a deterministic formulation based on the Pythagorean theorem, thereby avoiding the need for complex probabilistic modeling or parameter tuning. Experimental validation against a Vicon reference system demonstrates reliable performance for both static and dynamic joint motions, evaluated using root-mean-square error (RMSE) and Pearson correlation. Furthermore, motion-mapping teleoperation is successfully demonstrated in both simulated and physical robot environments. The results show that AKC enhances robustness and preserves anatomical consistency under long-duration, severe self-occlusion, even when paired with less reliable temporal filters, highlighting its practicality for real-time applications such as robot teleoperation and human-robot interaction.

AI.tldr: Deterministic arm-length constraints improve depth inference in markerless RGB-D motion capture under self-occlusion for teleoperation.
AI.motivation: Self-occlusion in single RGB-D setups degrades depth estimation; robust, real-time depth reconstruction is needed for upper-limb motion in robot teleoperation.
AI.method: Arm Kinematic Correction (AKC) enforces constant arm lengths by exploiting wrist positions and predefined arm segments via a Pythagorean-deterministic formulation to reconstruct occluded joint depths, avoiding probabilistic models or parameter tuning.
AI.result: Compared with Vicon, AKC yields reliable RMSE and Pearson correlation for static and dynamic motions; enables successful motion-mapping teleoperation in simulated and physical robots; demonstrates robustness under long-duration severe self-occlusion even with unreliable temporal filters.
AI.conclusion: AKC provides a practical, real-time solution for anatomically consistent depth reconstruction in markerless RGB-D systems, enhancing robustness for robot teleoperation and human–robot interaction.

title: Fuzzy-Geometric Branch-Point Modeling for Structure-Aware Augmentation of Handwritten Chinese Characters
summary: Data scarcity and structural distortion significantly limit handwriting recognition in high-security authentication. Existing augmentation methods often cause topological and morphological damage, particularly when processing complex Chinese characters where stroke intersections, ligatures, and sharp turns render traditional branch-point detection unreliable. To address this, this paper proposes a fuzzy geometry-driven structure-aware (FGSA) augmentation framework. We model branch points as fuzzy sets within the skeleton space, constructing a continuous branch-point membership field by integrating topological neighborhood evidence with direction field divergence. This membership field is adaptively optimized via an unsupervised surrogate objective, enabling robust stroke decoupling without manual annotation. Finally, kinematically-aligned samples are synthesized through parameterized cubic Bézier reconstruction and multi-strategy perturbations, ensuring a balance between structural fidelity and sample diversity. Moreover, we establish LZUSig, a large-scale, highly challenging dataset specifically dedicated to fine-grained structural degradation in Chinese handwritten signatures. Extensive experiments on CASIA-HWDB1.1, ChiSig, and LZUSig demonstrate that FGSA significantly reduces the word-level error rate ($Δ$WER), achieving optimal recognition gains over the compared baselines. More importantly, it strikes a robust trade-off among task gain, structural fidelity, and discriminative feature preservation, offering a highly controllable solution for handwriting augmentation.

AI.tldr: FGSA introduces a fuzzy-geometry, structure-aware augmentation for Chinese handwriting that models branch points as fuzzy sets in the skeleton, optimizes a branch-point membership field without labels, and synthesizes samples via kinematic cubic Bézier reconstruction with perturbations; shows improved word-level error rate on CASIA-HWDB1.1, ChiSig, and a new LZUSig dataset, with a strong fidelity-diversity control.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity and structural distortion limit handwriting recognition, particularly for complex Chinese characters where stroke intersections, ligatures, and sharp turns challenge topology-aware augmentation.
AI.method: Model branch points as fuzzy sets in skeleton space to create a continuous branch-point membership field using neighborhood evidence and direction-field divergence. Optimize this field with an unsupervised surrogate objective. Generate samples with parameterized cubic Bézier reconstruction and multi-strategy perturbations to balance structural fidelity and diversity. Introduce LZUSig dataset focusing on fine-grained structural degradation.
AI.result: FGSA reduces word-level error rate (ΔWER) and achieves optimal recognition gains over baselines across CASIA-HWDB1.1, ChiSig, and LZUSig. Demonstrates robust trade-off between task gain, structural fidelity, and discriminative feature preservation, and offers controllable augmentation.
AI.conclusion: FGSA provides a structure-preserving, controllable augmentation framework for handwriting recognition under data scarcity and degradation, with strong empirical gains and the introduction of a challenging new dataset (LZUSig) for evaluating structural degradation.

title: Sparsity Curse: Understanding RLVR Model Parameter Space from Model Merging
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) has emerged as a powerful post-training paradigm that surpasses Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) in eliciting reasoning intelligence and resisting catastrophic forgetting. Recent studies further reveal that RLVR induces highly sparse and off-principal parameter updates compared to SFT. This naturally raises the question: does such sparsity make RLVR models more amenable to model merging? If so, model merging would offer a scalable, training-free path to aggregate diverse reasoning capabilities from independently trained RLVR models. Surprisingly, we find the opposite, uncovering a sparsity curse: the sparse RLVR updates are spread farther apart in parameter space, forming near-orthogonal shortcuts that make aggregation inherently fragile. This is likely rooted in the stochasticity of RL optimization and the diversity of emergent reasoning patterns. Unlike SFT models that converge to shared, flat basins and merge naturally, RLVR models suffer severe degradation under standard merging methods. Through systematic empirical analysis of the update geometry, we characterize the mechanisms behind this failure and propose Sensitivity-aware Resolving Merging (SAR-Merging), a merging recipe tailored for the unique structure of RLVR parameter spaces. SAR-Merging resolves conflicts in overlapping update regions via Fisher Information-based sensitivity arbitration, followed by magnitude-aware sparsification and rescaling to preserve fragile reasoning pathways. Experiments on mathematical and coding benchmarks demonstrate that SAR-Merging substantially outperforms existing merging methods on RLVR models, enabling both single-task enhancement and multi-capability fusion.

AI.tldr: RLVR updates are sparse and near-orthogonal, making model merging fragile; the paper introduces SAR-Merging to enable robust, training-free merging of RLVR models.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable aggregation of diverse reasoning capabilities from independently trained RLVR models via merging despite sparse/off-principal updates that hinder existing methods.
AI.method: Systematic empirical analysis of update geometry; Fisher Information-based sensitivity arbitration; magnitude-aware sparsification; rescaling; applied to RLVR models.
AI.result: SAR-Merging substantially outperforms existing merging methods on RLVR benchmarks, enabling single-task enhancement and multi-capability fusion; demonstrated on mathematical and coding benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Sparsity in RLVR updates causes fragility in standard merging; a sensitivity-aware, sparsity-preserving approach enables robust model merging for RLVR models.

title: Shape Sensing of Continuum Robots using Direct Laser Writing
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Continuum robots offer a promising approach for minimally invasive and natural-orifice surgical procedures due to their inherent compliance and dexterity. However, this flexibility also makes estimating the current shape of the robot challenging. Several approaches have been used to reconstruct the shape of these robots, including imaging, optical sensing, magnetic sensing, and resistive sensing. Strain sensors fabricated using direct laser writing (DLW) could provide an alternative sensing method. This technique involves using a laser to induce carbonization of certain polymers to create graphene patterns, such as strain sensors. In this paper, we demonstrate how a flexible continuum joint and a DLW sensor can be machined as one monolithic structure using the same laser and the same setup. The fabricated sensors are characterized using linear and nonlinear models, which are used to predict the joint angle with error as low as 1.76 degrees. Furthermore, we demonstrate how a DLW sensor can be used to implement closed-loop control in a robotic joint, achieving tracking error under 3 degrees.

AI.tldr: Direct laser writing creates monolithic DLW graphene-pattern strain sensors on flexible continuum joints, enabling accurate angle estimation and closed-loop control with low error.
AI.motivation: Shape estimation is challenging in continuum robots; need compact, integrated sensing; DLW carbonization yields graphene-like sensors compatible with the joint material.
AI.method: Fabricate a flexible continuum joint and DLW sensor in a single monolithic process using the same laser. Characterize with linear and nonlinear models to map sensor output to joint angle; implement closed-loop control for tracking.
AI.result: Joint angle prediction error as low as 1.76 degrees; closed-loop tracking error under 3 degrees.
AI.conclusion: DLW-based DLW graphene-pattern strain sensors integrated in continuum joints provide accurate, compact sensing and reliable closed-loop control, highlighting potential for streamlined fabrication and real-time shape estimation in continuum robots.

title: Where Will They Go? Modelling Multimodal Pedestrian Manoeuvres from Ego-centric Videos
comment: Accepted at The IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) 2026
summary: Pedestrian trajectory prediction from an ego-centric camera is challenging since it depends on complex interactions with vehicles and scene context, as well as the intention of the pedestrian. By modelling correlation and intent from the historical and future trajectories of the pedestrian, it will usually result in a multimodal (i.e. multiple modes) distribution. Existing stochastic predictors often sample multiple futures from a single unimodal distribution, which can yield sub-optimal 'mixed-mode' trajectories that lie between distinct motion patterns and become implausible in real scenes. In this paper, we propose MMPM, a mode-aware framework that separately models future trajectory distributions into semantically meaningful modes based on the pedestrian's crossing behavior. MMPM consists of two modules: behavior-aware Pedestrian Interaction Module (PIM) that jointly captures pedestrian-vehicle and pedestrian-environment interactions by introducing gaze, head and hand gesture, and a CVAE-based Mode-aware Trajectory Predictor (MTP) module to model the future trajectory distributions on two modes, crossing and non-crossing the road, separately. A query-based decoder further enforces mode consistency during decoding. Experiments on PIE and JAAD datasets show that our method surpasses state-of-the-art baselines. Our proposed MTP is model-agnostic, which can be integrated into existing frameworks such as BiTrap-NP and SGNet-ED to further improve future trajectory prediction performance. We additionally introduce a data-driven validation protocol that matches predictions to spatio-temporally consistent ground-truth trajectories, demonstrating improved frame-wise displacement errors over previous work.

AI.tldr: A mode-aware trajectory prediction framework (MMPM) that decomposes pedestrian futures into semantically meaningful crossing and non-crossing modes using a behavior-aware interaction module and a CVAE-based mode-aware predictor, achieving improvements on PIE/JAAD and being integrable into existing models; introduces a data-driven validation protocol.
AI.motivation: Pedestrian trajectory prediction is inherently multimodal due to varying pedestrian intent and interactions with vehicles and scene context. Unimodal or single-sample predictors yield mixed-mode, implausible trajectories. A semantically grounded, mode-aware approach can produce more accurate and plausible futures.
AI.method: Two-part architecture: (1) Behavior-aware Pedestrian Interaction Module (PIM) capturing pedestrian-vehicle and pedestrian-environment interactions using cues like gaze, head pose, and hand gestures; (2) CVAE-based Mode-aware Trajectory Predictor (MTP) modeling distributions on two modes (crossing vs. non-crossing). A query-based decoder enforces mode-consistency during decoding. The MTP is designed to be model-agnostic and can be integrated into existing frameworks (e.g., BiTrap-NP, SGNet-ED). A data-driven validation protocol matches predictions to spatio-temporally consistent ground-truth trajectories.
