AI.result: On PIE and JAAD datasets, MMPM surpasses state-of-the-art baselines. The MTP component improves future trajectory prediction and is compatible with existing architectures. The proposed validation protocol demonstrates improved frame-wise displacement errors compared to prior work.
AI.conclusion: Mode-aware decomposition of future trajectories into semantically meaningful crossing and non-crossing modes reduces mixed-mode artifacts and yields more plausible multimodal predictions. The framework is model-agnostic and practical for integration into current systems, with a robust data-driven evaluation method.

title: On the Residual Scaling of Looped Transformers: Stability and Transferability
summary: Looped (weight-tied) Transformers apply a shared residual block $N$ times ($h \leftarrow h + \varepsilon\,f(h)$, same $f$ at each step), increasing effective depth without adding parameters. Prior depth-scaling analyses prescribe $\varepsilon = 1/\!\sqrt{L}$ for depth-$L$ residual networks. We show that this is insufficient for looped architectures: weight sharing makes residual updates correlated across iterations, requiring the stronger scaling $\varepsilon = 1/N$. For multi-layer blocks ($L$ unique layers looped $N$ times), we derive a factored parameterization $\varepsilon = λ/(N\!\sqrt{L})$ that separates the two sources of growth: $1/N$ controls the within-layer loop correlation, and $1/\!\sqrt{L}$ controls the across-layer variance. A key consequence is that the optimal learning rate depends only on the number of unique layers $L$, not on the loop count $N$, enabling direct hyperparameter transfer from small to large $N$ without retuning. Experiments on looped Transformers confirm that $1/N$ scaling improves trainability and yields better loss than $1/\!\sqrt{N}$ scaling across loop counts.

AI.tldr: For weight-tied (looped) Transformers, depth-scaling must use residual step size epsilon ~ 1/N, not 1/sqrt(L); with L unique layers looped N times, use epsilon = lambda/(N sqrt(L)). This yields learning-rate that depends only on L, enabling hyperparameter transfer from small to large N. Empirical results show 1/N scaling improves trainability and reduces loss across loop counts.
AI.motivation: Weight sharing across loop iterations induces correlation in residual updates, invalidating standard depth-scaling (1/sqrt(L)). The work seeks principled scaling laws for looped architectures and practical guidance for hyperparameter tuning.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of residual-update scaling in looped networks; introduction of a factored parameterization epsilon = lambda/(N sqrt(L)); separation of intra-loop correlation (1/N) from across-layer variance (1/sqrt(L)); empirical evaluation on looped Transformer variants across different N and L comparing 1/N versus 1/sqrt(N).
AI.result: 1/N scaling improves trainability and achieves lower loss than 1/sqrt(N) across loop counts; the optimal learning rate depends on L only, not on N; the proposed lambda/(N sqrt(L)) parameterization enables transferring hyperparameters from small to large N.
AI.conclusion: Standard depth-scaling (1/sqrt(L)) is insufficient for weight-tied loops. The corrected scaling is 1/N (with a factorized form for multiple layers), which decouples intra-loop and inter-layer growth and supports cross-N hyperparameter transfer in looped Transformers.

title: A Mixed-Reality Testbed for Autonomous Vehicles
summary: We propose a mixed-reality, hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testbed for autonomous vehicles that seamlessly integrates a physical testbed of mobile robots with a high-fidelity simulation environment. The virtual simulation enables the creation of diverse, safety-critical driving scenarios to validate state-of-the-art perception, planning, and control algorithms, while augmenting simulations with physical robots equipped with multimodal sensors in photorealistic virtual environments further facilitating rigorous validation. Our testbed also features vehicular connectivity using wireless communication and can accommodate a large number of agents through the combination of physical robots and virtual simulated agents, supporting research on multi-agent systems including Connected and Autonomous Vehicles (CAVs). Finally, we present a safety-guaranteed framework combining perception, planning and a novel online learning-based controller using Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) for CAVs. Experiments using the proposed framework are used to validate and demonstrate the key functionalities and the overall utility of the testbed to bridge the gap between simulation and real-world hardware deployment.

AI.tldr: A mixed-reality, hardware-in-the-loop testbed integrating physical robot platforms with high-fidelity simulation to validate autonomous-vehicle perception, planning, and control in diverse, safety-critical, multi-agent scenarios, featuring a safety-guaranteed framework based on perception, planning, and an online learning controller using Control Barrier Functions (CBFs); experimentally validated to bridge simulation and real hardware deployment.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between simulation and real-world hardware deployment for autonomous vehicles, enabling safe validation of perception, planning, and control in diverse, multi-agent scenarios and ensuring safety through formal guarantees.
AI.method: Develop a mixed-reality, hardware-in-the-loop testbed that combines a physical mobile-robot testbed with a high-fidelity simulation environment. Enable multimodal sensing on physical robots in photorealistic virtual environments, with wireless vehicular connectivity and scalable deployment of many agents (physical and simulated). Introduce a safety-guaranteed framework that integrates perception, planning, and an online-learning-based controller using Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) for CAVs. Validate the framework experimentally to demonstrate capabilities and utility in bridging simulation and real hardware.
AI.result: The framework and testbed are shown to function as intended: enabling integration of hardware and simulation, supporting multi-agent CAV research, and providing safety guarantees via the CBF-based controller, with experiments demonstrating key functionalities and the overall utility of bridging simulation and real hardware deployment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed mixed-reality HIL testbed with a safety-guaranteed CBF-based controller constitutes a versatile platform for validating autonomous-vehicle perception, planning, and control in diverse, safety-critical, multi-agent scenarios, and effectively bridges the gap between simulation and real-world hardware deployment.

title: DreamReg: Belief-Driven World Model for 2D-3D Ultrasound Registration
summary: Ultrasound (US) is widely used for surgical navigation, yet real-time registration between intraoperative 2D slices and preoperative 3D volumes remains challenging due to partial observability, speckle noise, and the action-dependent US acquisition. Existing methods are one-shot or short-horizon, making it hard for them to gather evidence over time or capture how surgeons adjust probe motion based on on-screen feedback. We propose DreamReg, a belief-driven world-model framework that formulates 2D-3D registration as belief updating over rigid transformations. DreamReg maintains a latent belief state that summarizes past observations and poses information, and continuously refines the transformation through learned dynamics as new slices arrive. During training, DreamReg is exposed to probe-motion trajectories that mimic clinical scanning behavior and learns to update its belief by conditioning pose refinement on the current US observation. During inference, DreamReg refines registration via internal imagination: it rolls out the learned world model to simulate candidate probe motions and their predicted observations, and integrates these imagined outcomes to converge to an accurate rigid transformation. Experiments on CAMUS and u-RegPro datasets demonstrate improved robustness and competitive registration accuracy for real-time guidance compared with state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A belief-driven world-model approach (DreamReg) for real-time 2D–3D ultrasound registration that updates pose by imagining future observations.
AI.motivation: Real-time 2D–3D registration during ultrasound is hindered by partial observability, speckle noise, and action-dependent US acquisition. One-shot or short-horizon methods struggle to accumulate evidence over time or model how surgeons adjust probe motion based on feedback.
AI.method: DreamReg maintains a latent belief state summarizing past observations and poses, and uses learned dynamics to refine the rigid transformation as new slices arrive. It is trained on probe-motion trajectories mimicking clinical scanning, updating its belief conditioned on current US observations. During inference, it performs internal imagination by rolling out the world model to simulate candidate probe motions and predicted observations, integrating these imagined outcomes to converge to the correct 3D pose.
AI.result: Experiments on the CAMUS and u-RegPro datasets show improved robustness and competitive registration accuracy for real-time guidance compared with state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: A belief-driven, model-based framework can effectively fuse temporal evidence and prospective probe motions for robust real-time US–CT/volumetric registration; the approach opens avenues for richer temporal reasoning in ultrasound-guided navigation and could extend to non-rigid or active learning settings.

title: Hierarchical Attention via Domain Decomposition

AI.tldr: A two-level, overlapping Schwarz–inspired attention operator improves learning of nonlocal inverse operators by combining local subdomain attention with a coarse global block, outperforming a global low-rank attention baseline with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: To efficiently learn global operators in a finite-dimensional SPD setting by merging local, subdomain corrections with coarse, global information, mitigating the limitations of dense global attention.
AI.method: Formulate the inverse operator M_θ^{-1} as a two-level additive structure: a coarse-level term Φ Q0 K0^T Φ^T plus a sum of local, overlapping-subdomain terms R_i^T D_i^{1/2} Q_i K_i^T D_i^{1/2} R_i. R_i restricts to subdomains, D_i are partition-of-unity weights, and Φ is coarse interpolation. Train on a 1D diffusion problem (homogeneous Dirichlet) discretized to SPD matrices; baseline is a global softmax-free low-rank attention QK^T.
AI.result: The domain-decomposition attention trains faster and yields more accurate approximations than the global low-rank baseline while using substantially fewer parameters, for synthetic Fourier RHS.
AI.conclusion: A two-level domain-decomposition attention operator effectively captures nonlocal interactions with reduced parameter count, offering improved training efficiency and accuracy over global attention in operator learning tasks and suggesting broader applicability to other nonlocal learning problems.

title: Observability and Consistency Analysis for Visual-Inertial Navigation with Anchored Feature Parameterizations
comment: Accepted to IEEE/RSJ IROS. 8 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables
summary: This paper presents an analysis of the observability and consistency properties of filtering-based visual-inertial navigation systems (VINS) that utilize anchored feature representations. The unobservable subspace of VINS with anchored landmark parameterizations is shown to be independent of the estimated landmark state, which leads to improved estimator consistency properties without any additional modifications. However, the unobservable subspace is still found to depend on the estimated navigation state, necessitating additional consistency-enforcing techniques. Two methods to improve the consistency of VINS with anchored feature representations are presented. Simulation results showcase that all estimators employing anchored feature paramterizations exhibit improved consistency properties compared to algorithms that estimate features resolved in a global reference frame, especially in scenarios where feature initialization may be poor. Real-world experiments on the TUM-VI dataset showcase that the use of anchored feature representations alone can yield comparable performance to consistency-improved estimators employing a global feature representation, demonstrating the benefit of using anchored feature parameterizations for VINS.

AI.tldr: Anchored feature representations decouple landmarks from state, improving estimator consistency in VINS; but unobservable subspace still depends on navigation state, requiring consistency-enforcing techniques; two methods proposed; simulated results show improved consistency over global-feature estimators, especially with poor feature initialization; real data (TUM-VI) shows anchored representations can match consistency-improved global methods.
AI.motivation: Study observability and consistency of filtering-based VINS with anchored landmark parameterizations; address inconsistency issues caused by unobservable subspaces and feature initialization; assess practical impact via simulation and real data.
AI.method: Perform observability analysis of VINS with anchored landmarks; derive that the unobservable subspace is independent of landmark estimates but dependent on the estimated navigation state; propose two consistency-improvement methods; run simulations comparing anchored vs global-feature estimators under various initialization conditions; validate on the TUM-VI dataset.
AI.result: Anchored representations yield improved estimator consistency relative to global-feature estimators, especially when feature initialization is poor; real-world results show anchored representation alone can achieve performance comparable to consistency-improved global methods; demonstrates practical benefit of anchored parameterizations.
AI.conclusion: Anchored feature parameterizations are a viable design choice for VINS, improving consistency and achieving competitive performance; however, some level of consistency-enforcing technique remains beneficial due to navigation-state dependence, suggesting a hybrid approach may be optimal.

title: Rethinking Air-Ground Collaboration: A Progressive Cross-Task Benchmark and Socialized Learning Framework
summary: Air-ground collaborative perception is crucial for robust visual understanding in real-world dynamic environments. However, existing studies typically formulate collaboration as single-task cross-view fusion, overlooking the functional dependencies among localization, target association, and fine-grained parsing. In addition, the heterogeneous nature of aerial and ground views introduces substantial geometric, scale, and occlusion discrepancies, making uniform feature sharing vulnerable to negative transfer. To tackle these issues, we model air-ground perception as a progressive cross-task collaboration task and construct the Air-Ground Progressive Collaboration (AGPC) benchmark, a spatio-temporally aligned benchmark comprising more than 745K raw video frames. Built upon this benchmark, we propose Socialized Co-Perception (SCP), a coarse-to-fine framework that organizes collaboration progressively from aerial global localization to ground target association and identity-aware parsing. Its core module, the Dual-Layer Router (DLR), decouples input-side multi-scale expert selection from output-side task-conditioned modulation, enabling selective cross-view and cross-task interaction while suppressing harmful interference. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of SCP. It achieves a 3.73\% coevolutionary gain and a 7.86\% improvement in average downstream performance. These results show that task-conditioned collaboration is more effective than uniform fusion for heterogeneous air-ground perception. The code is available at https://github.com/g1136639260-spec/AGSCP.

AI.tldr: A progressive cross-task air-ground perception framework (SCP) built on the AGPC benchmark using a Dual-Layer Router to enable task-conditioned collaboration, yielding notable co-evolutionary and downstream gains.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of single-task cross-view fusion and negative transfer due to heterogeneity between aerial and ground views; propose a progressive, multi-task collaboration paradigm and a large, aligned benchmark for air-ground perception across localization, target association, and parsing.
AI.method: Introduce AGPC benchmark (745K+ raw frames) and SCP framework that progresses collaboration from aerial global localization to ground target association and identity-aware parsing. Core module Dual-Layer Router (DLR) decouples input-side multi-scale expert selection from output-side task-conditioned modulation, enabling selective cross-view and cross-task interaction while suppressing harmful interference.
AI.result: Experimental results show a 3.73% coevolutionary gain and a 7.86% improvement in average downstream performance, demonstrating that task-conditioned collaboration outperforms uniform fusion for heterogeneous air-ground perception.
AI.conclusion: Task-conditioned collaboration is more effective than uniform fusion for air-ground perception; the code is available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Do as the Romans Do: Learning Universal Behaviors from Heterogeneous Agents
summary: Humans often acquire new skills by observing others, since observed behaviors implicitly reveal how to act in an environment. However, observations drawn from a heterogeneous population introduce conflicting behavioral signals, making it difficult to determine which behaviors are worth imitating. We address this challenge with General Reward Inference and Disentanglement (GRID), a social learning method that extracts universally useful behaviors from a heterogeneous population of demonstrators pursuing different goals. GRID decomposes per-agent reward functions into a general reward, capturing behaviors shared across all agents, and specific rewards, capturing individual preferences and objectives. Training exclusively on the general reward provides a new paradigm of generalist pretraining. It yields a generalist agent that internalizes universal environmental competencies, such as safety and basic task proficiency, without the mode-averaging bias that afflicts standard learning from demonstration techniques. This generalist serves as a superior prior for fine-tuning to downstream tasks, including preferences unseen during training. Experiments across a synthetic basis function decomposition, multi-agent Craftax, and a continuous autonomous driving simulator (Highway-Env) confirm that GRID successfully disentangles reward structure in a semantically meaningful way, outperforms standard learning from demonstration baselines, and enables more efficient and stable specialization.

AI.tldr: GRID learns a universal general reward from heterogeneous demonstrations by disentangling general and specific rewards, enabling generalist pretraining that reduces mode-averaging and improves downstream fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Observations from diverse demonstrators yield conflicting signals about what to imitate. A single rewarded objective cannot capture universal competencies; standard learning from demonstration is prone to mode-averaging bias. GRID aims to extract universally useful behaviors by separating shared (general) rewards from agent-specific (private) rewards.
AI.method: Decompose each demonstrator's reward into a general reward and a specific reward. Train using only the general reward to produce a generalist agent that encodes universal environmental competencies (e.g., safety, basic task proficiency). Use this generalist as a prior and fine-tune for downstream tasks, including preferences unseen during training. Validate on a synthetic basis function decomposition, multi-agent Craftax, and a continuous driving simulator (Highway-Env).
AI.result: GRID successfully disentangles reward structure into semantically meaningful components, outperforms standard learning-from-demonstration baselines, and enables more efficient and stable specialization when fine-tuning to downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling per-agent rewards into a general and a specific component yields a robust generalist pretraining paradigm. The generalist captures universal environmental competencies, providing a strong prior for downstream specialization and supporting efficient adaptation to unseen preferences across domains.

title: Modeling Branches for Active Manipulation using Iterative Parameter Estimation
summary: This study presents a method for modeling diverse plant branches by iteratively estimating material parameters to support delicate branch manipulation. Branch manipulation is necessary in agricultural robotics for plant repositioning, stabilizing, and clearing visual obstructions in dense foliage. The proposed method builds a tetrahedral branch model from point-cloud data and simulates its behavior using the finite element method. Using real observed deformation data, it iteratively estimates branch parameters and then computes an optimal path with a deformation-aware motion planner to move and stabilize branches within another robot's field of view. Across 30 trials on branches with varying geometries and material properties, the proposed method reduced the deformation energy by 35.69% while increasing the path length by 8.10% on average.

AI.tldr: A deformable-branch modeling and manipulation framework that builds a tetrahedral finite-element model from point clouds, iteratively estimates material parameters from observed deformations, and uses a deformation-aware planner to reposition branches for clearer robot view; evaluated on 30 trials with notable reductions in deformation energy and modest increases in path length.
AI.motivation: Enable delicate and reliable branch manipulation in agricultural robotics to reposition, stabilize, or clear visual obstructions in dense foliage.
AI.method: Construct a tetrahedral FE branch model from point-cloud data; simulate behavior with FE methods; use real deformation observations to iteratively estimate material properties; compute an optimal, deformation-aware path to move/position branches within a robot's field of view.
AI.result: Across 30 trials with varying geometries and materials, deformation energy decreased by 35.69% on average, while path length increased by 8.10% on average.
AI.conclusion: Integrating iterative material parameter estimation with deformation-aware planning improves branch manipulation by reducing deformation energy while incurring a moderate cost in travel distance; the approach shows promise for robust, vision-stabilizing manipulation in dense plant canopies.

title: From Bounding Boxes to Visual Reasoning: An On-Policy Data Annotation Tool for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are rapidly advancing toward sophisticated grounded structured visual reasoning. Training models for such advanced capabilities demands a new genre of data that seamlessly unifies spatial coordinates, open-vocabulary descriptions, structured attributes, and topological relationships into a singular representation. However, existing data annotation tools fundamentally fail to meet these intricate demands, suffering from three systematic bottlenecks: limited expressiveness, severe annotation-training decoupling, and poor data reusability. To bridge this infrastructure gap, we introduce an open-source annotation tool, ScreenAnnotator. First, we define a unified annotation atom schema that binds spatial, semantic, and structural primitives into a single unit. Second, we implement an on-policy annotation loop embedded with a Bayesian Annotation Verifier (BAV). Finally, we design a template-driven multi-task data synthesis process dynamically transforms static atoms into diverse multi-dimensional reasoning tasks, eliminating redundant re-annotation. The on-policy loop drives the annotation accept rate to nearly 100% on flowcharts and 77% on GUI screenshots, while steadily reducing per-image annotation time as labeled data accumulate. In the flowchart scenario, fine-tuning a VLM yields 76.1% average accuracy, which is a 35.1% point absolute gain. Our code is available at: https://github.com/WnQinm/Annotator.

AI.tldr: Introduces ScreenAnnotator, an open-source annotation tool with a unified annotation atom schema, on-policy Bayesian verification, and template-driven data synthesis to create expressive multi-task data for grounded visual reasoning; achieves high annotation accept rates and improves VLM fine-tuning performance.
AI.motivation: Current annotation tools cannot simultaneously express spatial, semantic, and structural primitives; suffer from annotation-training decoupling and poor data reusability, hindering training of grounded VLMs.
AI.method: 1) Define a unified annotation atom schema binding spatial, semantic, and structural primitives. 2) Implement an on-policy annotation loop with a Bayesian Annotation Verifier (BAV). 3) Use a template-driven, multi-task data synthesis process to transform static atoms into diverse reasoning tasks, reducing re-annotation.
AI.result: On-policy loop yields high accept rates (nearly 100% for flowcharts, 77% for GUI screenshots); per-image annotation time decreases with more labeled data. In flowchart fine-tuning, VLM achieves 76.1% average accuracy, a 35.1-point absolute gain.
AI.conclusion: ScreenAnnotator enables rapid, expressive, and reusable multi-task data for grounded visual reasoning; code is publicly available, facilitating adoption and further research.

title: Effects of sparsity and superposition on loss in simple autoencoders
summary: One of the major difficulties in the mechanistic interpretability of neural networks is the occurrence of polysemanticity, which suggests that each neuron is typically responsible for multiple different tasks, impeding a clean interpretation of their function. The seminal paper of Elhage et al. (2022) argues that this occurs due to superposition, a phenomenon where the neural network represents distinct features as non-orthogonal directions in a lower-dimensional space, a strategy that allows much greater compression of the data without sacrificing fidelity due to the feature sparsity of input vectors. Elhage et al. (2022) empirically validates these hypotheses in a rather natural and simple autoencoder with sparse inputs. The contribution of the present work is to analyze the mathematical basis for the occurrence and optimality of superposition, while rigorously corroborating some of their findings. In particular, we provide upper and lower bounds for the L2 reconstruction loss, tight in the very sparse regime, for power activation functions. A short list of interesting open problems are also included at the end.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes polysemanticity via superposition and derives L2 reconstruction loss bounds for sparse autoencoders with power activations, providing mathematical insight and confirming aspects of Elhage et al.'s claims.
AI.motivation: To understand why neurons exhibit polysemanticity, by grounding superposition in a mathematical framework and assessing its optimality in sparse representations.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis deriving upper and lower bounds on L2 reconstruction loss in very sparse regimes for power-activated autoencoders; corroboration of prior empirical findings.
AI.result: Tight L2 loss bounds in the very sparse regime for power activations; partial empirical corroboration of Elhage et al.'s superposition claims.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a mathematical basis for the occurrence and optimality of superposition, and highlights open problems for future work.

title: Do as I Do: Dexterous Manipulation Data from Everyday Human Videos
summary: How can we scalably generate data for robotic manipulation, especially on human-like platforms such as dexterous multi-fingered hands? Learning from human videos has recently emerged as a likely answer to this question. However, difficulties in estimating hand-object interaction and crossing the human-to-robot embodiment gap have hindered the adoption of abundant monocular RGB-only human videos as the primary source of robot manipulation data. In this work, we present DO AS I DO, an algorithm to reconstruct and retarget monocular RGB human videos to multi-fingered dexterous robotic hands. DO AS I DO reconstructs hand-object interactions from various egocentric and exocentric in-the-wild video sources. The algorithm then retargets these hand-object interaction estimates into a sequence of actions executable in the real world, yielding robot-complete manipulation data from disparate human videos. Overall, DO AS I DO outperforms previous state of the art in estimating hand-object interactions and extracting dexterous manipulation trajectories from RGB videos, as we show in experiments on datasets with ground truths and on a dataset of video clips collected online. Our experiments enable us to propose an efficacy playbook for practitioners collecting human data for manipulation.

AI.tldr: A pipeline (DO AS I DO) reconstructs and retargets monocular RGB videos of humans to multi-fingered robotic hands to generate scalable dexterous manipulation data, addressing hand-object interaction estimation and human-to-robot embodiment gaps. It outperforms prior methods on both estimation and trajectory extraction, validated on ground-truth and online-video datasets, and provides an efficacy playbook for data collection.
AI.motivation: Scalable data generation for dexterous manipulation on humanoid/hand-like robots using abundant monocular RGB videos; bridging the human-to-robot embodiment gap and improving hand-object interaction estimation from diverse video sources.
AI.method: Reconstructs hand-object interactions from both egocentric and exocentric in-the-wild RGB videos; retargets these interactions into action sequences executable by multi-fingered robotic hands, yielding robot-ready manipulation data from diverse human videos.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art in estimating hand-object interactions and extracting dexterous manipulation trajectories from RGB videos; validated on datasets with ground truth and on a dataset of online video clips.
AI.conclusion: Provides an efficacy playbook for practitioners to collect human data for manipulation and demonstrates a scalable path to generate robot manipulation data from monocular videos.

title: Quantification of Uncertainty with Adversarial Models in Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Reliable pixel-level uncertainty quantification holds the potential to transform clinical workflows by enabling high-fidelity longitudinal monitoring and distinguishing true pathological changes from artifacts. Ideally, these models provide the stability required for critical treatment planning and surgical intervention. However, standard deep learning models often suffer from miscalibration, yielding overconfident predictions that mask underlying vulnerabilities at subtle pathological boundaries. To address this, we propose QUAM-SM, a post-hoc framework using targeted adversarial search to identify "adversarially fragile" pixels. By actively seeking perturbations that expose predictive instability, our method highlights regions where decisions are most vulnerable to being flipped. Importantly, the framework disentangles epistemic uncertainty from aleatoric uncertainty. Experiments on two public datasets with multiple expert annotations demonstrate that QUAM-SM outperforms both standard and recent uncertainty estimation approaches in terms of reliability and boundary sensitivity. Code is available at https://github.com/HanaJebril/quam_sm

AI.tldr: QUAM-SM is a post-hoc adversarial search framework that locates pixel-level, adversarially fragile regions to quantify predictive instability in medical imaging. It disentangles epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty and outperforms standard and recent methods in reliability and boundary sensitivity across two public datasets with expert annotations; code is available online.
AI.motivation: Standard deep models often produce miscalibrated, overconfident pixel-level predictions, especially at subtle pathological boundaries, hindering reliable clinical decision-making and surgical planning. Reliable boundary-aware uncertainty is essential for longitudinal monitoring.
AI.method: A post-hoc framework (QUAM-SM) that uses targeted adversarial search to identify 'adversarially fragile' pixels, exposing where predictions are vulnerable to perturbations. It explicitly separates epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty and evaluates uncertainty reliability and boundary sensitivity on two public datasets with multiple expert annotations.
AI.result: QUAM-SM outperforms both standard and recent uncertainty estimation approaches in terms of reliability and boundary sensitivity across the two datasets. The authors provide code at the given GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a practical and effective means to achieve pixel-level uncertainty reliability in medical imaging, particularly at decision-critical boundaries, potentially improving longitudinal monitoring and treatment planning. It also supports reproducibility via public code.

title: TS-Fault: Benchmarking Time Series Forecasters Against Structural Faults
summary: Time series forecasting (TSF) underpins consequential decisions in energy, transportation, finance, and healthcare, yet TSF models are almost universally ranked by a single number (e.g., average error) on clean held-out data, under the implicit assumption that it predicts deployed reliability. However, real faults are not i.i.d noise but structured events with temporal shape, broken cross-variable dependencies, regime change coupled with missingness, and causal propagation across a sensing pipeline. Treating TSF robustness as a data-quality problem, we present TS-Fault, a benchmark that evaluates forecasting models under explicit, parameterized fault scenarios with controllable semantic difficulty. TS-Fault organizes recurring failures into four modes along two orthogonal axes (observation- vs mechanism-level; univariate vs multivariate) and injects each fault into the most prediction-critical window via a unified importance score. This design enables robustness to be tested against the structures models actually rely on, rather than reduced to generic noise sensitivity. We evaluate 21 models across 6 datasets, 4 modes, and 5 difficulty levels under a paired clean/corrupt protocol. The results reveal three findings that contradict common leaderboard intuition: (i) clean-data accuracy anti-correlates with robustness; (ii) clean rankings are preserved under observation-level faults but reshuffled under mechanism-level faults; and (iii) all catastrophic failures occur under mechanism-level faults, with foundation models achieving the highest clean-data accuracy yet exhibiting the greatest fragility. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/Ray-zyy/TS-Fault.

AI.tldr: TS-Fault is a benchmark for robust evaluation of time-series forecasting models under structured, parameterized faults; it reveals that clean accuracy does not imply robustness and shows mechanism-level faults cause catastrophic failures, even for strong models; code available on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Real faults in time-series data are structured and propagate through sensing pipelines, not i.i.d. noise. Robustness cannot be inferred from clean-data accuracy alone; a goal-driven benchmark is needed to stress-test models on semantically meaningful faults.
AI.method: Propose TS-Fault with four fault modes along two axes (observation-level vs mechanism-level; univariate vs multivariate), injecting faults into the most prediction-critical window via a unified importance score. The benchmark is parameterized by semantic difficulty and evaluated across 21 models, 6 datasets, 4 modes, 5 difficulty levels, using a paired clean/corrupt protocol.
AI.result: Three key findings emerge: (i) clean-data accuracy anti-correlates with robustness; (ii) clean rankings persist under observation-level faults but reshuffle under mechanism-level faults; (iii) catastrophic failures occur only under mechanism-level faults, with foundation models achieving highest clean accuracy yet greatest fragility.
AI.conclusion: TS-Fault provides a principled framework to assess forecasting robustness beyond clean data metrics and highlights the risk of relying on clean performance. The authors release code publicly for reproducibility.

title: Zero-Shot Long-Horizon Dexterous Manipulation via Multi-View 3D-Grounded VLM Reasoning
summary: We present a zero-shot framework for long-horizon dexterous manipulation that grounds language instructions into executable 3D task plans from calibrated multi-view RGB images. Rather than training an end-to-end policy, our system uses a vision-language model (VLM) to produce reference-frame task grounding and primitive-level 2D keypoints, then lifts them into 3D via multi-view fusion. This lifting combines triangulation of view-wise VLM groundings with reference-view ray voting, which searches along a semantic camera ray for geometrically consistent candidates across neighboring views. The resulting 3D keypoints support both pick-and-place and tool-use: for tool-use, we retrieve an object-centric atomic action corresponding to the inferred skill category and align its stored 6D tool trajectory to the scene; for dexterous execution, we expand the lifted grasp keypoint into a task-conditioned grasp affordance region and generate feasible grasp-motion pairs with an arm-hand motion generator. Real-world experiments show improved 3D grounding accuracy and execution reliability over single-view RGB-D grounding and fine-tuned VLA baselines. We further demonstrate long-horizon manipulation through closed-loop status verification and replan, enabling zero-shot execution on unseen objects and tool-use tasks in novel scenes.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot long-horizon dexterous manipulation by grounding language instructions into executable 3D task plans from calibrated multi-view RGB images. Uses a vision-language model to provide reference-frame grounding and 2D keypoints, lifted to 3D by multi-view fusion (triangulation + semantic ray voting). Supports pick-and-place and tool-use, with 6D trajectory alignment or grasp-affordance-based planning. Real-world results show improved 3D grounding accuracy and execution reliability over single-view grounding and fine-tuned baselines, enabling closed-loop replanning for unseen objects in novel scenes.
AI.motivation: Need for robust, scalable dexterous manipulation that generalizes beyond end-to-end policies and single-view grounding. Leverage language grounding and multi-view geometry to obtain 3D task representations for long-horizon manipulation.
AI.method: Ground language instructions with a vision-language model to produce reference-frame grounded 2D keypoints. Lift to 3D via triangulation across views and reference-view ray voting to find geometrically consistent 3D candidates. For tool-use, map to an object-centric atomic action and align a stored 6D trajectory to the scene. For dexterous execution, expand lifted grasp keypoints into a task-conditioned grasp-affordance region and generate feasible grasp-motion pairs with an arm-hand motion generator. Employ closed-loop status verification and replanning for zero-shot execution on unseen objects and tasks.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved 3D grounding accuracy and execution reliability over single-view RGB-D grounding and fine-tuned VLA baselines. Validates long-horizon manipulation with closed-loop verification and replanning, enabling zero-shot execution in novel scenes and with unseen objects and tools.
AI.conclusion: A grounding-and-planning framework that combines multi-view 3D lifting, semantic grounding, and closed-loop replanning to achieve zero-shot long-horizon dexterous manipulation.

title: URDF Synthesis from RGB-D Sequences via Differentiable Joint Inference and Energy-Consistent Verification
summary: Reconstructing simulation-ready digital twins of articulated objects from sensor observations remains constrained by two persistent gaps: (i) part-level geometric reconstruction is decoupled from kinematic-parameter estimation, and (ii) the recovered models often violate basic dynamic invariants such as energy conservation, leading to drift when the URDF is replayed in physics simulators. We present KinemaForge, a constraint-driven pipeline that jointly infers part-level shape, joint topology, and joint parameters from short RGB-D sequences and validates the result against an energy-consistent verifier built on differentiable rigid-body dynamics. The pipeline introduces three components: a kinematic constraint graph that encodes joint-part incidences as soft edges; a differentiable screw-axis solver that backpropagates from rendered observations through Featherstone's articulated-body algorithm to joint parameters; and an energy residual loss that penalises non-physical free responses of the reconstructed model. Across five PartNet-Mobility categories and an internal RGB-D benchmark, KinemaForge reduces the average joint-axis error from 4.52 degrees to 2.83 degrees (-37.4%) over the strongest geometric baseline (PARIS) and from 5.30 degrees to 2.83 degrees (-46.6%) over the interaction-based Ditto baseline, lowers long-horizon simulation drift by 64% (vs. PARIS) over 50 s rollouts, and yields URDFs whose closed-loop manipulation success rate improves by 14.6 percentage points over Ditto in our preliminary evaluation. Code and reconstruction data will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: KinemaForge is a constraint-driven pipeline that jointly recovers part-level geometry, joint topology, and joint parameters from short RGB-D sequences and enforces energy-consistency via a differentiable dynamics verifier to produce simulation-ready URDFs.
AI.motivation: Current pipelines decouple geometric reconstruction from kinematic parameter estimation and yield models that violate energy conservation, causing drift when replayed in physics simulators.
AI.method: Introduce a kinematic constraint graph with soft edges for joint-part incidences; a differentiable screw-axis solver backpropagating through Featherstone's articulated-body algorithm to fit joint parameters; an energy residual loss penalizing non-physical free responses; validate against an energy-consistent differentiable verifier over RGB-D data and PartNet-Mobility categories.
AI.result: Significant improvements: joint-axis error reduced from 4.52° to 2.83° versus PARIS (−37.4%), and from 5.30° to 2.83° versus Ditto (−46.6%); long-horizon drift reduced by 64% over 50 s rollouts; URDFs yield a 14.6 percentage-point higher closed-loop manipulation success vs Ditto; evaluation on five PartNet-Mobility categories and internal RGB-D benchmark.
AI.conclusion: KinemaForge yields energy-consistent, simulation-ready articulated models that integrate geometry and dynamics, enabling more reliable physics-based manipulation and control; code and data to be released upon acceptance.

title: Correcting Sensor-Induced Distribution Drift with Wasserstein Adversarial Learning
comment: This is a preprint sent to Nuclear Science and Techniques journal
summary: The quality of recorded data depends on the stability of the sensor system that acquires it. Sensor motion and aging can degrade the performance and stability of downstream data-driven methods. We present a Wasserstein-GAN-inspired approach for unsupervised inference of physically interpretable transformation parameters that map a changed detector response distribution back to a nominal reference distribution. In contrast to standard generative modeling, the generator is used as a learnable calibration transformation whose trainable weights represent the sought parameters, while the critic provides a distributional distance signal via the Wasserstein objective. We validate the approach on a tracking-detector toy model with controlled layer shifts and demonstrate its application on high-granularity Geant4-simulated calorimeter data with cell-wise aging effects. The method recovers aging coefficients for individual cells with correlation to ground truth and improves agreement between calibrated and reference energy-sum distributions, while exhibiting the expected degradation at increasing channel-to-channel noise levels. These results indicate that adversarial distribution matching can serve as a data-driven component of calibration strategies in settings where direct labels for degradation parameters are unavailable.

AI.tldr: Wasserstein-GAN-inspired unsupervised calibration that learns physically interpretable transformation parameters to map degraded detector response back to nominal distribution, using the generator as a calibration transform and the critic for the Wasserstein distance; validated on a toy tracker model with layer shifts and on Geant4-simulated calorimeter data with cell-wise aging; recovers per-cell aging coefficients correlated with ground truth and improves agreement between calibrated and reference energy-sum distributions, with expected degradation under higher channel-to-channel noise; demonstrates potential for data-driven calibration without direct degradation labels.
AI.motivation: Data quality in detectors hinges on stable sensor response; aging and motion introduce distributional shifts that degrade downstream learning. When degradation parameters lack direct labels, unsupervised methods that align current data distributions to a nominal reference are appealing. The work proposes a calibration transformation learned via an adversarial objective to recover a physically interpretable degradation map.
AI.method: Adopt a Wasserstein-GAN–like framework where a generator implements a parametric calibration transformation. The trainable weights correspond to interpretable degradation/aging parameters per sensor channel. The critic estimates the Wasserstein distance between the transformed (calibrated) distribution and the nominal reference distribution, guiding the calibration parameters to minimize distributional discrepancy without labeled degradation data.
AI.result: The approach recovers aging coefficients per cell that correlate with ground truth; improves agreement between calibrated and reference energy-sum distributions; validates on a toy tracking detector with controlled layer shifts and on high-granularity calorimeter data with cell-wise aging; performance degrades predictably with increasing channel-to-channel noise, consistent with expectations for noisy channels.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial distribution matching via a learnable calibration transform can serve as a data-driven calibration component when direct labels for degradation parameters are unavailable, enabling unsupervised inference of physically meaningful transformation parameters to restore nominal detector response.

title: Learning to Distort: Weakly-Supervised Image Quality Transfer for Prostate DWI Correction
summary: Single-shot echo-planar prostate diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is frequently complicated by geometric distortions, which impact the ability to derive reliable diagnoses from such images. Developing automated correction methods is challenged by the absence of paired distorted and undistorted clinical scans. In this paper, we first propose a novel weakly-supervised image quality transfer (IQT) framework from undistorted to distorted images that utilizes image quality assessment (IQA) signals to supervise the transfer process. Unlike traditional methods that require expensive, voxel-wise paired data or resort to developing unpaired algorithms, our approach utilizes image-level quality labels (here, distorted vs. undistorted) to establish latent quality prototypes within a pre-trained feature space. Recognizing that simulating realistic distortions is more reliable than direct unpaired correction, we describe a weakly-supervised prototype flow matching algorithm to explicitly regularize generative trajectories towards distorted prototypes, producing realistic susceptibility artifacts that mimic clinical degradations. By synthesizing these realistic pairs, we enable a second IQT model to be trained in the forward direction for distortion correction. Experimental results demonstrate that our generated images successfully mimic the diagnostic interference of real-world artifacts, which leads to more capable distortion correction IQT models. In addition to qualitative comparisons, we also conduct exhaustive quantitative evaluations that compare our approach with existing unpaired approaches (e.g., CycleGAN, UNIT-DDPM, and OT-FM) - as either forward or reverse alternatives - by assessing clinical downstream task performance in PI-RADS and Gleason score classification, using both in-distribution and external data sets.

AI.tldr: A weakly-supervised image quality transfer (IQT) framework for correcting geometric distortions in single-shot prostate DWI, using image-level quality labels to form distortion prototypes and a prototype flow matching strategy to synthesize realistic distorted pairs for forward correction, achieving improved downstream diagnostic task performance over unpaired methods.
AI.motivation: Geometric distortions in prostate DWI hamper reliable diagnosis and paired distorted-undistorted scans are scarce. A weakly-supervised approach aims to generate realistic distorted data and train effective correction models without voxel-wise pairs.
AI.method: (1) Learn latent distortion prototypes from undistorted vs. distorted labels in a pre-trained feature space. (2) Use weakly-supervised prototype flow matching to steer generative trajectories toward distorted prototypes, synthesizing realistic artifact pairs. (3) Train a second forward IQT model for distortion correction using synthesized pairs. (4) Evaluate via downstream tasks (PI-RADS, Gleason) on in-distribution and external data, comparing with unpaired baselines (CycleGAN, UNIT-DDPM, OT-FM).
AI.result: The synthetic distorted images plausibly replicate diagnostic interference of real artifacts, enabling improved distortion-correction IQT models and better downstream task performance compared to several unpaired baselines on multiple datasets.
AI.conclusion: Weakly-supervised IQT with prototype flow matching is effective for generating realistic distortions and enabling robust distortion correction, with demonstrated gains on clinically relevant tasks; the approach could generalize to other modalities with geometric distortions.

title: Fair Cognitive Impairment Detection Through Unlearning
summary: Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) is a medical condition characterized by a noticeable decline in memory, language, or thinking abilities. MCI detection from spontaneous speech is promising for scalable screening. However, learned models often exploit demographic cues correlated with labels, resulting in a large performance gap across subgroups. We present a multimodal framework that combines (i) cross-model fusion between modalities (speech, text, and image), and (ii) unlearning using gradient reversal that discourages the shared embedding from encoding task-irrelevant demographic attributes. Evaluated on the multilingual benchmarks TAUKADIAL and PREPARE, our method outperforms the state-of-the-art multilingual and multimodal baseline in MCI classification while substantially reducing the performance gap across patient subgroups (sex and language). We further analyze transfer across datasets, showing that demographic unlearning helps learn more robust representations for MCI detection.

AI.tldr: A multimodal MCI detection framework with demographic unlearning improves accuracy and fairness across subgroups on multilingual benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MCI screening via spontaneous speech is scalable, but models leak demographic cues, causing subgroup disparities.
AI.method: Integrates cross-model fusion across speech, text, and image modalities; applies gradient-reversal-based unlearning to suppress demographic attributes from the shared embedding.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art multilingual/multimodal baselines on MCI classification on TAUKADIAL and PREPARE, and substantially reduces performance gaps across sex and language; shows improved cross-dataset robustness.
AI.conclusion: Demographic unlearning plus multimodal fusion yields more accurate and equitable MCI detection with better generalization.

title: Bridging Single Distortion Artifacts and Mmultifactorial Clinical Quality: Few-shot Biparametric MRI Quality Assessment via Distortion-trained Prototypical Networks
summary: Clinical prostate multi-parametric MRI relies heavily on high-quality diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), yet reading DWI is frequently compromised by geometric distortion, often caused by rectal air. Assessing quality via the PI-QUAL scoring system is an emerging clinical standard, but it is subjective, time-consuming and suffers from a class imbalance where low-quality cases are diverse and relatively scarce. Using the PRIME clinical trial as an example, there are $6\%$ images with PI-QUAL scores lower than 4, $87\%$ of DWI issues are due to distortion. Many of the other clinical quality issues are under-represented. To address this common dual-scarcity of annotated clinical data, we propose a few-shot biparametric prototypical network for automated image quality assessment (IQA). Our framework utilizes a dual-branch 3D ResNet to fuse T2-weighted and DWI features, providing anatomical context to distinguish true morphology from distortion. To handle real-world heterogeneity, we introduce feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM) and a gradient reversal layer (GRL) to align feature distributions conditioned on varying b-values while suppressing acquisition-related biases. We demonstrate that a model meta-trained solely on comparatively objective, readily obtainable distortion labels can effectively adapt to predicting complex, multi-factorial clinical quality scores such as PI-QUAL using only five representative samples. Experimental results on two datasets show that our method significantly outperforms few-shot learning baselines for this challenging IQA task, offering a practically feasible and data-efficient solution for standardizing prostate MRI quality control in clinical workflows.

AI.tldr: Few-shot biparametric prototypical network automates prostate DWI quality assessment under dual-scarcity of annotations by meta-training on distortion labels and adapting to PI-QUAL with five samples.
AI.motivation: PI-QUAL is subjective, time-consuming, and imbalanced; many high-quality examples and diversity of distortions exist, making robust automated IQA challenging. A data-efficient method is needed to standardize clinical QC in prostate MRI.
AI.method: A dual-branch 3D ResNet fuses T2-weighted and DWI features. The model uses FiLM to condition features on varying b-values and a GRL to suppress acquisition biases. It is trained in a few-shot, biparametric prototypical framework and meta-trained on distortion labels; adaptation to PI-QUAL is achieved with only five representative samples.
AI.result: On two datasets, the approach significantly outperforms few-shot baselines for IQA, demonstrating data efficiency and practical potential for standardizing prostate MRI quality control in clinical workflows.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a practical, data-efficient solution to dual-scarcity IQA in clinical prostate MRI, capable of adapting to complex, multi-factor quality scores like PI-QUAL and improving consistency of image quality assessment.

title: Towards Anomaly Detection on Relational Data
summary: Relational databases are widely used for managing structured data in real-world systems. Detecting anomalies from such relational data is crucial for identifying fraud, risks, and abnormal behaviors, yet remains under-explored. The key challenges lie in the intrinsic complexity of relational data: multi-table attributes are high-dimensional and heterogeneous, making sparse abnormal clues easy to overwhelm by normal or irrelevant information; and anomalies may further manifest as abnormal connection patterns across different foreign-key relations, which existing tabular and graph anomaly detection methods are ill-suited to capture. To address them, we propose RelAD, a reconstruction-based framework that captures anomalies from both attribute and relational edge reconstruction. RelAD contains two core modules: conditional sparse-gated attribute reconstruction, which suppresses redundant multi-table attributes and emphasizes abnormal semantic blocks, and dual-view multi-relational edge reconstruction, which detects relation-specific abnormal connections from both intrinsic and behavioral entity profiles. The resulting attribute and relational signals are integrated through a lightweight fusion module to produce the final anomaly score. We further construct 6 benchmark datasets with systematic anomalies, on which extensive experiments show that RelAD consistently outperforms other baselines while achieving competitive efficiency.

AI.tldr: RelAD presents a reconstruction-based framework for detecting anomalies in relational databases by jointly modeling attribute-level and relation-level patterns; it uses conditional sparse-gated attribute reconstruction and dual-view multi-relational edge reconstruction, fused to produce anomaly scores, and validated on 6 benchmarks where it outperforms baselines with competitive efficiency.
AI.motivation: Relational data are high-dimensional and heterogeneous, with anomalies possibly manifesting as abnormal cross-table connection patterns. Existing tabular and graph anomaly detectors struggle to capture these challenges, necessitating a relational-aware reconstruction approach.
AI.method: Two core modules: (1) conditional sparse-gated attribute reconstruction to suppress redundant multi-table attributes and emphasize abnormal semantic blocks; (2) dual-view multi-relational edge reconstruction to detect relation-specific abnormal connections from intrinsic and behavioral entity profiles. A lightweight fusion module integrates attribute and relational signals into a final anomaly score.
AI.result: Constructed 6 benchmark datasets with systematic anomalies; extensive experiments show RelAD consistently outperforms baselines while achieving competitive efficiency.
AI.conclusion: RelAD effectively detects anomalies in relational data by leveraging both attribute and relational signals, demonstrating robust performance across challenging relational benchmarks and offering a scalable, practical approach.

title: Test-Time Adaptation in Optical Coherence Tomography Using Trajectory-Aligned Time-Independent Flow
summary: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is essential in ophthalmology, but inconsistent image quality especially in low-cost devices hinders automated analysis. To address this, we introduce a flow-matching-based test-time adaptation method that generates high-quality surrogate images from noisy inputs. Typically, domain gaps between test and training data cause pixel distribution mismatches during the denoising process. We overcome this by matching the test image's histogram to synthetic reference trajectories, successfully aligning the input with expected distributions. Additionally, we remove the network's time conditioning to account for slight deviations in real-world noise distributions. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in segmenting critical biomarkers for two stages of Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD). Code is available: https://github.com/Veit21/tta-flow.

AI.tldr: A flow-matching-based test-time adaptation (TTA) method for OCT denoising/segmentation that aligns test inputs to synthetic reference trajectories via histogram matching and removes time conditioning to handle real-world noise, achieving state-of-the-art AMD biomarker segmentation on two stages.
AI.motivation: Domain gaps between test and training data cause pixel distribution mismatches and degraded denoising in low-cost OCT devices; a robust, inference-time adaptation is needed to improve downstream AMD biomarker segmentation.
AI.method: Flow-matching-based TTA that generates surrogate high-quality images from noisy inputs. It aligns input distributions by histogram matching to synthetic reference trajectories and removes the network's time conditioning to accommodate real-world noise variability.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance in segmenting AMD biomarkers for two disease stages; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TTA framework effectively mitigates domain shift and noise variability in OCT, enabling reliable automated analysis on low-cost devices and potentially generalizing to other imaging modalities.

title: PACT: Preserving Anchored Cores in Task-vectors for Model Merging
summary: Model merging has emerged as a training-free alternative to multi-task learning, aiming to combine multiple task-specific fine-tuned models into a single multi-task model. Most existing model merging approaches follow the Task Arithmetic paradigm, which decomposes fine-tuned weights into pre-trained parameters and task vectors, and performs merging exclusively in the task-vector space. The effectiveness of this paradigm implicitly relies on the assumption that task-specific knowledge is encoded solely within task vectors. We argue that this assumption generally does not hold due to the intrinsic task preferences of pre-trained models. Specifically, we identify \textbf{Load-Bearing Wall (LBW) dimensions}, namely some task-critical knowledge that remains embedded in the pre-trained weights rather than being fully transferred into task vectors. We characterize LBW dimensions from both scalar-weight and subspace perspectives, thereby covering the major paradigms of existing model merging methods. Our analysis reveals that, by ignoring LBW dimensions, task-vector-based approaches fail to fully resolve task conflicts and may inadvertently damage task-specific knowledge encoded in the pre-trained model, leading to degradation. To address this issue, we propose PACT, which preserves the anchored task-specific cores (i.e., LBW dimensions) within task vectors by aligning their orthogonal complements with the subspace of the pre-trained weights. These aligned subspace components are then removed from the task vectors before applying existing model merging algorithms. Furthermore, we develop an efficient variant based on randomized SVD to improve scalability. PACT can be seamlessly integrated with existing methods. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that PACT consistently enhances mainstream model merging approaches and establishes new state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: PACT identifies Load-Bearing Wall (LBW) dimensions in pre-trained weights, preserves them during task-vector merging, and uses subspace alignment and randomized SVD to remove non-LBW components from task vectors. It integrates with existing merging methods and yields consistent gains, achieving new state-of-the-art on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The Task Arithmetic paradigm assumes task-specific knowledge resides entirely in task vectors. However, pre-trained weights contain task-critical information (LBW) that cannot be fully transferred to task vectors, causing conflicts and possible degradation when merging. This challenges the efficacy of existing, task-vector-based model merging methods.
AI.method: Define LBW dimensions from scalar-weight and subspace perspectives. Align orthogonal complements between task vectors and the pre-trained weight subspace to preserve anchored task cores (LBW). Remove the aligned subspace components from task vectors before merging with existing methods. Propose an efficient randomized SVD variant to scale to large models. PACT is designed to be plug-in with current merging approaches.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks show that PACT consistently improves mainstream model merging methods and achieves new state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: PACT provides a general, scalable enhancement to model merging by safeguarding LBW dimensions, enabling better retention of pre-trained knowledge while leveraging task vectors, and is compatible with existing merging techniques.

title: Performance Gap Analysis between Latin and Arabic Scripts HTR
comment: this paper accepted at TIPS workshop ICPR 2026
summary: Recent studies have shown that handwritten text recognition (HTR) systems perform worse on Arabic-script datasets than on Latin-script data. However, the reasons for this gap are still not well understood due to the lack of controlled comparisons. In this work, we present a comprehensive study of Arabic and Latin scripts HTR using a unified CRNN model for line-level HTR across nine datasets (including KHATT (Arabic), Muharaf (Arabic), NUST-UHWR (Urdu), PHTD (Persian), IAM (English), READ-2016 (German), and others) and di ferent training sizes (K in {100, 500, 1000, 2000, ..., Kfull}). Our results show the performance gap remains: it is large in low-resource settings, decreases with more data, but remains even at full scale, with a consistent difference of 5-7 CER points. We show that annotation quality matters, as many datasets contain labeling errors. Cleaning reduces error rates and narrows the gap, but does not eliminate it. In addition, we find that a fixed number of training samples provides less effective coverage in Arabic due to higher visual variability, requiring more data to learn similar representations. We compare recognition across datasets in terms of the number of text lines and the number of characters, showing an equivalence trade-off. We compare character frequency distributions across scripts and show that Arabic is significantly more heavy-tailed than Latin. Our error analysis reveals that around 30 percent of substitution errors in Arabic datasets (e.g., KHATT) are caused by confusion between visually similar characters, compared to about 15 percent in Latin-script datasets such as IAM.

AI.tldr: A unified CRNN study across nine datasets shows a persistent Arabic–Latin HTR gap (5–7 CER) that remains even with full data. Data quality and higher visual variability in Arabic drive the gap; cleaning helps but does not eliminate it. Arabic has a heavier-tailed character distribution and more substitutions from visually similar glyphs (≈30% vs ~15% in Latin). More data and targeted modeling are required to reduce confusability and coverage gaps.
AI.motivation: To understand the causes of the performance gap between Arabic-script and Latin-script handwritten text recognition under controlled conditions, quantify data-related factors, and assess how data cleaning and script-specific character distributions affect results.
AI.method: Unified CRNN model for line-level HTR trained on nine datasets (Arabic, Urdu, Persian, English, German, etc.). Vary training size K (e.g., 100, 500, 1000, 2000, ..., Kfull). Compare CER gaps, analyze annotation quality, examine dataset properties (number of lines and characters), study character frequency distributions, and perform error analysis focusing on substitutions and confusable glyphs.
AI.result: The performance gap remains: ~5–7 CER points, largest in low-resource settings, narrows with more data but persists at full scale. Annotation cleaning reduces error rates and narrows the gap, yet does not close it. Arabic requires more data to learn similar representations due to higher visual variability. Arabic has a heavier-tailed character distribution. About 30% of Arabic substitution errors (KHATT) arise from confusable characters, versus ~15% for Latin (IAM).
AI.conclusion: Closing the gap requires larger and higher-quality Arabic datasets and modeling approaches that address heavy-tailed character distributions and confusability between visually similar glyphs. Data curation and script-specific strategies are essential.

title: MetaboNet-Bench: A Multi-modal Benchmark for Glucose Forecasting in Type 1 Diabetes
comment: main content in 10 pages with 5 figures; supplementary section with 11 more pages and 5 more figures
summary: Glucose forecasting algorithms are an important aspect of glycemic control management in type 1 diabetes. So far, the research community has developed numerous algorithms and models for forecasting. However, it is well-recognized that the lack of standardized model performance evaluation benchmarks makes fair comparison difficult and hinders further innovation, and thus benchmark standardization is in urgent need. Furthermore, many published glucose forecasting algorithms are limited to CGM data alone, ignoring other multimodal signals such as insulin dosing and carbohydrate intake. Here, we introduce MetaboNet-Bench, a benchmark for multimodal glucose forecasting for patients with type 1 diabetes that provides an extensible open-source evaluation framework for comparison of glucose forecasting algorithms that leverage glucose, insulin, and carbohydrate data. We then demonstrate its utility by benchmarking several recently published glucose forecasting models and a custom multimodal time-series model, representing different model architectures. The results show that the benefit of adding data modalities is conditioned on the complexity of the model and that incorporating more clinical metrics helps identify meaningful gaps to fill for future research.

AI.tldr: Introduces MetaboNet-Bench, an extensible open-source benchmark for multimodal glucose forecasting in type 1 diabetes, enabling fair comparison of models using glucose, insulin, and carbohydrate data; benchmarks multiple models and shows multimodal data helps depending on model complexity and metric choices.
AI.motivation: There is no standardized benchmark for evaluating glucose forecasting models, and many studies rely on CGM data alone, hindering fair comparison and slowing progress. Multimodal data integration (glucose, insulin, carbs) is underexplored.
AI.method: Proposes MetaboNet-Bench as an open-source evaluation framework for multimodal glucose forecasting. Demonstrates utility by benchmarking several published glucose forecasting models and a custom multimodal time-series model across glucose, insulin, and carbohydrate inputs, using multiple clinical metrics.
AI.result: Findings indicate that the benefit of adding data modalities depends on model complexity; incorporating more clinical metrics helps identify meaningful gaps for future research and model improvements.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark enables standardized evaluation and fair comparison in multimodal glucose forecasting, guiding future research toward richer data integration and more robust models.

title: Stealthy World Model Manipulation via Data Poisoning
comment: 41 pages, 8 figures, 11 tables. Submitted to NeurIPS 2026
summary: Model-based learning agents use learned world models to predict future states, plan actions, and adapt to new environments. However, the process of updating world models from collected experience creates a training-time attack surface: adversarially poisoned fine-tuning trajectories can manipulate the learned dynamics and thereby corrupt downstream planning. In this paper, we propose SWAAP, the first two-stage data poisoning framework for learned world models. In the first stage, SWAAP identifies a harmful target world model that induces low-return behavior under planning while remaining close to clean dynamics, using first-order bilevel optimization enabled by a transition-gradient theorem. In the second stage, SWAAP realizes this target through stealth-constrained gradient matching, modifying only a limited fraction of fine-tuning transition targets so that the induced training gradients steer the victim model toward the adversarial target, while a prediction-error regularizer encourages the poisoned targets to remain close to the world model's natural approximation error. To assess attack stealthiness, we evaluate defenses and detectability across three stages of the poisoning pipeline: pre-training detection of poisoned transitions, robust training during fine-tuning, and test-time monitoring of the resulting world model. Across diverse continuous-control tasks, SWAAP causes substantial performance degradation while keeping poisoned transitions close to clean data and evading the evaluated non-adaptive residual/CUSUM/TRIM-style defenses. These results reveal a practical vulnerability in world-model adaptation pipelines and highlight the need for robustness methods that protect both world-model training data and learned dynamics.

AI.tldr: A two-stage data poisoning framework (SWAAP) targets learned world models by poisoning fine-tuning trajectories to derail planning, while staying stealthy near clean data; evaluated to degrade performance and evade defenses, highlighting robustness needs.
AI.motivation: Expose the training-time attack surface of learned world models and demonstrate how adversarial fine-tuning can corrupt dynamics and downstream planning.
AI.method: Two-stage attack: Stage 1 use first-order bilevel optimization with a transition-gradient theorem to identify a target world model that yields low planning returns yet stays close to clean dynamics. Stage 2 enact the target via stealth-constrained gradient matching, perturbing only a small subset of fine-tuning transition targets; add a prediction-error regularizer to keep poisoned targets near the world model's natural approximation error.
AI.result: SWAAP substantially degrades performance across diverse continuous-control tasks, while keeping poisoned transitions close to clean data and evading evaluated non-adaptive defenses (residual/CUSUM/TRIM). Demonstrates a practical vulnerability in world-model adaptation pipelines.
AI.conclusion: World-model adaptation pipelines are vulnerable to training-time data poisoning; robustness methods must protect both training data and learned dynamics to mitigate such attacks.

title: LARE: Low-Attention Region Encoding for Text-Image Retrieval
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Efficient Multimodal Question Answering (EMM-QA). Code: https://github.com/AbdulmalikDS/LARE ; Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/AbdulmalekDS/Dense-Set
summary: Image retrieval in crowded scenes is particularly challenging due to the salience bias of conventional visual encoders, which tend to focus on dominant objects while neglecting low-attention regions that are often crucial for fine-grained retrieval. We propose LARE (Low-Attention Region Encoding), a framework that explicitly models these overlooked regions. LARE adopts a dual-encoding strategy that encodes low-attention regions of an image and the full image in parallel, leading to more diverse and informative image embeddings. To evaluate image retrieval performance in challenging crowded scenes, we introduce Dense-Set, a challenging subset derived from COCO and Flickr30K. In this subset, images are re-captioned to provide richer descriptions of low-attention or previously overlooked regions. This dataset highlights the limitations of existing retrieval models and enables a more rigorous evaluation under densely crowded scene conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework improves retrieval performance by preserving subtle, non-dominant visual cues within the shared latent space.

AI.tldr: Dual-encoding LARE improves image retrieval in crowded scenes by explicitly encoding low-attention regions; evaluated on Dense-Set (COCO/Flickr30K) with re-captioned descriptions, yielding richer embeddings and better performance.
AI.motivation: Conventional encoders focus on salient objects, neglecting low-attention regions that carry fine-grained cues essential for retrieval in densely crowded scenes; this bias limits retrieval accuracy in such contexts.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-encoding framework that concurrently encodes low-attention regions and the full image, producing diverse, informative embeddings; introduces Dense-Set, a challenging subset derived from COCO and Flickr30K with re-captioned descriptions highlighting overlooked regions.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate improved retrieval performance by preserving subtle, non-dominant cues in a shared latent space; the approach yields more diverse embeddings and better handling of crowding effects.
AI.conclusion: Low-attention region encoding mitigates salience bias in image retrieval; the Dense-Set benchmark enables rigorous evaluation; the approach has potential for extension to other multimodal tasks and denser scene understanding.

title: BLADE: Scalable Bi-level Adaptive Data Selection for LLM Training
summary: As Large Language Model (LLM) datasets scale to trillions of tokens, data selection has emerged as a critical frontier to filter out uninformative noise and construct adaptive learning trajectories. Beyond static heuristic filtering, advanced data selection methods for LLM training largely follow two paradigms, each with fundamental limitations. Influence-based methods provide principled bi-level objectives but require intractable inverse-Hessian computations, while excess-loss methods are computationally efficient but rely on a static reference model that becomes misaligned with the evolving proxy model during training. We propose BLADE (Bi-Level Adaptive Data sElection), a Hessian-free framework for data selection. BLADE reformulates the bi-level optimization problem underlying influence-based methods as a penalized single-level objective via Lagrange multipliers, avoiding inverse-Hessian computation while revealing a principled connection to excess-loss based data selection. The resulting objective recovers an excess-loss form but replaces the static reference model with a dynamic one that stays synchronized with training. Theoretically, we prove that this penalized formulation guarantees first-order convergence. For efficient online batch selection, we instantiate BLADE as a memoryless randomized block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe algorithm. Extensive experiments show that BLADE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art data selection baselines, providing a practical recipe for LLM training.

AI.tldr: BLADE is a Hessian-free data selection method for LLM training that unifies influence-based and excess-loss approaches via a penalized single-level objective using Lagrange multipliers. It uses a dynamic reference model and a memoryless randomized block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe algorithm for online batch selection, with first-order convergence guarantees and strong empirical performance overruling baselines.
AI.motivation: As LLM datasets grow to trillions of tokens, effective data selection becomes essential. Influence-based methods are principled but computationally intractable due to inverse-Hessian terms; excess-loss methods are efficient but rely on a static reference model that drifts with training. A Hessian-free, adaptive approach is needed.
AI.method: Reformulate the bi-level optimum of influence-based methods as a penalized single-level objective via Lagrange multipliers, yielding a connection to excess-loss with a dynamically synchronized reference model.
