For online selection, instantiate a memoryless randomized block-coordinate Frank-Wolfe algorithm to choose data batches.
AI.result: The authors prove first-order convergence of the penalized formulation. Empirically, BLADE outperforms state-of-the-art data selection baselines across tasks, offering a practical, scalable recipe for LLM training.
AI.conclusion: BLADE provides a principled, scalable Hessian-free framework that unifies prior data selection paradigms, delivers convergence guarantees, and achieves strong empirical performance, suggesting it as a viable default for data selection in large-scale language model training.

title: Motion-Focused Latent Action Enables Cross-Embodiment VLA Training from Human EgoVideos
summary: Training generalist Vision-Language-Action(VLA) models typically requires massive, diverse robotic datasets with high-fidelity action annotations. While egocentric human manipulation videos are abundant and capture significant environmental diversity, the absence of action labels makes them difficult to use in conventional training paradigms. To address this, we propose a latent-action-based framework designed to extract general action priors from unlabeled human videos. The architecture features a Hybrid Disentangled VQ-VAE that decouples motion dynamics from environmental backgrounds through physical masks, enabling the construction of a cross-embodiment action codebook. By pre-training on human videos with the codebook, the VLM backbone learns deep representations of action intent. For adaptation to specific embodiments, we introduce an intent-perception decoupling strategy where the VLM predicts the action intent while a separate frozen visual encoder provides state-specific features to the action expert, thereby reducing action hallucinations. Results in simulation and real-world environments show that our method, pre-trained exclusively on unlabeled human videos, performs competitively with state-of-the-art VLA models trained on massive annotated datasets, requiring only 50 trajectories for downstream adaptation.

AI.tldr: Latent-action priors learned from unlabeled egocentric videos via a Hybrid Disentangled VQ-VAE; cross-embodiment action codebook enables generalist VLA pre-training, with intent-perception decoupling for adaptation; achieves competitive results using only 50 trajectories.
AI.motivation: Massive annotated robotic datasets for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are costly. Abundant unlabeled egocentric human videos with diverse environments can provide action priors if encoded effectively, reducing annotation needs and improving generalization.
AI.method: Train a Hybrid Disentangled VQ-VAE that separates motion dynamics from backgrounds using physical masks to build a cross-embodiment action codebook. Pre-train the VLM backbone on human videos to learn action intents. For embodiment adaptation, apply an intent-perception decoupling: a VLM predicts action intent while a frozen visual encoder supplies state-specific features to an action expert, mitigating action hallucinations.
AI.result: Pre-training on unlabeled human videos enables the model to approach state-of-the-art VLA performance trained on large annotated datasets, with only 50 trajectories required for downstream adaptation in both simulation and real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: Unlabeled egocentric videos can yield transferable latent action priors through disentangled, cross-embodiment representations. This reduces data labeling needs and supports competitive VLA capabilities, paving the way for scalable, generalist robots.

title: DINO-Med3D: Bridging Dimension and Domain Gaps in Volumetric Segmentation via Progressive Adaptation
comment: Accepted at MICCAI 2026. The camera-ready version and link will be made publicly available upon publication
summary: Although DINOv3 has demonstrated remarkable semantic discrimination in natural imagery, its direct application to volumetric medical segmentation is hindered by inherent dimension and domain disparities. To resolve these issues, we propose DINO-Med3D, a two-stage progressive framework that repurpose the pre-trained DINOv3 encoder for 3D medical tasks. In the first stage, we mitigate the dimension gap by introducing a multi-slice embedding module that incorporates pseudo-3D context, while simultaneously employing a segmentation proxy task to adapt representations learned from natural scenes to the medical domain. Subsequently, we further enhance volumetric understanding by adding lightweight 3D adapters into the frozen backbone to enforce global inter-slice continuity. Finally, to compensate for the spatial information loss inherent in the embedding process, we design a parallel detail recovery stream to explicitly preserve high-frequency boundary cues. Extensive experiments on five public datasets demonstrate that our approach successfully adapts DINOv3 to the medical domain and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Two-stage DINOv3-based framework (DINO-Med3D) enables 3D medical segmentation by bridging dimension and domain gaps: (1) stage-1 multi-slice embedding with segmentation proxy; (2) stage-2 lightweight 3D adapters and a parallel detail recovery stream; outperforms SOTA on five public datasets.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained DINOv3 on natural imagery lacks direct applicability to volumetric medical data due to dimension mismatch and domain shift; there is a need to adapt self-supervised features to 3D medical segmentation with limited labeled data.
AI.method: Stage 1: introduce a multi-slice embedding module to create pseudo-3D context and employ a segmentation proxy task to adapt natural-scene representations to the medical domain. Stage 2: freeze the backbone and add lightweight 3D adapters to enforce global inter-slice continuity; a parallel detail recovery stream preserves high-frequency boundary cues to compensate for embedding-induced spatial information loss.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five public datasets show that DINO-Med3D successfully adapts DINOv3 to the medical domain and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: DINO-Med3D effectively transfers DINOv3 to medical volumetric segmentation, improving performance by addressing dimensionality, domain, and high-frequency detail challenges.

title: scGTN: Deep Siamese Graph Transformer Network for Single-cell RNA Sequencing Clustering
comment: Accepted by Proceedings of the Thirty-Fifth International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI 2026)
summary: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) serves a pivotal role in characterizing gene expression at the cellular level, enabling the identification of cell types and advancing the understanding of cellular heterogeneity. Despite the significant progress in scRNA-seq data clustering, we argue that current methods always ignore the sparsity and noise, as well as the complex intercellular structural information inherent in scRNA-seq data. Toward this end, in this paper, we propose a novel single-cell RNA-seq clustering framework via deep Siamese Graph Transformer Network (termed scGTN), which explicitly integrates gene expression profile and intercellular structural dependencies for cell clustering. In particular, we formulate scRNA-seq data as a graph and construct two augmented graph views that serve as dual views to capture complementary intercellular information. Then, a Siamese graph transformer network is employed to explicitly incorporate shortest-path information and node-wise distances for capturing richer structural relationships between cells. Finally, we employ an optimal transport strategy to guide the cell clustering in a self-supervised manner. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark scRNA-seq datasets demonstrate that our scGTN consistently outperforms existing methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/W-RMSL/scGTN.

AI.tldr: A novel framework scGTN for scRNA-seq clustering using a deep Siamese Graph Transformer Network with dual augmented graph views, explicit intercellular structure modeling (shortest-path and node distances), and optimal transport–driven self-supervised clustering; reported improvements on benchmark datasets with code availability.
AI.motivation: To address the sparsity, noise, and complex intercellular structure in scRNA-seq data that existing clustering methods often neglect.
AI.method: Represent scRNA-seq data as a graph and construct two augmented graph views. Use a Siamese graph transformer to leverage shortest-path information and node-wise distances for richer intercellular relationships. Apply an optimal transport strategy to guide self-supervised clustering.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmark scRNA-seq datasets show consistent performance gains over existing methods; code is publicly available at the authors' repository.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scGTN framework effectively fuses gene-expression profiles with intercellular structural information to enhance clustering quality, delivering competitive (state-of-the-art) results and enabling reproducibility through released code.

title: Mem-World: Memory-Augmented Action-Conditioned World Models for Persistent Robot Manipulation
summary: Action-conditioned world models have emerged as a promising paradigm for robot learning, offering a scalable alternative to costly real-world experimentation by generating action-consistent video rollouts. However, persistent world modeling remains challenging in manipulation: frequent end-effector occlusions and rapid wrist-camera motion make the current observation insufficient for predicting future views, causing models to forget or hallucinate scene details seen in earlier frames. Existing memory retrieval strategies often fail to identify informative history in dynamic manipulation scenarios. To address this limitation, we propose Mem-World, a memory-augmented multi-view action-conditioned world model. At its core, we present W-VMem, a 4D wrist-view-centered surfel-indexed memory that anchors historical observations to temporally evolving surface elements. By explicitly modeling when and where scene elements are observed, W-VMem enables geometry-aware retrieval of relevant history frames conditioned on future actions. During generation, relevant history frames are selected via surfel-based rendering and scoring, providing informative and non-redundant context for prediction. Extensive experiments show that Mem-World generates persistent rollouts in complex manipulation scenarios, enables more reliable policy evaluation than Ctrl-World, improving the Pearson correlation with real-world performance by 14.5\%, and supports effective policy improvement through synthetic data generation, increasing success rates from 58\% to 72\% on long-horizon tasks.

AI.tldr: Mem-World introduces a memory-augmented, surfel-indexed memory (W-VMem) to anchor and retrieve informative history for action-conditioned world models in manipulation, yielding persistent rollouts and improved policy evaluation/improvement.
AI.motivation: Persistent, accurate world modeling in robot manipulation is hindered by end-effector occlusions and rapid motion that render observations incomplete, causing forgetting or hallucinations of past scene details; existing memory retrieval struggles in dynamic tasks.
AI.method: Proposes W-VMem: a 4D wrist-view-centered memory using surfels to anchor observations to evolving surface elements; select relevant history via surfel-based rendering and scoring to condition predictions; Mem-World is a memory-augmented multi-view action-conditioned world model.
AI.result: Empirical results show Mem-World yields persistent rollouts; more reliable policy evaluation than Ctrl-World with 14.5% higher Pearson correlation to real-world performance; synthetic data enables better policy improvement, raising success rates from 58% to 72% on long-horizon tasks.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware memory retrieval using surfel-indexed history effectively mitigates occlusion and motion-induced forgetting, enabling more reliable planning and data-efficient policy improvement in complex manipulation tasks.

title: Automatic ply-specific analyses of CFRP micrographs using shortest-path-based ply distinction
summary: We present an automated approach to distinguish between ply instances in semantic segmentation masks of high-resolution carbon-fiber reinforced polymer micrographs. Interpreting the segmentation mask as a graph with pixels as vertices, enables us to use a shortest-path algorithm yielding the ply-separating paths. Thereby, we bridge the gap between semantic segmentation and ply instance segmentation using global information. We successfully apply our approach on high-resolution micrographs featuring a broad range of characteristics like artificially added gaps in single or multiple plies, different stacking sequences and ply traversing cracks. Assigning each fiber pixel to a ply based on the calculated paths, allows for a comprehensive, quantitative ply analysis with respect to its microstructural properties like the local fiber volume fraction as well as locally resolved ply and interleaf layer thickness. These insights help to reveal manufacturing-induced inhomogeneities, draw conclusions on manufacturing parameters and link mechanical properties to underlying microstructural imperfections.

AI.tldr: Graph-based method to extract ply instances from semantic segmentation masks by treating the mask as a pixel graph and computing shortest-path ply-separating paths; enables ply-level analysis from segmentation.
AI.motivation: Need to obtain ply-level (instance) information from semantic segmentation masks in CFRP micrographs to quantify microstructure and relate it to manufacturing quality.
AI.method: Represent the segmentation mask as a graph with pixels as nodes, apply a shortest-path algorithm to obtain ply-separating paths, assign fiber pixels to plies based on these paths, and derive local fiber volume fraction and ply/interleaf thickness; robust to gaps, varying stacking sequences, and cracks.
AI.result: Demonstrated on high-resolution micrographs with varied features (gaps, stacking sequences, cracks); provides automated ply labeling and quantitative metrics for microstructure analysis.
AI.conclusion: The method bridges semantic and ply instance segmentation using global graph-based information, enabling ply-resolved microstructure analysis and linking manufacturing-induced inhomogeneities to mechanical properties.

title: InTrain: Intrinsic Trainability for Zero-Cost Neural Architecture Search
summary: Training-free neural architecture search promises efficient discovery of high-performance networks without costly training. However, existing zero-cost proxies rely on fragmented heuristics that fail to capture the fundamental question: what makes an architecture trainable? This paper introduces Intrinsic Trainability (InTrain), a unified theoretical proxy that formalizes trainability as an architectural invariant emerging from two synergistic components: geometric capacity and optimization resilience. We operationalize intrinsic trainability through analysis of neural information processing. Geometric capacity is quantified via the participation ratio of activation covariance eigenspectrum, capturing the effective dimensionality of representation manifolds. Optimization resilience is measured through cumulative gradient health, assessing the robustness of backpropagation across network depth. InTrain synthesizes these dimensions through a scale-invariant multiplicative coupling, which we hypothesize is essential for capturing their synergistic, non-additive relationship. Extensive experiments on standard NAS benchmarks and search spaces demonstrate that InTrain achieves ranking correlations on par with state-of-the-art ensemble-based proxies and outperforms other single-metric methods.

AI.tldr: Intrinsic Trainability (InTrain) is a training-free NAS proxy that unifies geometric capacity and optimization resilience into a scale-invariant multiplicative measure, achieving competitive ranking accuracy with state-of-the-art proxies.
AI.motivation: Zero-cost proxies often rely on fragmented heuristics and fail to capture what makes an architecture trainable. A unified, theoretically grounded measure of trainability is needed.
AI.method: Define intrinsic trainability as an architectural invariant from two components: geometric capacity (participation ratio of activation covariance eigenspectrum) and optimization resilience (cumulative gradient health). Combine multiplicatively in a scale-invariant way to capture their synergy. Validate via neural-information-processing analysis and NAS benchmarks.
AI.result: InTrain achieves ranking correlations comparable to ensemble-based proxies and outperforms single-metric methods on standard NAS benchmarks, demonstrating effectiveness and scalability.
AI.conclusion: InTrain provides a unified, trainability-oriented proxy for NAS that captures the non-additive interaction between geometry and optimization, enabling more efficient search.

title: OneCanvas: 3D Scene Understanding via Panoramic Reprojection
summary: Existing approaches to 3D scene understanding in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) either rely on complex, model-specific geometry encoders or large training budgets in pursuit of spatial reasoning. Instead, OneCanvas aggregates patch features from all views onto a single equirectangular panoramic canvas. Namely, each patch is unprojected to a 3D world coordinate using its depth and camera pose, then placed on the canvas at the continuous longitude and latitude of that point as seen from the canvas origin, with no rasterization or aggregation across overlapping views. A 3D position embedding of the patch's metric coordinates is added to its feature, restoring the depth lost when collapsing the world position to an angular canvas coordinate. Patches from all frames thus share one spatial coordinate system with no fusion or major architectural modifications of the backbone. The pretrained VLM consumes this representation as if it were an ordinary image. Because the canvas can be centered on any pose of interest, the same representation directly supports situated reasoning from a specific viewpoint, a common requirement in robotics and embodied AI. Thanks to this representation, we can also introduce a spatial pretraining curriculum: by procedurally placing patch features of objects, drawn from real images, at chosen 3D world positions on an otherwise empty canvas, we generate on-the-fly supervision spanning a broad range of spatial reasoning tasks, with answer distributions controlled to reduce spatial reasoning shortcuts. OneCanvas achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on SQA3D and VSI-Bench, and generalizes to out-of-distribution data on SPBench, using an order of magnitude less training compute than the strongest competing methods.

AI.tldr: A lightweight 3D spatial reasoning approach (OneCanvas) uses cross-view patch features projected onto a single equirectangular canvas with 3D position embeddings, enabling VLMs to perform 3D reasoning with minimal architectural changes and much less compute, achieving SOTA on SQA3D/VSI-Bench and strong generalization.
AI.motivation: Replace heavy, model-specific geometry modules and large training budgets with a simple, unified representation that supports situated reasoning from arbitrary viewpoints.
AI.method: Unproject depth+pose to 3D coordinates for each patch, map to long/lat on a panoramic canvas from a common origin, no view fusion or rasterization; add 3D metric position embedding to patch features; treat the canvas as a normal image for a pretrained VLM; employ a spatial pretraining curriculum by placing real-object patch features at chosen 3D positions on an empty canvas to supervise a broad set of spatial tasks.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on SQA3D and VSI-Bench; strong generalization to out-of-distribution data on SPBench; requires an order of magnitude less training compute than the strongest competing methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a simple, flexible, and compute-efficient pathway for 3D spatial reasoning in VLMs without major architectural changes, enabling effective situated reasoning and spatial curriculum pretraining.

title: BindEdit: Taming Attention Leakage for Precise Multi-Object Image Editing
summary: Real image editing enables precise manipulation of visual content, yet existing methods often fail in complex multi-object scenarios, causing semantic blending, object duplication, or incomplete edits. We attribute these failures to attention leakage, where signals across spatial regions and text tokens become entangled during the denoising process. Specifically, we identify two distinct forms of leakage: Edit-Token Leakage, where ambiguous token-region alignment leads to object blending, and Source Dominance Leakage, where tokens of unchanged source objects overwhelm the attention intended for target entities. To resolve these leakages, we propose \textbf{BindEdit}, which enforces attention-level constraints within a single diffusion trajectory. To suppress Edit-Token Leakage, BindEdit jointly regularizes cross- and self-attention so that each target token group is bound to its corresponding spatial region while maintaining instance-level separation. To suppress Source Dominance Leakage, a cross-attention re-balancing mechanism amplifies target token influence and attenuates residual source semantics within editable regions. Moreover, a region fidelity term ensures that each target concept is expressed coherently across the entire editing mask. Additionally, we propose a comprehensive multi-object benchmark encompassing diverse object counts and categories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BindEdit consistently outperforms existing methods within a single diffusion trajectory, maintaining robust performance across both single- and multi-object editing scenarios.

AI.tldr: BindEdit enforces attention-level constraints within a single diffusion trajectory to mitigate two forms of attention leakage (Edit-Token Leakage and Source Dominance Leakage) in real image editing, using cross-/self-attention regularization, cross-attention re-balancing, and a region fidelity term; plus a new multi-object benchmark. It claims SOTA performance for single-pass editing in both single- and multi-object scenarios.
AI.motivation: Real image editing with diffusion models suffers from semantic blending, object duplication, and incomplete edits in multi-object scenes due to attention leakage between spatial regions and text tokens during denoising. Two leakage modes are identified: Edit-Token Leakage (ambiguous token-region alignment) and Source Dominance Leakage (unchanged source tokens dominate attention).
AI.method: BindEdit jointly regularizes cross- and self-attention to bind target token groups to their corresponding spatial regions while preserving instance separation. It introduces cross-attention re-balancing to boost target-token influence and suppress residual source semantics within editable regions, and a region fidelity term to ensure coherent expression of each target concept across the edit mask. A comprehensive multi-object benchmark is proposed to evaluate editing across varying object counts and categories.
AI.result: Experiments show BindEdit consistently outperforms existing methods within a single diffusion trajectory, maintaining robust performance for both single- and multi-object editing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: BindEdit effectively mitigates attention leakage during diffusion-based editing, enabling more accurate and coherent edits in complex scenes, and is supported by a new benchmark that assesses multi-object editing performance.

title: Bounded Context Management for Tabular Foundation Models on Stream Learning
comment: Accepted as a spotlight oral (top 5%) at the 2nd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data (FMSD@ICML2026)
summary: Tabular stream learning requires predictions on sequentially arriving examples under distribution shift. While standard methods adapt by updating model states, tabular foundation models (TFMs) make predictions conditioned on a labeled context in an in-context manner, making them a natural alternative for stream learning. This shifts the challenge from how to update the model to how to manage the context. We propose a future information view that yields three practical requirements for context management: preserve recent examples, retain uncertain examples, and remove redundant examples. We instantiate these requirements as CURE (Context management via Uncertainty-aware admission and Redundancy aware Eviction), a context-managing policy with entropy-gated admission and redundancy-aware eviction. Across seven streams, CURE shows up to 27.0% relative improvement over classical stream learners, remains robust across multiple TFM backbones, and ranks first among other policy variants. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/morcellinus/CURE-ICML-FMSD.

AI.tldr: A future-information based context-management framework for tabular stream learning, CURE, uses entropy-based admission and redundancy-aware eviction to manage in-context predictions; it improves performance and robustness across seven streams and TFMs.
AI.motivation: Tabular stream learning demands accurate predictions under distribution shift. TFMs leverage in-context learning but require effective context management. There is a need to preserve informative recent examples, retain uncertain ones, and remove redundant ones to maintain performance over time.
AI.method: Introduce a future information view and instantiate CURE with two components: (1) entropy-gated admission to preserve uncertainty-aware contexts, and (2) redundancy-aware eviction to remove redundant past examples. Evaluate across seven streams and multiple TFM backbones, comparing to classical stream learners and policy variants.
AI.result: CURE achieves up to 27.0% relative improvement over classical stream learners, shows robustness across multiple TFMs, and ranks first among policy variants. Code and datasets are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Context management guided by uncertainty and redundancy principles improves tabular stream learning; CURE provides a robust, backbone-agnostic policy for maintaining informative context in non-stationary environments.

title: CABLE: Cloud-Assisted Bandwidth-efficient LMM-based Encoding for V2X Systems
summary: Cloud-hosted large multimodal models (LMMs) can provide strong open-vocabulary perception for Vehicle-to-Everything systems, but naively transmitting full-resolution frames from edge to cloud causes severe communication overhead and high cloud-side prefill latency. We present CABLE, a cloud-assisted bandwidth-efficient LMM-based encoding framework for edge-cloud perception. CABLE propagates the previous cloud segmentation mask on the edge using ego-motion compensation, refines it with residual-motion cues, and consolidates disconnected regions via a corridor envelope to form a robust region of interest (ROI). Only ROI-masked images are uploaded, while the cloud segmentation output is fed back as the prior for the next frame, forming a mask-to-ROI-to-LMM feedback loop. Experiments on five datasets (nuScenes, WOD-ZB, Waymo, KITTI, and CADC) show consistent communication savings while largely preserving perception, achieving $73$--$87\%$ ROI pixel-coverage reduction with $5$--$8\times$ estimated LMM prefill speedup at a modest detection-quality trade-off relative to full-frame inference.

AI.tldr: CABLE enables bandwidth-efficient cloud-assisted LMM-based edge-cloud perception for V2X by uploading only ROI-masked frames and using cloud segmentation priors in a feedback loop, achieving substantial communication savings (73–87% ROI reduction) and 5–8× prefill speedup with modest accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Cloud-hosted large multimodal models offer strong perception but transmitting full-resolution frames causes high communication overhead and cloud prefill latency in edge-cloud V2X systems; a bandwidth-efficient approach is required.
AI.method: Edge-side: propagate the previous cloud segmentation mask using ego-motion compensation; refine with residual-motion cues; consolidate disconnected regions into a corridor envelope to form a robust ROI. Upload only ROI-masked images; cloud segmentation output is fed back as the prior for the next frame, forming a mask-to-ROI-to-LMM feedback loop.
AI.result: Experiments on five datasets (nuScenes, WOD-ZB, Waymo, KITTI, CADC) show consistent communication savings and preserved perception, achieving 73–87% ROI pixel-coverage reduction and 5–8× estimated LMM prefill speedup with a modest detection-quality trade-off relative to full-frame inference.
AI.conclusion: CABLE demonstrates a practical, bandwidth-efficient edge-cloud perception framework for V2X with limited performance degradation, enabling near-real-time LMM-based perception under constrained bandwidth.

title: Physics-IQ Verified
summary: Video generative models ( VGMs) have become a new frontier that can be used not just for video generation but for a multitude of downstream tasks, including world modeling. To advance these tasks, a good video model must understand the physical reality of the world. Evaluating this understanding is an emerging field and has led to the Physics-IQ benchmark, which quantifies this explicitly by comparing model-generated videos to real-world videos of physical experiments. In this work, we present a systematic audit of the Physics-IQ benchmark, expose shortcomings and propose three solutions that sharpen how we can measure physical understanding of VGMs. Specifically, we improve prompt and ground-truth quality to reduce the influence of confounding factors and further introduce a sample-level scoring system that weights each sample and metric equally. Our resulting benchmark, Physics-IQ Verified, refines 57.6\% of all samples and improves over 34.8\% of prompts. In a comparison study using six image-to-video generative models, we observe moderate but meaningful ranking changes (Kendall's $τ= 0.46$). We hope Physics-IQ Verified advances the community by providing a more reliable signal toward physically accurate VGMs. The code for the benchmark can be accessed at https://github.com/google-deepmind/physics-iq-benchmark

AI.tldr: Systematic audit of Physics-IQ benchmark reveals confounds; introduces Physics-IQ Verified with prompt/ground-truth improvements and sample-level weighting, yielding more reliable physical understanding measures for VGMs.
AI.motivation: Enable reliable measurement of physical understanding in video generative models; address confounds and variability in the Physics-IQ benchmark.
AI.method: Audit shortcomings; propose three improvements: prompt quality, ground-truth quality, and a sample-level scoring system; construct Physics-IQ Verified by refining 57.6% of samples and 34.8% of prompts; evaluate six image-to-video models; report Kendall's tau = 0.46 changes; provide open-source code.
AI.result: Physics-IQ Verified offers tighter signal-to-noise in assessing physical understanding; more reliable ranking changes; notable but moderate correlation with prior results; 57.6% sample refinement, 34.8% prompt improvement; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Physics-IQ Verified improves reliability of VGMs' physical understanding benchmarks; encourages broader adoption and further refinement of evaluation protocols.

title: Dual-Channel Grounded World Modeling (DCGWM): Structural Prevention of Objective Interference Collapse via Heterogeneous External Grounding with Inward-Only Gradient Flow
comment: Position paper. Experimental validation in progress
summary: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) are a leading approach to world model representation learning. We identify a failure mode in JEPA-based world models grounded against two qualitatively distinct external signals: physical dynamics (sparse, high-magnitude, constraint-satisfying gradient corrections) and social-behavioral dynamics (diffuse, distribution-matching corrections). We term this Objective Interference Collapse (OIC): we argue that joint learning in a shared latent space causes the dominant channel to systematically collapse the subordinate channel's representational subspace, in a manner not resolvable by loss weighting alone. We propose Dual-Channel Grounded World Modeling (DCGWM), designed to structurally prevent OIC through a partitioned latent space (physical subspace Z_p, behavioral subspace Z_b) with inward-only gradient flow. A Physical Grounding Channel updates only Z_p via VICReg-style alignment to physical measurements; a Social-Behavioral Grounding Channel updates only Z_b via alignment to trajectories from an emergent multi-agent simulation. An Inter-Channel Interface Module couples the subspaces at the task level without cross-subspace gradients. An Asymmetric Grounding Adherence Loss penalizes rollout drift with a hard hinge for physical violations and a soft KL for behavioral divergence. A Generative Rendering Layer is architecturally isolated from the latent world model. We present three theoretical results: the partition removes the gradient-interference pathway implicated in OIC; each grounded subspace inherits anti-collapse guarantees from its alignment objective; and generative isolation is necessary under a stated assumption on the generative objective's geometry. This manuscript establishes the problem formulation and architecture; experimental validation is ongoing and will be reported in a future revision.

AI.tldr: DCGWM proposes partitioned latent space to prevent Objective Interference Collapse (OIC) in JEPA-based world models by separating physical and social-behavioral dynamics into Zp and Zb with inward-only gradients, dedicated groundings, and an interface module; theoretical results claim gradient-interference removal, anti-collapse inheritance, and necessary generative isolation; experimental validation is pending.
AI.motivation: To address a failure mode in joint JEPA-based world models where a single shared latent space causes dominance of one information channel over others (OIC). The work aims to preserve diverse, constraint-satisfying representations for physical dynamics and diffuse, distribution-matching representations for social-behavioral dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce Dual-Channel Grounded World Modeling (DCGWM) with partitioned latent space (Zp for physical, Zb for behavioral) and inward-only gradient flow. Physical grounding channel updates Zp via VICReg-style alignment to physical measurements; social-behavioral grounding channel updates Zb via alignment to trajectories from an emergent multi-agent simulation. An Inter-Channel Interface Module couples subspaces at the task level without cross-subspace gradients. An Asymmetric Grounding Adherence Loss combines a hard hinge for physical rollout violations with a soft KL for behavioral divergence. A Generative Rendering Layer is architecturally isolated from the latent world model.
AI.result: Three theoretical results are claimed: (1) partitioning removes the gradient-interference pathway implicated in OIC; (2) each grounded subspace inherits anti-collapse guarantees from its alignment objective; (3) generative isolation is necessary under a specified assumption about the geometry of the generative objective. The manuscript presents problem formulation and architecture with experimental validation slated for a future revision.
AI.conclusion: The work articulates a clear architecture to mitigate OIC in JEPA-based world models and outlines theoretical guarantees; empirical validation is forthcoming.

title: Does VLA Even Know the Basics? Measuring Commonsense and World Knowledge Retention in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Embodied Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are typically obtained by fine-tuning powerful pretrained VLMs on robotics data, yet it is unclear how much commonsense and factual knowledge they retain after adaptation. Failures on knowledge-sensitive tasks are ambiguous, conflating missing knowledge with poor generalization of low-level control. We introduce Act2Answer, a lightweight protocol that adapts VLM knowledge benchmarks to VLA evaluation by requiring agents to answer through action. Each question becomes a short tabletop episode where the agent performs a single object-placement action to select among candidate answers, yielding an action-grounded success rate with reduced control confounds. We curate a test suite of such environments across diverse commonsense and world-knowledge categories and introduce layerwise intent probing to localize answer-relevant information across the VLM backbone and action head. In a large-scale study of 7 VLA models and 9 VLM baselines, we systematically rank models across categories, finding that VLAs show solid performance on simple concepts while exhibiting larger gaps on richer semantic categories relative to their source VLMs, that VQA co-training is associated with better knowledge retention, and that answer-relevant signals peak in middle VLA layers but attenuate in upper layers. Act2Answer is available at https://tttonyalpha.github.io/act2answer/.

AI.tldr: Act2Answer converts knowledge benchmarks for VLMs into action-based tests for embodied VLA agents, using single object-placement actions to select among answers, enabling action-grounded knowledge evaluation with reduced control confounds.
AI.motivation: To disentangle knowledge retention from low-level control/generalization in embodied VLA systems and to diagnose how much commonsense and factual knowledge survives adaptation to robotics data.
AI.method: Introduce Act2Answer protocol: transform knowledge questions into short tabletop episodes where the agent executes a single object-placement action to pick the correct answer. Build a test suite across diverse commonsense/world-knowledge categories and apply layerwise intent probing to locate answer-relevant signals in VLM backbone vs. action head. Conduct a large-scale study with 7 VLA models and 9 VLM baselines.
AI.result: VLAs perform well on simple concepts but show larger gaps on richer semantic categories compared to their source VLMs. VQA co-training correlates with better knowledge retention. Answer-relevant signals peak at middle VLA layers and attenuate in upper layers.
AI.conclusion: Act2Answer provides a scalable, action-grounded benchmark that isolates knowledge retention in embodied models and reveals where in the network knowledge is captured, with implications for model design and training strategies (e.g., VQA co-training) to preserve knowledge during adaptation.

title: SP-TransientBench: A Real-Captured Single Photon Perception Benchmark
summary: Single-photon LiDAR (SPL) based on single-photon avalanche diode (SPAD) sensing enables time-resolved photon measurements with extreme sensitivity, offering unique potential for active 3D perception in photon-starved scenarios.However, real-world single photon perception remains fundamentally challenging due to unique measurement noise and complex multi-return transient phenomena, which jointly complicate geometric reconstruction and semantic scene understanding. Despite growing interest in SPAD-based sensing, existing studies are largely limited to simulated data or small-scale controlled captures. As a result, systematic evaluation of real-world single photon perception across depth estimation, multi-view reconstruction, and 3D semantic understanding remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce SP-TransientBench (STB), a real-captured multi-task benchmark for single photon perception. SP-TransientBenc comprises 10 diverse scenes and 10,297 views captured using a solid-state single-photon LiDAR at $256\times192$ resolution. Each view provides full time-of-flight histograms with multi-return behavior,standardized metadata, and calibrated camera poses for multi-view evaluation. We further provide 13-class 3D semantic annotations for selected scenes. By providing dedicated data splits and evaluation protocols for each task, STB enables consistent and reproducible benchmarking of real-world single photon perception across multiple 3D vision problems. The dataset and code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A real-captured, multi-task benchmark for single-photon perception (SP-TransientBench) enabling depth, multi-view reconstruction, and 3D semantic understanding with SPAD data.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between simulations/small-scale data and real-world single-photon perception by providing a large, standardized, multi-task real dataset for SPAD-based sensing.
AI.method: Collect 10 diverse scenes with 10,297 views using a solid-state SPAD LiDAR at 256x192; provide full time-of-flight histograms with multi-return behavior, standardized metadata, calibrated camera poses; annotate 13-class 3D semantics on selected scenes; define data splits and evaluation protocols for depth, multi-view reconstruction, and semantic tasks.
AI.result: Introduction of SP-TransientBench (STB) as a real-captured benchmark with multi-task support, ready for release upon acceptance; dataset covers depth, reconstruction, and semantics with rigorous evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: STB fills a critical gap by enabling reproducible benchmarking of real-world single-photon perception across multiple 3D vision tasks, facilitating systematic comparisons and method development.

title: Robust and Interpretable Adaptation of Equivariant Materials Foundation Models via Sparsity-promoting Fine-tuning
summary: Pre-trained materials foundation models, or machine learning interatomic potentials, leverage general physicochemical knowledge to effectively approximate potential energy surfaces. However, they often require domain-specific calibration due to physicochemical diversity as well as mismatches between practical computational settings and those used in constructing the pre-training data. To address this, we propose a sparsity-promoting fine-tuning method that selectively updates model parameters by exploiting the structural properties of E(3)-equivariant materials foundation models. On energy and force prediction tasks across molecular and crystalline benchmarks, our method matches or surpasses full fine-tuning and equivariant low-rank adaptation while updating only $\sim$3~\% of parameters, and in some cases as little as $\sim$0.5~\%. Beyond energy and force calibration, we further demonstrate task generalizability by applying our method to magnetic moment prediction and magnetism-aware total energy modeling. Finally, analysis of sparsity patterns reveals physically interpretable signatures, such as enhanced $d$-orbital contributions in transition metal systems. Overall, our results establish sparsity-promoting fine-tuning as a flexible and interpretable method for domain specialization of equivariant materials foundation models.

AI.tldr: A sparsity-promoting fine-tuning method for E(3)-equivariant materials foundation models updates a small fraction of parameters (~3%, sometimes ~0.5%) to adapt to domain-specific tasks, achieving energy/force accuracy on molecular and crystalline benchmarks comparable to or better than full fine-tuning and low-rank adapters. The approach extends to magnetic moment prediction and magnetism-aware energies, with sparsity patterns providing interpretable chemical insights (e.g., increased d-orbital contributions in transition metals).
AI.motivation: Domain-specific calibration is necessary due to physicochemical diversity and misalignment between pre-training conditions and practical settings. Full fine-tuning is expensive and risks overfitting; there is a need for efficient, interpretable adaptation for equivariant materials foundation models.
AI.method: The authors introduce sparsity-promoting fine-tuning that selectively updates model parameters by exploiting the structural properties of E(3)-equivariant materials foundation models. This approach is evaluated on energy and force prediction tasks across molecular and crystalline benchmarks and compared against full fine-tuning and equivariant low-rank adaptation.
AI.result: The method matches or surpasses full fine-tuning and equivariant low-rank adaptation while updating only about 3% of parameters (sometimes as little as 0.5%). It generalizes to magnetic moment prediction and magnetism-aware total energy. Analysis of sparsity patterns reveals physically interpretable signatures, such as enhanced d-orbital contributions in transition metal systems.
AI.conclusion: Sparsity-promoting fine-tuning is a flexible, interpretable strategy for domain specialization of equivariant materials foundation models, offering substantial parameter efficiency without sacrificing, and often improving, predictive performance.

title: UBP2: Uncertainty-Balanced Preference Planning for Efficient Preference-based Reinforcement Learning
summary: Preference-based RL provides an approach to learning reward models from pairwise comparisons of behaviors, bypassing the need for explicit reward design. However, existing methods typically rely on passive data collection and suffer from poor sample efficiency, especially during the early stages of learning. We introduce a model-based approach that actively directs exploration by jointly reasoning over uncertainties in the reward, dynamics, and value functions. Our method, Uncertainty-Balanced Preference Planning (UBP2), uses ensembles of reward, dynamics, and value function models to evaluate candidate trajectories according to a unified score that combines expected reward, terminal value, and epistemic uncertainty. Planning under this objective yields an explicit tradeoff between exploitation and information acquisition without requiring ad hoc exploration heuristics. Under standard regularity assumptions, we establish sublinear regret guarantees for both finite-horizon and infinite-horizon settings. Empirically, experiments on the Meta-World benchmark show UBP2 achieves substantially higher sample efficiency than model-free preference-based methods and non-optimistic model-based baselines.

AI.tldr: A model-based, uncertainty-aware approach to preference-based RL that actively explores to improve sample efficiency, with theoretical regret guarantees and empirical gains on Meta-World.
AI.motivation: Reduce sample-inefficient, passive data collection in preference-based RL by explicitly balancing exploration and exploitation via uncertainty in reward, dynamics, and value estimates.
AI.method: Ensemble models for reward, dynamics, and value; plan trajectories using a unified score combining expected reward, terminal value, and epistemic uncertainty; derive sublinear regret in finite/infinite horizon.
AI.result: Sublinear regret guarantees; substantial sample-efficiency gains in Meta-World compared to model-free preference-based methods and non-optimistic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that uncertainty-balanced planning can effectively guide exploration in preference-based RL with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.

title: Attention as Frustrated Synchronization
comment: 25 pages, 4 figures. Preliminary report at the 1-10M parameter scale
summary: A network of oscillators that synchronizes perfectly computes nothing further, so an attention architecture built from synchronization must locate its computation in structured departures from agreement. We introduce the Frustrated Synchronization Network (FSN), whose token states are phases on a torus and whose entire value pathway is one learned complex coupling kernel over harmonics and a one-step delay. Each component of the kernel is a frustration in the sense of the synchronization literature. The complex phases are static Kuramoto-Sakaguchi frustration angles, the signed harmonics are repulsive Daido components, and the delay term, which couples each token to the successors of the tokens it attends to, is algebraically identical to Kuramoto-Sakaguchi coupling whose frustration angle is the data's own transition, so next-token prediction is implemented as synchronization frustrated by the data. At matched one-million-parameter and training budgets on character-level text and code, the FSN's validation loss is below a tuned RoPE-SwiGLU transformer's at every epoch measured, and the comparison survives training the baseline to convergence: every thirty-epoch enwik8 seed finishes below the transformer's converged fifty-epoch loss of 1.611, and the FSN's completed fifty-epoch runs converge to 1.5953 +/- 0.0014. A variant with every feed-forward block replaced by mean-field coupling to learned collective modes, leaving no multilayer perceptron in the stack, tracks the transformer. On natural text the unfrustrated base layer falls behind the converged transformer at every copy depth, worst on long-range copy events; the kernel reverses the deficit at every depth of four and beyond. Headline comparisons are at the one-million-parameter scale; a scale ladder is complete through four million parameters with the advantage persisting, and remaining arms are marked as in progress.

AI.tldr: A Frustrated Synchronization Network (FSN) uses a learned complex coupling kernel over harmonics and a one-step delay to implement prediction as data-frustrated synchronization. It achieves competitive to better performance versus a tuned transformer (RoPE-SwiGLU) on character-level text/enwik8 and code, with a mean-field variant matching transformer, and shows strong scaling up to 4M parameters.
AI.motivation: To move beyond pure agreement in synchronization-based attention by introducing structured departures from synchronization (frustration) as the computational mechanism for next-token prediction, leveraging dynamical systems to model long-range dependencies and transitions in data.
AI.method: Token states are phases on a torus. A single learned complex coupling kernel over harmonics with a one-step delay encodes all interactions. Components include static Kuramoto-Sakaguchi frustration angles, Daido repulsive harmonics, and a delay term equivalent to Kuramoto-Sakaguchi coupling with the data's transition angle. Training compares FSN to a RoPE-SwiGLU transformer on character-level text and code; ablated variants replace feed-forward blocks with mean-field coupling to learned collective modes.
AI.result: On matched 1M-parameter budgets, FSN outperforms or matches tuned transformer baselines in validation loss across epochs on enwik8; e.g., 30-epoch seeds finish below the converged transformer's 1.611 loss, and 50-epoch FSN converges to 1.5953±0.0014. The mean-field variant tracks the transformer. On natural text, the unfrustrated base layer lags, but the kernel compensates from depth four onward. Scaling shows complete ladder to 4M parameters with persistent advantage; further arms are in progress.
AI.conclusion: Frustration-based synchronization provides a viable, scalable alternative for attention-like architectures, enabling competitive language modeling performance with potentially simpler or more interpretable dynamic kernels; the approach demonstrates that meaningful computation can arise from controlled departures from consensus in synchronized systems.

title: Visual-OPSD: Cross-Modal On-Policy Self-Distillation for Efficient Unified Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) interleave generated ''visual thoughts'' (VTs) with text reasoning to improve spatial tasks. This incurs roughly an order-of-magnitude inference cost from multi-step diffusion. We find this cost yields limited direct benefit. On ThinkMorph, removing or noising VTs barely changes accuracy across nine benchmarks. Once rendered, attention concentrates on the VT regardless of content. Yet a KL diagnostic shows that conditioning on a privileged VT trace shifts the model's completion distribution. This suggests the generation pathway encodes useful reasoning beyond the rendered pixels. Motivated by this gap, we propose Visual On-Policy Self-Distillation(Visual-OPSD). Teacher and student share identical weights but differ in context: the teacher sees privileged VTs while the student sees only the question. Token-level JSD distillation on on-policy student trajectories transfers the teacher's reasoning to a text-only student. Across nine benchmarks, Visual-OPSD improves over its generative teacher by $+3.40$pp with $14.3\times$ speedup (10.0s vs. 142.8s per sample) and outperforms same-scale VLMs by $+63.83$pp on VSP. A Gaussian-noise control ($+0.40$pp vs. $+10.28$pp for real VTs) and $58.4\%$ closure of the KL gap confirm that gains come from the semantic content of the generation pathway.

AI.tldr: Self-distilling a text-only model from a VT-conditioned teacher yields comparable or better accuracy with large speedup, indicating semantic content in generation pathways carries reasoning beyond rendered pixels.
AI.motivation: High inference cost of unified multimodal models that interleave visual thoughts (VTs) with text reasoning, yet observed limited gains; seek an efficient alternative that preserves reasoning without expensive VT generation.
AI.method: Visual-OPSD: on-policy self-distillation with teacher and student sharing weights; teacher conditioned on privileged VT traces; student sees only the question; perform token-level Jensen-Shannon divergence distillation on on-policy trajectories to transfer reasoning to a text-only student.
AI.result: Across nine benchmarks, +3.40 percentage points improvement over the generative teacher with 14.3x speedup (10.0s vs 142.8s per sample); outperforms same-scale VLMs by +63.83pp on VSP. Gaussian-noise control gives only +0.40pp versus +10.28pp for real VTs; 58.4% closure of the KL gap indicates gains arise from semantic content of the generation pathway.
AI.conclusion: The semantic information encoded in the VT generation pathway is transferable to a text-only model via on-policy self-distillation, yielding substantial efficiency and performance gains.

title: Contextualizing Biological Language Models across Modalities via Logit-Space Contrastive Alignment
summary: Pretrained biological language models expose per-token probability distributions through masked-token prediction, providing the likelihood interface central to sequence design, variant scoring, and mechanistic interpretation. Yet these distributions are learned from broad unlabeled corpora and are not naturally conditioned on task-specific biological contexts such as interaction partners, cellular environments, or therapeutic interventions. Existing contextual matching methods often distort this interface through pooled embeddings, contrastive latent spaces, or task-specific prediction heads. We introduce LOGICA (Logit-space Contrastive Alignment), a framework for context-conditioned prediction that performs contrastive learning directly in output-logit space. Using gated cross-modal adapters compatible with each model's native token head, LOGICA preserves the pretrained likelihood interface and converts contextualized token log-likelihoods into matching scores. Alignment is defined through context-sensitive token probabilities rather than proximity in a shared embedding space, enabling learning from sparse paired data across models with distinct vocabularies, without a shared tokenizer or decoder. LOGICA is particularly effective for mutation-local variant ranking, where comparisons reduce to context-conditioned likelihoods of mutant tokens at perturbed sites. Across protein--ligand binding, TCR--peptide activity, and drug-conditioned resistance prediction, LOGICA improves over prior state-of-the-art methods, including matched latent-contrastive and conditional MLM baselines, while retaining a token-level interface for interpretation and generation. On held-out-gene single-mutation drug-resistance prediction, LOGICA improves AUC from near-random latent-space baselines of $\sim$0.55 to $\sim$0.65.

AI.tldr: LOGICA offers a logit-space contrastive alignment framework that conditions pretrained biological language models on context while preserving the native token likelihood interface, improving context-conditioned variant ranking and cross-model predictions with notable AUC gains.
AI.motivation: Pretrained biological LMs learn broad chance distributions that are not conditioned on task-specific biological contexts (e.g., interaction partners, environment, therapies). Context wiring is needed without distorting the token-level likelihood interface, which many prior methods do by altering embeddings or adding task-specific heads.
AI.method: LOGICA performs contrastive learning directly in the output logit space. It uses gated cross-modal adapters that attach to each model’s native token head, preserving the pretrained likelihood distribution. Context-conditioned token probabilities are aligned across models with disjoint vocabularies (no shared tokenizer/decoder). Alignment is defined via context-sensitive token probabilities (not embedding proximity), enabling learning from sparse paired data across modalities.
AI.result: Across tasks (protein–ligand binding, TCR–peptide activity, drug-conditioned resistance), LOGICA outperforms prior state-of-the-art including matched latent-contrastive and conditional MLM baselines. On held-out-gene single-mutation drug-resistance prediction, AUC improves from ~0.55 (random/latent baselines) to ~0.65.
AI.conclusion: LOGICA preserves the token-level likelihood interface while enabling context-conditioned expectations via logit-space contrastive alignment. It supports heterogeneous vocabularies without shared tokenizers, demonstrates strong cross-domain gains, and offers a scalable approach for mutation-local variant ranking and related bioinformatics tasks; potential limitations include dependence on paired data, calibration of log-probabilities, and adapter overhead.

title: Show, Don't Ask: Generative Visual Disambiguation for Composed Image Retrieval with Turn-Valid Coverage
summary: Composed image retrieval (CIR) uses a reference image and a text modification to search for a target image. However, such queries often describe several possible images rather than one exact target, making the user's intent ambiguous. Recent methods address this by using conformal prediction to estimate ambiguity and by asking users clarifying text questions. However, these methods have two limitations: their coverage guarantee only holds at the first interaction, and text questions are often insufficient for resolving fine-grained visual differences such as appearance, attributes, or viewpoint. We propose CLARA, a clarification framework that resolves ambiguity by showing users a small panel of visual alternatives. Instead of answering text questions, the user simply selects the prototype image closest to the intended target. This provides a direct visual signal and avoids relying on a model to predict the user's answer. To maintain valid conformal guarantees across multiple interaction rounds, CLARA reweights calibration using the likelihood ratio induced by the user's selection. The displayed prototypes are also constrained to represent the current candidate set and are snapped to real corpus images, ensuring that generated images cannot artificially improve coverage. Experiments on open-domain and fashion benchmarks show that CLARA matches single-turn state-of-the-art retrieval performance, maintains nominal coverage across interaction rounds, and finds the intended target in fewer rounds than strong text-question baselines. Its advantage is especially clear when ambiguity involves viewpoint or fine-grained attributes, where visual clarification is more effective than textual questioning.

AI.tldr: CLARA introduces a visual-clarification framework for composed image retrieval (CIR) that shows a small panel of real-corpus prototype images for user selection, reweights conformal calibration across interaction rounds, and constrains prototypes to the current candidate set to maintain valid coverage; it achieves state-of-the-art single-turn performance, maintains nominal coverage over rounds, and requires fewer rounds than text-question baselines, especially when ambiguity involves viewpoint or fine-grained attributes.
AI.motivation: CIR queries are inherently ambiguous; relying solely on textual modifications often yields multiple plausible targets and textual questions may fail to resolve fine-grained or viewpoint-related differences. There is a need for robust, multi-round interaction with provable coverage guarantees.
AI.method: CLARA presents a small panel of real-corpus prototype images (displayed prototypes) that the user selects from to indicate the intended target. Instead of answering textual questions, the user chooses the prototype closest to the target. The framework reweights conformal calibration using the likelihood ratio induced by the user’s selection to maintain valid coverage across multiple rounds. Prototypes are constrained to the current candidate set and snapped to real corpus images to prevent artificial improvements in coverage.
AI.result: Empirical results on open-domain and fashion benchmarks show that CLARA matches single-turn state-of-the-art retrieval performance, preserves nominal conformal coverage across interaction rounds, and locates the intended target in fewer rounds than strong text-question baselines—particularly when ambiguity involves viewpoint or fine-grained attributes.
AI.conclusion: Visual clarification via prototype selection provides a direct, robust signal for resolving ambiguity in CIR, maintains conformal guarantees over multiple rounds, and improves retrieval efficiency, especially for fine-grained attributes and viewpoint-related queries.

title: Trainable Photonic Measurement for Physics-Informed PDE Learning
summary: Photonic quantum machine learning offers a route to trainable physical representations built from phase, interference and measurement. However, its role in scientific machine learning remains largely unexplored. Physics-informed neural fields provide a natural setting, because differential equations require trial spaces that preserve phase, frequency and derivative structure. Here we introduce a photonic quantum neural field in which coordinates become trainable optical phases, are mixed by multi-photon Fock-space interference and are decoded from photon-number measurements. The photonic circuit is optimized as the neural-field representation itself, not as a fixed feature map or hardware accelerator. Photonic measurement is therefore a trainable representation on which the physics-informed residual is minimized. Across seven elliptic, wave, nonlinear dispersive and inverse PDE benchmarks, we observe a phase-complexity transition: classical coordinate and Fourier-feature networks suffice in smooth regimes, whereas the photonic field is most accurate when residual derivatives amplify phase mismatch. In the hardest regimes it gives the lowest errors, with margins reaching an order of magnitude and about one quarter of the trainable parameters of classical baselines. Frozen and shuffled controls, together with noise stress tests, attribute this gain to learned interference and stable Fock-probability readout under compound perturbations. These results identify photonic quantum measurement as a representation-learning principle for scientific machine learning.

AI.tldr: A photonic quantum neural field uses trainable optical phases and multi-photon interference to learn a physics-informed representation for PDEs, achieving superior accuracy in hard regimes with fewer parameters than classical baselines.
AI.motivation: To explore the role of photonic quantum measurements as a representation-learning basis for scientific machine learning and to provide trial spaces for PDEs that preserve phase, frequency, and derivative structure.
AI.method: Introduce a photonic quantum neural field where coordinates are trainable optical phases, mixed via multi-photon Fock-space interference and decoded from photon-number measurements. The circuit itself is trained as the neural-field representation, optimizing the physics-informed residual without fixed feature maps or hardware acceleration. Evaluated on seven elliptic, wave, nonlinear dispersive, and inverse PDE benchmarks.
AI.result: A phase-complexity transition is observed: classical coordinate/ Fourier-feature nets perform well in smooth regimes, while the photonic field excels when residual derivatives amplify phase mismatch. In the hardest regimes it achieves the lowest errors (up to an order of magnitude better) with roughly one quarter of the trainable parameters compared with classical baselines. Control analyses (frozen/shuffled, noise stress tests) attribute gains to learned interference and stable Fock-probability readout under perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Photonic quantum measurement emerges as a representation-learning principle for scientific machine learning, offering an efficient, physics-aware neural-field approach for PDEs.

title: FlowObject: Flow Steering for Bridging Generative Priors and Reconstruction Fidelity
summary: Recovering complete 3D representations of objects from few casual image captures remains a significant challenge. Recent 3D generative models, particularly those based on Flow-Matching (FM), can synthesize high-quality textured assets; however, they often suffer from ''synthetic bias'' where learned priors override observational evidence, alongside a lack of alignment with the observed instance. Conversely, optimization-based methods like 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) provide high fidelity on visible surfaces but fail to reason about unobserved geometry. In this paper, we present FlowObject, a framework that reformulates sparse-view 3D reconstruction as a training-free, guided inverse problem. Our approach applies a dual-space guidance strategy to steer the Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) trajectory of a flow-matching model, enabling the completion of unseen regions through learned generative priors while enforcing strict consistency with real-world observations. By integrating a 3DGS refinement stage, FlowObject further bridges the gap between ''synthetic-looking'' generative outputs and photorealistic reconstructions. Comprehensive benchmarks on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that current state-of-the-art methods often struggle to achieve geometric completeness and observational consistency simultaneously, especially under severe occlusions. In contrast, our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art generative models and optimization-based frameworks in both geometric completeness and view-dependent appearance fidelity.

AI.tldr: FlowObject enables training-free, sparse-view 3D reconstruction by guiding a flow-matching model's ODE with dual-space constraints and refining with 3DGS, achieving more complete and photorealistic results under occlusion.
AI.motivation: to address synthetic bias in flow-based generative priors and incomplete geometry in optimization-based methods, aiming for both observational consistency and geometric completeness.
AI.method: dual-space guidance to steer the ODE trajectory of a flow-matching model; enforce strict consistency with real observations; integrate a 3D Gaussian Splatting refinement stage; training-free.
AI.result: On synthetic and real datasets, FlowObject outperforms state-of-the-art generative models and optimization frameworks in geometric completeness and view-dependent appearance fidelity, especially under severe occlusions.
AI.conclusion: FlowObject bridges the gap between learned priors and observed data, enabling robust, complete, and high-fidelity 3D reconstructions from sparse views.

title: Graph Grounded Cross Attention Transformer Neural Network for Structurally Constrained Full Event Sequence Generation in Predictive Process Monitoring
summary: Structurally constrained event sequence generation remains challenging because generated paths must preserve transition feasibility, temporal order, termination, and attribute consistency. In predictive process monitoring (PPM), this challenge appears as full event sequence generation, whereas existing work mainly addresses component tasks such as next activity, remaining time, outcome, and attribute prediction. This paper proposes the Graph Grounded Cross Attention Transformer Neural Network (GGATN) for this unified PPM task. GGATN uses a global process graph as structured activity memory, contextualizes sequence positions through Transformer self attention, and injects process topology through graph grounded cross attention. Unlike autoregressive decoding, GGATN generates activities, timestamps, length, and event level and sequence level attributes in a single pass, followed by Viterbi style graph constrained decoding for feasible paths and explicit termination. Experiments on six benchmark event logs show more reliable generation quality than local instruction prompted LLM baselines. GGATN achieves strong performance on sequence similarity, Damerau Levenshtein similarity, bigram based control flow similarity, and duration distribution, while maintaining zero hallucinated activities and zero sequence level attribute inconsistency. Ablation analyses confirm the global graph encoder as a stable structural prior. Interpretability analyses show how graph structure, sequence context, feedback refinement, and constrained decoding shape generation.

AI.tldr: GGATN is a non-autoregressive, graph-grounded Transformer that generates full event sequences in a single pass for predictive process monitoring, then applies Viterbi-style graph-constrained decoding to ensure feasible paths and termination.
AI.motivation: To address unified predictive process monitoring by generating complete, structurally feasible sequences (activities, timestamps, duration, and attributes) rather than only component tasks, overcoming limitations of autoregressive models and LLM prompts.
AI.method: Introduce Graph Grounded Cross Attention Transformer Neural Network (GGATN) with a global process graph as memory, Transformer self-attention for position context, and graph-grounded cross attention to inject topology. Generates activities, timestamps, length, and attributes in one pass, followed by Viterbi-style graph-constrained decoding for feasible paths and termination. Conduct ablations and interpretability analyses.
AI.result: GGATN achieves more reliable generation than local instruction-prompted LLM baselines across six benchmark logs, with strong sequence similarity, Damerau–Levenshtein similarity, bigram-based control-flow similarity, and duration distribution metrics; zero hallucinated activities and zero sequence-level attribute inconsistency. Ablations identify the global graph encoder as a stable structural prior; interpretability shows how graph structure, sequence context, feedback refinement, and constrained decoding shape generation.
AI.conclusion: GGATN provides a unified, structure-aware PPM solution that delivers feasible, high-quality full-sequence generation with interpretable mechanisms and explicit termination, advancing beyond component-level predictions.

title: Low-Rank Tensor Completion Based on Fractional Regularization with Ky Fan p-k Norm
summary: This paper addresses low-rank tensor completion (LRTC) by proposing a novel nonconvex surrogate, namely the ratio of the tensor nuclear norm to the tensor Ky Fan p-k norm (TNPK), to accurately approximate the tensor tubal rank. The TNPK possesses appealing properties, including scale invariance, parameter flexibility, and the existence of closed-form solutions under specific choices of p and k. With specific parameter settings of p and k, it reduces to the ratio of the tensor nuclear norm to the tensor Ky Fan k norm (TNK) or the ratio of the tensor nuclear norm to the tensor Frobenius norm (TNF). We construct a LRTC model and, under the tensor null space property (NSP), prove that low-rank tensors are local minimizers of the proposed model. Moreover, we derive the proximal operator of the Ky Fan p-k inverse-norm and further develop an efficient alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) algorithm with guaranteed subsequential convergence under mild conditions. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets validate the superior performance of our method against state-of-the-art competitors.

AI.tldr: Introduces TNPK, a nonconvex ratio surrogate for tensor tubal rank in low-rank tensor completion; encompasses TNK and TNF; provides NSP-based local optimality, a closed-form proximal operator for Ky Fan p-k inverse-norm, and an ADMM with subsequential convergence; shows leading performance on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Conventional convex relaxations (e.g., nuclear norm) often misestimate tensor rank. A nonconvex, scale-invariant, and parameter-tunable surrogate like TNPK can yield tighter rank approximation and improved recovery, while enabling efficient optimization and theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Define TNPK as the ratio of the tensor nuclear norm to the tensor Ky Fan p-k norm. Construct a low-rank tensor completion model minimizing TNPK under data constraints. Derive the proximal operator of the Ky Fan p-k inverse-norm. Develop an ADMM algorithm with proven subsequential convergence under mild conditions. Prove that, under the tensor null space property (NSP), low-rank tensors are local minimizers of the model.
AI.result: TNPK reduces to TNK or TNF for specific choices of p and k. The authors obtain closed-form solutions in these settings. The ADMM algorithm is efficiently implementable with guaranteed subsequential convergence. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world data demonstrate superior performance against state-of-the-art competitors.
AI.conclusion: TNPK provides a flexible, effective nonconvex surrogate for tensor rank in LRTC, unifying TNK and TNF as special cases, offering theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency through an NSP-based local optimality result and an ADMM framework with closed-form proximal steps.

title: Low-Cost Neuromorphic Fall Detection Using Synthetic Event Data and Hybrid SNNs
comment: 4 pages, 6 figures, presented at ICONS 2025 during the Poster Session, but not published
summary: This work presents the development of hybrid models that integrate spiking neural networks (SNNs) with components of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to learn from simulated event-based camera data (Dynamic Vision Sensor, DVS) generated from conventional smartphone videos. Aimed primarily at human fall detection, the approach leverages the energy efficiency and spatio-temporal processing capabilities of SNNs by converting video frames into event-based data. The proposed models are evaluated through simulations on multiple datasets, comparing their performance to that of traditional machine learning models. Results demonstrate significant gains in efficiency without sacrificing accuracy, underscoring the potential of combining SNNs and DVS technology for complex tasks in real-world environments.

AI.tldr: Hybrid SNN–CNN models process event-based data derived from ordinary videos (via DVS) for fall detection, achieving energy-efficient, real-time spatio-temporal learning with maintained accuracy across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Address the need for low-power, real-time human activity recognition; leverage the event-driven sparsity of DVS sensors and the temporal processing strengths of SNNs, combined with CNN components for robust feature extraction.
AI.method: Frame-to-DVS conversion of conventional video; design hybrid architectures that fuse spiking neural networks with CNN modules; train and evaluate on several datasets; compare against traditional machine learning baselines.
AI.result: Reported significant efficiency gains without sacrificing accuracy; demonstrates feasibility of SNN–DVS hybrids for complex, real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid SNN–CNN approaches using DVS data show promise for efficient, accurate fall detection and potentially other tasks; further work needed on hardware deployment, broader validation, and detailed benchmarking.

title: Benchmarking Large Vision-Language Models on Fine-Grained Image Tasks: From Evaluation to Diagnosis
summary: Recent advancements in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have demonstrated remarkable multimodal perception and reasoning capabilities. While numerous benchmarks have evaluated LVLMs from holistic or task-specific perspectives, their capabilities on fine-grained image tasks-fundamental to computer vision-remain insufficiently understood. To address this gap, we introduce FG-BMK, a comprehensive fine-grained evaluation benchmark containing 1.01 million questions and 0.28 million images, covering diverse scenarios from common object-centric domains to specialized domains. FG-BMK jointly evaluates dialogue-level fine-grained semantic recognition and feature-level visual discriminability through human-oriented and machine-oriented paradigms, enabling diagnostic analysis of whether LVLM failures arise from insufficient visual representations, weak visual-to-semantic grounding, or limited fine-grained knowledge. Through extensive experiments on a diverse set of representative LVLMs/VLMs, we find that current LVLMs remain inadequate fine-grained recognizers, with failures arising from intertwined bottlenecks in visual representations, semantic grounding, modality alignment, and category-level knowledge. We further analyze training design factors for improving fine-grained capabilities and examine how visual and linguistic perturbations affect LVLM predictions. These findings provide diagnostic insights into the limitations of current LVLMs and offer guidance for future data construction and model design in developing more reliable LVLMs for fine-grained visual tasks. Our code is open-source and available at https://fg-bmk.github.io/.

AI.tldr: FG-BMK: a large-scale fine-grained evaluation benchmark for LVLMs highlighting limitations in visual representations, grounding, and knowledge, with a diagnostic framework using dialogue and feature-level tasks; open-source.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in understanding LVLMs' fine-grained visual capabilities and diagnose bottlenecks in representations, grounding, and knowledge across diverse domains.
AI.method: Construct FG-BMK with 1.01M questions and 0.28M images; dual paradigms: dialogue-level semantic recognition and feature-level discriminability; human- and machine-oriented evaluation; extensive experiments across LVLMs/VLMs; perturbation analysis; analysis of training design factors.
AI.result: Current LVLMs underperform in fine-grained recognition; failures stem from intertwined bottlenecks (visual representations, grounding, modality alignment, and knowledge); some improvements possible via training design; visual and linguistic perturbations affect predictions; code open-source.
AI.conclusion: FG-BMK provides diagnostic insights and guidance for data construction and model design to build more reliable LVLMs for fine-grained tasks.

title: Private Learning with Public Feature Conditioning
comment: Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). 26 pages, 9 figures
summary: We study differentially private (DP) regression in settings where each data sample includes public, non-sensitive features -- common in applications such as recommendation and advertising systems.
