While such label-DP or semi-sensitive-feature settings have been primarily explored in the context of classification, effective approaches for regression remain underexplored. We introduce Cond-DP, a conditioned variant of DPSGD that leverages the structure of public feature matrices to improve optimization under privacy constraints. Motivated by the observation that these public features often exhibit rapidly decaying spectra, Cond-DP incorporates a data-driven conditioning matrix to reshape the optimization landscape and accelerate convergence. We provide convergence guarantees for convex, strongly convex, and non-convex settings, and recover standard DPSGD as a special case when the conditioning matrix is the identity. We show how to construct an effective conditioning matrix for Cond-DP directly from public features, enabling provably faster convergence than DPSGD in private linear regression without incurring additional privacy cost. Empirically, Cond-DP with this conditioning matrix consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across a wide range of datasets and model architectures under label DP, demonstrating strong and robust performance in practice.

AI.tldr: Cond-DP is a conditioned DP-SGD variant for regression when data include public features. It uses a data-driven conditioning matrix derived from public features to reshape the optimization landscape, exploiting their typically decaying spectra to accelerate convergence. It provides convergence guarantees for convex, strongly convex, and non-convex objectives, with standard DPSGD recovered when the conditioning matrix is identity. The conditioning matrix is constructed from public features and incurs no additional privacy cost, enabling provably faster convergence than DPSGD in private linear regression. Empirically, Cond-DP outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across datasets and architectures under label DP.
AI.motivation: In many practical settings (e.g., recommendation, advertising), each sample has public, non-sensitive features. While DP methods for classification with public features have been explored, regression with DP remains underexplored. The goal is to leverage public feature structure to improve optimization under DP without increasing privacy loss.
AI.method: Introduce Cond-DP, a conditioned variant of DPSGD that uses a conditioning matrix derived from the public feature matrix to reshape the gradient update. The public features are observed to have rapidly decaying spectra; the conditioning matrix rebalances scales along directions informed by this spectrum, improving convergence. The authors provide convergence guarantees for convex, strongly convex, and non-convex objectives, and show that DPSGD is a special case when the conditioning matrix is the identity. The conditioning matrix is constructed directly from public features, avoiding any additional privacy cost.
AI.result: Theoretical convergence guarantees across convex, strongly convex, and non-convex regimes. Demonstration that standard DPSGD is recovered with the identity conditioning matrix. A data-driven method to construct an effective conditioning matrix from public features yields provably faster convergence than DPSGD in private linear regression without extra privacy cost. Empirical results show Cond-DP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under label DP across diverse datasets and model architectures.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging public feature structure via a data-driven conditioning matrix substantially improves DP regression optimization. Cond-DP generalizes DPSGD and offers faster convergence with no additional privacy cost, with strong empirical performance. The approach is particularly effective when public features exhibit decaying spectra.

title: DREAM: Extending Vision-Language Models with Dual-Objective Encoding for Cross-Modal Retrieval
summary: In today's media-driven world, the exponential growth of video content across domains such as surveillance, education, and entertainment has made retrieving semantically relevant videos via natural language queries increasingly critical. Early video retrieval systems relied on handcrafted features or shallow cross-modal mappings, limiting their ability to capture complex semantics and temporal dynamics. While large-scale vision-language models have improved cross-modal alignment, challenges remain in modeling fine-grained temporal dependencies and nuanced linguistic structures. In this paper, we introduce DREAM: Dual-path Representation Enhancement and Alignment Model, a novel multimodal framework that addresses these limitations through enhanced visual and textual encoding. DREAM incorporates a hybrid language modeling strategy that combines masked and permuted language modeling objectives to capture both local and global linguistic semantics. On the visual side, we design a hierarchical vision encoder with cascaded group attention, which integrates spatial and temporal information through multi-stage token interaction and coarse-to-fine attention refinement. We validate DREAM through comprehensive evaluations on the widely-used MSRVTT, MSVD and LSMDC benchmark datasets, where it achieves new state-of-the-art R1 scores of 49.4%, 49.7% and 27.3%, respectively. Qualitative analyses further show the model's ability to maintain coherent attention across frames and align complex queries with dynamic video content. These findings underscore the effectiveness of hierarchical attention and dual-objective textual modeling in enabling robust, context-aware video retrieval, and pave the way for future research in advancing cross-modal representation learning.

AI.tldr: DREAM is a dual-path multimodal video retrieval model that fuses hierarchical visual encoding with a hybrid text modeling objective, achieving state-of-the-art results on MSRVTT, MSVD, and LSMDC.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of early handcrafted/shallow cross-modal mappings and to better capture fine-grained temporal dynamics and nuanced linguistic structures in video-language retrieval.
AI.method: A dual-path framework: a hierarchical visual encoder with cascaded group attention for spatio-temporal representation; a textual encoder trained with a hybrid objective combining masked language modeling (local semantics) and permuted language modeling (global semantics); cross-modal alignment for retrieval.
AI.result: New state-of-the-art R1 scores on MSRVTT (49.4%), MSVD (49.7%), LSMDC (27.3%); qualitative analysis shows coherent temporal attention and effective query-content alignment.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical attention and dual-objective text modeling substantially improve robust, context-aware video retrieval and suggest directions for future cross-modal representation learning.

title: RouteJudge: An Open Platform for Reproducible and Preference-Aware LLM Routing
comment: Accepted by Pluralistic Alignment Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: We present RouteJudge, an online pairwise preference evaluation framework for LLM routing systems, with a public platform available at https://routejudge.cn. Different from model-level response evaluation, RouteJudge focuses on router-level decision quality. For each user query, multiple routing strategies independently recommend candidate models under the same model pool and budget constraints. The selected model responses are then presented to users through anonymous pairwise comparisons, and the resulting user preferences are attributed back to the routing strategies behind the compared responses. Each evaluation record stores the query, routing decisions, model responses, preference labels, cost, latency, and task metadata, enabling preference-aware, cost-aware, and task-conditioned analysis of LLM routers. To support the continuous expansion of routing methods in RouteJudge, we further release ORBIT (Optimal Routing and Budgeted Inference Toolbox), a modular and extensible toolbox that standardizes the end-to-end workflow of LLM routing. ORBIT provides unified interfaces for benchmark loading, query representation, router implementation, budget-aware evaluation, and method comparison, allowing researchers to develop and evaluate routing algorithms under consistent protocols. It also serves as the submission and integration layer for RouteJudge: researchers can implement routing methods within ORBIT, validate them on existing routing benchmarks, and submit compatible routers for online preference-based evaluation. The code of ORBIT is available at https://github.com/AIGNLAI/LAMDA-ORBIT.

AI.tldr: Introduces RouteJudge, an online pairwise preference evaluation framework for LLM routing systems, and ORBIT, a modular toolbox to standardize end-to-end routing evaluation; aims to evaluate router-level decision quality and support continuous method development with a public platform and code release.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in evaluating LLM routing beyond model-level responses by focusing on router-level decision quality, enabling preference-aware and cost-aware analysis, as well as task-conditioned evaluation. Aims to provide standardized benchmarks and a reusable workflow to accelerate development of routing methods.
AI.method: For each user query, multiple routing strategies independently select candidate models from a common pool under a given budget. The selected model responses are presented to users via anonymous pairwise comparisons; user preferences are attributed back to the corresponding routing strategies. Evaluation records store query, routing decisions, model responses, preference labels, cost, latency, and task metadata. ORBIT provides modular interfaces for benchmark loading, query representation, router implementation, budget-aware evaluation, and method comparison, serving as the integration layer for RouteJudge. Code is released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.result: Proposes RouteJudge as a public platform and ORBIT as an extensible toolbox to enable preference-based, cost-aware, and task-conditioned evaluation of LLM routers under standardized protocols; releases and integration support facilitate the development and benchmarking of routing methods.
AI.conclusion: RouteJudge shifts evaluation focus from model-level to router-level decisions, enabling richer, preference-driven assessment of routing quality; ORBIT provides a modular, extensible workflow to support rapid development, benchmarking, and comparison of routing methods in LLM systems.

title: Taming I2V models for Image HOI Editing: A Cognitive Benchmark and Agentic Self-Correcting Framework
summary: Current image editing methods excel at static attributes but fail at complex Human-Object Interactions (HOI), a critical challenge unaddressed by existing benchmarks that conflate HOI with static attributes, relying on global metrics incapable of simultaneously assessing dynamic interaction validity and entangled human-object pair preservation. Thus, we first introduce HOI-Edit, a comprehensive benchmark with three progressive cognitive levels, which features an automated metric HOI-Eval that reliably evaluates instance-level interaction by letting VLM Q&A after thinking with images containing grounded Human-Object pairs. Considering the task's essence of remodeling dynamic relationships, we benchmark Image-to-Video (I2V) models, finding them inherently suited for dynamic editing due to their temporal generation capabilities. Crucially, beyond superior performance, this capability provides a "replay of the failure process," offering unique diagnosability into why errors occur. We thus propose SCPE (Self-Correcting Process Editing), a novel, agentic self-correcting framework that constrains the generation of I2V models through iteratively refined prompts, enabling the generated videos to more accurately present the target HOI. Extracted frames from these videos are the final editing results. On HOI-Edit, SCPE achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art (SOTA) editing models like Nano Banana on interaction. Code is available at https://github.com/oceanflowlab/HOI-Edit.

AI.tldr: Introduces HOI-Edit, a benchmark for dynamic Human-Object Interactions (HOI) with HOI-Eval metric; shows Image-to-Video (I2V) editing is suited for dynamic HOI editing; proposes SCPE self-correcting framework to iteratively refine prompts; achieves competitive results to SOTA and provides diagnosability via failure replay; code released.
AI.motivation: Current image editing methods excel at static attributes but struggle with complex HOI. Existing benchmarks conflate HOI with static attributes and rely on global metrics that fail to evaluate dynamic interaction validity or preservation of human-object pairs. A dedicated HOI-focused benchmark and diagnosable editing framework are needed.
AI.method: 1) Propose HOI-Edit benchmark with three progressive cognitive levels. 2) Develop HOI-Eval, an automated metric using Vision-Language Models performing Q&A after reasoning on images containing grounded HOI pairs. 3) Benchmark Image-to-Video models for dynamic editing; observe temporal generation offers diagnosability via failure replay. 4) Introduce SCPE (Self-Correcting Process Editing), an agentic loop that refines prompts to constrain I2V generation, producing frames as final edits. 5) Compare against SOTA models (e.g., Nano Banana) on HOI-Edit.
AI.result: SCPE achieves performance competitive with SOTA editing models on HOI-Edit interactions; the approach also provides replayability of failure processes, aiding diagnosability. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: HOI-Edit offers a comprehensive, multi-level benchmark to evaluate dynamic HOI editing and introduces a self-correcting, prompt-refinement framework that leverages I2V models for targeted HOI edits. This yields competitive results and improved diagnosability compared to existing methods.

title: Online Distributional Prediction via Latent Cluster Geometry Under Drift and Corruption
summary:
Online learning in non-stationary streams is often formulated as tracking a point estimate, but many applications require predicting the full data-generating distribution. We study online distributional prediction under drift and adversarial corruption. Our approach represents each candidate law through a latent cluster geometry: a variable-size configuration of centers that organizes probability mass and induces a predictive distribution. A Gibbs quasi-posterior over these configurations yields an online predictor by posterior averaging, and the resulting variable-dimensional posterior can be sampled with reversible-jump MCMC. The method therefore avoids specifying a parametric streaming law while retaining a structured latent space for uncertainty, regularization, and comparison.
  We evaluate performance by cumulative Wasserstein-1 regret against the time-varying true law. The analysis separates two effects: corruption perturbs the loss-based posterior update, whereas drift makes long-horizon posterior memory stale. We address the latter with a restarted variant that temporally localizes the same quasi-Bayesian update. The resulting high-probability bounds decompose into a PAC-Bayesian complexity term, a corruption-sensitive posterior perturbation term, and a dynamic optimal-transport term driven by \(A_T^{\mathrm{OT}}=\sum_{t=2}^T W_2^2(p_{t-1}^*,p_t^*)\). Under bounded support, stable latent geometry, predictive-map regularity, oracle realizability, localized restart windows, sublinear transport action, and sublinear corruption budget, the restarted predictor achieves sublinear cumulative Wasserstein regret. These guarantees require no parametric model for the stream, drift mechanism, or corruption process.

AI.tldr: Nonparametric online distributional prediction under drift and adversarial corruption via a variable-dimension latent-cluster model. Gibbs quasi-posterior with RJMCMC yields online predictive distributions, and a restarted variant achieves sublinear Wasserstein regret without assuming a parametric streaming law.
AI.motivation: In non-stationary environments, predicting the full data-generating distribution (not just a point estimate) under drift and corruption is crucial. Parametric streaming models are limiting; a flexible, nonparametric approach is needed that can adapt while providing uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Model candidate laws as variable-dimensional configurations of centers (latent clusters) that organize probability mass. Use a Gibbs quasi-posterior over configurations and posterior averaging for online prediction. Sample the variable-dimensional posterior with reversible-jump MCMC. Introduce a restarted variant to localize updates and mitigate drift memory. Derive high-probability bounds decomposed into PAC-Bayesian complexity, corruption perturbation, and dynamic optimal-transport terms.
AI.result: The restarted predictor achieves sublinear cumulative Wasserstein regret under stated assumptions (bounded support, stable latent geometry, predictive-map regularity, oracle realizability, localized restarts, sublinear transport action, sublinear corruption budget). Bounds hold without assuming a parametric model for the stream, drift, or corruption.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a nonparametric, structured latent-space approach to online distributional prediction in non-stationary/adversarial settings. It combines a flexible latent geometry with quasi-Bayesian Online learning and RJMCMC inference, yielding sublinear regret guarantees and uncertainty quantification without specifying a streaming law.

title: PorTEXTO: A European Portuguese Benchmark for Visual Text Extraction
summary: European Portuguese (pt-PT) is largely absent from OCR benchmarks, which skew toward high-resource languages. The few benchmarks that cover pt-PT focus on historical artifacts and literature. This work addresses modern OCR applications, introducing PorTEXTO, the first benchmark for contemporary and culturally relevant pt-PT visual text extraction. To ascertain quality, we employ an annotation pipeline combining transcriptions from a frontier LVLM with exhaustive review by native speakers. We observe a sharp performance drop from synthetic to real world samples in most models, and find that, currently, specialized multilingual data is a better driver for pt-PT performance than model size or resolution budget, motivating the release of open pt-PT OCR resources.

AI.tldr: Introduces PorTEXTO, the first benchmark for contemporary European Portuguese OCR; uses an LVLM-assisted transcription pipeline with native-speaker review; reveals a large synthetic-to-real performance drop and shows that targeted multilingual data beats model size or resolution in pt-PT OCR; advocates open pt-PT OCR resources.
AI.motivation: European Portuguese (pt-PT) is underrepresented in OCR benchmarks, which mostly cover high-resource languages or historical texts. There is a gap for modern, culturally relevant pt-PT visual text extraction to enable robust, real-world applications.
AI.method: Create PorTEXTO via an annotation pipeline: initial transcriptions from a frontier LVLM, followed by exhaustive review by native speakers. Evaluate OCR models on the benchmark and analyze the impact of data type (synthetic vs real), model size, resolution budget, and multilingual data on pt-PT performance.
AI.result: Models exhibit a sharp performance drop when moving from synthetic to real-world samples. Specialized multilingual training data provides larger performance gains for pt-PT than merely increasing model size or resolution. The work motivates releasing open pt-PT OCR resources.
AI.conclusion: PorTEXTO addresses a critical gap in pt-PT OCR benchmarks and demonstrates that data quality and language coverage are pivotal for pt-PT performance. This supports the release of open pt-PT OCR resources and guides future research toward multilingual, culturally relevant data rather than solely scaling models.

title: Bayesian Anytime Pareto Set Identification for Multi-Objective Multi-Armed Bandits
summary: Identifying Pareto optimal solutions is critical to support multi-objective decision-making. We introduce the first anytime Multi-Objective Multi-Armed Bandit algorithm for the Pareto Set Identification problem, taking a Bayesian approach: Top-Two Pareto Front Thompson Sampling (TTPFTS). We benchmark TTPFTS against state-of-the-art fixed-budget Pareto Set Identification algorithms on synthetic environments. Next, we demonstrate its practical utility in a challenging multi-objective molecular discovery setting by efficiently exploring an ultra-large synthesis-on-demand molecular library. Furthermore, we introduce a novel uncertainty quantification metric that estimates our algorithm's confidence in the predicted Pareto set. We demonstrate that this metric effectively proxies true performance, yielding a robust methodology for monitoring learning progress in complex settings. Finally, we complement these empirical findings with a theoretical proof of the algorithm's asymptotic correctness.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian anytime algorithm (Top-Two Pareto Front Thompson Sampling, TTPFTS) for Pareto Set Identification in multi-objective bandits; shows superior performance to fixed-budget baselines, scalable molecular discovery application, introduces an uncertainty-quantification metric, and provides asymptotic correctness guarantees.
AI.motivation: Identifying Pareto-optimal solutions is critical for multi-objective decision-making, especially under limited budgets and in large search spaces. An anytime Bayesian approach with uncertainty quantification can provide principled, scalable Pareto set identification.
AI.method: Develop Top-Two Pareto Front Thompson Sampling (TTPFTS). Benchmark against state-of-the-art fixed-budget Pareto set identification algorithms on synthetic tasks, and demonstrate in a large-scale molecular discovery setting. Introduce a new uncertainty quantification metric for confidence in the predicted Pareto set and prove asymptotic correctness of the algorithm.
AI.result: TTPFTS outperforms fixed-budget baselines on synthetic environments, demonstrates practical utility in ultra-large molecular discovery, introduces an uncertainty metric that correlates with true performance, and provides a theoretical proof of asymptotic correctness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TTPFTS framework delivers efficient Pareto set identification with principled uncertainty quantification and theoretical guarantees, scalable to very large search spaces such as synthesis-on-demand molecular libraries.

title: DVANet: Degradation-aware Visual-prior Alignment Network for Image Restoration
comment: All-in-One Image Restoration; Deep Unfolding; Degradation Representation; Visual Prior
summary: All-in-One image restoration aims to develop a unified restoration framework for handling diverse degradation types. Existing end-to-end methods usually regard the restoration process as a black-box mapping, lacking an explicit optimization interpretation. Although deep unfolding provides an interpretable iterative modeling paradigm for image restoration, existing methods mostly rely on fixed degradation assumptions or predefined degradation information, making them difficult to adapt to unified restoration requirements under complex degradations and locally damaged content. This limitation restricts their performance in degradation suppression and structural detail recovery. To address these issues, this paper proposes DVANet, a deep unfolding network inspired by the half-quadratic splitting optimization algorithm, which formulates unified image restoration under complex degradations as a collaborative unfolding process between degradation-aware observation consistency and visual-prior-guided reconstruction. Specifically, in the degradation-aware observation consistency branch, a degradation representation module is employed to extract global degradation attributes and local degradation cues, and degradation-conditioned mapping is used to enhance the model's adaptability to different degradation types. In the visual-prior-guided reconstruction branch, DINOv3 is introduced to provide structural and semantic information as hierarchical visual priors, thereby complementing the missing structural information in damaged regions and improving detail recovery. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DVANet achieves superior or competitive performance on multi-scenario degradation and cross-domain image restoration tasks, showing favorable degradation adaptability and generalization ability.

AI.tldr: DVANet is a deep-unfolding network for all-in-one image restoration based on half-quadratic splitting, coupling degradation-aware observation consistency with visual-prior-guided reconstruction. It uses a degradation representation module and degradation-conditioned mapping, plus DINOv3 priors, to adapt to multiple degradations and recover structural details.
AI.motivation: To achieve unified, interpretable restoration across diverse and complex degradations, addressing limitations of end-to-end black-box mappings and fixed-degradation unfoldings.
AI.method: A two-branch, deep-unfolding framework inspired by half-quadratic splitting. The degradation-aware observation consistency branch includes a degradation representation module for global/local degradation attributes and degradation-conditioned mapping to adapt to various degradations. The visual-prior-guided reconstruction branch uses DINOv3 as hierarchical visual priors to restore structure and detail. The framework alternates between observing degradation-consistent reconstructions and priors-based refinements.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that DVANet achieves superior or competitive performance on multi-scenario degradation and cross-domain restoration tasks, with strong degradation adaptability and generalization.
AI.conclusion: DVANet provides an interpretable, adaptable, and effective unified restoration approach by integrating degradation-aware observation consistency with priors-based reconstruction, achieving robust performance across diverse degradations.

title: Learning from Own Solutions: Self-Conditioned Credit Assignment for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has driven substantial progress in training LLMs for reasoning tasks, but representative methods such as GRPO assign uniform credit across all tokens, wasting gradient on routine tokens while under-crediting pivotal reasoning steps. Existing token-level credit assignment methods require resources beyond the model's own rollouts. GRPO variants rely on process reward models or ground-truth answers. Knowledge distillation assigns credit through per-token divergence but requires external teachers (On-Policy Distillation) or privileged information (On-Policy Self Distillation). However, these dependencies limit applicability in the pure RLVR setting. We observe that conditioning the model on its own verified trajectories induces a measurable per-token KL divergence between the original and conditioned distributions, and prove that distilling from a self-teacher constructed by verified trajectories leads to infeasible weighted-average solutions when multiple verified trajectories exist. We propose SC-GRPO (Self-Conditioned GRPO), which uses KL divergence mentioned before as a multiplicative weight on GRPO gradients. Across five benchmarks spanning math, code, and agentic tasks, SC-GRPO consistently outperforms 8.1% over GRPO and 5.9% over DAPO with stronger OOD performance. Moreover, SC-GRPO achieves higher performance than OPD.

AI.tldr: SC-GRPO introduces a self-conditioned GRPO that multiplies GRPO gradients by per-token KL divergence between the original and self-conditioned distributions, addressing credit-assignment inefficiency in RLVR without external teachers.
AI.motivation: Address inefficient token-level credit assignment in RLVR for LLM reasoning tasks by leveraging self-verified trajectories to weight gradients, removing dependence on process reward models, ground-truth answers, or external teachers.
AI.method: Propose SC-GRPO, which uses the KL divergence between the unconditioned policy and a policy conditioned on verified trajectories as a multiplicative weight on GRPO gradients; prove that self-teacher distillation is infeasible with multiple verified trajectories; evaluate on five benchmarks spanning math, code, and agentic tasks.
AI.result: SC-GRPO consistently outperforms GRPO (by 8.1%) and DAPO (by 5.9%), with stronger OOD performance, and surpasses OPD in evaluated settings.
AI.conclusion: Self-conditioned KL-weighted GRPO provides improved credit assignment in RLVR without external teachers, enhancing performance and generalization across tasks.

title: AMALIA-VL: A Native European Portuguese Open-Source Vision and Language Model
summary: Large Vision and Language Models (LVLMs) have advanced rapidly, yet European Portuguese (pt-PT) remains systematically underserved by existing open-source multimodal models, which either conflate it with Brazilian Portuguese or severely under-represent it in their training data mixes. We introduce AMALIA-VL, the first open-source instruction-tuned LVLM built natively for pt-PT, pairing a high-resolution vision encoder with dynamic image tiling and a fully open pt-PT-optimized language model via a learned connector. We contribute with a purposefully designed three-stage training process - vision-language alignment, general visual instruction tuning, and preference optimization - together with a pt-PT-centric multimodal data mix combining curated and translated public datasets with novel datasets that address the near-total absence of European Portuguese multimodal resources. Our evaluation shows that AMALIA-VL establishes a strong baseline for open-source pt-PT LVLMs.We will release model weights, training data, and construction pipelines along with machine-translated pt-PT evaluation benchmarks to help democratize pt-PT LVLM development.

AI.tldr: AMALIA-VL introduces the first open-source instruction-tuned LVLM native to pt-PT, with a three-stage training pipeline, dynamic image tiling, and a learned connector to a pt-PT language model; it will release weights, data, and benchmarks to democratize pt-PT LVLM development.
AI.motivation: European Portuguese (pt-PT) is systematically underserved by open-source LVLMs, with models conflating pt-PT with Brazilian Portuguese or lacking pt-PT in training data mixes. The work aims to enable a native, open, instruction-tuned pt-PT LVLM and to democratize access to data, models, and pipelines.
AI.method: Three-stage training: vision-language alignment, general visual instruction tuning, and preference optimization. Architectural setup includes a high-resolution vision encoder with dynamic image tiling and a learned connector to a fully open pt-PT-optimized language model. Data strategy combines curated and translated public multimodal datasets with novel pt-PT datasets to address the near-total absence of pt-PT multimodal resources.
AI.result: The paper claims AMALIA-VL establishes a strong baseline for open-source pt-PT LVLMs based on their evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The authors will release model weights, training data, construction pipelines, and machine-translated pt-PT evaluation benchmarks to support democratized development of pt-PT LVLMs.

title: Reinforcement Learning Foundation Models Should Already Be A Thing
summary:
Foundation models for language and vision are powered by internet-scale data, while structured domains (tabular prediction, time-series forecasting, graph learning, reinforcement learning) are not. The substitute is synthetic data, which shifts the burden from collection to prior design. Such priors already exist for many structured tasks: TabPFN and its successors solve tabular classification with a transformer pretrained on a synthetic Bayesian prior.
  We make two points. \textbf{First}, reinforcement learning is the conspicuous gap: sampling a synthetic MDP is as feasible as sampling a synthetic tabular dataset, yet no in-context RL work treats prior design as a primary objective. \textbf{Second}, MDPs admit a fixed-size sufficient statistic, independent of the episodes observed and tabular in shape, which makes them directly amenable to the attention-based architectures used for tabular foundation models, with a policy head replacing the supervised target. Together these define the agenda for an RL foundation model.
  As a proof of concept, we train one model entirely on synthetic MDPs and show that, with no task-specific tuning, it solves held-out tabular benchmarks in context, both online and offline: online, in far fewer episodes than UCB-VI and tabular Q-learning, and offline, competitively with VI-LCB.

AI.tldr: Proposes an RL foundation-model paradigm by using synthetic MDP priors, extending TabPFN-style synthetic-data pretraining to RL. Demonstrates a transformer trained solely on synthetic MDPs can perform held-out tabular benchmarks in-context without task-specific tuning, online with fewer episodes than UCB-VI and tabular Q-learning, and offline competitive with VI-LCB.
AI.motivation: Extend foundation-model ideas from language/vision to structured domains (RL) by leveraging synthetic priors. RL is the main missing piece where synthetic data design could serve as the primary objective. MDPs admit a fixed-size sufficient statistic, enabling attention-based architectures.
AI.method: Sample synthetic MDPs with a predetermined prior, train a transformer-based model on these MDPs with a policy head, and evaluate in-context on held-out tabular benchmarks (online and offline) without task-specific tuning.
AI.result: A model trained only on synthetic MDPs solves held-out tabular benchmarks in-context. Online, it requires far fewer episodes than UCB-VI and tabular Q-learning. Offline, it is competitive with VI-LCB.
AI.conclusion: Presents a concrete agenda for an RL foundation model: treat synthetic MDP priors as a primary design objective, leverage the fixed-size MDP representation with attention architectures, and show feasibility via a synthetic-data pretraining proof-of-concept. Further work should explore richer tasks, robustness to distribution shift, and comparisons on broader RL benchmarks.

title: ProductConsistency: Improving Product Identity Preservation in Instruction-Based Image Editing via SFT and RL
summary:
Recent advances in instruction-based image editing have enabled models to perform complex visual edits from natural language instructions. However, in product-centric scenarios where preserving product features, branding, and textual elements are critical, current open and closed source models often struggle to maintain this fine-grained object identity. This issue is further compounded by the lack of datasets for instruction-based product image editing with text fidelity constraints, leaving it largely treated as an implicit capability of instruction-based image editing models.
  In this work, we introduce the ProductConsistency dataset which is designed to improve product-centric image editing. Our approach includes a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset of 87k samples for product editing, a reinforcement learning (RL) dataset with 869 unique product images, and a new benchmark dataset, the ProductConsistency Benchmark, to allow rigorous and standardized evaluation of editing models. To guide RL training, we propose a Cyclic Consistency reward that enforces semantic preservation of product identity by using caption similarity between the original product description and captions generated from the edited image. We fine-tune both Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 and Flux.1-Kontext-dev using our dataset and demonstrate consistent improvements over baseline models in OCR and Perceptual metrics, and MLLM-based evaluations as well, indicating stronger product consistency, text rendering, and overall visual quality; with the Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 model achieving a 5x reduction in the character error rate. The code and pipeline is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ProductConsistency-6FCC/README.md

AI.tldr: Introduces the ProductConsistency dataset and benchmark to advance instruction-based product image editing with text fidelity. Includes an 87k-SFT dataset, a 869-unique-image RL dataset, and a Cyclic Consistency reward based on caption similarity; fine-tunes Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 and Flux.1-Kontext-dev with improved OCR, perceptual, and MLLM-based metrics, notably a 5x CER reduction for Qwen-Image-Edit-2511; code released.
AI.motivation: Preserve product identity, branding, and textual elements during instruction-based image edits in product-centric scenarios; address the lack of datasets and standardized evaluation for text-fidelity in editing.
AI.method: Construct a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset (87k samples) for product editing and a reinforcement learning (RL) dataset with 869 unique product images. Propose a Cyclic Consistency reward that uses caption similarity between the original product description and captions generated from the edited image. Fine-tune two models (Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 and Flux.1-Kontext-dev) on the dataset and evaluate on OCR, perceptual metrics, and MLLM-based metrics via the new ProductConsistency Benchmark.
AI.result: Observed consistent improvements over baselines in OCR, perceptual metrics, and multimodal language model (MLLM)-based evaluations, indicating stronger product consistency, text rendering, and overall visual quality. Qwen-Image-Edit-2511 achieved about a 5x reduction in character error rate (CER).
AI.conclusion: The proposed ProductConsistency dataset and Cyclic Consistency RL objective effectively improve instruction-based product image editing with text fidelity, offering a standardized benchmark and showing tangible gains on multiple evaluation fronts. Code and pipeline are available online.

title: Maturing Markov Decision Processes: Decision Making under Increasing Information and Shrinking Action Sets
summary: Sequential decision problems often exhibit an asymmetric evolution of information and decision flexibility: as a decision cycle unfolds, the agent receives richer information while feasible actions expire due to operational cutoffs, commitments, or resource constraints. Standard MDP formulations typically flatten this structure into stage-dependent state descriptions and action masks, thereby obscuring the nested information--action asymmetry that determines which decisions are urgent and which can be deferred. We introduce Maturing Markov Decision Processes (MMDPs), a formulation built around this information--action asymmetry. We characterize one of its key consequences through an expiring-action priority principle, which identifies the actions that must be resolved before the next stage. Motivated by this structure, we develop a structure-aware reinforcement learning framework with stage-aware policy design, expiring-action abstraction, and search-augmented learning with distillation. Experiments on a controlled multi-supplier replenishment problem, simplified cash-management environments of increasing complexity, and a production-scale simulator show that explicitly modeling this asymmetry improves learning efficiency and becomes increasingly valuable as decision problems scale.

AI.tldr: Maturing MDPs (MMDPs) model information–action asymmetry in sequential decisions; introduces a structure-aware RL framework with expiring-action abstraction; improves learning efficiency across several domains, especially as problems scale.
AI.motivation: Standard MDPs flatten time-varying information and expiring actions, obscuring urgency of decisions; explicit representation of asymmetric information evolution and action expiry can improve learning and policy design.
AI.method: Formalizes MMDP, derives expiring-action priority principle; develops structure-aware RL with stage-aware policies, expiring-action abstractions, and search-augmented learning with distillation.
AI.result: Empirical studies in multi-supplier replenishment, simplified cash-management, and production-scale simulator show improved learning efficiency; benefits amplify with problem scale.
AI.conclusion: Modeling information–action asymmetry yields more efficient learning and scalable performance; MMDP framework and techniques are valuable for sequential decision problems with deadline-driven decisions.

title: Urdu Katib Handwritten Dataset: A Historical Document Dataset for Offline Urdu Handwritten Text Recognition with CRNN-Based Baseline Evaluation
summary: Automatic Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) is inherently a challenging task, and its complexity is further increased when dealing with cursive scripts. Although significant efforts have been made on various cursive scripts, research regarding Urdu Handwritten Text Recognition (UHTR) has been relatively limited. This lag of research is primarily due to the unique challenges posed by its script, and the scarcity and unavailability of benchmark datasets. Therefore, to advance research in UHTR, this study presents a specialized real dataset called the Urdu Katib Handwritten Dataset (UKHD). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first offline Urdu handwritten text lines dataset specifically curated from the materials written by Katibs in historical times. It encompasses a diverse range of flat nib writing variations in the Nastalique calligraphic style. Additionally, the effectiveness of different CRNN-based hybrid models has been evaluated to identify the optimal architecture for Urdu Katib Handwriting Recognition (UKHR). Among the analyzed models, the CNN-BGRU-CTC model showed more robust performance, with low Character Error Rate (CER) and Word Error Rate (WER). This research work aims to support and encourage the research community in developing a robust recognition system for preserving Urdu handwritten literature.

AI.tldr: First offline Urdu handwritten line dataset from historical Katibs; UKHD; evaluates CRNN models; CNN-BGRU-CTC best with low CER/WER.
AI.motivation: Urdu HTR is underexplored due to script challenges and scarce benchmarks; need resources to preserve Urdu handwritten literature.
AI.method: Curated Urdu Katib Handwritten Dataset (UKHD) from historical Katibs in Nastalique style; evaluated multiple CRNN-based hybrids (CNN-BGRU-CTC among them) for offline line recognition using CTC loss.
AI.result: CNN-BGRU-CTC achieved the most robust performance with lower CER and WER than other models; demonstrates dataset utility for UHTR.
AI.conclusion: UKHD provides a valuable resource for developing robust Urdu handwritten recognition systems; enables preservation of Urdu literature; further improvements and larger datasets encouraged.

title: GateMem: Benchmarking Memory Governance in Multi-Principal Shared-Memory Agents
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/rzhub/GateMem and https://huggingface.co/datasets/Ray368/GateMem
summary: Memory benchmarks for LLM agents largely assume single-user settings, leaving shared assistants for hospitals, workplaces, campuses, and households understudied. In these deployments, multiple principals write to a common memory pool and query it under different roles, scopes, and relationships, so memory quality requires governance as well as recall. We introduce GateMem, a benchmark for multi-principal shared-memory agents. GateMem jointly evaluates utility for legitimate long-horizon requests with state updates, access control across contextual authorization boundaries, and agent-facing active forgetting after explicit deletion requests. It spans medical, office, education, and household domains, with long-form multi-party episodes, incremental memory injection, hidden checkpoints, structured judging, and leak-target annotations. Across diverse baselines and backbone models, no method simultaneously achieves strong utility, robust access control, and reliable forgetting. Long-context prompting often yields the best governance score at high token cost, while retrieval-based and external-memory methods reduce cost yet still leak unauthorized or deleted information. These results show current memory agents remain far from reliable shared institutional deployment.

AI.tldr: GateMem benchmarks multi-principal shared-memory agents evaluating utility, access control, and forgetting; current methods fail to satisfy all three; governance remains costly and leak-prone.
AI.motivation: In real-world deployments with multiple principals sharing a memory pool, governance over recall and deletion is essential; existing single-user benchmarks miss cross-principal dynamics.
AI.method: Develop GateMem with long-horizon episodes, memory injection, hidden checkpoints, structured judging, leak annotations; evaluate across medical/office/education/household domains; compare long-context prompting, retrieval-based, external memory baselines.
AI.result: No method achieves strong utility, robust access control, and forgetting; long-context prompts give governance but at high token cost; retrieval/external memory reduce cost but leak info.
AI.conclusion: Current memory agents are not ready for shared institutional deployment; GateMem reveals key tradeoffs and directions for improving multi-principal memory governance.

title: Hand-4DGS: Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting for 4D Hand Reconstruction from Egocentric Videos
summary: Dynamic 3D hand reconstruction from egocentric videos is essential for next-generation computing platforms such as AR/VR and AI glasses. Despite its importance, most prior works focus either on multi-view 3D hand reconstruction or on 4D human body reconstruction. Egocentric 4D hand reconstruction remains challenging due to fast head motion, rapid hand dynamics, severe occlusions, and inherent ambiguity from single-view observations. To address these challenges, we introduce Hand-4DGS, the first feed-forward framework for reconstructing dynamic 4D hands directly from egocentric videos, enabling both fast (~60 FPS) inference and strong generalization. Our approach incorporates a mesh-guided representation for structural priors and temporal convolutions to model dynamic motion. We evaluate our framework on two challenging egocentric datasets, H2O and ARCTIC, and demonstrate significant improvements over baselines. Our method benefits from the generalization capability of feed-forward networks and effective 2D image supervision through Gaussian splatting, without requiring expensive 3D hand pose ground-truth annotations.

AI.tldr: Introduces Hand-4DGS, a fast feed-forward pipeline for dynamic 4D hand reconstruction from egocentric video, achieving ~60 FPS with 2D supervision and mesh priors, evaluated on H2O/ARCTIC, outperforming baselines without 3D pose ground-truth.
AI.motivation: Dynamic 4D hand reconstruction from egocentric video is challenging due to fast head motion, rapid hand dynamics, severe occlusions, and single-view ambiguity. There is a need for real-time, generalizable 4D hand reconstruction directly from egocentric data without relying on multi-view setups or full 3D hand pose annotations.
AI.method: Proposes Hand-4DGS, a feed-forward framework that uses a mesh-guided representation as structural priors and temporal convolutions to model dynamic motion. It leverages Gaussian splatting for 2D image supervision, enabling training without expensive 3D hand pose ground-truth annotations.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant improvements over baselines on two challenging egocentric datasets, H2O and ARCTIC, and achieves fast ~60 FPS inference with good generalization across scenes and subjects.
AI.conclusion: First feed-forward method for reconstructing dynamic 4D hands directly from egocentric videos, providing real-time performance, effective 2D supervision, and reduced reliance on 3D annotation, with strong potential for AR/VR and AI glasses applications.

title: Target-confidence Recourse Using tSeTlin machines: TRUST
summary:
Counterfactual explanations are widely used to provide algorithmic recourse in high-stakes decision-making systems. Most existing methods seek the smallest change to an input that flips a model's decision. However, decision-makers often rely not only on predicted labels but also on confidence thresholds and risk margins. Counterfactuals that barely cross a decision boundary can be fragile and unstable under noise or model variation.
  In this paper, we propose Target-confidence Recourse Using tSeTlin machines (TRUST), a framework in which users explicitly specify the desired prediction confidence for recourse. Rather than generating counterfactuals and evaluating confidence afterward, TRUST directly searches for minimal changes that satisfy a user-defined confidence target, enabling comparison of recourse options in terms of cost, confidence, and robustness.
  We instantiate TRUST using a Probabilistic Tsetlin Machine (PTM) combined with Bayesian optimization. The probabilistic clause-based structure of PTM links prediction confidence to the stability of decision rules. We show that counterfactuals satisfying the same rules can still differ substantially in reliability depending on how securely they satisfy those rules, revealing whether decisions are supported by robust or fragile clause activations.
  Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that target-confidence counterfactuals produce more robust and interpretable recourse than conventional boundary-based approaches. Across multiple benchmarks, TRUST achieves perfect robustness while maintaining low recourse cost, including an L2 distance of 0.10 on the Haberman dataset at 0.92 confidence. By explicitly controlling confidence and exposing rule-level stability, TRUST provides actionable recourse for high-stakes decision support.

AI.tldr: Proposes TRUST to optimize counterfactual recourse for a target prediction confidence using a Probabilistic Tsetlin Machine and Bayesian optimization; demonstrates improved robustness and interpretability over boundary-based methods with low recourse cost.
AI.motivation: Decision-makers often require not only crossing a decision boundary but also reliable confidence and robustness to noise or model variation; conventional counterfactuals that barely cross boundaries can be fragile.
AI.method: Instantiate a target-confidence framework (TRUST) by combining a Probabilistic Tsetlin Machine (PTM) with Bayesian optimization to search for minimal input changes that satisfy a user-specified confidence target; connect prediction confidence to rule stability, enabling comparison of recourse options by cost, confidence, and robustness.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows the target-confidence counterfactuals are more robust and interpretable than boundary-based approaches; TRUST achieves high robustness with low recourse cost (e.g., Haberman: L2=0.10 at 0.92 confidence; reported “perfect robustness” across benchmarks).
AI.conclusion: Explicit control of confidence and exposure of rule-level stability yields actionable recourse for high-stakes decision support, outperforming traditional boundary-focused counterfactuals in robustness and interpretability while maintaining low cost.

title: When AUC Misleads: Polarization-Aware Evaluation of Deepfake Detectors under Domain Shift
summary: Recent advances in generative AI, such as diffusion models and face-swapping tools, have enabled the creation of highly realistic deepfakes, leading to real-world harms including financial fraud and non-consensual explicit content. In response, deepfake detection has become an active research area, with recent methods increasingly focusing on improving generalization to unseen manipulations. This is typically evaluated using the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) measured separately across multiple datasets. However, such an evaluation fails to reflect real-world scenarios where detectors face a mixture of data sources and varying artifact types. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel metric, Cross-dataset AUC (Cross-AUC) that averages per-domain AUCs with a measure of prediction polarization for taking into account the robustness to domain shift. The polarization extent is quantified by the Wasserstein Distance between class score distributions. Cross-AUC not only assesses the generalization capabilities of deepfake detectors under domain shifts more realistically, but it is also interpretable as it better explains the reason behind a drop in performance. Experiments performed on seven benchmark datasets demonstrate its practical relevance.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cross-dataset AUC (Cross-AUC) for evaluating deepfake detectors under domain shifts by averaging per-domain AUCs and incorporating a polarization measure via Wasserstein distance; validated on seven benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current cross-dataset AUC evaluations ignore mixed-domain data and varying artifact types, which can misrepresent real-world detector robustness; a metric that accounts for domain shift and provides interpretable diagnostics is needed.
AI.method: Define Cross-AUC as the average of per-domain AUCs with an added polarization term quantified by the Wasserstein distance between class-score distributions, capturing robustness to domain shifts and enabling interpretability of performance drops.
AI.result: Cross-AUC correlates with robustness to domain shifts and offers interpretable diagnostics for why performance degrades; validated empirically on seven benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: Cross-AUC provides a realistic, interpretable evaluation framework for deepfake detectors under heterogeneous data sources, guiding development of more robust detectors.

title: Seed-Guided Semi-Supervised Clustering by A-Contrario Anomaly Detection
summary: This paper introduces a semi-supervised clustering framework grounded in the statistical duality between grouping principles and anomaly detection. We address the challenge of robust cluster definition in noisy environments -- a task where partitioning algorithms often over-assign outliers and density-based methods remain sensitive to heuristic global parameters. Drawing on \textit{a-contrario} statistical reasoning and Gestalt proximity principles, we define a cluster as a maximal subset of data points containing no anomalies relative to a null hypothesis of uniform randomness. Central to this approach is the Perception algorithm, which utilises a principled expectation-based threshold ($\mathbb{E} < 1$) to identify outliers without manual parameter tuning. By treating clustering as the dual of anomaly detection, we employ an iterative ``clustering-by-exclusion'' mechanism. The algorithm is seed-guided, leveraging minimal user-provided labels to initialise robust cluster medians and form initial groups, which are subsequently expanded by admitting non-anomalous points. This approach naturally isolates fringe points, isolated noise, and emerging unknown clusters. We evaluate the method on synthetic and real-world benchmarks, including image and text datasets represented through raw, linear-reduced, and neighbourhood-preserving embeddings. Results demonstrate that with as few as 10--30 seeds per cluster, the proposed method achieves competitive and often very strong performance under a practical low-tuning benchmarking protocol, while maintaining linear scalability with respect to both observations and dimensionality for a fixed number of seeded clusters and iterations.

AI.tldr: Semi-supervised clustering via a-contrario duality with anomaly detection. A seed-guided, perception-based, clustering-by-exclusion framework that robustly defines clusters as non-anomalous under a uniform null, yielding scalable, low-tuning performance across image/text embeddings.
AI.motivation: Robust clustering in noisy data where outliers are over-assigned and density-based methods require sensitive global parameters; leverage statistical duality between grouping and anomaly detection to obtain parameter-light, robust clusters.
AI.method: Define clusters as maximal non-anomalous subsets under a uniform random null. Use the Perception algorithm with an expectation-based threshold (E < 1) to flag anomalies without manual tuning. Iterate via clustering-by-exclusion, seeding with minimal user labels to initialize robust cluster medians, then expand by admitting non-anomalous points; isolates fringe points and unknown clusters. Works across raw, reduced, and neighborhood-preserving embeddings; scalable to observations and dimensionality for fixed seeds/iterations.
AI.result: Evaluated on synthetic and real-world benchmarks (image and text), with 10–30 seeds per cluster achieving competitive to strong results under low-tuning benchmarking. Demonstrates linear scalability in N and D for a fixed number of seeded clusters and iterations.
AI.conclusion: The duality between clustering and anomaly detection via a-contrario reasoning yields a robust, scalable semi-supervised clustering framework. With minimal supervision, it isolates noise/fringe clusters and unknown structures, performing well across diverse representations and avoiding heavy parameter tuning typical of density-based methods.

title: Moebius: 0.2B Lightweight Image Inpainting Framework with 10B-Level Performance
summary: While 10B-level industrial foundation models have pushed the boundaries of image inpainting, their prohibitive computational costs severely hinder practical deployment. Constructing a highly optimized task-specific specialist offers a promising solution; however, extreme structural compression inevitably triggers a severe representation bottleneck. To conquer this, we propose Moebius, a highly efficient lightweight inpainting framework. We systematically reconstruct the diffusion backbone by introducing the Local-$λ$ Mix Interaction ($LλMI$) block. Comprising Local-$λ$ and Interactive-$λ$ modules, it elegantly summarizes spatial contexts and global semantic priors into fixed-size linear matrices, preserving complex latent interactions while drastically shedding parameters. Furthermore, to unlock the full representational capacity of this highly compact architecture, we synergistically pair it with an adaptive multi-granularity distillation strategy. Operating strictly within the latent space to avoid expensive pixel-space decoding, this strategy dynamically balances multiple gradient-based losses to achieve high-fidelity alignment. Extensive experiments across natural and portrait benchmarks demonstrate that this optimal synergy enables Moebius to rival or even surpass the generation quality of the 10B-level industrial generalist FLUX.1-Fill-Dev. Remarkably, Moebius achieves this using less than 2\% of the parameters (0.22B vs. 11.9B) while delivering a $>15\times$ acceleration in total inference time, setting a new efficiency standard for high-fidelity inpainting. Project page at https://hustvl.github.io/Moebius.

AI.tldr: Moebius is a highly efficient lightweight inpainting framework that replaces a large diffusion backbone with a compact LλMI block and latent-space adaptive distillation, achieving competitive quality with only 0.22B parameters (2% of 11.9B) and over 15× faster inference, rivaling a 10B+ industrial model.
AI.motivation: Tackling the prohibitive compute of 10B-scale generalist diffusion models for inpainting by designing a task-specific, compact architecture that preserves expressive power despite aggressive compression.
AI.method: Introduce Local-λ Mix Interaction (LλMI) comprising Local-λ and Interactive-λ modules to summarize spatial contexts and global semantics into fixed-size linear matrices. Pair this with an adaptive multi-granularity distillation strategy operating entirely in latent space, using dynamically balanced gradient-based losses to align representations with a larger teacher without pixel-space decoding.
AI.result: Empirical results on natural and portrait datasets show Moebius matches or exceeds the quality of the 11.9B FLUX model while using 0.22B parameters, and achieves >15× faster total inference time, highlighting a favorable efficiency-accuracy trade-off.
AI.conclusion: The combination of a compact diffusion backbone (LλMI) and latent-space distillation yields state-of-the-art efficiency for high-fidelity inpainting, establishing a new efficiency standard for task-specific specialist models.

title: Identifying Structural Biases from Causal Mechanism Shifts
summary: Causal discovery methods commonly assume that all data is independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.) and that there are no unmeasured variables affecting the system. In practice, these assumptions are often violated, leading to inaccurate inference. In this paper, we study how to identify hidden confounding and selection biases from causal mechanism shifts. In particular, we show that structural biases lead to dependent mechanism shifts. That is, by considering for which variables the mechanisms change given data from different environments, we can tell which variables are unbiased, which are subject to hidden confounding, and which are undergoing selection bias. We formalize this into an empirically testable criterion based on mutual information, and show under which conditions it identifies structural biases. To tell which nodes are subject to what kind of bias, we introduce the StruBI algorithm. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data show that StruBI works well in practice, accurately recovering affected variable sets and types of biases, outperforming the state-of-the-art by a wide margin.

AI.tldr: Proposes StruBI to identify hidden confounding and selection biases from causal mechanism shifts across environments, using a mutual-information-based criterion and showing strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Common causal-discovery assumptions (i.i.d. data and no unmeasured variables) are violated in practice, leading to biased inferences. There is a need to identify the type and source of biases from data shifts.
AI.method: The paper proves that structural biases induce dependent mechanism shifts. It defines an empirical mutual-information criterion to detect these shifts and introduces StruBI, an algorithm that classifies variables into unbiased, hidden-confounding affected, or selection-bias affected; validated on synthetic and real-world data.
AI.result: StruBI accurately recovers affected variable sets and bias types, and outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a wide margin.
AI.conclusion: Mechanism-shift analyses coupled with mutual-information criteria enable practical identification of structural biases in causal discovery, improving robustness under distributional shifts.

title: Scaling Learning-based AEB with Massive Unlabeled Data
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS)
summary: This paper studies how to scale learning-based automatic emergency braking (AEB) with massive unlabeled fleet data under production constraints. Our approach is based on meta-feedback semi-supervised learning (MF-SSL), where a teacher generates pseudo labels for unlabeled driving data and is updated using a small labeled anchor set as safety-critical feedback. In production, anchor ambiguity and labeled-unlabeled mismatch can amplify systematic pseudo-label errors, leading to spurious triggers. We propose a stabilized MF-SSL framework with (i) Noise-Aware Decoupling, which removes ambiguity-prone anchors from the teacher's supervised update path, and (ii) kinematics-gated pseudo-labeling with a teacher conflict penalty to suppress mismatch-induced risk hallucinations on unlabeled data while maintaining broad coverage. Extensive experiments show consistent gains as unlabeled data scale from 1M to 1B windows, improving safety while keeping comfort stable. The 1B-trained student model is deployed to hundreds of thousands of vehicles and validated over \$10^9$ km of driving, achieving a positive-to-false activation ratio exceeding 100:1 and a 35% improvement in accident-free driving mileage over a production rule-only baseline.

AI.tldr: A scalable meta-feedback semi-supervised learning (MF-SSL) framework for automatic emergency braking (AEB) uses a teacher-student setup with noise-aware and kinematics-gated pseudo-labeling to handle anchor ambiguity and label-unlabeled mismatch, achieving large-scale improvements and real-world deployment with high safety gains.
AI.motivation: Scale learning-based AEB with massive unlabeled driving data under production constraints while mitigating pseudo-label errors caused by anchor ambiguity and unlabeled-unlabeled mismatch, which can trigger spurious activations.
AI.method: MF-SSL: a teacher generates pseudo labels for unlabeled data; a small labeled anchor set provides safety-critical feedback. Stabilized MF-SSL introduces (i) Noise-Aware Decoupling to remove ambiguity-prone anchors from the teacher's supervised update path, and (ii) kinematics-gated pseudo-labeling with a teacher conflict penalty to suppress mismatch-induced risk hallucinations while preserving broad coverage.
AI.result: Experiments show performance gains as unlabeled data scales from 1M to 1B windows. The 1B-trained student is deployed to hundreds of thousands of vehicles and validated over 1e9 km, yielding a positive-to-false activation ratio > 100:1 and a 35% increase in accident-free driving mileage compared with a production rule-only baseline.
AI.conclusion: Stabilized MF-SSL effectively scales learning-based AEB using unlabeled data while controlling anchor ambiguity and mismatch risk, delivering substantial safety improvements in large-scale deployment with stable user comfort.

title: ROSA-TFormer: A Radar-Optical Sensor-Aware Temporal Transformer for Pinus sylvestris Plantation Classification in Northern Shaanxi Using GEE-Derived Sentinel-1/2 Time Series
summary: Accurate identification of Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica plantations is important for monitoring afforestation quality and ecological restoration in northern Shaanxi. This paper proposes ROSA-TFormer, a radar-optical sensor-aware temporal Transformer for P. sylvestris classification using Sentinel-1/2 time-series data generated on Google Earth Engine. The model integrates separate SAR and optical embedding branches, a sensor-aware gate, and temporal attention pooling to capture multi-source seasonal features. Experiments on monthly and half-month point-level datasets show that ROSA-TFormer achieves strong classification performance, with 99.67% overall accuracy, 99.56% macro F1, and 98.91% P. sylvestris F1 on the HalfMonth-dataBig dataset. Spatial block validation and ablation results further indicate the effectiveness of radar-optical temporal fusion and sensor-aware modeling. The results demonstrate the potential of ROSA-TFormer for point-level P. sylvestris plantation classification, while broader wall-to-wall validation remains necessary.

AI.tldr: ROSA-TFormer: a radar–optical sensor-aware temporal Transformer for Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica plantation classification using Sentinel-1/2 time-series on Google Earth Engine; shows high accuracy on HalfMonth-dataBig; supports radar–optical temporal fusion with sensor-aware modeling; broader wall-to-wall validation remains necessary.
AI.motivation: Accurate monitoring of P. sylvestris var. mongolica plantations is important for afforestation quality and ecological restoration in northern Shaanxi; requires effective fusion of SAR and optical time-series data and temporal modeling to track seasonal patterns.
AI.method: Proposes ROSA-TFormer with separate SAR and optical embedding branches, a sensor-aware gate, and temporal attention pooling to perform radar–optical temporal fusion on Sentinel-1/2 time-series data generated on Google Earth Engine; evaluated on monthly and half-month point-level datasets.
AI.result: Achieves 99.67% overall accuracy, 99.56% macro F1, and 98.91% P. sylvestris F1 on the HalfMonth-dataBig dataset; spatial block validation and ablation support effectiveness of radar–optical temporal fusion and sensor-aware modeling.
AI.conclusion: Shows strong potential for point-level P. sylvestris plantation classification; broader wall-to-wall validation is still required to confirm generalization.

title: Semantic Robustness Certification for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are now widely used in downstream tasks. However, real-world applications often expose VLMs to distribution shifts induced by semantic variation (e.g., shape, size, and style). Robustness certification determines if a model's prediction changes when transformations are applied to its input. While most certification frameworks study geometric or pixel-level transformations over inputs, this work proposes a novel framework that enables certifying VLM robustness under semantic-level transformations. Leveraging the open-vocabulary capability of VLMs, we use text prompts as semantic proxies to construct transformations parameterized by an extent that controls the degree of semantic variation. By characterizing the VLM decision boundary in closed form, our framework quantitatively certifies extent intervals for which the predicted class remains unchanged under the semantic transformation. Our framework is the first to certify VLM robustness under semantic-level variations without requiring additional data for each variation, making it practical to apply. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world data show that our framework enables certifying robustness under diverse semantic variations across scenarios.

AI.tldr: Certifies Vision-Language Model (VLM) robustness under semantic-level transformations using text prompts as semantic proxies, deriving a closed-form decision boundary to certify robustness intervals without additional data per variation.
AI.motivation: VLMs suffer distribution shifts from semantic variations (shape, size, style); existing certification focuses on geometric/pixel-level changes; need semantic-level robustness guarantees leveraging open-vocabulary VLM capabilities.
AI.method: Construct semantic transformations by varying text prompts as proxies for semantic changes; parameterize by an extent indicating degree of variation; derive closed-form characterization of the decision boundary to certify that the predicted class remains unchanged within an extent interval; does not require extra data per variation.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world data show successful certification of robustness under diverse semantic variations across scenarios.
AI.conclusion: First framework to certify VLM robustness under semantic-level variations without additional data for each variation; practical and broadly applicable.

title: Domain-Shift Aware Neural Networks for Unbalance Characterization in Rotating Systems
summary: This work investigates the application of a domain-shift aware neural network for regression tasks aimed at estimating unbalance masses in rotating shafts under varying operating conditions. Experimental data were collected from a test rig in which a primary shaft, equipped with a flange carrying unbalanced masses, was driven at different rotational speeds, while a secondary shaft could be optionally activated to introduce domain discrepancy. The unbalance masses were positioned at a fixed radial distance, and the dynamic response of the system was recorded using triaxial accelerometers. The inverse problem of mass estimation is formulated within a domain adaptation framework, where the network is trained with a maximum mean discrepancy strategy to align feature representations across source and target distributions. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of explicitly addressing domain shift in improving prediction accuracy, especially when the system's physical behavior and sources of domain discrepancy are not fully known and fall outside the training conditions. These findings highlight the potential of domain-shift aware models for regression tasks in Structural Health Monitoring.

AI.tldr: Domain-shift aware regression using MMD-based domain adaptation improves unbalance mass estimation in rotating shafts under varying operating conditions.
AI.motivation: To achieve robust unbalance mass estimation in SHM despite domain discrepancies arising from changing operating conditions and system configurations.
AI.method: Experimentally collect data from a test rig with primary and optionally a secondary shaft to induce domain shift; model the inverse mass estimation as a regression task; apply maximum mean discrepancy-based domain adaptation to align source and target feature distributions.
AI.result: The approach improves prediction accuracy by explicitly handling domain shift; benefits are pronounced when physical behavior and domain discrepancy sources are not fully known and extend beyond training conditions.
AI.conclusion: Domain-shift aware regression with MMD-based domain adaptation is promising for SHM regression tasks and supports reliable mass estimation across varying operating regimes.

title: GUMP-Net: An interpretable model-data-driven intelligent algorithm for multi-class pelvic segmentation
summary: Pelvic segmentation is one of the most important and fundamental research problems in precise and intelligent diagnosis and treatment, as well as surgical planning and navigation for pelvic fractures. By combining an improved geodesic active contour model with deep neural networks, we propose GUMP-Net, an interpretable model-data-driven intelligent algorithm for multi-class pelvic segmentation, in which three network modules are designed to constitute the overall segmentation framework together: the object detection module for automatic level set initialization, the edge detector module for learning an anatomy-aware edge detector function and the iteration module for deep level set evolution. Leveraging the advantages of level set representation and deep learning, GUMP-Net shows more accurate, robust and consistent segmentation performance, especially in small training data situation, compared to the state-of-the-art methods. Extensive experiments on pelvic datasets demonstrate the rationality and effectiveness of the proposed algorithm. Further experiments extended to ankle dataset indicate broader applications to other anatomies. The proposed algorithm not only provides an efficient segmentation method for complex fracture reduction, but also gives an interpretable geometric perspective for understanding deep learning segmentation.

AI.tldr: GUMP-Net combines an improved geodesic active contour with deep learning for multi-class pelvic segmentation, using three modules (auto initialization, anatomy-aware edge detection, deep level-set evolution). It improves accuracy and robustness, especially with small data, and extends to other anatomies, offering interpretability of deep segmentation via geometric insights.
AI.motivation: Accurate, robust, and interpretable multi-class pelvic segmentation is essential for diagnosis, surgical planning, and navigation, but it is challenging due to limited labeled data and complex anatomy. The work aims to integrate classic geometric segmentation with data-driven learning to enhance performance and interpretability.
AI.method: A three-module framework: (1) object detection module for automatic level-set initialization; (2) edge detector module learning an anatomy-aware edge detector function; (3) iteration module performing deep level-set evolution, combining geodesic active contour with deep networks for segmentation.
AI.result: GUMP-Net achieves more accurate, robust, and consistent segmentation than state-of-the-art methods, particularly in small data scenarios. Extensive pelvic datasets validate effectiveness; extended experiments on an ankle dataset suggest broader applicability and interpretability of the deep learning segmentation from a geometric perspective.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm offers an efficient segmentation method for complex fracture reduction and provides an interpretable geometric understanding of deep learning-based segmentation, with potential applicability to other anatomies.

title: Learning from Your Own Mistakes: Constructing Learnable Micro-Reflective Trajectories for Self-Distillation
summary: Self-distillation improves reasoning in large language models by using the model's own rollouts as training signal, typically through implicit logit-level alignment that minimizes KL divergence toward a privileged target distribution. However, because this supervision is generated via uncontrolled sampling, it provides no diagnostic insight into the model's specific errors or corrective guidance for its individual failure patterns. Consequently, the model learns to imitate a privileged distribution rather than receiving fine-grained corrections that pinpoint where and why its reasoning fails. In this paper, we propose Trajectory-Augmented Policy Optimization (TAPO), which advances self-distillation from implicit distributional alignment to explicit trajectory construction. During RL training, the model produces both correct and incorrect rollouts to the same query, and TAPO leverages this contrastive structure to construct micro-reflective corrections, new training trajectories that retain the model's erroneous reasoning up to the point of failure, then insert a natural-language diagnosis and corrected reasoning guided by a correct reference from the same sampling group. Since each trajectory is anchored in the learner's own prefix and solutions, the corrective signal preserves the model's on-policy distribution to a greater extent than the position-wise alignment imposed by KL-based methods. To integrate these trajectories, TAPO introduces difficulty-aware candidate selection at the model's capability boundary and decoupled advantage estimation to prevent gradient contamination. Experiments on AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and HMMT 2025 show that TAPO achieves consistent improvements over GRPO under the same number of training steps. Further analysis demonstrates that TAPO strengthens both first-pass reasoning and error-correction effectiveness.

AI.tldr: TAPO replaces implicit logit alignment with explicit trajectory-level feedback to diagnose and correct specific reasoning errors, yielding on-policy, targeted improvements over standard self-distillation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of diagnostic insight and error-specific guidance in traditional self-distillation, which uses uncontrolled sampling and KL alignment toward a privileged target distribution.
AI.method: During RL fine-tuning, generate both correct and incorrect rollouts for the same prompt. Construct micro-reflective, NL-diagnosed corrected trajectories anchored to the model's own prefix; use difficulty-aware candidate selection at the capability boundary and decoupled advantage estimation to integrate these trajectories while preserving the on-policy distribution.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on AIME 2024/2025 and HMMT 2025 shows TAPO consistently outperforms GRPO under the same training steps, with improvements in both first-pass reasoning and error-correction capability.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, trajectory-based corrections provide targeted feedback that preserves the learner's distribution and enhances both reasoning and error-correction, representing a substantive advance over KL-based self-distillation.

title: A Controlled Benchmark of Quantum-Latent GAN Augmentation for Brain MRI
