comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Medical image classification is often constrained by limited labeled data, motivating generative augmentation; recently, quantum generative models have been proposed for this purpose, frequently reporting accuracy gains. However, such claims are typically based on single training runs, do not match the parameter budgets of the quantum and classical generators, and do not characterize the data regime in which any benefit appears. We present a controlled benchmark that isolates the contribution of a quantum generator to brain-MRI augmentation. Images are encoded into a KL-regularized latent space in which a conditional Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty is trained using either a variational quantum generator or a classical generator of near-identical parameter count (1648 vs. 1632). Synthetic samples are decoded and used to augment a pretrained classifier across labeled data fractions from 5% to 100%, evaluated over eight random seeds with paired significance testing (with multiple-comparison correction) and with intraset diversity and latent-distribution analyses. Across all fractions, no augmentation variant significantly outperforms real-data-only training, and the quantum and classical generators are statistically indistinguishable. Any low-data benefit behaves as regularization rather than faithful data expansion:synthetic samples are off distribution and severely mode collapsed precisely where data is scarce, and the quantum generator is no more diverse thanits classical counterpart. We release the protocol as a testbed for rigorous evaluation of quantum generative augmentation in medical imaging.

AI.tldr: Across a controlled benchmark on brain-MRI augmentation, a variational quantum generator offers no advantage over a near-identical classical generator. Both fail to outperform real-data-only training, even across data fractions and multiple seeds; any observed benefit at low data is due to regularization and not genuine data expansion, with synthetic samples being off-distribution and mode-collapsed. The study provides a rigorous protocol for evaluating quantum generative augmentation.
AI.motivation: To rigorously assess whether quantum generative models provide measurable benefits for data augmentation in medical imaging, isolating the quantum component from classical baselines and controlling for parameter budgets, data regimes, and evaluation rigor.
AI.method: Encode images into a KL-regularized latent space; train a conditional Wasserstein GAN with gradient penalty using either a variational quantum generator or a classical generator with nearly identical parameter counts (1648 vs 1632). Decode synthetic samples to augment a pretrained classifier, evaluating across data fractions from 5% to 100% with eight random seeds, paired significance tests with multiple-comparison correction, and analyses of intraset diversity and latent distributions.
AI.result: No augmentation variant significantly outperforms real-data-only training across all fractions; quantum and classical generators are statistically indistinguishable. Low-data benefits are reg of regularization rather than true data expansion; synthetic samples are off-distribution and highly mode-collapsed where data is scarce; quantum generator is not more diverse than its classical counterpart.
AI.conclusion: A controlled benchmark shows no measurable advantage of quantum-generated augmentation over classical or real data, underscoring the need for rigorous, standardized evaluation protocols for quantum generative methods in medical imaging.

title: Transformer Geometry Observatory TGO-I: Spectral Geometry Observatory
summary: Despite the widespread adoption of Vision Transformers (ViTs) and their success across numerous computer vision applications, the fundamental understanding of their dimensional and representational geometry remains relatively underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce Transformer Geometry Observatory (TGO), a systematic framework of experiments and analysis pipelines designed to investigate the representational geometry and dynamics of Vision Transformers. TGO-I, the first installment of the framework, focuses on the spectral geometry of ViT representations. Using a ViT-Small/16 model trained on ImageNet-100, we analyze Effective Rank, Stable Rank, Participation Ratio, Spectral Entropy, Spectral Flatness, Spectral Anisotropy, covariance structure, eigenspectra, and singular value spectra throughout training. Our results reveal a consistent increase in dimensional utilization, accompanied by decreasing anisotropy, increasing spectral entropy, increasing participation ratio, and progressively flatter eigenspectra. Contrary to the common intuition that training should concentrate information into a small number of dominant directions, we observe a progressive redistribution of variance across representational dimensions. This phenomenon is particularly pronounced in the final CLS token representation, which exhibits the highest effective dimensionality and lowest anisotropy within the network.

AI.tldr: TGO-I reveals that ViT representations progressively utilize more dimensions with increasing entropy and a flattening spectrum during training, culminating in highly dimensional, low-anisotropy CLS representations, challenging the notion of information concentration.
AI.motivation: To address the limited fundamental understanding of Vision Transformer (ViT) representational geometry and dynamics, and to provide a systematic framework (Transformer Geometry Observatory) for analyzing their geometry.
AI.method: Apply a suite of geometry and spectral metrics (Effective Rank, Stable Rank, Participation Ratio, Spectral Entropy, Spectral Flatness, Spectral Anisotropy, covariance structure, eigenspectra, and singular value spectra) to ViT-Small/16 trained on ImageNet-100, tracked throughout training.
AI.result: Dimensional utilization increases over training; anisotropy decreases; spectral entropy and participation ratio increase; eigenspectra become progressively flatter; variance redistributes across dimensions; the final CLS token exhibits the highest dimensionality and lowest anisotropy.
AI.conclusion: Training of ViTs does not merely concentrate information into a few dominant directions; instead, variance spreads across more dimensions, with the CLS token achieving peak dimensionality and isotropy, revealing dynamic spectral geometry that informs ViT design and analysis.

title: Investigating Inductive Biases for Machine Learning Emulation of Sudden Stratospheric Warmings in Idealised Isca Simulations
summary: Machine-learning emulators are increasingly used for weather prediction and have the potential to extend skill on subseasonal-to-seasonal timescales by learning dynamically important sources of predictability. A key challenge is whether the models can exploit predictability anchors, such as stratospheric variability, that influence tropospheric circulation beyond short lead times. We test how architectural inductive bias affects emulation of sudden stratospheric warming (SSW) dynamics using paired idealised Isca simulations that differ only in an imposed wave-2 heating perturbation. Across convolutional, transformer, and graph-based architectures trained for one-step prediction, model differences are modest when the stratosphere is dynamically quiet but widen substantially when SSW-like variability is active. Our results identify explicit three-dimensional vertical coupling as a key inductive bias for machine-learning emulation of stratospheric dynamics. However, Eliassen-Palm flux diagnostics show that low forecast error does not guarantee physically faithful wave-mean-flow interaction, with coherent errors remaining in stratospheric wave-driving structure.

AI.tldr: 3D vertical coupling is a key inductive bias for ML emulators of stratospheric dynamics; model differences across architectures become pronounced under SSW-like variability, and low numeric error does not guarantee physically faithful wave-mean-flow interactions.
AI.motivation: Evaluate how architectural inductive biases in ML emulators affect the learning and replication of stratospheric dynamics, particularly sudden stratospheric warmings (SSWs), to improve subsesonal-to-seasonal forecast skill.
AI.method: Train one-step predictors using convolutional, transformer, and graph-based architectures on paired idealized Isca simulations that differ only by an imposed wave-2 heating perturbation; assess performance and physical fidelity, including EP-flux diagnostics.
AI.result: When the stratosphere is dynamically quiet, architectural choices yield modest differences; under SSW-like variability, differences widen substantially. A three-dimensional vertical coupling inductive bias emerges as crucial for emulating stratospheric dynamics. However, low forecast error does not guarantee physically faithful wave-mean-flow interaction, with coherent errors persisting in the stratospheric wave-driving structure as revealed by EP-flux diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: In ML emulation of stratospheric dynamics, embedding a 3D vertical coupling inductive bias is essential for capturing SSW behavior; evaluation must go beyond raw forecast error to include diagnostics of wave-mean-flow interactions to ensure physical fidelity.

title: FoMoE: Breaking the Full-Replica Barrier with a Federation of MoEs
summary: Pre-training Large Language Models (LLMs) typically demands large-scale infrastructure with tightly coupled hardware accelerators. While increasing model and dataset scale remains the dominant driver of performance, Mixture-of-Experts (MoEs) architectures have recently achieved state-of-the-art results by decoupling parameter count from computational cost. This efficiency enables training massive models on constrained compute budgets, yet it typically requires the high-speed interconnects of a single datacenter. To overcome these physical limits, recent approaches such as DiLoCo and Photon use low-communication data-parallel methods to enable scaling across geographically distributed, weakly connected data centers. However, these methods suffer from a fundamental inefficiency: they require full model replicas at every site, which imposes prohibitive memory constraints and communication overheads. In this work, we introduce FoMoE, a system that breaks the full-replica paradigm by partitioning expert layers across workers. We demonstrate that FoMoE: (I) reduces communication costs by up to 1.42x over efficient baselines and 45.44x over DDP via partial expert replication in the studied regimes; (II) achieves empirical throughput speedups of up to 1.4x through a novel skip-token mechanism; and (III) shows stable routing in the trained proxy regimes and projects the communication/memory benefits to 100B-scale configurations through system modelling.

AI.tldr: FoMoE partitions expert layers across workers to replace full replicas, reducing inter-node communication and memory usage while enabling throughput gains and scalable MoE training across geo-distributed data centers.
AI.motivation: Training extremely large language models with MoE architectures efficiently across geographically distributed, weakly connected data centers, mitigating memory and communication bottlenecks of full replication.
AI.method: Partition expert layers across workers (partial expert replication) and introduce a skip-token mechanism; evaluate communication, memory, and throughput against baselines (efficient MoE, DiLoCo/Photon, DDP); model scalability to 100B-scale configs.
AI.result: Communication costs reduced up to 1.42x vs efficient baselines and 45.44x vs DDP; empirical throughput up to 1.4x; stable routing in proxy regimes; projections to 100B-scale configurations via modeling.
AI.conclusion: Partial expert replication in FoMoE can significantly improve efficiency and scalability of distributed MoE-based LLM training, enabling cheaper, scalable training across geo-distributed data centers.

title: A Hybrid LSTM--Vision Transformer Architecture for Predicting HRRR Forecast Errors
comment: This manuscript is a preprint and has been submitted for peer review to the Artificial Intelligence for the Earth Systems journal. The content is subject to change based on the outcome of the peer review process and should not be considered final or definitive. Copyright in this Work may be transferred without further notice
summary: Forecast errors in high-resolution numerical weather prediction (NWP) systems are often linked to unresolved planetary boundary layer (PBL) processes, convection, terrain-induced circulations, and other vertically structured atmospheric phenomena. Previous work demonstrated that Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks can successfully predict forecast errors in the High-Resolution Rapid Refresh (HRRR) model using mesonet observations, but we believe performance degradation is linked to periods of complex vertical atmospheric evolution. To address this limitation, we develop a hybrid LSTM-Vision Transformer (LSTM-ViT) framework that combines temporal sequence learning from surface observations with atmospheric profiles from the New York State Mesonet profiler network. The LSTM-ViT framework is trained to predict HRRR hourly precipitation, 10 m wind speed, and 2 m temperature forecast errors at individual mesonet stations. Across all three predictors, incorporation of profiler-derived atmospheric structure improves forecast error prediction skill relative to the baseline LSTM architecture, with the largest gains occurring at shorter forecast lead times and during periods of enhanced PBL activity. Improvements are particularly pronounced for precipitation forecast error, where the LSTM-ViT framework achieves approximately a twofold increase in predictive skill relative to the baseline LSTM while better capturing convectively driven error evolution and reducing degradation associated with PBL processes. These results demonstrate that combining temporal sequence learning with vertically informed attention mechanisms provides a physically meaningful pathway for improving forecast error prediction in operational NWP systems. Our research offers forecasters enhanced guidance regarding model bias and forecast confidence.

AI.tldr: Hybrid LSTM-ViT using profiler atmospheric profiles improves HRRR forecast error prediction, especially for precipitation, with biggest gains at short lead times during strong PBL activity.
AI.motivation: Address performance degradation of pure temporal models during complex vertical evolution by incorporating vertical atmospheric structure via profiler data and attention.
AI.method: Hybrid LSTM-ViT combining temporal sequences from surface observations with New York State Mesonet profiler profiles; trained to predict HRRR hourly forecast errors for precipitation, 10 m wind, and 2 m temperature at mesonet stations.
AI.result: Profiler-informed architecture yields higher predictive skill than baseline LSTM across all targets, with largest gains at short leads and during enhanced PBL; precipitation error shows ~2x improvement; better capture of convective error evolution and reduced PBL-driven degradation.
AI.conclusion: Integrating temporally driven sequence learning with vertically informed attention is a physically meaningful approach to improve forecast-error prediction in operational NWP and can enhance forecaster confidence.

title: Strategic Feature Selection
summary: When algorithmic predictors inform resource allocation in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, these predictors must account for strategic manipulation of input features. The typical solution is to redesign the predictor itself to explicitly account for strategic interactions. In practice, however, decision makers are often constrained to adjusting coarser levers within existing prediction pipelines. For example, healthcare organizations often select which features to exclude based on perceived manipulability, while using standard regularization procedures to shrink the coefficients of retained features. In this work, we initiate a formal study of strategic classification through feature selection and its interaction with ridge regularization. Our main finding is that excluding individual features based on their manipulability alone is generally suboptimal. We provide a fine-grained characterization of the performance of a feature subset under optimal regularization, yielding new insights for policy design. Motivated by this characterization, we develop a practical algorithm for jointly choosing the feature set and the level of ridge regularization. Through a real-world case study on a healthcare payments benchmark, we illustrate how our algorithm can guide the design of coarse policy levers in practice. Our results provide a principled, practical framework for mitigating the effects of strategic behavior in algorithmic decision-making systems.

AI.tldr: Jointly selecting features and ridge regularization to defend against strategic feature manipulation; excluding features by manipulability alone is suboptimal; a practical algorithm and a healthcare payments case study illustrate policy guidance.
AI.motivation: Algorithmic predictors in high-stakes domains face strategic manipulation. Practitioners often only adjust coarse levers (e.g., drop certain features, regularize others) rather than redesigning the predictor. There is a need to formally study how feature selection interacts with ridge regularization to mitigate manipulation.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis provides a fine-grained characterization of performance for a feature subset under optimal ridge regularization. Based on this, the paper develops a practical algorithm that jointly optimizes the feature set and the ridge penalty. A real-world healthcare payments benchmark demonstrates practical applicability.
AI.result: Excluding features based solely on manipulability is generally suboptimal. The characterization yields new insights for policy design. The proposed algorithm effectively guides joint feature-selection and regularization choices, improving robustness to strategic behavior.
AI.conclusion: Presents a principled, practical framework for mitigating strategic behavior in algorithmic decision-making systems by coordinating feature selection and ridge regularization, with demonstrated real-world relevance.

title: A Multi-Domain Benchmark for Detecting AI-Generated Text-Rich Images from GPT-Image-2
summary: Text-rich images often contain privacy-sensitive, transactional, or decision-relevant information. As recent multimodal image generation models become increasingly capable of synthesizing realistic textual content and structured visual designs, detecting AI-generated text-rich images has become an important challenge for digital trust and content authenticity. Existing benchmarks, however, largely focus on object-centric images and provide limited coverage of scenarios where textual semantics and layout organization are central. In this paper, we introduce a multi-domain benchmark for detecting text-rich images generated by OpenAI's GPT Image 2. The benchmark contains 8,602 images across six representative categories: commercial posters, infographics, academic posters, receipts, tables, and UI screenshots. Using this benchmark, we evaluate five representative AI-generated image detectors in a zero-shot setting and analyze their overall, category-wise, and post-processing robustness. Our results show that detector performance is highly domain-dependent: methods that perform well in some categories often fail on others, and even the strongest conventional detector exhibits severe sensitivity to JPEG compression. We further conduct an exploratory evaluation with a multimodal vision-language model, revealing both its promise and its limitations on structured formats. These findings highlight the need for text- and layout-aware detection methods for modern AI-generated images. Our dataset is released at XXX.

AI.tldr: A multi-domain benchmark for detecting GPT Image 2 generated text-rich images; 8,602 images across six categories; zero-shot evaluation of five detectors shows domain-dependent performance and JPEG sensitivity; exploratory multimodal model analysis; dataset release planned.
AI.motivation: As AI-generated text-rich visuals become more realistic, existing detection benchmarks—primarily object-centric—fail to capture the challenges posed by structured text and layouts. There is a need for text- and layout-aware detectors to maintain digital trust.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark of 8,602 images across six domains (commercial posters, infographics, academic posters, receipts, tables, UI screenshots) generated by GPT Image 2. Evaluate five representative AI-generated image detectors in a zero-shot setting, analyzing overall, category-wise, and post-processing robustness (including JPEG compression). Conduct an exploratory evaluation with a multimodal vision-language model to assess its ability to handle structured formats.
AI.result: Detector performance is highly domain-dependent; methods strong in some categories fail in others. Even the best conventional detector is severely affected by JPEG compression. The multimodal model shows promise but also limitations when handling structured formats.
AI.conclusion: There is a clear need for text- and layout-aware detection methods tailored to text-rich and structured-image content. The released dataset (abstract mentions release URL) will support future research in this area.

title: Pareto Q-Learning with Reward Machines
comment: Accepted at the ICAPS 2026 Workshop on Bridging the Gap Between AI Planning and (Reinforcement) Learning (PRL)
summary: We present Pareto Q-Learning with Reward Machines (PQLRM), a multi-objective reinforcement learning algorithm for tasks whose reward structure is specified by a set of reward machines (RMs). PQLRM combines Pareto Q-Learning (PQL), which maintains sets of vector-valued Q-estimates to approximate the Pareto front, with enhancements from Q-Learning with Reward Machines (QRM), which exploits the factored automaton structure of the reward signal. This yields a multi-policy algorithm that remains sample-efficient under non-Markovian, RM-encoded rewards. Experimental trials show that PQLRM converges faster than a naive PQL baseline applied to the cross-product MDP and can synthesize Pareto-optimal policies that QRM cannot.

AI.tldr: Introduces PQLRM, a multi-objective RL algorithm combining Pareto Q-Learning with reward machines to efficiently learn Pareto-optimal policies under RM-encoded rewards; demonstrates faster convergence and Pareto policy synthesis beyond QRM.
AI.motivation: Tackle multi-objective RL with complex, non-Markovian reward signals encoded by Reward Machines (RMs) and achieve sample-efficient learning of multiple Pareto policies.
AI.method: Integrates Pareto Q-Learning (which maintains vector-valued Q-estimates to approximate the Pareto front) with Q-learning with Reward Machines (QRM) by exploiting the factored automaton structure of the RM, yielding a multi-policy algorithm that remains sample-efficient under RM-encoded rewards.
AI.result: Empirically, PQLRM converges faster than a naive PQL baseline on the cross-product MDP and can synthesize Pareto-optimal policies that QRM cannot.
AI.conclusion: PQLRM provides a sample-efficient approach for multi-objective RL with RM-encoded rewards, enabling Pareto-optimal policy synthesis where QRM alone falls short.

title: A Unified Framework for Efficient Remote Sensing Visual Question Answering: Adapting Dual, Hybrid, and Encoder-Decoder Architectures
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures, accepted and to be presented at 2026 IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium (IGARSS 2026), scheduled for 9 to 14 August 2026 in Washington D.C
summary: Visual Question Answering (VQA) in the Remote Sensing (RS) domain presents unique challenges due to the high resolution, multi scale object distribution, and semantic complexity of aerial imagery. While general domain Foundation Models have achieved remarkable success, their direct application to RSVQA is hindered by massive domain shifts and the computationally prohibitive nature of full fine tuning. This study presents a comparative analysis of RS Adapter, a Parameter Efficient Fine Tuning (PEFT) strategy, applied across three distinct Vision Language Model (VLM) architectures: the Dual Encoder CLIP, the Encoder Decoder BLIP, and the Hybrid FLAVA. We introduce a unified architectural surgery pipeline that injects lightweight bottleneck adapters into the attention and MLP layers of frozen backbones, enabling rapid adaptation with less than 5 percent of trainable parameters. Experimental results on the high resolution RSVQA x dataset demonstrate that while all adapted models achieve convergence, the Hybrid FLAVA architecture offers a superior balance of multimodal reasoning and retrieval capabilities compared to its unimodal counterparts. Our findings establish a new baseline for resource efficient VQA in disaster assessment and urban monitoring.

AI.tldr: RSAdapter-based PEFT across three VLMs (CLIP, BLIP, FLAVA) for RSVQA; achieves convergence with <5% trainable parameters; FLAVA offers best balance of multimodal reasoning and retrieval; sets a resource-efficient baseline for disaster assessment and urban monitoring.
AI.motivation: Address domain shift and high computational cost of applying general-purpose foundation models to high-resolution remote sensing VQA. Evaluate parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) across diverse VLM architectures and propose a unified bottleneck-adapter pipeline.
AI.method: Inject lightweight bottleneck adapters into the attention and MLP layers of frozen backbones, creating a unified surgical pipeline. Apply to three architectures: Dual Encoder CLIP, Encoder-Decoder BLIP, and Hybrid FLAVA. Train with <5% of parameters. Evaluate on RSVQAx high-resolution dataset focused on disaster assessment and urban monitoring.
AI.result: All adapted models converge. Hybrid FLAVA demonstrates a superior balance between multimodal reasoning and retrieval compared to unimodal counterparts.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a new resource-efficient baseline for RSVQA. FLAVA+PEFT emerges as the preferred choice for combined reasoning and retrieval in RS tasks, with implications for disaster response and urban monitoring.

title: Anomaly Detection for Sparse and Irregular Multivariate Time Series with Latent SDEs
summary: Multivariate time series anomaly detection (MTSAD) is critical for a wide range of application areas, such as industrial monitoring, cybersecurity, or healthcare. Real-world data is often sparse, irregularly sampled or partially observed, yet existing methods assume uniformly sampled time series. We propose a generative approach based on Latent SDEs that projects the observed time series on a continuous-time stochastic dynamical system, directly being able to handle missing observations and irregular sampling, while also naturally capturing possible cyclic behavior that many real-world use cases inherently possess. Experiments on six anomaly benchmark datasets show that our proposed method ranks first among state-of-the-art baselines. We further demonstrate that our method remains robust under severe data sparsity, while performance significantly degrades for the tested baseline methods. These results highlight latent SDEs as a natural inductive bias for anomaly detection in multivariate time series, especially in presence of real-world irregularities.

AI.tldr: Latent SDE-based generative model for multivariate time series anomaly detection that handles irregular sampling and missing data, achieving state-of-the-art performance and robustness to sparsity.
AI.motivation: Real-world MTS are often sparse and irregularly sampled; existing methods assume uniform sampling and struggle with missing data; a continuous-time generative approach can naturally address these issues and capture cyclic dynamics.
AI.method: A latent stochastic differential equation framework that embeds the observed MTS into a continuous-time latent dynamical system, enabling direct handling of irregular timestamps and missing observations, with a generative model that captures potential cyclic behavior.
AI.result: Evaluations on six anomaly benchmark datasets show the method ranks first among state-of-the-art baselines and remains robust under severe data sparsity, while competing methods degrade.
AI.conclusion: Latent SDEs provide a natural inductive bias for anomaly detection in multivariate time series with irregularities, improving robustness to sparsity and capturing cyclic patterns.

title: OrthoReg: Orthogonal Regularization for Hybrid Symbolic-Neural Dynamical Systems
summary: Dynamical systems are fundamental to modeling the natural world, yet modeling them involves a persistent trade-off: manually prescribed mechanistic models are interpretable by design but often overly simplistic and misspecified; in contrast, flexible data-driven neural methods lack physical insight. Hybrid modeling aims for the best of both worlds by combining a prescribed or symbolic, physics-based component with a flexible neural network. A critical challenge, however, is that the neural component may relearn mechanistic parts, yielding redundant and uninterpretable models, especially when the symbolic structure itself is discovered from data. Existing methods based on standard $L^2$ regularization rely on a projection argument that breaks when the symbolic component is learned through sparse discovery, allowing the neural augmentation to overlap with symbolic structure. We introduce \textbf{OrthoReg} (Orthogonal Regularization), which directly penalizes overlap between the symbolic and neural components, preventing symbolic structure from being absorbed by the neural residual. This yields a complementary decomposition: the symbolic part captures what the library can express, and the neural part captures what remains. On benchmark dynamical systems with partial library mismatch, OrthoReg improves symbolic recovery and out-of-distribution behavior.

AI.tldr: OrthoReg introduces an orthogonal regularization term that penalizes overlap between the symbolic (library-based) component and the neural residual in hybrid dynamical system models, enabling a complementary decomposition and improving symbolic recovery and OOD behavior under partial library mismatch.
AI.motivation: Hybrid modeling combines physics-based symbolic terms with neural networks, but standard L2 regularization fails when the symbolic component is discovered sparsely; the neural part can absorb symbolic structure, reducing interpretability; we need to enforce separation between components.
AI.method: Add an orthogonality-based penalty that minimizes the inner product (or correlation) between the symbolic and neural components in the model, promoting near-orthogonal decomposition; implement during training with a regularization weight; evaluate on benchmark dynamical systems with incomplete libraries.
AI.result: OrthoReg yields better symbolic recovery and improved out-of-distribution generalization, demonstrating effective prevention of redundant neural absorption of symbolic structure.
AI.conclusion: Orthogonal regularization provides a simple, effective mechanism to preserve interpretability in hybrid physics-informed models, with potential applicability to broader library-discovery contexts; practical considerations include hyperparameter tuning and library sufficiency.

title: Confidence is Not Reliability: Rethinking MC Dropout in Brain Tumour Segmentation
summary: Glioma segmentation in multiparametric MRI is a critical component of treatment planning. A segmentation model that fails silently on treatment-critical sub-regions represents a patient safety risk that overlap-based metrics such as Dice scores cannot expose. We ask whether voxel-level uncertainty estimation via Monte Carlo (MC) Dropout can reliably identify segmentation errors in clinically critical sub-regions, and whether calibration failure modes are detectable from standard reporting metrics alone. In an empirical two-model case study on 126 BraTS21 patients, we evaluate a high-performance pretrained SegResNet and a locally trained UNet with residual units (UNet-Res). MC dropout preserved segmentation accuracy ($|Δ\text{Dice}|$ $<0.01$) while achieving strong uncertainty-error alignment (AUROC for entropy (H) $\approx$0.97), indicating uncertainty correctly ranks erroneous voxels above correct ones. Entropy-based patient stratification identified a high-uncertainty subgroup with substantially lower segmentation performance (median whole-tumour Dice $0.835$ vs. $0.925$), supporting uncertainty as a practical triage signal. However, global alignment can mask important region-specific differences. Despite similar AUROC, UNet-Res exhibited near-zero enhancing tumour entropy ($0.054$) and Expected Calibration Error (ECE) of $0.915$, with a Dice of only $0.714$, indicating severely miscalibrated confidence on the most clinically critical sub-region, a failure mode invisible to standard Dice and AUROC reporting. These findings demonstrate that strong uncertainty-error alignment is necessary but insufficient for clinical safety: sub-region-specific calibration assessment must accompany AUROC evaluation when selecting models for clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty estimation via MC Dropout can identify voxel-level segmentation errors and triage uncertain regions, but sub-region calibration is essential; AUROC alone is not enough for clinical safety.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether voxel-level uncertainty from MC Dropout identifies errors in critical subregions of glioma segmentation and whether standard metrics reveal calibration issues, addressing patient safety.
AI.method: Two-model study (SegResNet and UNet-Res) on 126 BraTS21 patients. Apply MC Dropout for voxel-level uncertainty. Assess uncertainty-error alignment (AUROC of entropy), stratify patients by entropy, evaluate Dice; examine calibration via ECE, especially region-specific (enhancing tumor) calibration.
AI.result: Entropy AUROC ≈ 0.97 indicating good uncertainty-error ranking; high-uncertainty group shows worse whole-tumor Dice (0.835 vs 0.925). UNet-Res exhibits near-zero enhancing-tumor entropy and very high ECE (0.915) with Dice 0.714, indicating severe subregion miscalibration invisible to Dice/AUROC.
AI.conclusion: Strong overall uncertainty-error alignment is necessary but insufficient for clinical safety. Sub-region calibration assessment is required alongside AUROC when selecting models for deployment.

title: REVES: REvision and VErification--Augmented Training for Test-Time Scaling
summary: Test-time scaling via sequential revision has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning. However, standard post-training methods primarily optimize single-shot objectives, creating a fundamental misalignment with multi-step inference dynamics. While recent work treats this as multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL), conventional approaches optimize over the multi-step trajectories directly, failing to further exploit the high-quality mistakes in intermediate steps that model can learn from correcting them. We propose a two-stage iterative framework that alternates between online data/prompt augmentation and policy optimization. By converting the intermediate steps (``near-miss'' answers) in the successful recovery trajectories into decoupled revision and verification prompts, our approach concentrates training on both effective answer transformation and error identification. This approach enables efficient off-policy data generation and reduces the computational overhead of long-horizon sampling compared to standard multi-turn RL. On LiveCodeBench, using publicly available test cases as feedback, we observe gains of +6.5 points over the RL baseline and +4.0 points over standard multi-turn training. Beyond coding, our approach matches the previously reported SOTA result on circle packing while using the smallest base model (4B) and far fewer rollouts than the much larger evolutionary search systems. Math results under ground-truth verification further confirm improved correction ability. It also generalizes to out-of-distribution constraint-satisfaction puzzles such as n\_queens and mini\_sudoku, where correctness is defined entirely by problem constraints. Code is available at https://github.com/yxliu02/REVES.git.

AI.tldr: A two-stage iterative REVES framework leverages near-miss intermediate steps by converting them into decoupled revision and verification prompts, enabling efficient off-policy training for improved multi-step reasoning and reduced long-horizon RL. It achieves gains on LiveCodeBench (+6.5 over RL, +4.0 over standard multi-turn), matches SOTA circle packing with a 4B model and fewer rollouts, and generalizes to constraints puzzles like n-queens and mini-sudoku; code released.
AI.motivation: Standard post-training objectives are typically single-shot and misaligned with multi-step inference dynamics. Existing RL-based approaches optimize on trajectories but underutilize informative intermediate mistakes, leading to inefficiencies and higher computational cost.
AI.method: Two-stage iterative framework (online data/prompt augmentation and policy optimization). It converts intermediate steps from successful recovery trajectories—“near-miss” answers—into decoupled revision prompts and verification prompts. This concentrates training on effective answer transformations and error identification, enables efficient off-policy data generation, and reduces long-horizon sampling overhead compared to conventional multi-turn RL.
AI.result: Empirical gains on LiveCodeBench: +6.5 points over RL baseline and +4.0 points over standard multi-turn training. Demonstrates SOTA performance on circle packing with a 4B base model and far fewer rollouts than evolutionary search systems. Math results under ground-truth verification show improved correction ability. Generalizes to out-of-distribution constraint-satisfaction puzzles (n_queens, mini_sudoku). Code available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields more data-efficient, correction-focused multi-step learning for LLMs, reducing RL overhead while achieving strong generalization across domains; it leverages high-quality intermediate mistakes to guide policy improvement, and scales well with smaller models.

title: Essential Subspace Merging for Multi-Task Learning
summary: Model merging aims to enable multi-task learning by integrating the capabilities of multiple models fine-tuned from the same pre-trained checkpoint into a single model. Its core challenge is inter-task interference among task-specific parameter updates. In this paper, we analyze the output shifts induced by task updates and observe that their energy is concentrated in a small number of principal directions. We call the subspace spanned by these directions the essential subspace. In contrast, most remaining directions carry little task-relevant energy, but their accumulation across multiple task updates can cause severe interference during merging. Motivated by this observation, we propose Essential Subspace Decomposition (ESD), which decomposes each task update according to the principal components of its activation shift. Based on ESD, we introduce Essential Subspace Merging (ESM), a training-free static merging method that orthogonalizes and fuses essential components into one compact multi-task model. We further extend ESM to ESM++, a training-free dynamic merging method that decomposes task-specific residuals into low-rank experts and selects the most relevant expert through prototype-based routing during forward inference. Extensive experiments across multiple task sets and model scales demonstrate that ESM and ESM++ effectively preserves task knowledge while reducing inter-task interference.

AI.tldr: ESD/ESM/ESM++ provide a training-free framework for multi-task model merging by isolating essential, low-rank activation-shift directions to reduce inter-task interference and preserve task knowledge.
AI.motivation: Inter-task interference during merging of separately fine-tuned task models. Activation shifts induced by task updates are energy-concentrated in a small set of principal directions; ignoring the rest can cause interference when merging.
AI.method: Essential Subspace Decomposition (ESD) decomposes each task update along the principal components of its activation shift. Essential Subspace Merging (ESM) orthogonalizes and fuses the essential components, enabling static, model-compact merging without training. ESM++ extends to dynamic merging by splitting residuals into low-rank experts and routing via prototypes to select the most relevant expert at inference.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse task sets and model scales show that ESM/ESM++ preserve task knowledge while reducing inter-task interference compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: ESM provides a training-free static merging solution and ESM++ offers a dynamic, routing-based extension, together enabling efficient, interference-resilient multi-task merging.

title: NeuMesh++: Towards Versatile and Efficient Volumetric Editing with Disentangled Neural Mesh-based Implicit Field
summary: Recently neural implicit rendering techniques have evolved rapidly and demonstrated significant advantages in novel view synthesis and 3D scene reconstruction. However, existing neural rendering methods for editing purposes offer limited functionalities, e.g., rigid transformation and category-specific editing. In this paper, we present a novel mesh-based representation by encoding the neural radiance field with disentangled geometry, texture, and semantic codes on mesh vertices, which empowers a set of efficient and comprehensive editing functionalities, including mesh-guided geometry editing, designated texture editing with texture swapping, filling and painting operations, and semantic-guided editing. To this end, we develop several techniques including a novel local space parameterization to enhance rendering quality and training stability, a learnable modification color on vertex to improve the fidelity of texture editing, a spatial-aware optimization strategy to realize precise texture editing, and a semantic-aided region selection to ease the laborious annotation of implicit field editing. Extensive experiments and editing examples on both real and synthetic datasets demonstrate the superiority of our method on representation quality and editing ability. Project page: https://zju3dv.github.io/neumeshplusplus/

AI.tldr: Mesh-based neural radiance field with disentangled vertex codes enabling diverse editing (geometry, texture, semantics) via local space parameterization, vertex-color modulation, spatial-aware texture optimization, and semantic region selection.
AI.motivation: Limitations of existing neural rendering methods for editing (mostly rigid or category-specific edits) motivate a flexible, mesh-based representation that supports a wide range of edits with better quality and stability.
AI.method: Encode neural radiance field on mesh vertices with disentangled geometry, texture, and semantic codes. Introduce a local space parameterization, learnable vertex color modification for texture editing, spatial-aware texture optimization, and semantic-aided region selection to guide edits. Support mesh-guided geometry editing, texture swapping/painting/filling, and semantic-guided editing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real and synthetic datasets show improved representation quality and broader editing capabilities compared to baselines, with successful demonstrations of diverse editing tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed mesh-based, disentangled radiance field enables efficient, comprehensive editing in neural rendering, delivering superior quality and flexibility across geometry, texture, and semantic operations.

title: Some Complexity Results for Robustness Verification for Binarized Neural Networks
summary: This paper studies the computational complexity of verification problems for Binarized Neural Networks (BNNs), where activations (and sometimes weights) are binary. We analyze two problems: satisfiability and robustness under uniform image occlusion. We show that BNN satisfiability is NP-complete via a reduction from Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT), and that uniform occlusion induces a piecewise-constant structure in the network output, enabling a polynomial-time robustness-checking algorithm.

AI.tldr: Satisfiability of Binarized Neural Networks is NP-complete; robustness under uniform image occlusion is tractable (polynomial-time) due to piecewise-constant network output.
AI.motivation: Clarify fundamental verification limits for BNNs to guide practical safety verification and design of robust architectures.
AI.method: (1) Prove NP-hardness by reducing SAT to BNN satisfiability; (2) show that uniform occlusion imposes a piecewise-constant structure on the output, enabling a polynomial-time robustness-check algorithm.
AI.result: BNN satisfiability is NP-complete; robustness under uniform occlusion can be checked in polynomial time.
AI.conclusion: There is a complexity contrast: satisfiability remains computationally hard, while robustness verification under the specified occlusion model is efficiently solvable, informing verification strategies for BNNs.

title: Beyond the Current Observation: Evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models in Controllable Non-Markov Games
summary: Deploying multimodal foundation models as closed-loop policies increasingly requires conditioning actions on observations that are no longer visible. However, existing benchmarks either expose the full state, conflate hidden-state reconstruction with other agent skills, or test recall only after an episode has ended. We introduce RNG-Bench (Reconstructive Non-Markov Games), a benchmark suite designed to isolate a base model's ability to reconstruct past observations and act on them during multi-step interaction. RNG-Bench includes two complementary games: Matching Pairs, where card identities briefly revealed at specific locations must later be recalled, and 3D Maze, where egocentric views must be integrated into a spatial map. Both games are evaluated under a unified harness with three controlled difficulty axes: grid size, visual pattern, and observation modality. The benchmark further introduces a head-to-head duel protocol to control for instance-level variance and a Memory Gap metric that disentangles forgetting from poor action selection. The hardest configurations require contexts of roughly 128K tokens and 350 image inputs per episode, and remain far from saturated by frontier MLLMs. Memory Gap analysis shows that most residual errors stem from forgetting earlier observations rather than from suboptimal decision making. Finally, fine-tuning Qwen3.5-9B on optimal-policy rollouts and filtered model demonstrations improves performance on RNG-Bench and transfers to existing benchmarks without degrading general multimodal capability.

AI.tldr: RNG-Bench introduces Reconstructive Non-Markov Games to isolate a model's ability to reconstruct past observations for action during multi-step interaction. It features two games (Matching Pairs and 3D Maze), a unified, multi-axis difficulty harness, a head-to-head duel protocol, and a Memory Gap metric. Key findings: hardest settings demand ~128K tokens and ~350 image inputs per episode; most errors stem from forgetting rather than poor action selection. Fine-tuning Qwen3.5-9B on optimal rollouts improves RNG-Bench performance and transfers to other benchmarks without harming multimodal capability.
AI.motivation: To address benchmarks that reveal full state, conflate hidden-state reconstruction with other skills, or only test recall post-episode. The goal is to isolate and quantify a base model's memory reconstruction ability during ongoing interaction, and to provide scalable, controlled evaluation with disentangled forgetting from decision quality.
AI.method: Introduce two complementary games (Matching Pairs and 3D Maze) and a unified harness with three controlled difficulty axes (grid size, visual pattern, observation modality). Implement a head-to-head duel protocol to reduce instance variance and a Memory Gap metric to separate forgetting from action quality. Provide instructions for varying contexts with token and image counts to probe memory.
AI.result: Empirical results show that the hardest RNG-Bench configurations require roughly 128k tokens and 350 image inputs per episode and remain challenging for frontier multimodal LLMs. Memory Gap analysis indicates residual errors predominantly arise from forgetting earlier observations rather than poor action selection. Fine-tuning Qwen3.5-9B on optimal-policy rollouts and filtered demonstrations improves RNG-Bench performance and transfers to existing benchmarks without degrading general multimodal capability.
AI.conclusion: RNG-Bench offers a focused, scalable benchmark to evaluate reconstructive memory in multimodal, closed-loop policies. The main bottleneck is memory, not decision quality. Targeted fine-tuning can boost performance and generalization across benchmarks while preserving multimodal capabilities.

title: GrapNet: A Programmable Dynamic-Architecture Neural Graph Substrate
summary: Programmability is a missing first-class interface in fixed-tensor neural networks: editing a relation, freezing a subgraph, auditing a local function, or changing the execution backend should be an operation on the neural program rather than ad-hoc parameter surgery. GrapNet studies this graph-as-network setting. The graph is the architecture and executable program, not an input data graph. Each compute node owns its next-layer child references and a trainable allocation vector aligned with those references; deleting a relation physically removes both the child reference and the corresponding allocation coordinate. Structural rules and execution policies live outside the node core, so the same child-owned graph can be grown, frozen, structurally edited, grouped into trainable family blocks, routed by attention over active relations, or lowered to dense snapshots after topology stabilizes. GrapNet composes with conventional modules through a vector-valued parent interface: dense layers, CNN encoders, ResNet feature extractors, attention blocks, and transformer representations can all feed one sensory GrapNode per coordinate. The evaluation is organized as a programmability stress suite rather than as a new replay benchmark. In a matched ten-seed Split Fashion-MNIST study, a plastic GrapNet+ER head reaches 63.16 percent seen-class accuracy versus 51.08 percent for a parameter-larger dense MLP+ER under the same seen-class loss and replay memory, with paired delta 12.08 points and p=1.3e-5. On Split CIFAR-10 with a frozen ImageNet ResNet-18 encoder, the same substrate improves the online head over MLP-256 by 3.81 points, with p=0.0026. These results support GrapNet as an editable neural graph substrate whose core value is structural programmability with faithful execution views.

AI.tldr: GrapNet introduces a programmable neural graph substrate where the network’s topology is itself a editable program; nodes own their outgoing edges and an allocation vector, enabling structural edits, routing, and eventual dense snapshots, with demonstrated improvements on continual/online learning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Fixe-tensor neural nets lack a first-class programmable interface for editing relations, freezing subgraphs, auditing functions, or changing backends; GrapNet treats the graph as the neural program to address this gap.
AI.method: A graph-as-network where the architecture is the executable program. Each compute node stores its next-layer child references and an aligned trainable allocation vector. Deleting a relation removes both the reference and the allocation coordinate. Structural rules and execution policies live outside the node core, enabling growth, freezing, topology edits, grouping into trainable blocks, attention-driven routing, or lowering to dense snapshots after topology stabilizes. GrapNet integrates with conventional modules via a vector-valued parent interface, supporting dense layers, CNNs, ResNets, attention, and transformer components. Evaluation uses a programmability stress suite, including matches on Split Fashion-MNIST and Split CIFAR-10 with a frozen encoder.
AI.result: In Split Fashion-MNIST (ten seeds), GrapNet+ER head achieves 63.16% seen-class accuracy vs 51.08% with a parameter-larger dense MLP+ER under the same constraints (delta 12.08 points; p=1.3e-5). In Split CIFAR-10 with a frozen ImageNet ResNet-18 encoder, the GrapNet substrate improves the online head over MLP-256 by 3.81 points (p=0.0026).
AI.conclusion: GrapNet demonstrates that structuring neural networks as editable graphs yields faithful execution views and enduring programmability benefits, validating the graph-as-network paradigm as a viable substrate for programmable neural computation.

title: Compute Efficiency and Serial Runtime Tradeoffs for Stochastic Momentum Methods
summary:
Stochastic momentum methods such as heavy ball (HB), Nesterov momentum, and variants of Accelerated SGD (ASGD) [Kidambi et al., 2018] are widely used in modern training, but their stochastic benefits depend on two distinct quantities: serial runtime, the number of iterations needed to reach a target accuracy, and compute efficiency (CE), the inverse total gradient-query or FLOP cost. Larger batches reduce serial runtime without hurting CE only when the contraction gap grows linearly with batch size.
  We study stochastic HB and ASGD for consistent linear regression with Gaussian covariates and prove finite-dimensional, discrete-time lower bounds on their batch-size tradeoffs. Our first result shows that HB does not improve the CE frontier over SGD for arbitrary spectra; rather, it preserves SGD-level CE over a larger batch-size window, allowing larger batches to reduce serial runtime until HB reaches its deterministic accelerated scale. This window can be a factor $\sqrtκ$ larger than the SGD critical batch size. For ASGD, the picture is more spectrum-dependent: for rapidly decaying power-law spectra, ASGD improves small-batch CE over HB/SGD, but as batch size grows it trades this CE advantage for improved serial runtime. Synthetic linear-regression experiments verify these qualitative regimes, including near-overlap of ASGD and HB for slowly decaying spectra and the predicted CE--serial tradeoff for rapidly decaying spectra.

AI.tldr: HB does not improve the compute efficiency (CE) frontier over SGD for linear regression with Gaussian covariates; ASGD's benefits are spectrum-dependent, offering small-batch CE gains for rapidly decaying spectra but trading CE for serial runtime at larger batches. The results establish finite-dimensional, discrete-time lower bounds on batch-size tradeoffs and delineate a spectrum-dependent CE–serial tradeoff window.
AI.motivation: To understand when stochastic momentum methods (HB, Nesterov, ASGD variants) actually improve the compute-efficiency vs serial-speed tradeoffs in stochastic optimization, particularly as batch size varies.
AI.method: The authors analyze consistent linear regression with Gaussian covariates and derive finite-dimensional, discrete-time lower bounds on batch-size tradeoffs for stochastic heavy-ball (HB) and accelerated SGD (ASGD). They examine spectral properties of the data (spectra of the design matrix), including rapidly decaying power-law spectra, and compare against SGD. They also conduct synthetic linear-regression experiments.
AI.result: HB preserves SGD-level CE over a larger batch-size window, enabling serial runtime reduction up to the deterministic accelerated scale; this window can be up to a factor sqrt(kappa) larger than SGD’s critical batch size. ASGD’s advantage is spectrum-dependent: for rapidly decaying spectra, ASGD improves small-batch CE over HB/SGD, but as batch size grows, it trades CE for serial runtime; for slowly decaying spectra, ASGD and HB nearly overlap. Experiments validate the regime predictions and the CE–serial tradeoffs.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic momentum benefits are not universal; they depend on the spectral properties of the problem and the batch size. HB mainly provides a larger window to reduce serial runtime without hurting CE, while ASGD can offer small-batch CE gains in certain spectral regimes but loses CE advantage at larger batch sizes. The results clarify when momentum is advantageous and explain empirical observations regarding batch-size effects.

title: Native Active Perception as Reasoning for Omni-Modal Understanding
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. Code and models: https://github.com/harryhsing/omniagent
summary: Passive models for long video understanding typically rely on a "watch-it-all" paradigm, processing frames uniformly regardless of query difficulty, causing computational cost to grow with video duration. Although interactive frameworks have emerged, they often rely on global pre-scanning, and their context cost still scales with video length. We propose OmniAgent, the first native omni-modal agent that formulates video understanding as a POMDP-based iterative Observation-Thought-Action cycle. OmniAgent executes on-demand actions to selectively distill audio-visual cues into a persistent textual memory, effectively decoupling reasoning complexity from raw video duration. To operationalize this, we introduce (1) Agentic Supervised Fine-Tuning to bootstrap native active perception via best-of-N trajectory synthesis with dual-stage quality control, and (2) Agentic Reinforcement Learning with TAURA (Turn-aware Adaptive Uncertainty Rescaled Advantage), which leverages turn-level entropy to steer credit assignment toward pivotal discovery turns. Crucially, OmniAgent exhibits positive test-time scaling, where performance improves as the number of reasoning turns increases, validating the efficacy of active perception. Empirical results across ten benchmarks (e.g., VideoMME, LVBench) demonstrate that OmniAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models. Notably, on LVBench, our 7B agent outperforms the 10$\times$ larger Qwen2.5-VL-72B (50.5% vs. 47.3%).

AI.tldr: OmniAgent introduces a native omni-modal agent that treats video understanding as a POMDP-based iterative Observation-Thought-Action cycle, enabling on-demand actions and persistent textual memory to decouple reasoning from video duration. It achieves positive test-time scaling, state-of-the-art open-source performance, and strong results on LVBench (7B model beating a 10x larger model).
AI.motivation: Passive video understanding models incur costs that scale with video duration due to uniform frame processing; existing interactive methods rely on global pre-scanning with still cost scaling with length. The work seeks to make reasoning cost independent of video length by enabling active, on-demand perception.
AI.method: Formulate video understanding as a POMDP with an Observation-Thought-Action loop. The agent performs on-demand actions to distill audio-visual cues into a textual memory. Introduces (1) Agentic Supervised Fine-Tuning with best-of-N trajectory synthesis and dual-stage quality control, and (2) Agentic Reinforcement Learning with TAURA (Turn-aware Adaptive Uncertainty Rescaled Advantage), using turn-level entropy to guide credit assignment toward pivotal discovery turns.
AI.result: OmniAgent shows positive test-time scaling: more reasoning turns improve performance. It achieves state-of-the-art results among open-source models across ten benchmarks (e.g., VideoMME, LVBench). On LVBench, a 7B OmniAgent surpasses the 10x larger Qwen2.5-VL-72B (50.5% vs. 47.3%).
AI.conclusion: Active perception with a POMDP-based iterative cycle enables decoupling of reasoning from raw video duration and yields strong open-source performance, validating the approach and highlighting the benefit of on-demand, turn-aware perception.

title: Zero-Shot Active Feature Acquisition via LLM-Elicitation
summary: Active feature acquisition (AFA) sequentially selects which features to observe to reach a classification or ranking decision. Its central limitation is reliance on large amount of labeled data to fit probabilistic models guiding acquisition. Large language models (LLMs) supply unsupervised domain knowledge, but are poor sequential planners. Asking one to both know and decide conflates capabilities best kept separate. Here, we develop a framework for zero-shot AFA through disciplined elicitation: asking the LLM only for what it can be trusted to return, the unary deviations and pairwise co-variations that are the sufficient statistics of a Markov random field (MRF). We apply our framework to two settings: binary classification and top-$k$ identification. In practice, the LLM reliably returns only discriminative statistics, what distinguishes the classes rather than each class in isolation, which precludes classical AFA. We apply a maximum-entropy closure that resolves this gauge ambiguity. We evaluate on a cohort of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) patients, an active clinical setting where diagnostic ambiguity and patient heterogeneity obstruct stable treatment strategies. Our framework outperforms the LLM both on real labels and on its own extracted beliefs. Where it matters most, on the hardest patients, our top-$k$ acquisition policy markedly outperforms all existing methods.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot active feature acquisition framework that elicits trustworthy LLM-derived statistics (unary deviations and pairwise covariations) as sufficient statistics of a Markov random field, uses maximum-entropy closure to resolve gauge ambiguity, and applies a top-k acquisition policy for binary classification and ranking in an IBD setting, outperforming the LLM baseline and existing methods, especially on difficult cases.
AI.motivation: AFA typically requires labeled data to fit probabilistic models; LLMs offer unsupervised domain knowledge but are poor sequential planners. The proposed approach separates knowledge from decision by having the LLM provide only statistics it can reliably output, reducing reliance on large labeled datasets and enabling zero-shot acquisition.
AI.method: Extract discriminative unary deviations and pairwise covariations from the LLM as sufficient statistics of an MRF; apply maximum-entropy closure to fix gauge ambiguity; develop zero-shot AFA for binary classification and top-k identification; evaluate on an inflammatory bowel disease patient cohort with challenging diagnostic scenarios.
AI.result: The framework outperforms both the LLM’s extracted beliefs and existing AFA baselines, with particularly strong gains for hardest cases under the top-k acquisition policy.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot AFA via disciplined LLM elicitation coupled with MRF-based maximum-entropy closure is a viable strategy to integrate unsupervised LLM knowledge with principled probabilistic feature acquisition, yielding robust performance in heterogeneous clinical settings.

title: Forecasting what Matters: Decision-Focused RL for Controlled EV Charging with Unknown Departure Times
summary: The recent growth of EV adoption poses challenges for power systems, including increased peak demand and potential grid instability. Smart control of EV charging -- e.g., based on reinforcement learning (RL) -- can alleviate these issues by learning temporal and contextual patterns from historical data. Yet, in real-world scenarios, key features, such as departure time, often are unavailable. This, in turn, makes it harder for an RL agent to learn and execute an effective charging policy. To mitigate this uncertainty, a trained forecaster can approximate the unknown features from available data. However, since these forecasting models are typically trained for accuracy (rather than their impact on a downstream agent's decision quality), their errors may propagate and hinder the overall performance of a controller that is using the forecasts. To avoid this, we propose a decision-focused RL (DF-RL) framework in which the forecaster is trained end-to-end, i.e., with feedback from the charging policy actions taken by the RL agent. Such joint training of both the forecaster and controller ultimately results in higher-quality actions: our proposed DF-RL method yields superior charging decisions compared to other baselines, achieving up to a 14% improvement in total reward and a 55% reduction of unsupplied energy (i.e., charging that failed to happen because the EV already left), relative to the RL method without departure time forecasting.

AI.tldr: Introduces a decision-focused RL (DF-RL) framework that end-to-end-trains a forecaster and an RL-based EV charging controller to handle unseen departure times; shows performance gains over standard RL with forecasting.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty from missing departure time features hampers RL charging policy; forecasters optimized for accuracy may degrade downstream decision quality; need end-to-end training to align forecasting with decision performance.
AI.method: Integrates a forecast model with the RL controller and trains them jointly so forecasting errors directly affect policy optimization; uses feedback from controller actions to optimize forecaster with respect to downstream rewards; benchmarked against baselines without departure-time forecasting.
AI.result: DF-RL yields higher total reward and reduces unsupplied energy by up to 14% and 55%, respectively, compared to RL without departure-time forecasting.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end, decision-focused training improves control performance under feature uncertainty and can generalize to similar CPS control problems with missing critical features.

title: Be Your Own Teacher: Steering Protein Language Models via Unsupervised Reward Optimization
summary: Protein language models (PLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for controllable biomolecular design, yet their post-training adaptation typically relies on costly wet-lab validation or curated preference datasets. To overcome this supervision bottleneck, we introduce unsupervised reward optimization of PLMs, a comprehensive framework for steerable protein generation without ground-truth labels. Our key insight is that task-agnostic rewards, which combine intrinsic model uncertainty with extrinsic semantic consistency informed by protein representation models, exhibit strong correlation with controllability measures across base models and temperature regimes. Building upon this discovery, we propose two offline algorithms: Soft Reward Optimization (SRO) and Binarized Reward Optimization (BRO), which effectively maximize the classical RLHF objective induced by these proxy rewards. Extensive experiments on compositional out-of-distribution prompts demonstrate that both methods significantly outperform competitive baselines (DPO, KTO), while approaching oracle performance across multiple sampling temperatures, model scales and protein families. Moreover, PLMs fine-tuned with unsupervised rewards can achieve consistently higher coverage compared to their base model in pass@k evaluations. By enabling self-improvement of PLMs through their own generated experience, our framework provides a scalable pathway toward controllable biomolecular design in settings where labeled preferences or experimental feedback are scarce or unavailable.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised reward optimization for protein language models enables steerable biomolecular design without ground-truth labels, via two offline algorithms (SRO and BRO) that maximize an RLHF objective using task-agnostic rewards combining model uncertainty and semantic consistency; achieves strong, near-oracle performance on compositional out-of-distribution prompts and higher pass@k coverage without labeled data.
AI.motivation: Reduce dependence on costly wet-lab validation and curated preference data by leveraging self-generated, label-free signals that correlate with controllability across models and temperatures.
AI.method: Define task-agnostic rewards combining intrinsic model uncertainty with extrinsic semantic consistency from protein representation models. Develop two offline algorithms, Soft Reward Optimization (SRO) and Binarized Reward Optimization (BRO), to maximize the RLHF objective induced by these proxy rewards for controllable protein generation.
AI.result: SRO and BRO significantly outperform competitive baselines (DPO, KTO) on compositional out-of-distribution prompts across sampling temperatures, scales, and protein families, approaching oracle performance. Unsupervised rewards also improve pass@k coverage compared to base models.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised rewards enable scalable, label-free self-improvement of PLMs for controllable biomolecular design, offering a practical pathway when labeled feedback is scarce or unavailable.

title: Machine Unlearning for the XGBoost Model with Network Intrusion Datasets
summary: Machine Unlearning (MU) has emerged as an important technique for removing specific data points from trained models without requiring full retraining. However, most existing MU research focuses on deep learning and image data, leaving a gap in the domain of network intrusion detection, which relies heavily on tabular data. This work introduces XGBoost-Forget, an unlearning approach for the XGBoost model, to address this gap. The approach is evaluated on two tabular Network Intrusion (NI) datasets, IoT-23 and GeNIS, using multiple metrics to assess model performance, unlearning efficiency, and forgetting quality. The results show that XGBoost-Forget maintains predictive performance close to the original model while providing significantly faster unlearning, demonstrating its potential for MU in tabular NI settings.

AI.tldr: XGBoost-Forget provides an unlearning method for XGBoost in tabular network intrusion detection, enabling faster unlearning with minimal accuracy loss on IoT-23 and GeNIS.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in machine unlearning research for tabular data and network intrusion detection, enabling removal of data points without full retraining.
AI.method: Develop XGBoost-Forget to implement an unlearning procedure for XGBoost models; evaluate on two tabular NI datasets (IoT-23, GeNIS) using metrics for predictive performance, unlearning efficiency, and forgetting quality.
AI.result: XGBoost-Forget preserves predictive performance close to the original model while achieving significantly faster unlearning, demonstrating potential for MU in tabular NI settings.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates effectiveness of MU for gradient-boosted tree models in tabular network intrusion data and highlights its practicality for real-world NI systems; further work could broaden datasets and analyze robustness.

title: Online Reward-Punishment Learning from Fixed-Channel Perceptual Event Streams without Environment Rewards
summary:
We study online reward-punishment learning when the environment provides no scalar reward or evaluative label. At each step the agent receives only a fixed-channel perceptual packet, and quantities such as pain, energy, contact, damage, or cognitive error are treated as perceptual dimensions whose valence must be inferred from transition consequences. OHIRL separates four roles: M_psi learns next-packet prediction, D_omega models residual dynamics, C_eta is a fixed internal post-transition trajectory evaluator, and B_xi learns to use the resulting value evidence for later policy updates and action scoring. C_eta uses a recovery-positive and persistence/growth-negative residual-regulation orientation; a coefficient-origin audit shows that equal-unit, raw-equal, and random monotone variants preserve more than 92% of the released top-action rankings, while sign inversion preserves 0%.
  The reward-free protocol exposes observation transitions while withholding environment rewards, delayed external evaluators, success labels, and action-goodness labels. A conditional error decomposition separates B_xi evidence-estimation error from residual policy-optimization error. In a 2x2-XOR packet task, medicine and chili acquire opposite value under visual XOR contexts, and the same pain or spice increase can be positive or negative depending on consequence structure; B_xi reaches 0.952 balanced reward-sign accuracy. In a full online-interleaved audit, M_psi reaches holdout R2=0.907, B_xi reaches 0.940 sign accuracy, and the policy reaches 0.979 optimal-action accuracy, while immediate packet scores, prediction-error rewards, shuffled targets, zero reward, and error-reduction controls collapse. Hidden-reward CartPole and Taxi controls, public-context no-leakage audits, and module-role ablations further test information boundaries and component necessity.

AI.tldr: OHIRL is a reward-free online learning architecture with four modular roles (M_psi, D_omega, C_eta, B_xi) that infer value from perceptual transitions, achieving high action-ranking and sign-accuracy across tasks, while certain monotone invariants preserve performance and sign inversion devastates it.
AI.motivation: To enable value-like learning without scalar rewards or evaluative labels by inferring valence from transition consequences, and to understand how modular components contribute to policy optimization and action scoring under reward-free conditions.
AI.method: Introduce OHIRL with four roles: M_psi learns next-packet prediction, D_omega models residual dynamics, C_eta provides a fixed internal post-transition trajectory evaluator with recovery-positive and persistence/growth-negative residual-regulation, and B_xi uses value evidence for policy updates and action scoring. Evaluate in a reward-free protocol across tasks including a 2x2-XOR packet task, full online-interleaved audits, hidden-reward CartPole and Taxi controls, public-context no-leakage audits, and module-role ablations.
AI.result: Equal-unit, raw-equal, and random monotone variants preserve >92% of top-action rankings; sign inversion preserves 0%. B_xi achieves 0.952 balanced reward-sign accuracy. In a full online-interleaved audit, M_psi achieves holdout R2=0.907, B_xi achieves 0.940 sign accuracy, and the policy achieves 0.979 optimal-action accuracy; other baselines (immediate packet scores, prediction-error rewards, shuffled targets, zero reward, error-reduction controls) collapse.
AI.conclusion: Rewards are recoverable from observation transitions via a modular architecture; the decomposition clarifies error sources (evidence-estimation vs residual policy optimization) and identifies invariant properties that preserve action rankings. The results support the necessity of component roles and motivate further analyses of information boundaries and ablations.

title: Mechanism-Guided Selective Unlearning for RLVR-Induced Reasoning
summary: We propose MAST (Mechanism-Aligned Selective Targeting), a mechanism-guided method for unlearning RLVR-induced reasoning with substantially lower collateral damage than standard full-parameter updates. In matched SFT/RLVR checkpoints on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B and Qwen3-1.7B-Base, the SFT-to-RLVR increment differs sharply from the SFT update in token-level delta-log-probability, and full-parameter gradient ascent forgets only by damaging retain MATH and GSM8K. MAST ranks attention-projection tensors by off-principal energy, update magnitude, and forget-gradient coupling magnitude, then updates only the top-ranked subset. On the primary model, MAST induces statistically significant target forgetting (MATH forget 45/150 to 37/150; McNemar p=0.0078) while preserving GSM8K (+0.8 pp) and MATH retain (-0.5 pp). The advantage reproduces across seeds, NPO/SimNPO objectives, and Qwen3, where MAST preserves GSM8K while full-parameter unlearning collapses it.

AI.tldr: MAST selectively updates top-ranked attention-projection tensors to unlearn RLVR-induced reasoning with substantially less collateral damage than full-parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Unlearning RLVR-induced reasoning without harming unrelated capabilities; standard full-parameter unlearning can degrade performance on held-out tasks (collateral damage).
AI.method: Rank attention-projection tensors by off-principal energy, update magnitude, and forget-gradient coupling magnitude; update only the top-ranked subset; validate on matched SFT/RLVR checkpoints (Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, Qwen3-1.7B-Base) and compare against full-parameter gradient ascent; measure forgetting and retention on MATH and GSM8K datasets across seeds and objectives.
AI.result: MAST achieves significant target forgetting (MATH forget 45/150 → 37/150; McNemar p=0.0078) while preserving GSM8K (+0.8 percentage points) and MATH retain (-0.5 pp). Reproducible across seeds and NPO/SimNPO objectives; on Qwen3, MAST preserves GSM8K while full-parameter unlearning collapses it.
AI.conclusion: Mechanism-aligned selective targeting enables targeted forgetting with lower collateral damage and generalizes across models, seeds, and objectives, outperforming full-parameter unlearning in preserving unrelated capabilities.

title: EfficientRollout: System-Aware Self-Speculative Decoding for RL Rollouts
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a representative post-training paradigm for LLMs, enabling strong reasoning and agentic capabilities. However, rollout generation remains a dominant latency bottleneck because autoregressive sampling decodes responses sequentially and a small number of long-tailed generations often determine completion time. Speculative decoding (SD) offers a natural way to address this bottleneck, as it is a well-established technique for serving fixed LLMs that reduces latency by rapidly drafting tokens and accepting them through parallel verification while preserving the target-model distribution. However, its practical speedups do not directly carry over to RL rollouts: (i) the evolving target policy makes any fixed drafter increasingly mismatched with the policy's output distribution; and (ii) active batch sizes shrink throughout rollout decoding, shifting decoding from compute-bound to memory-bound regimes where parallel verification can exploit underutilized compute. Therefore, accelerating RL rollouts requires both a drafter that remains effective under long, high-temperature generations from an evolving policy and system-aware use of SD that avoids compute-bound regimes. We present EfficientRollout, a system-aware self-SD framework designed to address this gap for RL rollouts. EfficientRollout induces a quantized drafter from the target model (i.e. self-speculative decoding), keeping it coupled to the evolving policy without separate drafter pretraining or online adaptation. It further coordinates a system-aware SD toggle policy with acceptance-aware draft-length adaptation, enabling speculation only in beneficial regimes while matching the drafting budget to evolving drafter quality. EfficientRollout reduces rollout and end-to-end latency by up to 19.6% and 12.7%, respectively, over an accelerated AR rollout baseline, while preserving final model quality.
