AI.tldr: System-aware self-speculative decoding (EfficientRollout) accelerates RL rollouts for LLMs by using a quantized drafter tied to the evolving policy and adaptive drafting decisions, achieving up to ~19.6% rollout latency and ~12.7% end-to-end latency reductions while preserving model quality.
AI.motivation: RL rollout latency is dominated by autoregressive decoding; speculative decoding alone fails due to policy drift and changing batch dynamics; need a method that stays aligned with the evolving policy and system constraints.
AI.method: Construct a quantized drafter from the target model (self-SD) and couple it to the evolving policy without extra pretraining; implement a system-aware SD toggle policy and acceptance-aware draft-length adaptation to apply speculation only when beneficial, matching draft budget to drafter quality.
AI.result: Latency improvements: rollout latency down 19.6%, end-to-end latency down 12.7% compared to an accelerated AR rollout baseline; final model quality preserved.
AI.conclusion: EfficientRollout provides a practical, policy-aware, system-aware speculative decoding framework for RL rollouts that mitigates the latency bottleneck without compromising performance, by maintaining drafter-policy alignment and adaptive drafting strategies.

title: STARE: Surprisal-Guided Token-Level Advantage Reweighting for Policy Entropy Stability
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards algorithms like GRPO have emerged as the dominant post-training paradigm for complex reasoning in LLMs, yet commonly suffer from policy entropy collapse during training. We conduct a first-order gradient analysis of token-level entropy dynamics under GRPO and identify a token-level credit assignment mismatch: the per-token entropy variation decomposes into the product of the trajectory-level advantage and an entropy sensitivity function over the next-token distribution, yielding an advantage-surprisal four-quadrant structure and a near-criticality property. Motivated by it, we propose STARE (Surprisal-guided Token-level Advantage Reweighting for policy Entropy stability), which identifies entropy-critical token subsets via batch-internal surprisal quantiles, selectively reweights their effective advantages, and incorporates a target-entropy closed-loop gate for stable entropy regulation. Across model scales from 1.5B to 32B and three task families (Short CoT, Long CoT, and Multi-Turn Tool Use), STARE sustains stable RL training over thousands of steps while maintaining policy entropy within the target band. On AIME24 and AIME25, STARE outperforms DAPO and other competitive baselines by 4%-8% in average accuracy, with reflection tokens and response length growing in tandem, indicating sustained exploration-exploitation balance that further unlocks RL training potential.Code is available at https://github.com/hp-luo/STARE.

AI.tldr: Identifies a token-level credit assignment mismatch in GRPO-based RL for LLMs, caused by an interaction between trajectory-level advantage and next-token surprisal, leading to entropy instability. Proposes STARE, a surprisal-guided reweighting scheme with a target-entropy gate, that stabilizes policy entropy and improves accuracy on AIME benchmarks while preserving exploration across multiple model scales and task types.
AI.motivation: Policy entropy collapse during reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (GRPO) hinders sustained exploration and learning in large language models. There is a need for principled mechanisms to regulate entropy without sacrificing performance, across model scales and diverse reasoning tasks.
AI.method: First-order gradient analysis of token-level entropy dynamics under GRPO reveals a per-token credit assignment mismatch: the per-token entropy change equals the product of trajectory-level advantage and an entropy-sensitivity function over the next-token distribution, forming an advantage-surprisal four-quadrant structure with near-criticality. The authors introduce STARE (Surprisal-guided Token-level Advantage Reweighting for policy Entropy stability) that (1) identifies entropy-critical token subsets using batch-internal surprisal quantiles, (2) selectively reweights their effective advantages, and (3) applies a target-entropy closed-loop gate to regulate entropy stability.
AI.result: STARE enables stable RL training from 1.5B to 32B models across Short CoT, Long CoT, and Multi-Turn Tool Use tasks. On AIME24/25, STARE outperforms DAPO and baselines by 4–8% in average accuracy. Reflection tokens and response length grow with STARE, indicating maintained exploration-exploitation balance. Code is released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: STARE provides a principled mechanism to stabilize entropy during RL fine-tuning of LLMs by targeting entropy-critical tokens via surprisal and enforcing a target entropy. It demonstrates robust performance gains across model scales and task families and suggests improved exploration-exploitation dynamics as a pathway to unlock RL training potential. Potential limitations may include sensitivity to surprisal estimation, batch statistics, and hyperparameter choices for the entropy gate.

title: DIPHINE: Diffusion-based $Φ$-ID Neural Estimator
summary: Uncovering the true informational architecture of real-world complex systems requires disentangling how their components uniquely store, redundantly share, and synergistically integrate information over time. Integrated Information Decomposition ($Φ$ID) is a framework for decomposing the information dynamics of multivariate systems into sixteen non-overlapping atoms that characterize redundant, unique, and synergistic modes of information storage, transfer, and integration. Existing methods to compute $Φ$ID are restricted to Gaussian or discrete systems, preventing its application to continuous non-Gaussian dynamical systems. We address this limitation by proposing DIPHINE (Diffusion-based $Φ$-ID Neural Estimator), the first neural estimator that leverages score-based diffusion models to jointly estimate all the mutual information terms required by $Φ$ID from a single amortized network, recovering the sixteen atoms through Möbius inversion. We provide a theoretical analysis of error propagation through the inversion, showing that the Jacobian of the mapping from mutual informations to atoms is integer-valued and that the synergy-to-synergy atom is provably the hardest to estimate. We demonstrate accurate recovery of ground-truth atoms on synthetic benchmarks, superior performance compared to established mutual information estimators, and the ability to extract physiologically interpretable information-dynamic structure on an application involving real data without any distributional assumptions.

AI.tldr: Introduces DIPHINE, a diffusion-based neural estimator for ΦID enabling non-Gaussian continuous systems; jointly learns all mutual information terms via a score-based diffusion model and recovers the sixteen ΦID atoms through Möbius inversion; provides theoretical error analysis and shows strong empirical performance on synthetic benchmarks and real data.
AI.motivation: Understanding the information dynamics of real-world complex systems requires decomposing storage, sharing, and integration of information over time. ΦID provides sixteen non-overlapping atoms, but existing estimation methods are limited to Gaussian or discrete variables, restricting application to continuous non-Gaussian dynamics.
AI.method: Proposes DIPHINE (Diffusion-based Φ-ID Neural Estimator), a neural estimator that uses score-based diffusion models to jointly estimate all mutual information terms required by ΦID from a single amortized network, followed by Möbius inversion to recover the sixteen atoms. The paper provides a theoretical analysis of error propagation, showing the Jacobian of the MI→atom mapping is integer-valued and that the synergy-to-synergy atom is the hardest to estimate.
AI.result: Accurate recovery of ground-truth atoms on synthetic benchmarks and superior performance versus established mutual information estimators. Demonstrates extraction of physiologically interpretable information-dynamic structure on real data without distributional assumptions.
AI.conclusion: First neural estimator for ΦID in continuous non-Gaussian settings; broadens ΦID applicability to real-world systems and provides theoretical guarantees on error propagation, with demonstrated practical utility in real-data scenarios.

title: Explaining Attention with Program Synthesis
summary: A longstanding goal of research on interpretable deep learning is to replace opaque neural computations with human-meaningful symbolic descriptions. In this paper, we propose an approach for approximating the behavior of components of deep networks with executable programs. We focus on attention heads in transformer language models. For a given head, we first compute its associated attention matrices on a collection of randomly selected training examples. Next, we prompt a pre-trained language model with a summary of these matrices, and instruct it to generate a set of Python programs that can reproduce the associated attention patterns given only text from the input sentence. Finally, we re-rank programs according to how well our final set of programs predict behavior on held-out inputs. We demonstrate that a set of fewer than 1,000 such generated programs can reproduce the attention patterns of heads in GPT-2, TinyLlama-1.1B, and Llama-3B, achieving an average Intersection-over-Union similarity above 75% on TinyStories. Moreover, the best-fit programs can replace neural attention heads without substantially affecting model behavior: replacing 25% of attention heads with programmatic surrogates across the three models incurs only a 16% average perplexity increase, while maintaining performance on a variety of downstream question answering benchmarks. This work contributes a scalable pipeline for reverse-engineering attention heads in transformer models using human-readable, executable code, advancing a path toward symbolic transparency in neural models.

AI.tldr: A scalable approach to reverse-engineer transformer attention heads into executable Python programs; a small set of programs can reproduce head behavior and replace a subset of heads with modest performance costs across models.
AI.motivation: Increase interpretability of deep models by translating opaque attention computations into human-readable symbolic representations, enabling transparency and potential symbolic manipulation.
AI.method: For each head, compute attention matrices on random training examples; prompt a pre-trained language model with a summary of these matrices to generate Python programs that reproduce attention from text inputs; re-rank programs by predictive fidelity on held-out inputs; apply the best programs as surrogates to replace heads; evaluate on multiple models and downstream tasks.
AI.result: A set of <1000 generated programs can reproduce attention patterns of heads in GPT-2, TinyLlama-1.1B, and Llama-3B with IoU >75% on TinyStories; replacing 25% of heads with programmatic surrogates incurs ~16% average perplexity increase but preserves performance on QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable pipeline for reverse-engineering attention heads into executable, human-readable code, advancing symbolic transparency in neural networks and enabling partial substitution of neural components with interpretable surrogates.

title: Seeing Before Reasoning: Decoupling Perception and Reasoning for Shortcut-Resilient Multimodal On-Policy Self-Distillation
summary: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) trains a model on its own rollouts and uses a frozen copy to provide dense token-level targets conditioned on a reference target. This works well for LLM reasoning, but a direct extension to multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can create a shortcut: the privileged target may guide tokens mainly based on the text reference target rather than the image. We propose ViGOS, a visually grounded OPSD framework for MLLM post-training. The student first writes a visual description and then reasons toward the final answer. For valid rollouts, an image-only perception teacher supervises the description, while a privileged reasoning teacher supervises the reasoning and final answer on the same student prefix. A reference teacher is used only for invalid rollouts to recover the output format. Across general vision-language, expert reasoning, visual math, spatial grounding, and visual-language-prior benchmarks, ViGOS keeps the main benefits of OPSD and improves image-grounded behavior in shortcut-prone settings.

AI.tldr: ViGOS introduces visually grounded OPSD for multimodal LLMs by using a visual perception teacher and a privileged reasoning teacher during post-training, with a reference teacher for invalid rollouts. It preserves OPSD benefits while improving image-grounded behavior in shortcut-prone settings.
AI.motivation: Extending on-policy self-distillation to multimodal LLMs risks shortcuts where the privileged (text) target dominates token generation, undermining visual grounding. ViGOS aims to enforce visual-grounded reasoning during post-training.
AI.method: Student produces a visual description before reasoning toward the final answer. For valid rollouts, an image-only perception teacher supervises the description and a privileged reasoning teacher supervises the reasoning and final answer on the same prefix. A reference teacher is used only for invalid rollouts to recover the output format.
AI.result: ViGOS retains the main benefits of OPSD and enhances image-grounded behavior across general vision-language, expert reasoning, visual math, spatial grounding, and visual-language-prior benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ViGOS provides a robust visually grounded post-training framework for MLLMs, mitigating shortcut-prone behavior while preserving OPSD advantages and delivering broad multimodal grounding improvements.

title: The Reward Was in Your Data All Along: Correcting Flow Matching with Discriminator-Guided RL
summary:
Score- and flow-matching models often rely on preference-based reinforcement learning for two purposes: aligning with subjective preferences and, surprisingly, recovering properties such as visual realism and coherent object structure that matching-based training is intended to learn from the data itself. We argue that this reflects a structural mismatch. Matching losses measure $\ell_2$ regression error on the velocity or score field under training-time marginals, a proxy poorly aligned with the visual and semantic properties that determine sample quality at inference. Given a reward aligned with these properties, RL sidesteps the mismatch by evaluating the model on its own samples and following the reward landscape directly. The challenge is to obtain such a reward without relying on human preferences, which are expensive and conflate data realism with annotator inclinations.
  We propose Discriminator-Guided RL (DRL). DRL trains a discriminator to separate data from base-model samples in a pretrained representation space and uses its logit as the reward in KL-regularized RL. The pretrained space restricts the discriminator to perceptually meaningful directions, and the logit estimates the log-likelihood ratio between data and model, which is the optimal reward for targeting the data distribution. Across SiT, JiT, REPA, and RAE, DRL reduces guidance-free FID (e.g., $9.38 \to 2.62$ on SiT) and semantic-space FD (e.g., $88.2 \to 19.3$ on DINOv3 for SiT), with consistent gains across all backbones, and improves human-preference rewards without training on them. It also yields a better Pareto frontier between preference reward and image fidelity under subsequent preference-based post-training, increasing alignment while reducing low-level artifacts such as oversaturation and excessive brightness.

AI.tldr: Discriminator-Guided RL (DRL) uses a pretrained discriminator in perceptual space to provide a KL-regularized RL reward, aligning generative models with the data distribution without relying on human preferences, yielding substantial improvements in FID and semantic diversity across multiple backbones and reducing artifacts.
AI.motivation: Address structural mismatch: matching losses under training-time marginals target velocity/score fields that do not align with perceptual/semantic qualities governing sample quality; human preferences are expensive and conflated with realism; create a reward that directly reflects data likelihood without human bias.
AI.method: Train a discriminator to separate real data from model samples in a pretrained representation space; use the discriminator logit as the reward for KL-regularized RL; apply across multiple backbones (SiT, JiT, REPA, RAE); evaluate on objective metrics (FID, semantic-space FD) and human-preference rewards.
AI.result: Significant reductions in guidance-free FID (e.g., 9.38 → 2.62 on SiT) and semantic-space FD (88.2 → 19.3 on DINOv3 for SiT); gains consistent across backbones; improves human-preference rewards without training on them; improves Pareto frontier between preference reward and image fidelity after preference-based post-training.
AI.conclusion: Discriminator-guided RL provides a data-distribution-aligned reward without human labeling, reducing artifacts and improving both objective and human-aligned metrics; demonstrates a scalable approach to reward design for generative vision models and a favorable trade-off curve in performance versus preference alignment.

title: Geometric and Stochastic Analysis of Discontinuities in Sparse Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (SMoE) architectures are now widely deployed in state-of-the-art language and vision models, where conditional routing allows scaling to very large networks. However, this very Top-$k$ expert selection that enables conditional routing also renders the SMoE map inherently discontinuous. In the vicinity of these discontinuity surfaces, even inputs that are arbitrarily close may activate substantially different sets of experts resulting in significantly different outputs. In this work we give a rigorous geometric and stochastic analysis of these discontinuities. We first classify them by order, determined by the number of tied experts at a switching event. Using measure-theoretic slicing arguments, we establish asymptotic volume estimates for the thickened discontinuity surfaces, showing that lower-order discontinuity sets dominate, whereas higher-order ones occupy a vanishingly small relative volume. Next, modeling random perturbations in the input space via a diffusion process, we prove that the path eventually encounter a discontinuity, and moreover that the first hit almost surely occurs on an order-1 discontinuity with explicit finite-time probability bounds. We further derive occupation-time bounds that quantify the duration the random path spend in the neighborhoods of each discontinuity order. These theoretical results imply that inputs are more likely to lie near lower order discontinuities. Motivated by this insight, we propose a simple smoothing mechanism that can be directly applied to existing SMoEs, softly incorporating experts near discontinuities; our analysis guarantees that the added computational overhead remains small while providing localized smoothing near discontinuities, and experiments across language and vision tasks show that smoothing not only enforces continuity of the SMoE map but also enhances empirical performance.

AI.tldr: Provides a rigorous geometric and stochastic analysis of discontinuities in top-k Sparse Mixture-of-Experts, classifies discontinuities by order, shows lower-order sets dominate in volume, proves hitting and occupation-time properties under diffusion noise, and introduces a lightweight smoothing mechanism that improves continuity and empirical performance.
AI.motivation: SMoEs enable conditional routing to scale large models, but top-k selection creates discontinuities leading to unstable outputs. A formal understanding and a remedy are needed.
AI.method: Measure-theoretic slicing to estimate volumes of thickened discontinuities; stochastic diffusion modeling of input perturbations to study first-hit times and occupation durations; derivation of finite-time probability bounds; proposal of soft smoothing near discontinuities with low overhead; empirical validation on language and vision tasks.
AI.result: Discontinuity surfaces are organized by order; lower-order discontinuities occupy most of the volume; a random perturbation path almost surely hits an order-1 discontinuity with explicit finite-time bounds; occupation-time bounds quantify time near each order; smoothing enforces continuity and can improve task performance with minimal overhead.
AI.conclusion: Smoothing near discontinuities is effective, minimally costly, and improves empirical performance; inputs are likely near lower-order discontinuities, justifying the proposed smoothing strategy and its general applicability to SMoEs in language and vision models.

title: Smoothness-Based Derandomization of PAC-Bayes Bounds
summary: We study PAC-Bayes derandomization for smooth loss functions. Our goal is to obtain generalization bounds that hold with high probability for deterministic predictors by exploiting smoothness properties of both the loss and the predictor class. We show that passing from the Gibbs predictor to the deterministic predictor at the posterior mean has a precise cost, given by the generalization gap of the Jensen gap class. We control this class through its Rademacher complexity, leading to bounds for deterministic predictors that involve flatness quantities expressed in terms of parameter Jacobians and Hessians of the score map. The framework applies to both bounded and unbounded smooth loss functions, and we specialize the results to linear predictors and smooth neural networks. Finally, the Jacobian and Hessian quantities appearing in the theory motivate a practical regularizer. For BatchNorm networks, we compute this regularizer with respect to effective BatchNorm weights obtained by folding the BatchNorm transformation into the adjacent affine weights. Experiments on CIFAR-10 illustrate the behavior of this regularizer under different batch sizes.

AI.tldr: PAC-Bayes derandomization for smooth losses yields deterministic generalization bounds via Jensen-gap controlled by Rademacher complexity; introduces Jacobian/Hessian flatness regularizers; applicable to linear and smooth neural networks; BatchNorm folding; CIFAR-10 experiments.
AI.motivation: Obtain high-probability generalization bounds for deterministic predictors by exploiting smoothness of loss and predictor class, quantifying the cost of moving from a randomized (Gibbs) predictor to its posterior mean.
AI.method: Develop PAC-Bayes derandomization framework; bound Jensen-gap generalization error using Rademacher complexity of the gap class; express bounds in terms of Jacobian/Hessian (flatness) quantities of the score map; specialize to linear models and smooth neural nets; propose a practical regularizer based on these quantities; for BatchNorm networks fold BN into adjacent affine weights to compute the regularizer; evaluate on CIFAR-10 across batch sizes.
AI.result: Deterministic generalization bounds that include flatness terms (Jacobian/Hessian) and quantify the cost of derandomization via Jensen-gap; the regularizer is computable and yields observable effects in experiments; the framework applies to both bounded and unbounded smooth losses; BN folding enables practical computation of the regularizer.
AI.conclusion: The work links PAC-Bayes derandomization with flatness-based regularization, enabling principled, computable bounds and regularizers for smooth models, with practical validation on CIFAR-10 and applicability to BatchNorm architectures.

title: JourneyFormer: Encoding Airbnb Guest Journey with Sequence Modeling
summary: Sequence modeling has become increasingly popular in recommendation and ranking algorithms, owing to its capacity to model users' historical behaviors and infer user intentions. Despite its theoretical simplicity, the practical deployment of a sequence model in production is non-trivial due to complexity of the sequence and sparse labels. For example, in Airbnb, guest sequences are often long, exploratory and complex, and we focus on booking labels, which are sparse. As such, we are often required to make various design decisions regarding data and modeling to strike a balance between effectiveness and scalability. This work delved into these production challenges and deployed JourneyFormer, a sequence modeling solution for search ranking at Airbnb. We detail crucial design considerations, covering aspects such as guest event selection, ID embeddings, model architecture, and label attribution. Additionally, we describe several tailored strategies to accelerate model training and inference. JourneyFormer has been successfully deployed within Airbnb's production, where its effectiveness and impact have been evidenced not only by improved offline ranking metrics but also by significant gains in key business metrics through online A/B testing across 2 production surfaces.

AI.tldr: Airbnb deployed JourneyFormer to production for search ranking to handle long, exploratory guest sequences with sparse booking labels, emphasizing data/model design and inference acceleration; it improved offline metrics and delivered online business gains across two surfaces.
AI.motivation: To address real-world challenges of deploying sequence models in production: long, complex user sequences and sparse target labels demand careful data selection, embedding strategies, architecture choices, and scalable training/inference.
AI.method: Developed JourneyFormer, a sequence-modeling solution for ranking with focused design decisions on guest event selection, ID embeddings, model architecture, and label attribution, plus tailored strategies to accelerate training and inference for production deployment.
AI.result: In production at Airbnb, JourneyFormer improved offline ranking metrics and produced significant online business gains in A/B tests on two surfaces.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the practicality and effectiveness of sequence-based ranking models in large-scale production, highlighting the importance of targeted design choices and optimization for scalability and measurable business impact.

title: INDEQS: Informed Neural controlled Differential EQuationS
summary: Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDE) provide a powerful continuous-time framework for forecasting time series, but standard graph-based extensions typically learn spatial structure purely from data, even in settings where a directed graph structure is known a priori. We introduce Informed Neural controlled Differential EQuationS (INDEQS), a graph-based NCDE forecasting method that incorporates prior knowledge of a directed graph at distinct architectural positions. INDEQS separates inner mixing of hidden states across graph nodes from outer mixing between vector field and control, and offers both a lightweight graph-constrained variant and a more expressive variant, learning additional graph connections from data via adaptive graph convolutions. To systematically study when graph informedness is beneficial in forecasting, we devise a continuous advection simulation on directed graphs, yielding synthetic spatio-temporal datasets with known ground-truth flow structure. We then evaluate INDEQS on two real-world tasks: river discharge forecasting on a hydrological network and traffic flow prediction on PeMS08. Across these synthetic and real-world benchmarks, outer informedness consistently improves mean absolute error over an uninformed NCDE with comparable parameter count, particularly on larger graphs, while inner informedness offers a more parameter-efficient alternative when strict adherence to a known adjacency is desired. A comparison of discrete convolutional and continuous-time decoders further shows that continuous decoders yield better accuracy and greater temporal flexibility on real-world tasks. An implementation of INDEQS and the advection simulation is available at https://github.com/Mitchi1/indeqs.

AI.tldr: INDEQS adds graph-informed architecture to Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs) by separating inner (state) and outer (vector field-control) graph mixing. It provides a lightweight, graph-constrained and a more expressive variant with adaptive graph convolutions, plus a continuous advection synthetic benchmark. Across synthetic and real data (river discharge; PeMS08), outer-informedness consistently reduces MAE vs uninformed NCDE with similar parameter counts, especially on larger graphs; inner-informedness offers a parameter-efficient alternative when strict adherence to known adjacency is desired. Continuous-time decoders outperform discrete decoders in accuracy and temporal flexibility. Code is available.
AI.motivation: Standard NCDEs with graph extensions often learn spatial structure solely from data, ignoring available prior directed graphs. There is a need to leverage known graph structure while maintaining model flexibility and efficiency, and to understand when such informedness helps forecasting.
AI.method: Propose INDEQS, a graph-informed NCDE framework that decouples inner (hidden-state) graph mixing from outer (vector-field/control) graph mixing. Offers a lightweight, graph-constrained variant and a more expressive variant that learns extra graph connections via adaptive graph convolutions. Includes a synthetic continuous advection benchmark on directed graphs to study when graph-informedness helps, and evaluations on river discharge forecasting and traffic flow (PeMS08). Compares discrete vs continuous decoders.
AI.result: Outer informedness consistently improves mean absolute error over an uninformed NCDE with comparable parameter counts, notably on larger graphs. Inner informedness provides a parameter-efficient alternative when strict adherence to a known adjacency is desired. Continuous-time decoders yield better accuracy and temporal flexibility than discrete decoders on real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: Informed graph structure can significantly enhance NCDE-based forecasting when incorporated at the appropriate architectural positions. The framework supports both strict adherence to known graphs and learning additional connections, with continuous decoders offering practical advantages.

title: ChronoSurv: A Clinical Pathway-Guided Graph Framework for Multimodal Survival Analysis
comment: Accepted at MICCAI 2026. Submitted version due to embargo
summary: Accurate survival prediction is essential for personalized treatment planning in head and neck cancer, yet remains challenging due to the heterogeneous and high-dimensional nature of multimodal clinical data. While deep survival models have improved predictive performance over classical statistical approaches, existing methods typically rely on static fusion strategies or temporally agnostic modeling, limiting their ability to capture structured clinical workflows. In this work, we propose ChronoSurv, a heterogeneous hierarchical directed graph framework for multimodal survival analysis. ChronoSurv represents patient care as a progression-aware clinical trajectory using directed graphs aligned with key diagnostic steps. A hierarchical topology incorporates fine-grained, coarse, and global representations, further supporting flexible adaptation to missing modalities, while heterogeneous message passing models complex and asymmetric relationships across modalities and clinical steps. Experimental results on two public datasets demonstrate that ChronoSurv achieves state-of-the-art discriminative performance while maintaining statistically reliable calibration. Comprehensive ablation studies further confirm the contribution of each architectural component, highlighting the potential of trajectory-aware graph modeling for multimodal survival prediction.

AI.tldr: Trajectory-aware, multimodal survival predictor for head and neck cancer using a hierarchical, heterogeneous directed graph that mirrors clinical workflows; achieves state-of-the-art discrimination with reliable calibration.
AI.motivation: To overcome challenges in survival prediction from heterogeneous, high-dimensional multimodal data and limitations of static fusion or temporally agnostic models; leverage structured clinical workflows to improve discrimination and calibration.
AI.method: Introduce ChronoSurv, a heterogeneous hierarchical directed graph framework that models patient care as trajectory-aware clinical graphs aligned with diagnostic steps. It uses a hierarchical topology (fine-grained, coarse, global) to capture multi-scale representations and supports flexible handling of missing modalities. Heterogeneous message passing captures complex, asymmetric relationships across modalities and clinical steps.
AI.result: On two public datasets, ChronoSurv achieves state-of-the-art discriminative performance and reliable calibration. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each architectural component (trajectory alignment, hierarchical topology, and heterogeneous message passing).
AI.conclusion: Trajectory-aware graph modeling is a promising approach for multimodal survival prediction; ChronoSurv demonstrates effective integration of clinical workflows and heterogeneous data, yielding superior predictive performance and calibration.

title: Complementary Attention Head Pruning for Efficient Transformers
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted for presentation at the International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN) 2026
summary: The remarkable success of Transformer-based models in natural language processing stems from architectural scaling, which leads to a large number of parameters and hinders deployment in resource-constrained environments. While structured pruning offers a pathway to compression, existing state-of-the-art methods often rely on gradient-based importance ranking or stochastic gating, which suffer from instability, structural degeneration, and the need for extensive manual hyperparameter tuning. In this paper, we introduce CAHP (Complementary Attention Head Pruning), a novel post-hoc framework that redefines head selection as a global graph-theoretical problem. Rather than evaluating heads in isolation, CAHP utilizes graph-based clustering combined with information-theoretic distance measures to identify and preserve a topologically diverse subset of complementary attention heads. Without requiring a predefined sparsity level or pruning ratio, the framework automatically determines the number of selected attention heads across layers by identifying a diminishing marginal performance curve, where pruning additional heads leads to a sharp degradation in performance, as determined by the chosen polynomial degree. Extensive evaluations on the SST-5 and MNLI benchmarks, across different Transformer model scales, demonstrate that CAHP consistently outperforms competitive baselines, particularly in high-compression regimes. Furthermore, our structural analysis shows that CAHP avoids the "proximity bias" of gradient-based pruning methods, which tend to preserve heads mainly in layers close to the output, and instead retains a functionally critical set of attention heads in the model's intermediate layers.

AI.tldr: CAHP reframes attention head pruning as a global graph-theoretic selection problem, using clustering and information-theoretic distances to pick a diverse, complementary set of heads without a predefined sparsity target.
AI.motivation: Transformer models grow in parameter count, making deployment difficult; gradient-based or stochastic pruning suffers from instability, structural degeneration, and hyperparameter sensitivity. A post-hoc, data-driven method is needed to maintain performance under high compression.
AI.method: CAHP constructs a global head-competition graph across layers, applies graph-based clustering and information-theoretic distance measures to identify a topologically diverse set of complementary attention heads, and automatically determines the number of heads to keep by detecting a diminishing marginal performance curve (via a chosen polynomial degree). No fixed sparsity ratio is required.
AI.result: Empirically, CAHP outperforms competitive baselines on SST-5 and MNLI across multiple Transformer scales, particularly under high compression. Structural analysis indicates CAHP avoids proximity bias, preserving functionally critical heads in intermediate layers rather than near the output.
AI.conclusion: CAHP provides a robust, post-hoc pruning framework that achieves high compression with stable performance, leveraging topology-aware head selection to maintain functional diversity and mitigate common pruning issues.

title: AGDN: Learning to Solve Traveling Salesman Problem with Anisotropic Graph Diffusion Network
comment: Accepted at the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD 2026)
summary: The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) is a cornerstone of combinatorial optimization and arises in many practical scenarios. Although graph-based learning approaches have been explored for TSP, the question of how to exploit graph structure more effectively remains open. We present the Anisotropic Graph Diffusion Network (AGDN), a new Graph Neural Network framework designed to solve TSP. Our method tackles two central difficulties: (1) the lack of informative topological prior in fully connected TSP graphs, and (2) losing connected nodes in the optimal solution after the commonly used graph sparsification techniques. To overcome these issues, we construct a MixScore transition matrix that merges node similarity with pairwise distance, and we develop an anisotropic graph diffusion strategy that supports efficient information exchange across multiple hops. Comprehensive experiments spanning diverse instance sizes and node distributions show that AGDN consistently outperforms existing methods while keeping computation time competitive. Furthermore, AGDN generalizes well to problem sizes and distributions beyond those seen during training. The implementation is publicly available at: https://github.com/LabRAI/AGDN.

AI.tldr: AGDN introduces MixScore and anisotropic diffusion for TSP on fully connected graphs, addressing topological priors and sparsification pitfalls; it achieves superior performance with competitive runtimes and good generalization, with code released.
AI.motivation: TSP graphs are fully connected and lack informative topological priors; conventional sparsification can drop critical connections, harming optimal tours. There is a need for a GNN framework that effectively exploits graph structure for TSP while preserving important connections.
AI.method: Propose MixScore transition matrix that combines node similarity with pairwise distance. Develop an anisotropic graph diffusion strategy enabling efficient multi-hop information exchange. Integrate these into a Graph Neural Network framework (AGDN) tailored for TSP.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across varying instance sizes and node distributions show AGDN consistently outperforms existing methods. Computation time remains competitive. AGDN generalizes well to problem sizes and distributions unseen during training.
AI.conclusion: AGDN effectively leverages structural priors via MixScore and anisotropic diffusion to improve TSP solving, with strong empirical performance and generalization. The approach is made publicly available as open-source.

title: A Human-in-the-Loop Bayesian Optimization Framework for Constraint-Aware Bioprocess Development
summary: This work presents an extension to Pareto Front Guided Sampling (PFGS), a Human-in-the-Loop (HitL) Bayesian Optimization (BO) framework in which Gaussian process (GP) surrogate-derived quantities are reformulated as objectives of a multi-objective optimization problem, and the resulting Pareto front is exposed to a domain expert for interactive candidate selection rather than returning a single automated recommendation. The framework is extended in two directions: constrained optimization is addressed by incorporating the posterior probability of satisfying output specification limits as an explicit Pareto objective, computed analytically from the GP posterior distribution; robust optimization is addressed by a Monte Carlo sampling strategy that estimates expected lower-confidence performance over a user-defined variability of input perturbations, capturing performance degradation under likely implementation deviations. The resulting multi-dimensional Pareto representation renders trade-offs between predicted performance, model uncertainty, probabilistic constraint satisfaction, and input robustness simultaneously visible through pairwise two-dimensional projections on an interactive dashboard, enabling selection criteria to be iteratively refined as the surrogate model improves and development objectives evolve. The framework is showcased on an eight-dimensional fed-batch Chinese Hamster Ovary (CHO) cell culture simulator demonstrating systematic identification of high-performing, feasibility-compliant, and perturbation-resilient operating conditions, and illustrating how expert-defined requirements provide a principled stopping criterion and support informed allocation of experimental resources.

AI.tldr: Extends Pareto Front Guided Sampling (PFGS) in a HitL Bayesian Optimization framework to handle constraints and robustness, exposing a multi-objective Pareto front for expert-driven selection; demonstrated on an eight-dimensional CHO cell culture simulator.
AI.motivation: To enable interactive, constraint-aware, robust optimization in Bayesian optimization by explicitly presenting trade-offs to domain experts, addressing uncertainty and variability in bioprocess design.
AI.method: Two extensions to PFGS: (1) incorporate the posterior probability of satisfying output specification limits as an explicit Pareto objective, derived analytically from the GP posterior; (2) introduce a Monte Carlo sampling strategy to estimate expected lower-confidence performance under input perturbations, capturing degradation due to implementation variability. The framework yields a multi-objective Pareto front (performance, model uncertainty, constraint satisfaction probability, robustness) displayed via pairwise 2D projections on an interactive dashboard for iterative expert-driven selection.
AI.result: Applied to an eight-dimensional fed-batch CHO cell culture simulator, enabling systematic identification of high-performing, feasible, and perturbation-resilient operating conditions; demonstrates how expert-defined requirements can serve as stopping criteria and guide experimental resource allocation.
AI.conclusion: The approach makes trade-offs among performance, uncertainty, constraints, and robustness transparent, enabling principled, expert-guided optimization in complex bioprocess settings and supporting iterative refinement as the surrogate model improves.

title: SCAN: Enhance Time Series Anomaly Detection via Multi-Scale Neighborhood-Centered Clustering
summary: Time series anomaly detection plays a crucial role in a wide range of real-world applications. Reconstruction-based methods have become the mainstream paradigm, but they suffer from over-generalization and under-generalization problems, which are challenging to balance. To address this, we introduce multi-scale clustering to enhance reconstruction-based methods. At the representation level, we integrate the cluster center representations of normal patterns to constrain the model to target representative normal patterns for reconstruction, preventing dominance of powerful capacity and representation capability. At the anomaly criterion level, we derive anomaly confidence score based on cluster membership probability and combine it with reconstruction error, providing dual criteria for detection. Furthermore, the effectiveness of the cluster center representations and anomaly confidence score depends on the clustering performance. Accordingly, we extract neighborhood-centered representations for multi-view clustering to improve clustering performance. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world datasets from diverse application domains demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of SCAN.

AI.tldr: A reconstruction-based time-series anomaly detector, SCAN, uses multi-scale clustering to ground reconstructions in normal patterns via cluster centers and introduces a dual-criterion anomaly score (cluster-membership probability plus reconstruction error). It further enhances clustering via neighborhood-centered representations for multi-view clustering, achieving state-of-the-art results on diverse real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the trade-off between over-generalization and under-generalization in reconstruction-based anomaly detection by anchoring reconstructions to representative normal patterns and by using cluster-based uncertainty as an auxiliary anomaly score.
AI.method: 1) Representational constraint with cluster center representations of normal patterns to limit model capacity from modeling anomalous patterns; 2) Anomaly criterion via a cluster-membership probability to yield an anomaly confidence score, combined with reconstruction error for dual criteria; 3) Neighborhood-centered representations for multi-view clustering to boost clustering performance; 4) Extensive experiments validating SCAN across diverse real-world datasets.
AI.result: SCAN achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple real-world time-series datasets across diverse application domains.
AI.conclusion: Multi-scale clustering improves reconstruction-based anomaly detection by constraining representations to normal patterns and introducing a cluster-based anomaly score; clustering quality directly influences effectiveness; neighborhood-centered multi-view clustering further enhances performance, yielding robust, domain-general anomaly detection.

title: Detecting Hidden ML Training With Zero-Overhead Telemetry
comment: Technical AI Governance Research workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Hardware-enabled monitoring of GPU workloads underpins many proposals for AI compute governance, but if developers can defeat monitoring mechanisms, such schemes are unworkable. We evaluate the adversarial robustness of GPU workload classification using only zero-overhead, privacy-preserving NVML telemetry: content-agnostic signals that observe physical effects of computation without accessing model weights, training data, or hyperparameters. Across 5 rounds of monitor-evader iteration, we evaluate 20 evasion strategy families on 9 GPU models spanning 4 architecture generations. We develop a classifier that achieves 98.2% binary accuracy at identifying training workloads across the whole corpus, and 43-87% accuracy against the most challenging unexpected workloads even when they are adversarially disguised.

AI.tldr: Zero-overhead NVML telemetry can classify GPU workloads with high accuracy, but robust evasion remains feasible; 5 rounds of evasion across 9 GPUs show persistent classification strength for standard workloads but degrade for adversarial disguises.
AI.motivation: Evaluate robustness of hardware-enabled, privacy-preserving GPU workload monitoring against evasion strategies, to inform AI compute governance and privacy-preserving telemetry.
AI.method: Five rounds of monitor-evader iterations. Twenty evasion-strategy families tested on 9 GPU models across 4 architecture generations. Built a classifier on content-agnostic NVML telemetry (no model weights/data/hyperparameters). Assessed binary workload-identification performance on training vs. other workloads, including adversarial disguises.
AI.result: The classifier achieves 98.2% binary accuracy for identifying training workloads across the full corpus. Against the most challenging unexpected workloads when adversarially disguised, accuracy ranges from 43% to 87%.
AI.conclusion: NVML-based, privacy-preserving GPU monitoring is highly effective for standard training workloads but remains vulnerable to carefully crafted evasions. The approach offers strong privacy benefits but may require additional defenses (e.g., multi-modal signals or anomaly detection) to resist adversarial disguise.

title: Structured Inference with Large Language Gibbs
summary: The knowledge encoded in large language models (LLMs) can serve as a substrate for structured reasoning over variables describing a complex world, but accessing this knowledge in a probabilistically coherent manner poses a difficult inference problem. We propose Large Language Gibbs, a scheme for structured probabilistic inference that uses conditional distributions of an LLM as transition operators. Rather than sampling structured objects through single-pass autoregressive generation, we iteratively resample individual variables conditioned on others using an LLM's next-token conditionals. This approach avoids order-dependent biases and produces a stationary distribution that reflects a compromise between all local conditionals. We apply this approach to sampling from synthetic distributions, consistent reasoning tasks, and Bayesian structure learning. The results suggest that the use of LLM conditionals in MCMC is a practical alternative to one-pass generation for structured probabilistic inference under a world prior accessible through noisy LLM conditionals.

AI.tldr: Proposes Large Language Gibbs (LLG): a structured probabilistic inference scheme using LLM next-token conditionals as MCMC transition operators to iteratively resample variables and obtain a stationary distribution that blends local conditionals; applicable to synthetic distributions, reasoning tasks, and Bayesian structure learning; acts as a practical alternative to one-pass generation under a world prior accessible via noisy LLM conditionals.
AI.motivation: Access the probabilistic knowledge encoded in LLMs for structured reasoning. Autoregressive generation risks order biases and may not yield coherent joint distributions; need a method that yields a stationary, globally coherent distribution reflecting all local conditionals.
AI.method: Treat conditional distributions from an LLM as MCMC transition operators. Iteratively resample individual variables conditioned on the others, rather than generating all variables in one pass. This creates a Markov chain whose stationary distribution reflects a compromise among local conditionals.
AI.result: Demonstrated on sampling from synthetic distributions, consistent reasoning tasks, and Bayesian structure learning. The results indicate that LLM conditionals in MCMC can be a practical alternative to one-pass generation for structured probabilistic inference given a world prior via noisy LLM conditionals.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based conditional distributions can be effectively used as MCMC transitions to perform structured probabilistic inference, offering a viable, flexible approach when a world prior is accessible through noisy LLM conditionals and when one-pass generation is inadequate for joint reasoning.

title: Risk Stratification for ICU Delirium using Pervasive Ambient Sensing Information
summary: Delirium is a common and serious complication in the Intensive Care Unit (ICU), associated with increased morbidity, prolonged hospital stays, and higher healthcare costs. Despite its prevalence, early prediction and prevention remain challenging. Environmental factors such as ambient sound and light may influence the onset of delirium, yet they are often overlooked in risk assessments. In this study, we examined whether light intensity and sound pressure levels can independently predict delirium across multiple prediction horizons. We evaluated four efficient sequential neural network models on data collected from 9 ICUs across 309 patients to predict delirium for 10 prediction-window sizes. We reported feature importance and direction of influence using Shapley Additive Explanations analysis. The convolutional model achieved the strongest discrimination, with AUC = 0.80 on sound data and on combined data. Sound features were the dominant predictors overall. Integrating sound with light improved short-term ($<1$ week) prediction, with the combined model assigning the highest risk immediately after the sensing period. These findings suggest that passive ambient sensing, especially sound, can add a clinically meaningful, interpretable signal for delirium risk estimation and offer a practical pathway to enrich multimodal ICU prediction and prevention strategies.

AI.tldr: Ambient sensing (sound) predicts ICU delirium; four efficient sequential neural nets; data from 9 ICUs with 309 patients; 10 prediction windows; AUC 0.80 for sound and for combined data; SHAP identifies sound as dominant predictor; light augments short-term prediction when combined.
AI.motivation: Delirium in the ICU is common and costly; ambient environmental factors (noise, light) may influence delirium risk but are often neglected in risk assessments; exploring passive sensing could enable early, interpretable risk prediction.
AI.method: Evaluate four efficient sequential neural network models on ambient data (sound and light) from 9 ICUs, 309 patients, predicting delirium across 10 prediction-window sizes; use SHAP to assess feature importance and direction; identify best-performing model (convolutional).
AI.result: Convolutional model achieved strongest discrimination with AUC = 0.80 on sound data and on combined data. Sound features were the dominant predictors. Integrating sound with light improved short-term prediction (<1 week), with the combined model assigning the highest risk immediately after sensing.
AI.conclusion: Passive ambient sensing, especially sound, provides a clinically meaningful, interpretable signal for delirium risk and offers a practical pathway to enriching multimodal ICU prediction and prevention strategies.

title: P-K-GCN: Physics-augmented Koopman-enhanced Graph Convolutional Network for Deep Spatiotemporal Super-resolution
summary: High-fidelity simulation of spatiotemporal dynamics is computationally prohibitive, necessitating efficient super-resolution techniques to reconstruct high-resolution data from coarse-grained inputs. Traditional data-driven methods often lack physical constraints, and simple physics-informed learning struggles with irregular spatial geometries and intricately evolving temporal dynamics. To tackle these challenges, we propose a Physics-augmented Koopman-enhanced Graph Convolutional Network (P-K-GCN) for spatiotemporal super-resolution on irregular geometries. Specifically, a continuous spline-based GCN is first designed to extract spatial dependencies directly from coarse graph, and Koopman operator theory is incorporated to project the nonlinear dynamics into a compact latent space where temporal progression is linearized. Second, we augment the optimization objective with a physics-based loss to force the data-driven reconstructions to adhere to physical laws for improving predictive fidelity and robustness. Finally, we provide a rigorous theoretical analysis, establishing that the physics augmentation and Koopman regularization mathematically guarantees a reduction in super-resolution error by diminishing Rademacher complexity and tightening generalization bounds. We evaluate our framework on reconstructing spatially high-resolution cardiac electrodynamics across a 3D heart geometry from sparse low-resolution measurements. Numerical experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior accuracy compared to baseline models.

AI.tldr: A physics-augmented Koopman-enhanced Graph Convolutional Network (P-K-GCN) for spatiotemporal super-resolution on irregular geometries, combining a spline-based GCN for spatial dependencies, Koopman operator theory to linearize temporal dynamics in latent space, and physics-based loss for improved fidelity, with theoretical guarantees (reduced Rademacher complexity) and empirical validation on 3D cardiac electrodynamics reconstruction from sparse data.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity spatiotemporal simulations are computationally prohibitive; efficient super-resolution on irregular geometries is needed. Data-driven methods lack physical constraints, and basic physics-informed learning struggles with irregular geometries and intricate temporal dynamics.
AI.method: 1) Continuous spline-based GCN to extract spatial dependencies from a coarse graph; 2) Koopman operator theory to project nonlinear dynamics into a compact latent space where temporal evolution is approximately linear; 3) Physics-based loss to enforce physical laws during optimization; 4) Theoretical analysis showing physics augmentation and Koopman regularization reduce super-resolution error via diminished Rademacher complexity and tighter generalization bounds; 5) Evaluation on reconstructing high-resolution cardiac electrodynamics on a 3D heart geometry from sparse measurements.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate superior accuracy of the proposed method compared with baseline models.
AI.conclusion: The framework yields improved predictive fidelity and robustness on irregular geometries with spatiotemporal dynamics, supported by theoretical generalization guarantees, and shows promise for high-fidelity super-resolution in biomedical electrodynamics and similar domains.

title: Diffusion-Proof: Recipe for Formal Theorem Proving Beyond Auto-Regressive Generation
summary: Enhancing the formal math reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) has become a key focus in both mathematical and computer science communities in recent years. While significant progress has been made in using state-of-the-art Auto-Regressive (AR) LLMs for formal theorem proving, these models suffer from inherent limitations. Their next-token prediction generation methods may yield suboptimal performance due to the challenges of long-range coherence and the compounding of errors over long sequences. Recent advancements in diffusion LLMs (dLLMs), which generate text through iterative denoising of a multi-token block, offer a promising alternative. However, the application of dLLMs to formal mathematics, where maintaining long-range coherence is critical, remains largely understudied. To address the challenges above, we propose **Diffusion-Proof**, to the best of our knowledge, the first framework to train and apply dLLMs for formal theorem proving. Our frameworks contain training and inference methods for two models. The first one is *dLLM-Prover-7B*, which performs whole-proof writing with long-range coherent tactic usage. The second one is *dLLM-Corrector-7B*, which is a novel large block diffusion-based correction model. It leverages the in-filling capabilities of dLLMs to perform local proof correction using bi-directional information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that **Diffusion-Proof** relatively significantly outperforms the AR LLM baseline trained under the same dataset. **Diffusion-Proof** achieves an absolute improvement of **1.61%** on ProofNet-Test and **6.14%** on MiniF2F-Test benchmarks compare to the baseline. Notably, **Diffusion-Proof** successfully resolves one IMO problem that more advanced thinking model DeepSeek-Prover-V2-7B could not solve, showcasing the unique advantage of dLLMs in formal theorem proving.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-Proof introduces the first diffusion-based framework for formal theorem proving, featuring dLLM-Prover-7B for whole-proof generation with long-range coherence and dLLM-Corrector-7B for local proof correction via bi-directional in-filling. It outperforms autoregressive baselines on ProofNet-Test (+1.61% absolute) and MiniF2F-Test (+6.14% absolute), and solves an IMO problem that a stronger model could not.
AI.motivation: AR LLMs struggle with long-range coherence and error accumulation in formal proofs. Diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) offer iterative denoising over multi-token blocks, potentially improving coherence and correction in formal mathematics. This work aims to adapt and evaluate dLLMs for formal theorem proving.
AI.method: Proposes Diffusion-Proof, a two-model framework: (1) dLLM-Prover-7B for generating entire proofs with coherent, long-range tactic usage; (2) dLLM-Corrector-7B, a diffusion-based in-filling correction model that uses bidirectional information to perform local proof corrections. Training and inference procedures are provided to enable end-to-end formal proof generation and correction.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows substantial gains over a same-dataset autoregressive baseline: +1.61 percentage points on ProofNet-Test and +6.14 percentage points on MiniF2F-Test. The framework also solves a challenging IMO problem that DeepSeek-Prover-V2-7B could not solve, indicating the potential advantages of dLLMs in formal proofs.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based LLMs can deliver enhanced long-range coherence and effective local correction in formal theorem proving, outperforming AR baselines on standard benchmarks and solving problems previously out of reach for stronger autoregressive models. This supports diffusion as a promising direction for formal mathematical reasoning with LLMs.
